Result: silent, permanent loss of health/nutrition data (and chat messages) that the UI already reported as saved — an integrity failure for a PII/health app where the user has no indication anything was dropped.

Independent verifier — confirmation
offline-queue.ts:143-167:
const token = currentToken();
...
  if (token) headers['Authorization'] = `Bearer ${token}`;
...
  const res = await fetch(item.url, { method: item.method, headers, ... credentials: 'include' });
  // 2xx = applied; 4xx = definitively rejected ... stop retrying.
  if (res.status < 500) { await this.remove(item.id); }
--- currentToken() offline-queue.ts:41: return new Date(s.expiresAtUtc).getTime() > Date.now() ? s.token : null;
--- interceptor offline.interceptor.ts:81-89 returns synthetic HttpResponse status:202 'Queued (offline)' (optimistic ack) for whitelisted food/exercise/hydration/coffee/supplement/sleep/weight/activity/chat mutations.

Verified against source. The offline interceptor (offline.interceptor.ts:81-89) returns a synthetic 202 to the UI for a narrow whitelist of append-style health/nutrition/chat mutations, so the UI reports success. On flush, currentToken() (offline-queue.ts:35-45) returns null once the persisted session's expiresAtUtc has passed — identical semantics to AuthService.token (auth.ts:138-141), which App-issued short-lived JWTs will hit if the device stays offline past expiry, or if the session is invalidated (force-logout / SessionVersion bump). With a null token, line 150 attaches no Authorization header; the app is bearer-based, so the server answers 401/403. credentials:'include' only adds cookies, which do not authenticate this API. The blanket `if (res.status < 500)` at line 166 then removes the item from IndexedDB — 401/403 are < 500 — so the queued mutation is permanently discarded and never retried, even though a fresh login moments later would have replayed it. The code comment (line 164) mislabels 401/403 as a 'definitive verdict', but an expired/missing token is a transient auth condition, not a rejection of the payload. Net effect: silent, permanent loss of health/PII data and chat messages the UI already acknowledged as saved — a genuine integrity failure with no user indication. Not mitigated elsewhere: the interceptor deliberately does not persist Authorization, relying entirely on flush to re-attach a fresh bearer, and flush has no guard for the token-absent / 401 / 403 case. Fix (prose): do not treat 401/403 (or a null token) as definitive; skip flushing when currentToken() is null and retain items on 401/403 so a later authenticated flush replays them. Corrected line points to the removal decision (166); severity High is appropriate.

Remediation (approach)

Treat authentication-not-ready responses (HTTP 401 and 403) as RETRYABLE rather than definitive: keep the queued item and stop the flush so it is re-attempted after re-authentication. Simplest robust approach: when currentToken() is null, skip the flush entirely (return without touching the queue) and let a later online/SW/login-triggered flush run once a valid bearer exists; additionally, when a fetch returns 401/403, do NOT remove the item — break out of the loop (mirroring the network-error branch) so nothing is discarded on an auth gap. Only remove on 2xx or a genuine client-data rejection (e.g. 400/409/422), not on auth failures. Consider re-running flush from the login success path so a reconnect after token refresh drains the backlog.

High Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

HealthSyncScheduler advances the day cursor BEFORE the pull, so any transient pull failure permanently skips (loses) that day of health data

src/Api/Services/Health/HealthSyncScheduler.cs:137-161 Services & background workers correctness
Evidence
conn.LastSyncCursorDate = day;
await db.HealthConnections.Where(c => c.Id == conn.Id)
    .ExecuteUpdateAsync(s => s.SetProperty(c => c.LastSyncCursorDate, day), ct);
var pull = await provider.PullDayAsync(conn, day, tz, ct);
if (!pull.Ok)
{
    // Back off: stop the window, keep the cursor where it is (we'll resume here next tick).
    status = pull.ToSyncStatus();
    ...
    break;
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The comment claims a failure 'keeps the cursor where it is (we'll resume here next tick)', but the cursor was already persisted to `day` (the day about to be pulled) BEFORE calling PullDayAsync. When the provider returns a TRANSIENT failure (RateLimited or Error — Fitbit returns RateLimited on HTTP 429, which is expected under the keyless/quota-limited API), the loop breaks with the cursor stamped at the failed day. Next tick computes start = cursor.AddDays(1), so the failed day is never re-pulled — a full day of the user's health data (steps, sleep, resting HR, workouts) is silently and permanently lost. This directly contradicts the documented invariant 'RateLimited records the status and retries next tick'. Because LastSyncUtc is also stamped, the connection isn't reconsidered for another 6h (SyncCadence), compounding the loss. The stamp-first pattern is only safe when a re-pull actually happens (crash → restart re-pulls the same day); on an in-process transient failure the code advances past the unpulled day instead of re-pulling it.

Independent verifier — confirmation
HealthSyncScheduler.cs:137-148:
conn.LastSyncCursorDate = day;
await db.HealthConnections.Where(c => c.Id == conn.Id)
    .ExecuteUpdateAsync(s => s.SetProperty(c => c.LastSyncCursorDate, day), ct);
var pull = await provider.PullDayAsync(conn, day, tz, ct);
if (!pull.Ok)
{
    // Back off: stop the window, keep the cursor where it is (we'll resume here next tick).
    status = pull.ToSyncStatus();
    ...
    break;
}
--- resume logic, line 122-123:
var start = conn.LastSyncCursorDate is { } cursor ? cursor.AddDays(1) : ...

Confirmed. At line 137-139 the cursor is stamped AND persisted to `day` (the day about to be pulled) BEFORE PullDayAsync (141). On a transient failure (!pull.Ok — includes RateLimited from HTTP 429 mapped at FitbitHealthProvider.cs:400, and Error), the loop breaks at 148 with the cursor left at the FAILED day. The next-tick resume at line 122-123 computes start = cursor.AddDays(1), i.e. the day AFTER the failed day, so the failed day is never re-pulled — that day's health signals are silently and permanently lost. This directly contradicts the class docstring invariant (line 27: 'RateLimited records the status and retries next tick') and the inline comment (line 144: 'keep the cursor where it is (we'll resume here next tick)') — 'resume here' is false because resume starts at cursor+1, not cursor. The stamp-first pattern would only be crash-safe if a subsequent run re-pulled the same day, but the resume math advances past it, so it is not even truly crash-safe. LastSyncUtc is also stamped at 156-159, so the connection isn't reconsidered for another 6h (SyncCadence), compounding the loss. No de-dup log or other mechanism recovers the skipped day. Severity High is appropriate: silent permanent loss of a full day of user health data on an EXPECTED transient condition (429), for a system handling health PII. Anchored line adjusted to 137 (the stamp-before-pull) which is the root; the finding's cited 133-161 range is accurate.

Remediation (approach)

Only advance LastSyncCursorDate AFTER a day is successfully pulled and mapped, not before. Alternatively keep the stamp-first optimization for crash-safety but, on a non-Ok pull, roll the cursor back to day-1 (the last successfully synced day) before breaking, so the failed day is retried next tick. Distinguish transient (RateLimited/Error) from terminal (AuthExpired) statuses and never let a transient failure move the cursor past an unpulled day.

High Correctness Endpoints correctness & validation CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Cycle PUT /day-log upsert has no unique-violation catch — concurrent same-day writes throw an unhandled 500

src/Api/Endpoints/CycleEndpoints.cs:193-195 Unhandled null/empty & concurrency error path
Evidence
var row = await db.CycleDayLogs
    .FirstOrDefaultAsync(d => d.UserEmail == caller.Email && d.LocalDate == req.Date, ct);
if (row is null)
{
    row = new CycleDayLog { ... };
    db.CycleDayLogs.Add(row);
}
... 
row.UpdatedUtc = now;
await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);   // no try/catch for the unique (UserEmail, LocalDate) index
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

CycleDayLogs has a unique index on (UserEmail, LocalDate) (UsageDbContext.cs:1235). The handler does a find-then-insert with NO catch for a unique violation. Two concurrent PUTs for the same day (e.g. a mobile client double-firing, or two open tabs) both read null and both INSERT; the loser gets a Postgres 23505 that surfaces as an unhandled DbUpdateException -> HTTP 500 on this sensitive health surface. The near-identical sibling JournalEndpoints.PUT /day (JournalEndpoints.cs:127-144) explicitly catches TrackerVisibility.IsUniqueViolation, reloads, and re-applies — CycleEndpoints omits that guard entirely.

Independent verifier — confirmation
CycleEndpoints.cs:166-193 — `var row = await db.CycleDayLogs.FirstOrDefaultAsync(d => d.UserEmail == caller.Email && d.LocalDate == req.Date, ct); if (row is null) { row = new CycleDayLog {...}; db.CycleDayLogs.Add(row); } ... row.UpdatedUtc = now; await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);` (no try/catch). UsageDbContext.cs:1235 — `e.HasIndex(x => new { x.UserEmail, x.LocalDate }).IsUnique();`. Contrast JournalEndpoints.cs:127-144 — `try { await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct); } catch (DbUpdateException ex) when (TrackerVisibility.IsUniqueViolation(ex)) { db.ChangeTracker.Clear(); row = await db.JournalEntries.FirstAsync(...); ... await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct); }`.

Verified all three legs. Unique index on (UserEmail, LocalDate) exists (UsageDbContext.cs:1235). The Cycle PUT /day-log handler does a find-then-conditional-insert and calls SaveChangesAsync at line 193 with no catch for a unique-violation. Two concurrent PUTs for the same day both read null, both Add, and the loser gets Postgres 23505 surfacing as an unhandled DbUpdateException -> HTTP 500. The near-identical sibling JournalEndpoints PUT /day (127-144) explicitly catches TrackerVisibility.IsUniqueViolation, clears the tracker, reloads the winning row, re-applies the partial fields, and re-saves — proving this is the codebase's own established pattern that Cycle omits. Real error-path gap on a sensitive health surface; High is defensible given the demonstrated sibling fix, though the trigger is a transient double-write race rather than a deterministic every-request failure. Anchored on line 193 (the unguarded SaveChangesAsync).

Remediation (approach)

Wrap the SaveChangesAsync in the same try/catch the Journal sibling uses: on TrackerVisibility.IsUniqueViolation, clear the change tracker, reload the winning row by (email, date), re-apply the partial-field mutations, and save again. Apply the same pattern to any other find-then-insert upsert in this file.

High Correctness Frontend correctness CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

TrackerStore.load() has no request-generation guard — stale response wins, can render another user's health day

src/Web/src/app/core/tracker-store.ts:66-106 Race conditions on rapid navigation
Evidence
async load(): Promise<void> {
  this.loading.set(true);
  this.error.set(null);
  try {
    const day = await firstValueFrom(this.api.trackerDay(this.date(), this.viewUser() ?? undefined));
    this.day.set(day);
  } ...
}
// viewUserTracker/setDate/shiftDate all just: this.<sig>.set(x); await this.load();
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

load() awaits an HTTP GET then unconditionally does this.day.set(day) with no check that date()/viewUser() still match what was requested. The tracker page fires load() on every prev/next-day tap (shiftDate at tracker.ts:616/619), on date-picker change, and on switching whose tracker you view (viewUserTracker, tracker.ts:701/704). If two loads overlap (trivial on a phone: tap next-day twice, or switch view-user while a day is still loading), whichever RESPONSE resolves last wins — not whichever the user last requested. Because viewUser() gates whether add/delete controls show (readOnly computed) and whose macros/weight/sleep render, a slow response for user A can overwrite the freshly-loaded self view (or vice-versa), showing one person's health/PII data under another person's selected context, or the wrong calendar day's totals. readOnly even falls back to viewUser() !== null, so the controls can desync from the rendered data.

Independent verifier — confirmation
tracker-store.ts:62-73 — async load(): Promise<void> { this.loading.set(true); this.error.set(null); try { const day = await firstValueFrom(this.api.trackerDay(this.date(), this.viewUser() ?? undefined)); this.day.set(day); } ... }  |  line 47: readonly readOnly = computed(() => this.day()?.readOnly ?? this.viewUser() !== null);  |  lines 103-106: viewUserTracker(userId){ this.viewUser.set(userId); await this.load(); }  |  tracker.ts:615-625,701-704 fire these via `void` (shiftDate/setDate/viewUserTracker) with no awaiting/guarding.

Confirmed by reading tracker-store.ts (full class) and tracker.ts call sites. load() awaits the HTTP GET and unconditionally does this.day.set(day) with no request-generation/sequence guard, AbortController, or switchMap-style cancellation, and it does not re-check that date()/viewUser() still equal the requested values. All entry points (setDate/shiftDate/viewUserTracker) mutate the signal then await load(), and the component invokes them fire-and-forget via `void` (tracker.ts 616,619,625,701,704), so overlapping loads are trivially reachable on a phone (double next-day tap, or switch view-user mid-load). Whichever RESPONSE resolves last wins, not whichever the user last requested — so a slow response for another user (viewUser targets another AppUser id) can overwrite the self view, rendering one person's health/PII day under another person's selected context, or the wrong calendar day's totals. The readOnly computed (line 47) additionally falls back to viewUser() !== null, so add/delete control visibility can desync from the rendered data. Location and High severity are accurate; note the server still authorizes each request per-user, so this is a UI context/PII-confusion + control-desync race, not an auth bypass — still High for a health/PII surface. Corrected line points at the unconditional set path (66-67).

Remediation (approach)

Add a monotonically-increasing request sequence (or an AbortController / switchMap-style latest-wins). Capture the seq (and ideally the requested date+viewUser) before the await, and only apply this.day.set(day) / this.error.set(...) / this.loading.set(false) if that seq is still the latest issued. Discard results from superseded loads so only the last-requested date+user can mutate the visible signals.

High Correctness Frontend correctness CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Offline interceptor queues PUT edits and DELETEs for tracker mutations, contradicting its own "append-only" safety invariant and creating replay data-corruption

src/Web/src/app/core/offline.interceptor.ts:30-40 offline-queue correctness
Evidence
const QUEUEABLE_URL_SUBSTRINGS = [
  '/api/tracker/food',        // log a food
  ...
const QUEUEABLE_METHODS = new Set(['POST', 'PUT', 'PATCH', 'DELETE']);
...
function isQueueableUrl(url: string): boolean {
  for (const sub of QUEUEABLE_URL_SUBSTRINGS) {
    if (!url.includes(sub)) continue;
    if (sub === '/api/chat/channels/') return url.includes('/messages');
    return true;
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The module docstring and the whitelist comment both promise the queue is limited to "APPEND-style, owner-scoped, replay-safe" writes and that "any DELETE/record-edit" is "left completely untouched and errors normally". But QUEUEABLE_METHODS includes PUT and DELETE, and isQueueableUrl matches by substring, so `PUT /api/tracker/food/{id}` (updateFood — a record EDIT) and `DELETE /api/tracker/food|hydration|coffee|supplement|sleep/{id}` and `DELETE /api/tracker/activity` all match. When offline (status===0) these are silently queued and the UI is given an optimistic 202 success. A queued EDIT replayed minutes/hours later on the next 'online' wake will clobber any newer edit the user (or another device) made to that same row in the meantime — last-writer-wins with a stale body — corrupting logged health/nutrition data. A queued DELETE reports success to the user immediately even though the row is still present until replay, so the day roll-up the user sees (after the store's load()) can disagree with the server, and the deletion silently applies later. This is exactly the class of irreversible/order-sensitive replay the interceptor documents it excludes.

Independent verifier — confirmation
offline.interceptor.ts:15-16 docstring: "any DELETE/record-edit... is left completely untouched and errors normally." BUT line 30: const QUEUEABLE_METHODS = new Set(['POST', 'PUT', 'PATCH', 'DELETE']); and lines 33-39 isQueueableUrl uses url.includes(sub). Confirmed edit/delete endpoints in api.ts: L994 put(`/tracker/food/${id}`) [updateFood — record EDIT], L999 delete(`/tracker/food/${id}`), L1625/1635/1645/1655 delete hydration/coffee/supplement/sleep, L1664 put(`/tracker/activity`), L1669 delete(`/tracker/activity`) — all match the '/api/tracker/food|hydration|coffee|supplement|sleep|activity' substrings and are PUT/DELETE, so all are queued + optimistically 202'd when offline.

Verified against the actual code. The interceptor's own docstring guarantees the queue is append-only and explicitly excludes "any DELETE/record-edit," yet QUEUEABLE_METHODS (line 30) includes PUT and DELETE and isQueueableUrl matches by substring, so record-edit (PUT /api/tracker/food/{id} = updateFood, confirmed api.ts:993-995) and multiple DELETEs (api.ts:999,1625,1635,1645,1655,1669) plus PUT /api/tracker/activity (upsertActivity, api.ts:1663-1664) are all queued and given a synthetic 202 when offline (status===0). The status===0 guard only prevents double-APPLY of appends; it does NOT make an EDIT replay order-safe: a queued stale-body PUT food-edit replayed minutes/hours later is last-writer-wins and clobbers any newer edit to the same row from another device/session, corrupting logged health/nutrition data — exactly the order-sensitive/irreversible class the module claims to exclude. Queued DELETEs also give the user an immediate false success while the row persists server-side until replay, so the store's reloaded day roll-up can disagree with the server. Real defect, correctly located, contradicts the stated safety invariant; High severity is appropriate (user-owned health-data integrity, though requires the offline-write + concurrent-newer-edit window). Best line anchor is line 30 (the method set) alongside the docstring at lines 12-16.

Remediation (approach)

Restrict the offline queue to genuinely append-only, idempotent-on-replay writes. Remove PUT and DELETE from QUEUEABLE_METHODS (keep POST, and PATCH only if every whitelisted PATCH target is truly append-style), OR make isQueueableUrl match the exact append endpoints (e.g. require the path to END at the collection segment with no trailing /{id}) so that record-scoped edits and deletes fall through to normal error handling. Re-audit each whitelisted substring against the actual verb the tracker uses, and align the code with the documented invariant so edits/deletes error normally when offline instead of being optimistically acked.

High Data & Correctness EF entities & migrations CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Deleting a user orphans all their email-keyed PII/health data (no FK, no cleanup) — GDPR/retention gap

src/Api/Endpoints/UsersEndpoints.cs:201-207 EF entities & migrations integrity
Evidence
var user = await db.Users.Include(u => u.Permissions).FirstOrDefaultAsync(u => u.Id == id, ct);
...
var removedEmail = user.Email;
db.Users.Remove(user);
await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The admin 'delete user' only cascades to entities that have a configured FK to AppUser (UserPermissions, SavedView, GoogleCalendarConnection). The overwhelming majority of user-owned data — health data (Medication, MedicationLog, VitalReading, CycleProfile, CyclePeriod, CycleDayLog, SleepEntry), JournalEntry, all tracker entities, ChatMessage, Notification, ActivityEvent, HealthConnection, HabitPact, etc. — is keyed by a plain UserEmail STRING with no FK relationship to AppUser (see UsageDbContext.cs entity configs, e.g. CycleProfile ln 1204-1212, Medication ln 1239-1255, JournalEntry ln 1287-1299). Because there is no FK and no email-based cleanup in the delete handler, deleting the AppUser row silently leaves all of that health/PII data (cycle, meds, vitals, journals, GPS-linked activity) in the database indefinitely. For an app that advertises handling health data and location, this is a data-retention/right-to-erasure defect: a 'deleted' user's most sensitive records survive, and if the same email later re-registers they inherit the stale rows.

Independent verifier — confirmation
UsersEndpoints.cs:201-205: `var removedEmail = user.Email; db.Users.Remove(user); await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct); await tx.CommitAsync(ct); await audit.LogAsync("user.deleted", removedEmail, null, ct);` — removedEmail is used ONLY for the audit log, never to purge data. UsageDbContext.cs:224-225: AppUser's ONLY relationship is `e.HasMany(x => x.Permissions).WithOne(...).HasForeignKey(p => p.UserId).OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade)`. UsageDbContext.cs:1206/1211 CycleProfile, 1241/1254 Medication, 1289/1298 JournalEntry are all `x.UserEmail` with `HasIndex` only — no FK to AppUser (grep for `HasForeignKey(x => x.UserEmail)`/`HasOne<AppUser>` returned zero matches).

Verified by reading the delete handler (UsersEndpoints.cs:188-208) and the entity configs. AppUser has exactly one configured FK relationship — Permissions (cascade via UserId, line 224-225). All health/PII/tracker entities are keyed by a plain UserEmail string with HasIndex only; a grep for any FK on UserEmail or any HasOne<AppUser> relationship returned zero matches. The delete handler removes only the AppUser row (cascading solely to UserPermissions) and captures removedEmail purely for the audit log — no email-based cleanup, and no user-deletion event handler exists anywhere in src/Api (grep confirmed). Deleting a user therefore orphans cycle/med/vitals/journal/activity data indefinitely, and a re-registering email would inherit the stale rows. Genuine right-to-erasure/data-retention gap for an app advertising health + location data. Severity High and the line/file locations are accurate.

Remediation (approach)

Make user deletion (and disablement, if erasure is intended) explicitly purge or anonymize all email-keyed owned data in the same transaction — enumerate every UserEmail/OwnerEmail/SharerEmail-keyed table and delete rows for the removed email, or introduce real AppUser FKs with cascade for owned data so the database enforces cleanup. At minimum, document and implement a data-erasure routine so 'delete user' does not leave orphaned health/PII rows. Consider a scheduled orphan-sweep as a backstop.

High Data & Correctness EF entities & migrations CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

ModelPricing rows are HasData-seeded with explicit ids 1–14, so the Postgres identity sequence is never advanced — the first ~14 runtime inserts via POST /api/pricing throw duplicate-PK (23505)

src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:162-163 Seed logic correctness / identity-sequence integrity
Evidence
e.HasIndex(x => x.ModelPattern).IsUnique();
            e.HasData(PricingSeed.Rows);   // PricingSeed sets Id = 1..14 explicitly
// runtime insert with no explicit Id (ApiEndpoints.cs:154-163):
//   var row = new Data.Entities.ModelPricing { ModelPattern = ..., ... };
//   db.ModelPricings.Add(row);  await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

ModelPricing.Id is a Postgres GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY column. HasData seeds ids 1–14 with explicit values, which do NOT advance the column's identity sequence (it still starts at 1). The pricing-manage endpoint POST /api/pricing (src/Api/Endpoints/ApiEndpoints.cs:151-164) inserts a new ModelPricing WITHOUT setting Id, so the DB generates Id=1,2,3,… — every one of which collides with an already-seeded row and raises a unique-violation on PK_ModelPricings. An admin adding a custom model price gets a 500 error, repeatedly, until the sequence eventually climbs past 14. This is a latent production break of the pricing-management feature (a permissioned admin action) that only manifests after deploy against a freshly-seeded DB.

Independent verifier — confirmation
UsageDbContextModelSnapshot.cs:3830-3834 — b.Property<int>("Id").ValueGeneratedOnAdd()...; NpgsqlPropertyBuilderExtensions.UseIdentityByDefaultColumn(b.Property<int>("Id")); | UsageDbContext.cs:162 — e.HasData(PricingSeed.Rows); | PricingSeed.cs:15-69 — new() { Id = 1, ... } ... new() { Id = 14, ... } (explicit Ids 1..14) | ApiEndpoints.cs:154-163 — var row = new Data.Entities.ModelPricing { ModelPattern = ... }; db.ModelPricings.Add(row); await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct); (no explicit Id set)

Verified against all four relevant artifacts. The snapshot confirms ModelPricing.Id is a Postgres GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY column (UseIdentityByDefaultColumn). PricingSeed.Rows seeds 14 rows with explicit Id = 1..14 (Ids 1-14 all present), applied via HasData -> migrationBuilder.InsertData, which under Npgsql does NOT advance the identity sequence. I grepped all migrations for any ALTER SEQUENCE / RESTART and found none, so the sequence still starts at 1. The POST /api/pricing endpoint (ApiEndpoints.cs:151-164, permission-gated PricingManage) builds a ModelPricing without setting Id and calls SaveChangesAsync, so the DB generates Id=1,2,3,... each colliding with an already-seeded PK -> 23505 unique violation on the primary key -> 500. The feature self-heals only after ~14 attempts push the sequence past 14. This is a genuine latent production break of a shipped admin feature on a freshly-seeded DB; caveats (admin-only, POST-create only, self-healing) make it defensibly High but arguably Medium. Cited file is correct; anchor line for the root cause is the HasData call at UsageDbContext.cs:162 (the endpoint is a secondary site at ApiEndpoints.cs:161). Remediation in prose: after seeding explicit-id rows, advance the identity sequence to MAX(Id)+1 (e.g. emit a raw ALTER ... RESTART / setval in a migration), or seed the rows at runtime via the DbSet so the sequence advances naturally, or exclude Id from HasData and let all rows be DB-generated.

Remediation (approach)

After seeding HasData rows with explicit ids on a Postgres identity column, reset the table's identity sequence to MAX(id)+1 (e.g. run a setval on the pricing table's identity sequence in a migration, or in the startup seed block after MigrateAsync). Alternatively, stop assigning explicit ids in PricingSeed and seed the rows at runtime like IngestionSources/ExerciseLibrary (which use Add without ids and therefore advance the sequence correctly). Apply the same audit to any other HasData table that is also runtime-insertable.

High Data & Privacy Logging / PII / DTO exposure CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Health, GPS and other sensitive response bodies are captured into RequestLog and replayed to any activity.view holder (cross-permission PII leak)

src/Api/Infrastructure/RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:36-40 PII in request/response logs
Evidence
private static readonly string[] BodyExcluded =
{
    "/api/users", "/api/audit", "/api/access-policy",
    "/api/chat/contacts", "/api/chat/directory",
};
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The middleware captures and persists the request/response body of EVERY /api route not in Excluded/BodyExcluded, and GET /api/logs (gated only by Permissions.ActivityView) returns those bodies verbatim (ObservabilityEndpoints.cs lines 76-77). BodyExcluded/Excluded do NOT cover the app's most sensitive read endpoints: GET /api/family/cycle (menstrual periods, symptoms, day-logs — special-category health data; CycleEndpoints.cs:95-120), GET /api/meds and /api/vitals (medication names/doses, vitals — health data; MedsEndpoints.cs:120/347), GET /api/family/locations and /api/location/admin (PRECISE lat/lng GPS; LocationDtos.cs:56-99,159-167). LogRedaction only masks secret KEYS, not these values, so a full JSON of another person's cycle symptoms, medications, or exact coordinates lands in the RequestLog table. Any user holding activity.view — a DISTINCT permission from cycle.track / location.view-all / the owner scope — can read them back through /api/logs, defeating the household/owner scoping the source endpoints enforce. This is a cross-permission escalation to special-category health data and precise location.

Independent verifier — confirmation
RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:36-40 BodyExcluded = { "/api/users", "/api/audit", "/api/access-policy", "/api/chat/contacts", "/api/chat/directory" }; line 85 ResponseBody = capture is not null ? CaptureResponseBody(...) : null. ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:76-77 RequestBody = r.RequestBody, ResponseBody = r.ResponseBody; line 83 .RequireAuthorization().RequirePermission(Permissions.ActivityView). CycleEndpoints.cs:90-92 MapGroup("/api/family/cycle").RequirePermission(Permissions.CycleTrack); MedsEndpoints.cs:115/347 "/api/meds","/api/vitals" .RequirePermission(Permissions.TrackerSelf); LocationEndpoints.cs:165/200 "/api/location/admin" .RequirePermission(Permissions.LocationViewAll); FamilyLocationsEndpoints.cs:52 GET "/locations". LogRedaction.cs:17 SecretKeys masks only secret KEYS; IsSensitivePath (28-33) covers only auth/notifications/shares/ingest-keys/family/calendar — none of cycle/meds/vitals/locations.

Verified end-to-end. The middleware captures request/response bodies of every /api route not in Excluded (line 23-28) or BodyExcluded (36-40). BodyExcluded covers only email-bearing admin paths; it does NOT include /api/family/cycle (menstrual periods/symptoms/day-logs — health data), /api/meds & /api/vitals (medication names/doses/vitals), /api/family/locations & /api/location/admin (precise lat/lng GPS). LogRedaction masks only secret KEYS (token/password/etc.) and a small path allowlist (auth/notifications/shares/ingest-keys/family-calendar) — none of these sensitive endpoints — so full JSON of symptoms, meds, and coordinates lands verbatim in RequestLog.ResponseBody (line 85). GET /api/logs replays RequestBody/ResponseBody verbatim (ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:76-77) gated ONLY by Permissions.ActivityView (line 83), which is a DISTINCT permission from CycleTrack / TrackerSelf / LocationViewAll and from the owner-scoping the source endpoints enforce (e.g. cycle is owner-only per CycleEndpoints.cs:12-13,101). Thus an activity.view holder can read back another household member's special-category health data and precise location, defeating the source scoping. Real cross-permission escalation; High severity and cited file/lines are accurate. correctedLine set to 36 (the BodyExcluded array, the fix locus); the replay sink is ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:76-83.

Remediation (approach)

Treat sensitive read surfaces the same way the admin email paths are treated: add the health/location route prefixes (family/cycle, meds, vitals, location, family/locations, and any other endpoint that emits health, GPS, or private household data) to a body-suppression list so the request line is still logged but the bodies are never persisted; or invert the policy to an allowlist where bodies are captured only for an explicitly-approved set of low-sensitivity routes. Alternatively gate body capture on data classification metadata attached to each endpoint rather than a hand-maintained path denylist that must be updated every time a sensitive endpoint is added.

High Data & Privacy Logging / PII / DTO exposure CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

RequestLoggingMiddleware captures REQUEST bodies of health/cycle/meds/location mutations verbatim (not just responses)

src/Api/Infrastructure/RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:55 PII / secrets in request logs
Evidence
var captureBodies = !SkipBodyCapture(path);
var requestBody = captureBodies ? await CaptureRequestBodyAsync(ctx.Request) : null;
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Prior rounds flagged that sensitive RESPONSE bodies are captured and replayed. The symmetric, distinct vector is the inbound REQUEST body: PUT /api/cycle/day-log carries reproductive-health free-text (Mood, Symptoms, FlowLevel, Intimacy, Protected, Notes — CycleEndpoints.cs:182-190), POST/PUT /api/meds carries medication names+doses, POST /api/vitals carries blood-pressure/weight, and POST /api/location carries precise Lat/Lng the user submits. None of these paths are in BodyExcluded or IsSensitivePath, so captureBodies stays true and CaptureRequestBodyAsync stores the raw submitted body into RequestLog.RequestBody, which GET /api/logs replays in full to any activity.view holder. activity.view is an independently-grantable 'Admin' permission (Permissions.cs:239) distinct from cycle/meds/location.view-all, so a holder who was never granted health or location access reads everyone's raw intimate-health and GPS input from the action log.

Independent verifier — confirmation
RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:53-55: `var captureBodies = !SkipBodyCapture(path); ... var requestBody = captureBodies ? await CaptureRequestBodyAsync(ctx.Request) : null;` — SkipBodyCapture only matches BodyExcluded = {/api/users,/api/audit,/api/access-policy,/api/chat/contacts,/api/chat/directory} (lines 36-40). LogRedaction.cs:28-33 IsSensitivePath covers only /api/auth,/api/notifications,/api/shares,/api/ingest-keys,/api/family/calendar; field-level SecretKeys (line 16-17) mask only token/password/etc. ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:38,76,83: GET /api/logs returns r.RequestBody, `.RequirePermission(Permissions.ActivityView)`. Permissions.cs:167 ActivityView is an Admin key distinct from cycle.track(96)/location.self+view-all(113-116). LocationEndpoints.cs:64-79 POST body req.Lat/req.Lng; CycleEndpoints.cs:182-190 Mood/Symptoms/FlowLevel/Intimacy/Protected/Notes.

Confirmed. Inbound REQUEST bodies are captured verbatim (line 55) for all logged /api paths except the five email-bearing admin paths in BodyExcluded. Reproductive-health mutations (PUT /api/family/cycle/day-log: Mood/Symptoms/FlowLevel/Intimacy/Protected/Notes), medication mutations (/api/meds), vitals (/api/vitals: BP/weight), and precise GPS input (POST /api/location: Lat/Lng) are NOT in BodyExcluded and NOT in IsSensitivePath, so they are stored raw in RequestLog.RequestBody. Field-level redaction only masks secret KEYS (token/password/apiKey/etc.), never health free-text or coordinates. GET /api/logs (ObservabilityEndpoints.cs) replays RequestBody gated ONLY by activity.view — a standalone Admin permission (Permissions.cs:167) independent of cycle.track/location.view-all/meds view. So an activity.view holder with no health or location grant reads everyone's raw intimate-health and GPS submissions. Distinct from prior response-body findings; the request-body vector is real. Two minor inaccuracies in the finding text (non-disqualifying): the cycle route is /api/family/cycle not /api/cycle, and the replay endpoint is ObservabilityEndpoints.cs (the finding said 'GET /api/logs', which is correct, though it cross-referenced fields via CycleEndpoints correctly). Cited file, lines 53-55, and evidence quote are exact. High severity upheld.

Remediation (approach)

Do not persist request bodies for the health/reproductive/medication/location/vitals path families. Extend the body-suppression allowlist to cover every domain that carries same-user special-category PII in its inbound payload (cycle, meds, vitals, location, sleep notes, journal), or invert the model to an allowlist of paths whose bodies are safe to log. The request line (method/path/status/timing/bytes) can still be recorded for diagnostics with the body dropped, exactly as done for the admin email paths.

High Data & Privacy Logging / PII / DTO exposure CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

BodyExcluded allowlist is scoped only to cross-user EMAIL paths and structurally cannot protect same-user special-category PII (cycle/meds/GPS/vitals)

src/Api/Infrastructure/RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:36-40 PII / DTO exposure
Evidence
private static readonly string[] BodyExcluded =
{
    "/api/users", "/api/audit", "/api/access-policy",
    "/api/chat/contacts", "/api/chat/directory",
};
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The suppression list was designed for one threat only — leaking OTHER users' email addresses out of admin pickers (see the header comment lines 30-35). By construction it omits every health, reproductive, medication, vitals, and GPS path, so the middleware treats the app's MOST sensitive data (reproductive/intimacy logs, meds, precise coordinates) as freely loggable while carefully protecting a comparatively low-sensitivity email field. This is a second-order design gap: as new sensitive domains ship (cycle, meds, vitals, sleep, journal all post-date this list), each is captured by default with no gate. An enterprise handling health data under GDPR Art. 9 / HIPAA-adjacent expectations must default-deny body capture for special-category data, not default-allow.

Independent verifier — confirmation
RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:36-40 — private static readonly string[] BodyExcluded = { "/api/users", "/api/audit", "/api/access-policy", "/api/chat/contacts", "/api/chat/directory", }; ... header (30-35) states it exists because these "admin paths carry OTHER users' emails". LogRedaction.cs:28-33 IsSensitivePath only covers /api/auth, /api/notifications, /api/shares, /api/ingest-keys, /api/family/calendar; Redact (41-43) only strips SecretKeys (token|password|secret|...). Sensitive endpoints exist and are NOT covered: CycleEndpoints.cs:90 MapGroup("/api/family/cycle"), MedsEndpoints.cs:115 "/api/meds" + 347 "/api/vitals", JournalEndpoints.cs:76 "/api/journal", LocationEndpoints.cs:37 "/api/location", TrackerEndpoints.cs:119 "/api/tracker".

Verified against actual code. BodyExcluded (lines 36-40) is by construction an email-only allowlist (header comment lines 30-35 says so). The only other body-suppression gate, LogRedaction.IsSensitivePath (LogRedaction.cs:28-33), covers auth/notifications/shares/ingest-keys/family-calendar — NOT cycle/meds/vitals/journal/location/tracker. Field-level redaction (Redact, lines 41-43) only matches secret KEY NAMES (token/password/secret/etc.), so health/reproductive/GPS VALUES pass through verbatim and are persisted to RequestLog with request AND response bodies. I confirmed these sensitive endpoints exist and are unguarded: /api/family/cycle, /api/meds, /api/vitals, /api/journal, /api/location, /api/tracker. Concretely, GET /api/family/cycle/ (CycleEndpoints.cs:95-120) returns the owner's periods + private day-logs, captured and redacted only for secret keys, then replayable to any activity.view holder. Note /api/health IS fully excluded from logging (Excluded, line 25), so that one specific path is safe — but that does not cover the other special-category domains. Severity High is appropriate: special-category (GDPR Art.9) data lands default-allow in an internal activity log, though scope is limited to authenticated activity.view holders (not an unauthenticated public leak), which is why it is High rather than Critical. Finding is correctly located at lines 36-40; anchoring to line 36.

Remediation (approach)

Re-architect the capture decision as default-deny for bodies: capture bodies only for an explicit allowlist of non-sensitive diagnostic paths, or classify every endpoint's data sensitivity and suppress bodies for anything above a threshold. Add a regression test asserting that a representative request to each sensitive domain produces a RequestLog row whose RequestBody/ResponseBody is null. Document the sensitivity classification so new domains inherit suppression by default.

High Infrastructure & Operations CloudFormation / Caddy / TLS CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

EC2 instance does not enforce IMDSv2, exposing role credentials (SSM secret + KMS decrypt) to SSRF

deploy/cloudformation.yaml:85-95 IAM least-privilege / instance credential theft
Evidence
Instance:
    Type: AWS::EC2::Instance
    Properties:
      InstanceType: !Ref InstanceType
      ImageId: !Ref LatestAmiId
      SubnetId: !Ref SubnetId
      SecurityGroupIds: [!Ref SecurityGroup]
      IamInstanceProfile: !Ref InstanceProfile
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The Instance block has no MetadataOptions/HttpTokens: required, so IMDSv1 stays enabled by default. The InstanceProfile grants ssm:GetParameter(s)/ByPath on /usage-iq/* plus kms:Decrypt. This host runs a public-internet SPA/API that fetches external food/AI providers (USDA, FatSecret, WorkoutX, Gemini) — a single SSRF or RCE in that app can hit the unauthenticated IMDSv1 endpoint (169.254.169.254) with no token, steal the instance role's temporary credentials, and then read and decrypt every /usage-iq/* SecureString (JWT signing key, DB password, Google OAuth secret). IMDSv2's token requirement is the standard mitigation that blocks the classic SSRF-to-credential-theft chain (Capital One-class incident).

Independent verifier — confirmation
deploy/cloudformation.yaml:85-92
  Instance:
    Type: AWS::EC2::Instance
    Properties:
      InstanceType: !Ref InstanceType
      ImageId: !Ref LatestAmiId
      SubnetId: !Ref SubnetId
      SecurityGroupIds: [!Ref SecurityGroup]
      IamInstanceProfile: !Ref InstanceProfile
(no MetadataOptions/HttpTokens block anywhere in the Instance properties, lines 85-107)
--- role grant, lines 72-77 ---
                Action: [ssm:GetParameter, ssm:GetParameters, ssm:GetParametersByPath]
                Resource: !Sub arn:aws:ssm:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:parameter/usage-iq/*
              - Effect: Allow
                Action: kms:Decrypt

Verified against the actual template. The AWS::EC2::Instance (lines 85-107) has no MetadataOptions, so HttpTokens defaults to 'optional' and IMDSv1 (unauthenticated 169.254.169.254) remains reachable. The attached InstanceProfile (line 92) -> InstanceRole grants ssm:GetParameter(s)/ByPath on /usage-iq/* (lines 72-73) and kms:Decrypt via SSM (lines 74-77), so credentials harvested from IMDS can read and decrypt every /usage-iq/* SecureString (JWT key, DB password, OAuth secret). The SecurityGroup opens 80/443 to 0.0.0.0/0 (lines 50-51), making this a public internet-facing host; app code makes outbound calls to external providers (SSRF surface), and a grep confirms no MetadataOptions/HttpTokens exists anywhere in the repo (only unrelated Discord SSRF-guard test files). No compensating control mitigates the classic SSRF/RCE-to-IMDSv1 credential-theft chain. Severity High is correct. Anchor line adjusted to 85 (the Instance resource declaration).

Remediation (approach)

Add a MetadataOptions block to the EC2 instance that requires IMDSv2 (set HttpTokens to required), and set a small HttpPutResponseHopLimit (1 for host processes, 2 if containers must reach it) so credentials are not reachable via an unauthenticated IMDSv1 request from an SSRF vector.

High Infrastructure & Operations CloudFormation / Caddy / TLS CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Deploy role can run arbitrary root shell on the PII/health/payments host via AWS-RunShellScript

deploy/cloudformation.yaml:150-151 IAM least-privilege
Evidence
- Sid: SsmSendToThisInstance
  Effect: Allow
  Action: ssm:SendCommand
  Resource:
    - !Sub arn:aws:ssm:${AWS::Region}::document/AWS-RunShellScript
    - !Sub arn:aws:ec2:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:instance/${Instance}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The grant is correctly scoped to one instance, but the document it permits is the generic AWS-RunShellScript, which executes attacker-chosen shell as root. Any compromise of the GitHub OIDC deploy role (leaked workflow token, malicious PR that alters the deploy step, dependency in the runner) yields full root RCE on the single host that holds Postgres (PII, cycle/meds health data, payments), the decrypted JWT signing key, and the DB password in its .env. Prior rounds flagged only the ability to READ command results account-wide; the ability to WRITE/execute arbitrary commands via the open-ended RunShellScript document is a distinct and larger blast radius.

Independent verifier — confirmation
deploy/cloudformation.yaml:146-151
          - Sid: SsmSendToThisInstance
            Effect: Allow
            Action: ssm:SendCommand
            Resource:
              - !Sub arn:aws:ssm:${AWS::Region}::document/AWS-RunShellScript
              - !Sub arn:aws:ec2:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:instance/${Instance}
--- corroboration ---
.github/workflows/deploy.yml:48  --document-name AWS-RunShellScript (runs arbitrary "commands" as root)
deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:9-11,28  Postgres + Jwt__Key + DB_PASSWORD colocated on the same single Instance

Verified against the actual file. The deploy policy grants ssm:SendCommand scoped to the generic AWS-managed AWS-RunShellScript document (line 150) targeting the single app instance (line 151). AWS-RunShellScript executes caller-supplied shell; on Linux the SSM agent runs it as root and no run-as constraint is set, so this is effectively arbitrary root RCE on that host. deploy.yml:44-48 confirms the document is actually invoked with free-form "commands". docker-compose.prod.yml confirms that same single instance runs Postgres (all PII/health/payments data), holds Jwt__Key (JWT signing key, line 28), DB_PASSWORD, and other provider secrets via .env — so a compromise of the OIDC deploy role yields full data + key exfiltration. This is distinct from the previously-flagged "*"-scoped ssm read results (SsmReadResults, lines 152-155): that is READ; this is WRITE/EXECUTE, a materially larger blast radius. Instance scoping limits WHICH host but not WHAT runs. Least-privilege fix (prose): replace the AWS-RunShellScript grant with a purpose-built custom SSM document that runs only the fixed deploy script (no free-form command parameter), and grant SendCommand only on that document ARN. High severity is justified by the concentration of crown-jewel data/keys on the single target.

Remediation (approach)

Replace the AWS-RunShellScript grant with a purpose-built, version-pinned custom SSM document that runs only the fixed remote-deploy step (no free-form commandline parameter), and restrict ssm:SendCommand to that document ARN. Additionally constrain SendCommand with a condition on the document version and, where possible, the ssm:resourceTag/Project=usage-iq tag so the deploy identity cannot be repurposed to run ad-hoc shell.

High Infrastructure & Operations CloudFormation / Caddy / TLS CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

AMI auto-resolves per deploy, so an 'idempotent' re-run can replace the instance and destroy all Postgres data

deploy/cloudformation.yaml:25-27 No backups / disaster recovery
Evidence
LatestAmiId:
  Type: AWS::SSM::Parameter::Value<AWS::EC2::Image::Id>
  Default: /aws/service/ami-amazon-linux-latest/al2023-ami-kernel-default-x86_64
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

ImageId is bound to a public SSM parameter that resolves to whatever the newest AL2023 AMI is at each 'cloudformation deploy'. provision.sh (and the README) advertise re-running as 'idempotent - safe to re-run', but when AWS publishes a new AMI the resolved value changes, CloudFormation detects an ImageId change and REPLACES the EC2 instance. The root EBS volume is DeleteOnTermination: true and the Postgres/Caddy data live in Docker named volumes on that root volume, so a routine re-provision silently destroys all PII/health/payment data and the ACME cert state. This is a second-order interaction between the auto-resolving AMI, the single-volume layout, and the no-backup posture that earlier rounds did not connect.

Independent verifier — confirmation
cloudformation.yaml:25-27:
  LatestAmiId:
    Type: AWS::SSM::Parameter::Value<AWS::EC2::Image::Id>
    Default: /aws/service/ami-amazon-linux-latest/al2023-ami-kernel-default-x86_64
cloudformation.yaml:89: ImageId: !Ref LatestAmiId
cloudformation.yaml:95: Ebs: { VolumeSize: !Ref DiskSizeGb, VolumeType: gp3, DeleteOnTermination: true, Encrypted: true }
provision.sh:38: # 3. Create everything (idempotent — safe to re-run).
provision.sh:40-45: aws cloudformation deploy ... --parameter-overrides VpcId="$VPC" SubnetId="$SUBNET"  (no LatestAmiId pin)
docker-compose.prod.yml:12-13,63,67-69: pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data, caddy_data:/data; volumes: pgdata: caddy_data: caddy_config:

Verified against the actual files. LatestAmiId (lines 25-27) is a public "latest" SSM AMI parameter that AWS updates whenever a new AL2023 AMI ships; it feeds ImageId on the EC2 instance (line 89). ImageId is an immutable property on AWS::EC2::Instance — a changed value forces CloudFormation to REPLACE the instance. The root volume (lines 93-95) is the only EBS volume, DeleteOnTermination: true, and Postgres data (pgdata) plus Caddy ACME state (caddy_data) live in Docker named volumes on that root filesystem (docker-compose.prod.yml). No backup mechanism exists anywhere in deploy/ (no RDS, no EBS snapshots, no AWS::Backup). provision.sh re-runs `cloudformation deploy` with no LatestAmiId override and both the script (line 38) and README advertise re-running as "idempotent — safe to re-run", so a routine re-provision after AWS publishes a new AMI silently replaces the instance and destroys all PII/health/payment data and cert state. The mechanism, location, and High severity are all correct. Minor caveat: the push-to-main deploy path only runs remote-deploy.sh via SSM (not cloudformation deploy), so the destructive trigger is specifically a provision.sh re-run coinciding with a new AMI publication — a plausible and explicitly-advertised operation. Confirmed.

Remediation (approach)

Decouple data durability from instance lifecycle: put Postgres data on a separate, retained EBS volume (DeleteOnTermination: false, plus an automated snapshot/AWS Backup plan) or move to managed RDS. Pin the AMI to a specific ID for controlled upgrades, or add an UpdateReplacePolicy/DeletionPolicy strategy, and correct the README/provision.sh claim that re-running is unconditionally safe.

High Observability & Operations Observability & ops readiness CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

No error reporting, alerting, or log aggregation is shipped anywhere — errors only reach one container's stdout

src/Api/Infrastructure/GlobalExceptionHandler.cs:10 Error reporting / alerting
Evidence
logger.LogError(exception, "Unhandled exception on {Method} {Path}", context.Request.Method, context.Request.Path);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

A repo-wide search for Sentry/OpenTelemetry/ApplicationInsights/CloudWatch/Serilog/Seq/Datadog/Prometheus returns zero matches; the only logging is Microsoft.Extensions.Logging to console. The prod stack is a single EC2 container (deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml, restart: unless-stopped) with logs going only to the container's stdout — there is no external sink, no aggregation, and no alerting. A 500-error spike, a wave of AuthExpired Fitbit tokens, a dead Gemini key, or a DB failover produces no page, email, or dashboard signal; the operator only learns of an incident by SSM-ing into the host and reading `docker logs`. For an app handling auth, PII, health data, payments and GPS this means security- and availability-relevant failures can go undetected indefinitely.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Infrastructure/GlobalExceptionHandler.cs:10 — logger.LogError(exception, "Unhandled exception on {Method} {Path}", context.Request.Method, context.Request.Path);  // only sink is ILogger→console. Backend grep for Sentry/OpenTelemetry/ApplicationInsights/CloudWatch/Serilog/Seq/Datadog/Prometheus over **/*.cs = "No matches found". deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml api service has restart: unless-stopped and NO logging: driver → default json-file/stdout only, single EC2 host.

Verified. GlobalExceptionHandler.cs:10 logs unhandled exceptions only via ILogger, which resolves to the ASP.NET Core default console provider → container stdout. A backend-scoped grep (**/*.cs) for Sentry/OpenTelemetry/ApplicationInsights/CloudWatch/Serilog/Seq/Datadog/Prometheus/Honeycomb/NewRelic returns zero matches; Program.cs configures no telemetry/log-aggregation (only a RequestLoggingMiddleware). The single @opentelemetry/api hit is a transitive dep in the Angular package-lock, not wired to the API. deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml runs a single EC2 stack (restart: unless-stopped) with NO `logging:` driver configured, so logs stay on the local json-file driver / container stdout — no external sink, no aggregation, no alerting. The claim is correctly located and impactful: 500 spikes, expired OAuth tokens, a dead Gemini/USDA key, or a DB failover produce no page/email/dashboard; the operator must SSM in and read `docker logs`. For an app handling auth/PII/health/payments/GPS this is a legitimate availability- and security-observability gap. Note this is an operational/readiness gap (degraded incident detection) rather than an exploitable code bug — no incorrect behavior results from the line itself — but High is defensible given the data sensitivity. Line 10 is the correct anchor (the sole error sink).

Remediation (approach)

Wire an error/exception sink and log shipping: register a structured-logging provider that forwards to a durable, queryable store (e.g. CloudWatch Logs on this AWS host, or an APM/error tracker), and add alerting rules on 5xx rate, unhandled-exception count, and repeated auth/token failures. At minimum ship the container's stdout to CloudWatch via the awslogs/awsfirelens log driver so logs survive a container restart and can be alarmed on. Treat this as production-blocking given the data sensitivity.

High Observability & Operations Observability & ops readiness CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Container healthcheck and compose service_healthy gate probe a liveness stub that never checks the database; the real readiness check is wired to nothing

src/Api/Endpoints/ApiEndpoints.cs:18 Health checks depth (readiness vs liveness)
Evidence
api.MapGet("/health", () => Results.Ok(new { status = "ok" })).AllowAnonymous();
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK (src/Api/Dockerfile:27-28: `curl -fsS http://localhost:8080/api/health`) and Compose's `web depends_on api condition: service_healthy` (deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:46-48) both probe /api/health, which returns 200 unconditionally without touching Postgres. The genuine readiness check that verifies the DbContext — AddHealthChecks().AddDbContextCheck (Program.cs:352) exposed at /health/ready (Program.cs:549) — is not probed by the Dockerfile, Compose, Caddy, or nginx. As a result the API is reported 'healthy' and admitted into rotation the instant the process listens, even when the database is unreachable or migrations haven't completed, so requests are served straight into DB failures instead of being held back until the datastore is actually ready.

Independent verifier — confirmation
ApiEndpoints.cs:18 `api.MapGet("/health", () => Results.Ok(new { status = "ok" })).AllowAnonymous();` | Dockerfile:27-28 `HEALTHCHECK ... CMD curl -fsS http://localhost:8080/api/health || exit 1` | Program.cs:352 `builder.Services.AddHealthChecks().AddDbContextCheck<UsageDbContext>("database");` | Program.cs:549 `app.MapHealthChecks("/health/ready");` | docker-compose.prod.yml:46-48 `web: depends_on: api: condition: service_healthy`

Read all four cited files and confirmed every load-bearing claim. /api/health (ApiEndpoints.cs:18) returns 200 unconditionally with no DB access. The Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK (lines 27-28) probes exactly that liveness stub, and Compose's web->api service_healthy gate (lines 46-48) depends on that Dockerfile healthcheck. The genuine DB readiness check AddDbContextCheck<UsageDbContext> is registered (Program.cs:352) and mapped only at /health/ready (Program.cs:549); a grep of deploy/ shows no reference to /health/ready anywhere (Caddyfile/nginx/compose/Dockerfile), so the real readiness probe is wired to nothing. Partial mitigation: the api service does gate on db service_healthy (compose lines 23-25, db pg_isready healthcheck lines 14-18), so Postgres TCP is up before the API starts — this narrows the 'DB unreachable at boot' window. However the core defect stands: the container/orchestration health signal flips to healthy the moment the process listens, independent of DbContext connectivity or migration completion (EF migrations typically run in-process after the listener binds), and it never re-checks DB reachability during the container lifetime, so requests can be admitted into a DB-down state. Real readiness-vs-liveness gap on a production AWS stack; High severity is justified.

Remediation (approach)

Point the container HEALTHCHECK and the compose dependency gate at the DB-backed readiness endpoint (/health/ready), and keep the cheap /api/health only as a pure liveness probe. Tag the DbContext check as 'ready' and expose distinct liveness vs readiness endpoints (predicate-filtered health checks) so a DB outage marks the instance not-ready rather than falsely healthy.

High Observability & Operations Observability & ops readiness CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Production Postgres data lives only in a local Docker volume on one EC2 host with no backup/snapshot story

deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:5-18 Backup / restore
Evidence
db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: ccusage
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD}
      POSTGRES_DB: ccusage
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

All application data — accounts/permissions, PII, health data (cycle/meds/wearable), payments handles, GPS locations — is stored in a self-managed postgres:16-alpine container whose only persistence is the local Docker named volume `pgdata` on a single EC2 instance. There is no automated backup, snapshot, or point-in-time-recovery configured in deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml or deploy/cloudformation.yaml, and the instance's EBS root volume is provisioned with DeleteOnTermination: true (cloudformation.yaml:95). If the host is terminated, the volume is corrupted, or a bad migration/`ExecuteDeleteAsync` prune misfires, there is no restore path and the data is permanently lost. For sensitive personal/health/payment data this is an unacceptable durability posture.

Independent verifier — confirmation
deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:5-13  db:\n    image: postgres:16-alpine\n    ...\n    volumes:\n      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data  (volume declared at line 67: `pgdata:`). cloudformation.yaml:95  Ebs: { VolumeSize: !Ref DiskSizeGb, VolumeType: gp3, DeleteOnTermination: true, Encrypted: true }. Grep for backup|snapshot|pg_dump|restore across deploy/ returns only the pgdata volume lines — no backup mechanism exists.

Verified against all six files in deploy/. Production Postgres persists only to the local Docker named volume `pgdata` on a single EC2 host (one Instance resource in cloudformation.yaml). No automated backup, EBS snapshot (no DLM/AWS Backup resource), pg_dump cron, or PITR exists anywhere in the deploy tree — grep for backup/snapshot/pg_dump/restore hits only the volume declaration; remote-deploy.sh never dumps the DB. The instance's EBS root is DeleteOnTermination: true (cloudformation.yaml:95), so instance replacement/termination permanently loses all data. The app stores auth/permissions, PII, health data, payment handles, and GPS locations, so this is an unacceptable durability posture. Not mitigated anywhere. Severity High is correct. Cited lines 5-18 are accurate; anchoring at line 5 (the db service). Encryption-at-rest is present but is orthogonal to durability/backup.

Remediation (approach)

Introduce a durable backup/restore mechanism: scheduled pg_dump/base-backup shipped to encrypted, versioned S3 (with retention), or migrate the datastore to managed RDS with automated backups and PITR. Verify restores periodically, and decouple the data volume's lifecycle from instance termination.

High Observability & Operations Observability & ops readiness CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Readiness check /health/ready is orphaned — container liveness gate probes DB-free /api/health, so the API reports healthy while Postgres is down

src/Api/Program.cs:549 Health checks depth (readiness vs liveness)
Evidence
app.MapHealthChecks("/health/ready");   // registered with AddDbContextCheck<UsageDbContext>("database") at line 352
// But the Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK (src/Api/Dockerfile:28) and compose (docker-compose.yml:54) both probe /api/health:
//   api.MapGet("/health", () => Results.Ok(new { status = "ok" })).AllowAnonymous();  (ApiEndpoints.cs:18)
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The only readiness probe that checks DB connectivity (/health/ready) is wired to nothing. Both the Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK and docker-compose.prod.yml probe /api/health, which returns {status:"ok"} without ever touching the database. On the single EC2 host, docker-compose.prod.yml gates 'web depends_on api: condition: service_healthy' — that gate is satisfied by pure liveness. If Postgres is unreachable (failover, disk-full, crash-loop) the API container stays 'healthy', web keeps routing to it, and every request 500s while orchestration and any external monitor believe the service is up. Nobody is alerted and the container is never restarted. Readiness vs liveness is implemented but the meaningful one is dead code.

Independent verifier — confirmation
Program.cs:549  app.MapHealthChecks("/health/ready");   (DB check registered at Program.cs:352: builder.Services.AddHealthChecks().AddDbContextCheck<UsageDbContext>("database");)
Dockerfile:28  CMD curl -fsS http://localhost:8080/api/health || exit 1
docker-compose.yml:54  test: ["CMD", "curl", "-fsS", "http://localhost:8080/api/health"]
ApiEndpoints.cs:18  api.MapGet("/health", () => Results.Ok(new { status = "ok" })).AllowAnonymous();
deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:47-48  api: condition: service_healthy  (web depends_on api; no per-service api healthcheck block, so the Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK applies)
Repo-wide grep: "/health/ready" appears ONLY in Program.cs:549 and README.md — no Dockerfile/compose/Caddy/monitor references it.

Verified by reading each cited file. Program.cs:549 registers /health/ready backed by AddDbContextCheck (Program.cs:352) — the only probe that touches Postgres. A repo-wide grep proves it is orphaned: it appears only in Program.cs and README.md; nothing probes it. Both the container HEALTHCHECK (Dockerfile:28) and docker-compose.yml:54 curl /api/health, which resolves to ApiEndpoints.cs:18 — Results.Ok({status:"ok"}) with AllowAnonymous(), never touching the DB. (There is a second /api/health group in HealthEndpoints.cs, but it is the Fitbit wearable feature, requires auth + HealthSync permission, and defines only sub-routes like /status; it does not shadow the anonymous liveness GET and would not be reachable by an unauthenticated probe.) On the single EC2 host, deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml has no api-service healthcheck override, so the Dockerfile liveness HEALTHCHECK satisfies web depends_on api: service_healthy. If Postgres fails (failover/disk-full/crash-loop), the API container stays "healthy," web keeps routing, every DB request 500s, and nothing restarts or alerts. Correctly located, real, and High severity for an app handling auth/PII/health data on AWS. Remediation: point the container HEALTHCHECK and both compose probes at /health/ready (or add a liveness-vs-readiness split wiring the orchestration gate to the DB-aware readiness endpoint), keeping /api/health for pure liveness only.

Remediation (approach)

Point the container HEALTHCHECK and any compose/orchestration readiness gate at /health/ready (the DB-backed check) for the 'is it ready to serve' decision, and keep /api/health as a separate liveness probe used only to decide restarts. Alternatively tag the DbContext check as 'ready' and expose /health/live (no checks) for liveness and /health/ready (predicate: ready-tagged) for readiness, then wire each probe to the correct URL. Ensure the compose service_healthy dependency uses the readiness endpoint.

High Observability & Operations Observability & ops readiness CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

TokenProtector derives its at-rest AES key from Jwt:Key, so rotating the JWT signing key silently and irreversibly bricks every encrypted refresh token, surfaced only as indistinguishable AuthExpired

src/Api/Services/TokenProtector.cs:15-20 Secret rotation story / graceful degradation
Evidence
public TokenProtector(IConfiguration config)
{
    var k = config["Jwt:Key"] ?? throw new InvalidOperationException("Jwt:Key is required.");
    // Domain-separated 256-bit key derived from the app secret.
    _key = SHA256.HashData(Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes("usage-iq:share-token:" + k));
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The JWT signing key is also the master key for everything encrypted at rest via TokenProtector: Fitbit refresh tokens (FitbitHealthProvider.Protect/Unprotect), Google Calendar refresh tokens, per-user Discord webhook URLs, and share tokens. Because the two concerns are coupled, rotating Jwt:Key — which JWT-key hygiene and any incident-response/2FA posture would eventually require — changes the derived AES key and makes every previously stored ciphertext undecryptable. Worse, the failure is silent: Unprotect() swallows the AES-GCM auth-tag mismatch and returns null (TokenProtector.cs:57-60), and MintAccessTokenAsync maps a null/undecryptable refresh token to HealthPullStatus.AuthExpired (FitbitHealthProvider.cs:288-291). So a JWT-key rotation would mass-disconnect every wearable and calendar connection while the system reports each as a normal 'auth expired, please reconnect' — operators get no distinguishing signal that a key rotation, not user revocation, caused an account-wide outage. This makes the already-absent rotation mechanism actively dangerous rather than merely missing.

Independent verifier — confirmation
TokenProtector.cs:17-19: var k = config["Jwt:Key"] ?? throw ...; _key = SHA256.HashData(Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes("usage-iq:share-token:" + k)); | TokenProtector.cs:57-60: catch { return null; } | Health/FitbitHealthProvider.cs:287-291: var refreshToken = protector.Unprotect(conn.EncryptedRefreshToken); if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(refreshToken)) { logger.LogWarning(...); return (HealthPullStatus.AuthExpired, null); }

Verified against actual code. The at-rest AES-256 key is SHA256("usage-iq:share-token:" + Jwt:Key), so it is deterministically coupled to the JWT signing key (TokenProtector.cs:15-19). Rotating Jwt:Key changes _key and makes all prior AES-GCM ciphertext fail the auth-tag check. Unprotect swallows that failure and returns null (57-60). Grep confirms the SAME protector encrypts Fitbit + Google Calendar refresh tokens, Discord webhook URLs, and share/wrapped/bill share tokens across ~9 files. For Fitbit, a null decrypt maps to HealthPullStatus.AuthExpired (Health/FitbitHealthProvider.cs:287-291) — indistinguishable from genuine user revocation; the only signal is a LogWarning, not surfaced to operators/users as distinct. Enterprise impact is real: a normal key-hygiene/incident-response Jwt:Key rotation would irreversibly mass-disconnect every wearable/calendar connection and break stored webhooks, silently. Severity High upheld. Only correction: the finding's secondary path 'src/Api/Services/FitbitHealthProvider.cs' is actually 'src/Api/Services/Health/FitbitHealthProvider.cs'; the primary cited file/lines (TokenProtector.cs:15-20) are correct.

Remediation (approach)

Decouple the at-rest encryption key from the JWT signing key: introduce a dedicated, separately-rotatable data-encryption secret (its own SSM parameter) for TokenProtector, and support key versioning (store a key id/version alongside each ciphertext) so a rotation can decrypt-with-old / re-encrypt-with-new rather than orphaning data. At minimum, distinguish an undecryptable-ciphertext outcome from a provider-revoked outcome in the status/logs so a bulk decryption failure is alarming rather than looking like ordinary reconnect prompts, and document that Jwt:Key must never be rotated without a re-encryption migration.

High Performance & Scalability Query performance & indexes CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

No index on UsageRecord.TimestampUtc despite being the default sort + the ORDER BY for every capped/session/CSV path

src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:128-137 Missing DB index for hot sort column
Evidence
e.HasIndex(x => x.DedupKey).IsUnique();
e.HasIndex(x => x.LocalDate);
e.HasIndex(x => new { x.ProjectId, x.LocalDate });
e.HasIndex(x => x.Model);
e.HasIndex(x => x.SessionId);
e.HasIndex(x => x.IsSidechain);
e.HasIndex(x => x.Source);
e.HasIndex(x => x.MachineName);
e.HasIndex(x => x.ReportedByUser);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

UsageRecords is the largest, hottest table. TimestampUtc is the default ORDER BY in RecordsAsync (UsageQueries.cs:565-566), the ordering in SessionAsync (209), StatsAsync (133), WriteRecordsCsvAsync (600), and the OrderByDescending(TimestampUtc) inside FilteredCapped (44) used by Calendar/Heatmap/Stats. With no index, Postgres must sort the entire filtered set on every one of these requests. On the paged records grid (the app's main table view) a default-sorted page forces a full sort of all matching rows before Skip/Take, and the CSV export and sessionization paths sort millions of rows each call — turning routine dashboard loads into full-table sorts that scale linearly with data volume.

Independent verifier — confirmation
UsageDbContext.cs:128-137 — e.HasIndex(x => x.DedupKey).IsUnique(); e.HasIndex(x => x.LocalDate); e.HasIndex(x => new { x.ProjectId, x.LocalDate }); e.HasIndex(x => x.Model); e.HasIndex(x => x.SessionId); e.HasIndex(x => x.IsSidechain); e.HasIndex(x => x.Source); e.HasIndex(x => x.MachineName); e.HasIndex(x => x.ReportedByUser); [no TimestampUtc index]. Confirmed against DB snapshot Migrations/UsageDbContextModelSnapshot.cs:5212-5231 (same index set, no TimestampUtc). UsageQueries.cs:565-566 `(_, false) => q.OrderBy(r => r.TimestampUtc), _ => q.OrderByDescending(r => r.TimestampUtc)` is the DEFAULT records-grid sort; line 600 `Filtered(f).OrderBy(r => r.TimestampUtc)` (CSV, no paging); line 209 (session); line 44 `Filtered(f).OrderByDescending(r => r.TimestampUtc)` (FilteredCapped).

Verified in both the model config (UsageDbContext.cs:128-137) and the EF snapshot (UsageDbContextModelSnapshot.cs:5212-5231): UsageRecords is indexed on DedupKey, LocalDate, (ProjectId,LocalDate), Model, SessionId, IsSidechain, Source, MachineName, ReportedByUser — but NOT on TimestampUtc. TimestampUtc is the default ORDER BY for the paged records grid (UsageQueries.cs:565-566), CSV export (600, unpaged full sort), sessionization (209), and FilteredCapped/Stats (44,133). With no matching index Postgres must add a sort node over the filtered set on every such request; the CSV and session paths sort the entire filtered set with no pagination. Minor mitigation: date-bounded dashboard queries filter on the indexed LocalDate column, shrinking the set the sort runs over — but LocalDate ≠ TimestampUtc, so a sort is still unavoidable, and wide/unbounded ranges and the default-sorted grid still force large sorts on the hottest table. High severity is defensible (it sits at the High/Medium boundary given the LocalDate-index mitigation for the common case). Remediation: add a descending index on TimestampUtc (and consider a composite (LocalDate, TimestampUtc) or filter-leading composite) so the default ordering can be served from an index.

Remediation (approach)

Add a non-unique index on TimestampUtc to the UsageRecord entity configuration (a descending index matches the newest-first default). Because the timestamp reads are almost always combined with a LocalDate/date-range filter, also consider a composite that leads with the common range-filter column so both the WHERE and the ORDER BY are served by one index; validate with EXPLAIN against the actual RecordsAsync and FilteredCapped queries.

High Performance & Scalability Query performance & indexes CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

UsageRecord.TimestampUtc — the DEFAULT records sort and every row-materializing path — has no index

src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:128-136 Missing DB index for hot sort column
Evidence
e.HasIndex(x => x.DedupKey).IsUnique();
e.HasIndex(x => x.LocalDate);
e.HasIndex(x => new { x.ProjectId, x.LocalDate });
e.HasIndex(x => x.Model);
e.HasIndex(x => x.SessionId);
e.HasIndex(x => x.IsSidechain);
e.HasIndex(x => x.Source);
e.HasIndex(x => x.MachineName);
e.HasIndex(x => x.ReportedByUser);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

TimestampUtc is the DEFAULT sort key of the user-facing records grid: ApiEndpoints line 59 passes sort ?? "timestamp", and RecordsAsync (UsageQueries.cs:565-566) maps the timestamp/default case to OrderBy(Descending)(r => r.TimestampUtc). It is ALSO the ordering used by FilteredCapped (UsageQueries.cs:44 — feeding Calendar/Heatmap/Stats), by SessionAsync (line 209), and by WriteRecordsCsvAsync (line 600). None of those columns is indexed (LocalDate is a DATE column, not the timestamp). Prior round only flagged the cost/input/output sorts, explicitly leaving the timestamp path unmentioned — yet timestamp is the one that fires on the default grid load and on the capped row pulls. On a large UsageRecords table Postgres must sort the whole filtered set (or do a full scan when the filter is only ReportedByUser/date), which is the single most common read in the product. This is the highest-frequency unindexed sort in the app.

Independent verifier — confirmation
UsageDbContext.cs:128-136 — index list has no TimestampUtc: `e.HasIndex(x => x.DedupKey).IsUnique(); e.HasIndex(x => x.LocalDate); e.HasIndex(x => new { x.ProjectId, x.LocalDate }); e.HasIndex(x => x.Model); e.HasIndex(x => x.SessionId); e.HasIndex(x => x.IsSidechain); e.HasIndex(x => x.Source); e.HasIndex(x => x.MachineName); e.HasIndex(x => x.ReportedByUser);` (TimestampUtc only appears at line 114 as `.Property(x => x.TimestampUtc).HasColumnType(...)`, never HasIndex). UsageQueries.cs:565-566 default sort `(_, false) => q.OrderBy(r => r.TimestampUtc), _ => q.OrderByDescending(r => r.TimestampUtc)`; :44 `Filtered(f).OrderByDescending(r => r.TimestampUtc).Take(RowMaterializationCap)`; :600 CSV `Filtered(f).OrderBy(r => r.TimestampUtc)`.

Verified against actual code. TimestampUtc is the DEFAULT sort key of the user-facing records grid (RecordsAsync switch default at UsageQueries.cs:565-566), the ordering of FilteredCapped (line 44) which feeds Calendar/Heatmap/Stats, and CSV export (line 600). The DbContext index list (UsageDbContext.cs:128-136) indexes DedupKey, LocalDate, {ProjectId,LocalDate}, Model, SessionId, IsSidechain, Source, MachineName, ReportedByUser — but NOT TimestampUtc; the only other TimestampUtc reference (line 114) is a column-type declaration, not an index. LocalDate is a separate DATE column and cannot serve the timestamp sort. Prior rounds flagged only cost/input/output sorts, leaving this highest-frequency path uncovered. On a large UsageRecords table the default grid load (often unfiltered or only date/user-filtered) forces a full sort/scan on an unindexed column — a real scalability defect for the app's most common read. SessionAsync (line 209) is the one mitigated case (filters on indexed SessionId first, small subset). High is appropriate: performance/scalability, not correctness or security. Note the real file path is src/Api/Services/UsageQueries.cs (finding said src/Api/Data/), but the cited DbContext file/lines for the actual defect are correct.

Remediation (approach)

Add a descending index on TimestampUtc (and, better, a composite that leads with the common equality/range filters then TimestampUtc, e.g. (ReportedByUser, TimestampUtc DESC) and (LocalDate, TimestampUtc DESC)) so the default records grid, the capped Calendar/Heatmap/Stats pulls, session drill-down, and CSV export can satisfy their ORDER BY from an index instead of sorting the whole filtered set. Match the index sort direction to the dominant query (descending).

High Performance & Scalability Query performance & indexes CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

No index on UsageRecord.TimestampUtc despite it being the DEFAULT sort for every records/CSV/Calendar/Heatmap/Stats query

src/Api/Services/UsageQueries.cs:44 Missing DB indexes for hot filter/sort columns
Evidence
private IQueryable<UsageRecord> FilteredCapped(UsageFilterQuery f) =>
    Filtered(f).OrderByDescending(r => r.TimestampUtc).Take(RowMaterializationCap);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The indexes declared on UsageRecord (UsageDbContext.cs lines 128-136 / model snapshot 5212-5231) cover DedupKey, LocalDate, (ProjectId,LocalDate), Model, SessionId, IsSidechain, Source, MachineName, ReportedByUser -- but NOT TimestampUtc. Yet TimestampUtc is the ORDER BY column for: RecordsAsync's default sort (the endpoint passes sort ?? "timestamp", ApiEndpoints.cs:59, so the paged records grid -- the hottest read on the app -- always lands here), WriteRecordsCsvAsync (line 600, OrderBy TimestampUtc), and FilteredCapped which backs CalendarAsync/HeatmapAsync/StatsAsync (lines 44,66,111,133). On a multi-million-row UsageRecords table Postgres must perform a full external sort (or an index scan on the wrong index) for every one of these requests. Prior round #3 only flagged the CostUsd/InputTokens/OutputTokens sorts and asserted this TimestampUtc OrderBy is "inert" for legitimate ranges -- that is incorrect: the Sort node executes on the entire filtered set on every call regardless of whether the Take ceiling is reached. A date-filtered records page still needs (LocalDate, TimestampUtc) to avoid a sort.

Independent verifier — confirmation
UsageQueries.cs:44  FilteredCapped: Filtered(f).OrderByDescending(r => r.TimestampUtc).Take(RowMaterializationCap);
UsageQueries.cs:565-566 (RecordsAsync default sort): (_, false) => q.OrderBy(r => r.TimestampUtc); _ => q.OrderByDescending(r => r.TimestampUtc);
UsageQueries.cs:600 (CSV): Filtered(f).OrderBy(r => r.TimestampUtc)...
UsageDbContext.cs:128-136 indexes: DedupKey, LocalDate, (ProjectId,LocalDate), Model, SessionId, IsSidechain, Source, MachineName, ReportedByUser — no TimestampUtc index.

Verified against source. TimestampUtc is the DEFAULT ORDER BY for RecordsAsync (lines 565-566, reached via the endpoint's sort??"timestamp"), the CSV export (line 600), and every FilteredCapped call (line 44 → Calendar/Heatmap/Stats). No index on TimestampUtc exists (UsageDbContext.cs:128-136). The prior round's "inert" rejection is wrong: the Take(RowMaterializationCap) guard being unreached by legitimate ranges does NOT make the ORDER BY free — Postgres still executes a Sort node to satisfy the ordering before LIMIT unless an index supplies it. For the records grid's default all-time view (no date filter), this is a full external sort of a multi-million-row table on every page load and every CSV export — a real hot-path defect.
