Mitigation is partial: date-filtered queries use the LocalDate index (line 129) to shrink the set first, so a (LocalDate, TimestampUtc) composite index would eliminate the sort in the common filtered case, and a plain TimestampUtc index would cover the unfiltered default. Severity High stands; primary fix site is the index declaration block UsageDbContext.cs:128-136, but the OrderBy evidence cited at UsageQueries.cs:43-44 is correct.

Remediation (approach)

Add a descending index on TimestampUtc to UsageRecord, and ideally a composite (LocalDate, TimestampUtc DESC) so the common date-window + newest-first records/CSV read is served by a single index range scan without a separate sort. Verify with EXPLAIN that the paged-records default path and the FilteredCapped ordering both use it.

High Security Endpoints: admin / users / fleet / ingest CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Precise GPS coordinates from /api/location responses are persisted into RequestLog and replayed to any activity.view holder (bypassing location.view-all)

src/Api/Infrastructure/RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:36-40 Location/GPS privacy + Observability access control
Evidence
private static readonly string[] BodyExcluded =
{
    "/api/users", "/api/audit", "/api/access-policy",
    "/api/chat/contacts", "/api/chat/directory",
};
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

GET /api/location/admin (gated by location.view-all) returns every user's precise lat/lng, and GET /api/location/me returns the caller's own precise history. Neither /api/location nor /api/location/admin appears in the middleware's Excluded list (which skips logging entirely) nor in BodyExcluded (which drops PII-bearing bodies), and LogRedaction.IsSensitivePath has no /api/location prefix while LogRedaction.Redact only scrubs secret KEY names (token/password/webhook), never coordinates. So the JSON response body containing exact lat/lng for all users is captured verbatim into RequestLog.ResponseBody (truncated at 4096 chars). GET /api/logs then serves that stored body to any holder of the activity.view permission (ObservabilityEndpoints.cs line 83) — a permission that is entirely separate from location.view-all and is even defaultable, whereas the Location keys are deliberately never defaultable. Result: a user who was never granted location.view-all can read the persisted precise-location JSON of the whole household off the Activity page, defeating the stated 'precise lat/lng is NEVER exposed to non-admins' posture. The inbound request bodies of POST /api/location and PATCH /api/location/settings (also carrying lat/lng) are captured the same way.

Independent verifier — confirmation
RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:36-40 — BodyExcluded = { "/api/users", "/api/audit", "/api/access-policy", "/api/chat/contacts", "/api/chat/directory" } (no /api/location). LogRedaction.cs:28-33 IsSensitivePath lists /api/auth,/api/notifications,/api/shares,/api/ingest-keys,/api/family/calendar (no /api/location). LogRedaction.cs:16-17 SecretKeys = token|...|password|...|webhookUrl (no lat/lng/coordinate). LocationEndpoints.cs:200 GET /admin gated .RequirePermission(Permissions.LocationViewAll) returns ToDto(x){Lat=x.Lat,Lng=x.Lng} (lines 203-214). ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:77-83 GET /api/logs returns r.ResponseBody, gated only .RequirePermission(Permissions.ActivityView). Permissions.cs:355-362 IsDefaultable excludes LocationKeys but NOT ActivityView → activity.view is defaultable, location.view-all is not.

Verified end-to-end. GET /api/location/admin (LocationViewAll) and GET /api/location/me return JSON bodies containing precise Lat/Lng via ToDto. The response body is captured by RequestLoggingMiddleware: /api/location is neither in Excluded (no logging) nor BodyExcluded (drop body) — lines 23-28 and 36-40. LogRedaction.IsSensitivePath has no /api/location entry, and Redact only scrubs secret KEY names (token/password/webhook/etc.), never numeric lat/lng values, so the coordinates survive verbatim into RequestLog.ResponseBody (truncated at 4096 chars). /api/logs (ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:83) then replays ResponseBody to any activity.view holder. Confirmed activity.view is defaultable while all LocationKeys are deliberately non-defaultable (Permissions.cs:355-362), so the two capabilities are separable — a user without location.view-all can read the whole household's precise coordinates off the Activity page, defeating the documented 'precise lat/lng is NEVER exposed to non-admins' posture (LocationEndpoints.cs:13-14). Inbound POST /api/location and PATCH /api/location/settings request bodies (also carrying lat/lng) are captured the same way. Severity High is appropriate. Line anchored to the BodyExcluded array declaration (line 36) where the /api/location prefix omission lives.

Remediation (approach)

Add the location routes to the middleware's body-exclusion (or full-exclusion) policy so their request/response bodies are never persisted, mirroring how /api/users and /api/audit are handled — i.e. treat any path under /api/location as body-excluded so only the request line (method/path/status/timing) is logged, never the coordinate payload. Alternatively, gate the /api/logs stored-body fields so a coordinate-bearing body is only ever returned to a caller who also holds location.view-all, or extend LogRedaction to strip lat/lng numeric fields from captured bodies. Prefer excluding the bodies at capture time so the sensitive data never lands in the database at all.

High Security Injection / committed secrets / config CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Committed default EmailRevealKey "Starbucks" is the live PII-unmask gate in production (no prod override)

src/Api/appsettings.json:42-43 committed secrets / weak default secret
Evidence
"Users": {
    "_comment": "Email-visibility gate: requests whose X-Email-Reveal-Key header equals EmailRevealKey see real emails on the Users page; otherwise other users' emails are masked. Overridable via env (Users__EmailRevealKey) or appsettings.Local.json.",
    "EmailRevealKey": "Starbucks"
  }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The email-reveal gate controls whether real user email addresses are returned unmasked from GET /api/users and GET /api/audit (UsersEndpoints.cs ToDto maskEmail / MaskEmailsInText). EmailsRevealed() reads cfg["Users:EmailRevealKey"], and the value is the committed constant "Starbucks". deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml (the production environment block, lines 26-41) sets Jwt__Key, Google__*, DB creds, etc. but NEVER sets Users__EmailRevealKey, and no deploy script/SSM entry supplies it (grep across deploy/ and .github/workflows found none). Therefore in production the gate value is the literal string committed to the public repo (itdept-ops/usage-iq). Any user who can reach these UsersManage endpoints — an admin, a compromised/borrowed admin session, or anyone who simply reads the public source — can send header X-Email-Reveal-Key: Starbucks and unmask every user's real email (PII), fully defeating the masking control that is otherwise applied even to admins. A committed secret in git history also cannot be rotated by editing the file alone.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/appsettings.json:42  "EmailRevealKey": "Starbucks"  |  src/Api/Endpoints/UsersEndpoints.cs:296-302  var configured = cfg["Users:EmailRevealKey"]; ... FixedTimeEquals(...provided..., ...configured...)  |  deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:26-41 api environment sets Jwt__Key/Google__*/DB/Gemini but NO Users__EmailRevealKey  |  grep 'Users__|EmailRevealKey' over deploy/ and .github/ => No matches found

Verified all four load-bearing claims. (1) appsettings.json:42 commits the literal EmailRevealKey "Starbucks" in the public repo. (2) EmailsRevealed() in UsersEndpoints.cs:294-303 reads cfg["Users:EmailRevealKey"] and, when the X-Email-Reveal-Key header matches, returns UNMASKED real emails from GET /api/users (line 98) and GET /api/audit (line 49), including bypassing MaskEmailsInText on audit detail. Masking otherwise applies even to admins (only the caller's own row stays real), so the reveal key is the sole bypass. (3) docker-compose.prod.yml:26-41 sets Jwt__Key, Google__*, DB creds, Gemini, etc. but never Users__EmailRevealKey. (4) Grep for Users__/EmailRevealKey across deploy/ and .github/ returned no matches — no SSM/deploy override. Hence the production gate value IS the committed public constant "Starbucks", letting anyone who reads the source (or an authorized/compromised admin session) send X-Email-Reveal-Key: Starbucks to unmask all users' PII emails, defeating the masking control. The constant-time compare is moot when the secret is public, and a git-history secret cannot be rotated by editing the file alone. High severity, line 42, is correct.

Remediation (approach)

Do not commit a real, functional value for the reveal key. Ship a blank EmailRevealKey in appsettings.json (the code already treats empty as 'never reveal', which fails safe), and require the operational value to be injected only via the Users__EmailRevealKey environment variable sourced from AWS SSM at deploy time, exactly as Jwt__Key and the Google secrets already are. Add Users__EmailRevealKey to the api service environment in deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml and to the SSM-to-.env plumbing in remote-deploy.sh. Rotate the value away from 'Starbucks' since it is exposed in git history, and treat it as a real secret going forward.

High Security Backend auth core CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Force-logout (SessionVersion bump) does not tear down an already-open SignalR hub connection

src/Api/Hubs/ChatHub.cs:181-47 SignalR hub auth / session-version force-logout
Evidence
public override async Task OnConnectedAsync()
{
    var email = Email;
    ...
    if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(email) || !await HasReadAsync(scope, email))
    { Context.Abort(); return; }
    ... // no sv re-check here or in any hub method
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The JWT 'sv' (SessionVersion) claim is validated only at the initial handshake (Program.cs OnTokenValidated). Once a chat websocket is established it lives for the connection lifetime; SignalR is registered with a bare AddSignalR() (Program.cs:237) so there is no CloseOnAuthenticationExpiration teardown. Every per-call re-check in the hub (HasReadAsync/HasSendAsync, HasPermissionAsync) verifies only IsEnabled + the chat permission — never SessionVersion. So an admin force-logout (POST /api/users/{id}/logout, which only increments SessionVersion and leaves the account enabled+permissioned) does NOT drop an already-open hub connection: the user can keep reading channel broadcasts and calling SendMessage/ToggleReaction/MarkRead over the stale session indefinitely. The real-time SessionRevoked push is explicitly best-effort UI only and a compromised/malicious client can simply ignore it. This defeats the stated force-logout invariant for the live chat surface (which carries DMs/PII).

Independent verifier — confirmation
ChatHub.cs:181-187 HasPermissionAsync: `return await db.Users.AsNoTracking().Where(u => u.Email == email && u.IsEnabled).AnyAsync(u => u.Permissions.Any(p => p.Permission == permission));` (no SessionVersion check). Program.cs:237 `builder.Services.AddSignalR();` (bare). Program.cs:313-316 OnTokenValidated: `var tokenSv = ...; if (tokenSv != user.SessionVersion) { ctx.Fail("Session has been invalidated."); ... }` (runs only at handshake). UsersEndpoints.cs:220-230 force-logout only `user.SessionVersion += 1;` then best-effort `try { await hub.Clients.User(user.Email).SendAsync("SessionRevoked", ct); } catch { }`.

Verified across all four cited files. sv/SessionVersion is validated only in JwtBearer OnTokenValidated, which fires at the initial hub handshake — never again for the WebSocket lifetime. AddSignalR() is bare (no CloseOnAuthenticationExpiration), and the hub's per-call HasReadAsync/HasSendAsync/HasPermissionAsync check only IsEnabled + chat permission, not SessionVersion. Force-logout only increments SessionVersion (account stays enabled+permissioned); the SessionRevoked push is documented best-effort UI-only that a malicious client can ignore. Result: an already-established hub connection survives an admin force-logout and can keep reading channel broadcasts and invoking SendMessage/ToggleReaction/MarkRead/JoinChannel over the stale session until the socket drops. presence.Remove only affects the roster, not the socket. Real gap on the DM/PII-bearing live-chat surface; High is appropriate. The 26-47 anchor (OnConnectedAsync) is acceptable as the connect-path evidence, but the more precise root-cause anchor is the per-call permission checks at ChatHub.cs:181-187 that omit the SessionVersion comparison.

Remediation (approach)

Re-validate the session stamp for the lifetime of the hub connection, not just at handshake: either enable SignalR's authentication-expiration close so connections are dropped when their bearer token is no longer valid, or extend the per-call DB check (HasPermissionAsync) to also compare the connection's token 'sv' claim against the user's current SessionVersion and abort the connection when they diverge, mirroring the HTTP OnTokenValidated check. Also re-run the check on OnReconnected/keepalive.

High Security Endpoints: admin / users / fleet / ingest CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Precise GPS coordinates leak into request logs replayed to activity.view holders

src/Api/Infrastructure/RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:36-40 Location/GPS privacy
Evidence
private static readonly string[] BodyExcluded =
{
    "/api/users", "/api/audit", "/api/access-policy",
    "/api/chat/contacts", "/api/chat/directory",
};
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

LocationEndpoints documents the invariant that a user's precise lat/lng is NEVER exposed to non-admins except the sharer themselves. But the request-logging middleware captures full request AND response bodies for every /api path not in Excluded/BodyExcluded, and /api/location is in neither list. LogRedaction only redacts named secret keys (token/password/webhook…) and whole-value-redacts /api/auth,/api/notifications,/api/shares,/api/ingest-keys,/api/family/calendar — lat/lng are none of these. So POST /api/location bodies ({lat,lng,accuracyM}) and its response DTO (Lat/Lng/City), plus GET /api/location/me history responses (every stored fix with precise coordinates), are persisted verbatim in RequestLog.RequestBody/ResponseBody. GET /api/logs (gated only by activity.view, NOT location.view-all) returns RequestBody/ResponseBody in full. A holder of activity.view who lacks location.view-all can therefore read any user's precise coordinates and movement history from the action log — exactly the cross-user precise-location exposure the location feature is built to prevent.

Independent verifier — confirmation
RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:36-40 BodyExcluded = { "/api/users","/api/audit","/api/access-policy","/api/chat/contacts","/api/chat/directory" } (no /api/location); LogRedaction.cs:28-33 IsSensitivePath lists auth/notifications/shares/ingest-keys/family/calendar (no location) and :16-17 SecretKeys has no lat/lng/city; RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:84-85 RequestBody=requestBody, ResponseBody=...; ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:76-77 RequestBody=r.RequestBody, ResponseBody=r.ResponseBody gated at :83 .RequirePermission(Permissions.ActivityView); LocationEndpoints.cs:13-14 "precise lat/lng is NEVER exposed to non-admins except the sharer themselves".

Verified end-to-end. /api/location is in neither Excluded nor BodyExcluded, so ShouldLog=true and SkipBodyCapture=false → full request/response bodies captured. LogRedaction.IsSensitivePath does not include location and SecretKeys does not include lat/lng/city, so coordinates persist verbatim in RequestLog.RequestBody/ResponseBody. GET /api/logs (ObservabilityEndpoints.cs) returns both bodies in full and is gated ONLY by Permissions.ActivityView — no location.view-all check. Owner attribution is recoverable via the UserId mapping. This defeats the documented LocationEndpoints invariant that precise lat/lng is never shown to non-admins. A holder of activity.view lacking location.view-all can read any user's precise coordinates and movement history. High is appropriate: real cross-user precise-location/PII exposure, though it requires an authenticated insider with activity.view. Finding correctly located at the BodyExcluded array (lines 36-40).

Remediation (approach)

Add the /api/location path prefix (and any other precise-coordinate-bearing routes such as the chat location-share and family-locations endpoints) to the middleware's body-capture skip list so their bodies are never persisted, mirroring the existing email-bearing BodyExcluded treatment; alternatively teach LogRedaction to strip lat/lng/accuracy numeric fields from any captured body. Keep the request line logged for diagnostics but drop the coordinate-bearing bodies.

High Security Endpoints: admin / users / fleet / ingest CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Ingest key of a deleted or disabled user stays live — filter checks only hash + RevokedUtc, and delete/disable never revoke keys

src/Api/Auth/IngestKeyFilter.cs:36-44 Ingest key auth
Evidence
var match = await db.IngestKeys.AsNoTracking().Where(k => k.KeyHash == hash && k.RevokedUtc == null).Select(k => new { k.Id, OwnerEmail = k.User != null ? k.User.Email : null }).FirstOrDefaultAsync(ct); if (match is null) return Unauthorized(...);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The ingest credential is validated purely on hash-matches-a-non-revoked-row; it never checks that the owning user still exists or is enabled. Deleting a user (DELETE /api/users/{id}) only removes the AppUser: the IngestKey FK is OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.SetNull) (UsageDbContext.cs:392-393), so the key's UserId becomes null but RevokedUtc stays null and the key keeps authenticating — now attributing ingested usage to a null owner. Disabling a user is even weaker: it touches neither the keys nor SessionVersion, so every key the disabled user created keeps ingesting. Revocation is a SEPARATE manual admin step (POST /api/fleet/revoke-keys). Net effect: offboarding a user (disable, or even hard-delete) does not sever their machine credential — a departed insider's reporter keeps pushing data indefinitely, and the leaked/stolen key of a since-deleted account is still accepted.

Independent verifier — confirmation
IngestKeyFilter.cs:35-40 — .Where(k => k.KeyHash == hash && k.RevokedUtc == null) ... if (match is null) return Unauthorized("Invalid or revoked ingest key."); | UsageDbContext.cs:391-393 — "// A deleted user must not cascade-delete usage-bearing keys: orphan them instead." e.HasOne(x => x.User).WithMany().HasForeignKey(x => x.UserId).OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.SetNull); | UsersEndpoints.cs:202 — db.Users.Remove(user); (no key revocation) | UsersEndpoints.cs:177 — user.IsEnabled = req.IsEnabled; (no key revocation, no SessionVersion bump)

Verified against the actual code. The ingest filter (IngestKeyFilter.cs:35-40) authenticates solely on KeyHash match + RevokedUtc == null; it never checks the owning user's existence or IsEnabled state. The FK is OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.SetNull) (UsageDbContext.cs:391-393), with an explicit comment that deleted users orphan the key rather than revoke it, so a deleted user's key keeps authenticating (RevokedUtc stays null) and ingests with a null owner. The disable path (UsersEndpoints.cs:157-186 upsert, setting user.IsEnabled) touches neither the keys nor SessionVersion, so a disabled user's keys keep ingesting. Delete (UsersEndpoints.cs:188-208) only Removes the AppUser. Revocation is a wholly separate manual admin action (FleetEndpoints.cs:107-132, POST /api/fleet/revoke-keys). Net: offboarding (disable or hard-delete) does not sever the machine credential — a real enterprise offboarding gap on an auth credential. Severity High is correct: it requires an issued/leaked key or an insider-created key and admins have a manual revoke path, so not Critical, but the automatic-offboarding failure on a live ingest credential warrants High. The cited range 35-44 is fair; the precise offending validation is the WHERE clause at line 36.

Remediation (approach)

Have the ingest filter reject keys whose owner is missing or disabled: join to the owning AppUser and require it to exist and be IsEnabled (a legacy key with no linked user is a policy decision — either reject it or keep it, but do so explicitly). Independently, cascade credential revocation off the user lifecycle: when a user is disabled or deleted, set RevokedUtc on all their ingest keys (match by UserId and legacy CreatedByEmail, as fleet.revoke-keys already does) instead of relying on SetNull, so an orphaned key cannot keep authenticating.

High Security & Correctness Reporter / agent / ingestion CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

QuickAddClient re-sends X-Ingest-Key across auto-followed cross-host redirects (a second leak surface distinct from IngestClient)

src/ReporterCore/QuickAddClient.cs:36-38 Credential handling / secret transport
Evidence
_http = new HttpClient
{
    BaseAddress = new Uri(baseUrl.TrimEnd('/') + "/"),
    Timeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(30),
};
_http.DefaultRequestHeaders.Add("X-Ingest-Key", key);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Prior rounds flagged the IngestClient redirect leak and the QuickAddWindow missing http-guard, but QuickAddClient is a SEPARATE HttpClient that also carries the ingest key as a DefaultRequestHeader on a handler whose AllowAutoRedirect defaults to true. If the configured server URL (settable in Settings, and allowed to be plain http per SettingsWindow.OnSave line 93) issues a 3xx to a different host, HttpClient re-emits the custom X-Ingest-Key header to the redirect target, disclosing the bearer secret to an attacker-controlled or misconfigured host. Because the key owns the acting user for /api/family/quick-add (family.use), the leaked secret grants write access to the victim's family lists/notes.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/ReporterCore/QuickAddClient.cs:31-36
        _http = new HttpClient
        {
            BaseAddress = new Uri(baseUrl.TrimEnd('/') + "/"),
            Timeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(30),
        };
        _http.DefaultRequestHeaders.Add("X-Ingest-Key", key);
--- corroborating (http allowed): src/Agent/Views/SettingsWindow.xaml.cs:93
        if (!Uri.TryCreate(url, UriKind.Absolute, out var parsed) || (parsed.Scheme != "http" && parsed.Scheme != "https"))

Confirmed and correctly located. QuickAddClient builds an HttpClient without a custom handler, so AllowAutoRedirect defaults to true, and line 36 attaches X-Ingest-Key as a DefaultRequestHeader. .NET's HttpClient re-emits custom headers (unlike Authorization, which it strips) on auto-followed redirects, including cross-host 3xx targets. SettingsWindow.OnSave line 93 explicitly permits a plain-http base URL, so a MITM or misconfigured/hostile server can 3xx to an arbitrary host and receive the bearer ingest key, which owns the acting user for /api/family/quick-add (family.use) and grants write access to the victim's family data. This is a genuinely separate HttpClient from IngestClient.cs (which has the identical defect but was a prior-round finding), so it is a distinct, unmitigated second leak surface. No allowlist, handler config disabling redirects, or per-request header scoping mitigates it. Cited lines 29-38 accurately bracket the constructor; the header add is line 36. High severity is appropriate.

Remediation (approach)

Construct QuickAddClient's HttpClient with an HttpClientHandler/SocketsHttpHandler that sets AllowAutoRedirect = false, or attach the ingest key per-request only after verifying the final request host matches the configured base host, mirroring whatever fix is applied to IngestClient. Also apply the same cleartext-http guard the console reporter uses before sending.

High Testing & CI/CD Test coverage & quality CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Frontend test coverage is effectively zero: one stale scaffold spec for an entire Angular SPA

src/Web/src/app/app.spec.ts:4-23 Frontend test coverage
Evidence
describe('App', () => {
  ...
  it('should render title', async () => {
    const fixture = TestBed.createComponent(App);
    await fixture.whenStable();
    const compiled = fixture.nativeElement as HTMLElement;
    expect(compiled.querySelector('h1')?.textContent).toContain('Hello, ccusage-web');
  });
});
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

This is the ONLY *.spec.ts in the whole src/Web app (Glob over src/Web/src/**/*.spec.ts returns exactly this file). The SPA contains security-critical client logic — the App shell's routePerm/routePermSubtrees IDOR-enforcement map, enforceCurrentRoute() live-revocation redirect, email-masking initialsOf(), AuthService permission gating, and route guards — none of which has any test. A regression that, e.g., drops a route from routePerm or breaks hasPermission would ship undetected. For an app handling auth, PII, health, payments and GPS, an entirely untested frontend authorization/session layer is an enterprise-grade coverage gap.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/src/app/app.spec.ts:17-22 — it('should render title', async () => { const fixture = TestBed.createComponent(App); await fixture.whenStable(); const compiled = fixture.nativeElement as HTMLElement; expect(compiled.querySelector('h1')?.textContent).toContain('Hello, ccusage-web'); }); | Glob over src/Web/src/**/*.spec.ts returns exactly this one file. | app.ts:315 routePerm, :341 routePermSubtrees, :374 initialsOf, :749-753 enforceCurrentRoute()/hasPermission are all untested.

Verified by reading the file and Glob: app.spec.ts is the ONLY *.spec.ts in the entire src/Web Angular SPA, and it is a stale CLI scaffold that asserts the default template text 'Hello, ccusage-web' (which no longer matches the real shell). I confirmed in app.ts that the security-critical client logic the finding names is real and present: routePerm map (l.315), routePermSubtrees (l.341), email-masking initialsOf() (l.374), and the live-revocation enforceCurrentRoute() using auth.hasPermission (l.749-753), plus permission-gated nav/quicklinks throughout. None of it has any test. For an app handling auth, PII, health, payments and GPS, an entirely untested client-side authorization/session layer is a legitimate High-severity coverage gap: a regression dropping a route from routePerm or breaking hasPermission would ship undetected. Note the client checks are a defense-in-depth/UX layer (server must also enforce), which is why this is High rather than Critical — but the finding is accurate and correctly located.

Remediation (approach)

Treat the frontend as a first-class test target: add specs for the security-relevant units — AuthService (hasPermission/hasAnyPermission/session-version handling), any route guards / canMatch page-registry twins, App.enforceCurrentRoute and the routePerm map, and email-masking helpers. Delete or rewrite this scaffold spec. Establish a minimum-coverage gate so the suite cannot regress back to a single file.

High Testing & CI/CD Test coverage & quality CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

CI never executes frontend tests — the web job only builds, so any spec (passing or failing) is unenforced

.github/workflows/ci.yml:61 CI/CD
Evidence
  web:
    name: Build Angular app
    ...
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The web CI job runs only `npm run build`; there is no `npm run test` / `ng test` step anywhere in ci.yml (the only test job is the .NET one on line 44-45). Even the specs that DO exist are never run in CI, so the frontend can never fail a PR on a broken test. This structurally guarantees the frontend test suite provides zero regression protection regardless of how many specs are later added, and it hides the fact that the existing app.spec.ts is already broken (see the 'Hello, ccusage-web' finding).

Independent verifier — confirmation
.github/workflows/ci.yml:47-61
  web:
    name: Build Angular app
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: src/Web
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: src/Web/package-lock.json
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build

Verified by reading ci.yml in full. The web job (lines 47-61) runs only `npm ci` then `npm run build` (build = node scripts/ng-build-ci.mjs). There is no `npm run test`/`ng test` step in the web job or anywhere in ci.yml; grep across .github/workflows confirms the only test job is the .NET one (lines 34-45). package.json defines a `test: ng test` script and a real frontend spec exists (src/Web/src/app/app.spec.ts), yet neither is executed in CI, so frontend specs cannot fail a PR — zero regression protection for the Angular app that fronts auth/PII/health/payment flows. Claim is accurately located and the mechanism is real; not mitigated elsewhere (deploy.yml only docker-builds). Severity High is defensible for an enterprise app given the structural guarantee that no frontend test can ever gate a PR; the only nuance is that today just one scaffold spec exists so present-day blast radius is limited (a Medium argument), but the finding as stated is correct. Anchoring line to 61 (the build step where a test step is missing).

Remediation (approach)

Add a CI step to the web job that runs the Angular unit tests headlessly (the @angular/build:unit-test / Vitest runner configured in angular.json) and fails the job on any failing test, mirroring how the .NET `test` job gates the API. Run it on both push and pull_request.

High Testing & CI/CD Test coverage & quality CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

CI never runs the Angular test suite — the web job only builds; the configured vitest runner is unwired, so the entire SPA has zero enforced coverage

.github/workflows/ci.yml:61 Frontend test coverage
Evidence
  web:
    name: Build Angular app
    ...
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

angular.json defines a `test` target (`@angular/build:unit-test`, vitest) and package.json exposes `"test": "ng test"`, so a runner IS configured — but the CI `web` job invokes only `npm run build`, never `npm test`. Combined with the fact that the .NET job (line 45) is the only place tests execute, the whole Angular SPA — app JWT/permission handling in core/auth.ts, permission-guarded routes, the email-masking UI, the offline queue, the ~3438-line core/api.ts, and every mobile twin — has NO test executed on any PR or push. This is not merely sparse coverage: it is structurally impossible for a frontend regression (including a security regression in token/permission logic) to be caught by CI. It also explains why the single stale app.spec.ts (asserting the deleted 'Hello, ccusage-web' scaffold) was never noticed failing — nothing ever runs it.

Independent verifier — confirmation
.github/workflows/ci.yml lines 47-61:
  web:
    name: Build Angular app
    ...
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build
(no `npm test`/`ng test` step). angular.json:81-82 `"test": { "builder": "@angular/build:unit-test"`; package.json:10 `"test": "ng test"`. grep of .github/workflows/*.yml for npm test/ng test/unit-test = zero hits. app.spec.ts:21 still asserts `'Hello, ccusage-web'`.

All claims verified by reading the files. The CI `web` job (ci.yml lines 47-61) runs only `npm ci` + `npm run build`; there is no frontend test step anywhere. A test runner IS configured (angular.json line 81-82 `@angular/build:unit-test` vitest; package.json line 10 `"test": "ng test"`) but never invoked. grep across both workflow files (ci.yml, deploy.yml) confirms zero `npm test`/`ng test`/`unit-test` invocations — the only test job is .NET (line 45). The stale app.spec.ts:21 still asserts the deleted 'Hello, ccusage-web' scaffold, corroborating that nothing runs the suite. For a security-sensitive SPA (JWT/permission logic in core/auth.ts, permission-guarded routes, email-masking UI, offline queue) it is structurally impossible for CI to catch a frontend regression. Correctly located; anchored the line at 61 (the `npm run build` step that stands where a test step should be). High severity is defensible for enterprise standards, though strictly it is a CI-process/coverage gap rather than a runtime code defect.

Remediation (approach)

Add a step to the `web` CI job that runs the Angular unit tests headlessly (e.g. `npm test` with a CI/watch=false vitest configuration) and fails the job on test failure. Before enabling it, fix or delete the stale app.spec.ts so the newly-wired job is green, then begin adding real specs for the security-sensitive core services (auth/token persistence, permission guards, email-masking rendering).

High Testing & CI/CD Test coverage & quality CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

CI never executes any frontend test — the `web` job only builds, so the SPA has zero test enforcement

.github/workflows/ci.yml:61 Frontend test coverage
Evidence
  web:
    name: Build Angular app
    ...
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The dedicated `test` job (line 34) runs ONLY the .NET suite; the `web` job stops at `npm run build`. `npm run test` (ng test) is never invoked in CI. Combined with there being exactly one, broken frontend spec, this means the entire Angular SPA — auth token handling, permission-gated routing, email-masking rendering, offline-queue mutation replay, tracker-store request-generation — has NO automated test enforcement on any PR or merge to main. A regression that, e.g., leaks another user's health day (the known TrackerStore stale-response bug) or force-navigates users off their page (the known /me empty-permissions bug) would ship with a green CI. For an app handling PII, health, payments, and location this is a critical-path enforcement gap.

Independent verifier — confirmation
.github/workflows/ci.yml lines 47-61:
  web:
    name: Build Angular app
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: src/Web
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        ...
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build

package.json line 10: "test": "ng test"  (never invoked in ci.yml)

Verified by reading the full workflow. The web job (lines 47-61) ends at `npm run build`; it never runs `npm run test`. `npm run test` = `ng test` (package.json line 10) and appears nowhere in CI. The dedicated `test` job (lines 34-45) runs only `dotnet test` on the .NET API. So no Angular test executes on any PR or push to main. The single app-level spec (src/Web/src/app/app.spec.ts) even asserts scaffold text 'Hello, ccusage-web' (line 21), which would fail against the real app — corroborating the 'one broken spec' point. For an SPA handling auth tokens, permission-gated routing, email masking, and health/PII data, having zero frontend test enforcement is a legitimate High enforcement gap. Minor wording caveat: the finding says the `test` job 'stops at build' but that is the `web` job — the `test` job is a separate .NET-only job; the substantive claim (no frontend test in CI) is fully correct. Core defect location is the missing test step in the web job, anchored at line 61.

Remediation (approach)

Add a step to the `web` job (or a new `web-test` job) that runs the frontend unit tests in headless/CI mode and fails the pipeline on any failing spec, mirroring how the `test` job gates the .NET suite. Make the frontend test run a required status check for merges to main so coverage regressions are actually blocked, and grow the spec suite to cover the security-critical core services before relying on the gate.

Medium severity 93

Medium Accessibility & Robustness Frontend accessibility CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Desktop tablists declare role=tab/aria-selected but omit the arrow-key roving-tabindex contract the mobile twins implement

src/Web/src/app/features/meds/meds.page.ts:76-81 Frontend accessibility
Evidence
<div class="md-head__windows" role="tablist" aria-label="Window">
  @for (w of windows; track w.value) {
    <button type="button" class="md-chip" role="tab"
            [class.is-on]="window() === w.value" [attr.aria-selected]="window() === w.value"
            [disabled]="loading()" (click)="setWindow(w.value)">{{ w.label }}</button>
  }
</div>
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Declaring role=tablist/role=tab sets an ARIA authoring contract: a screen reader announces "tab, N of M" and users expect Left/Right (or Up/Down) arrow keys to move between tabs with only the selected tab in the page tab sequence (roving tabindex). These hand-rolled desktop tablists (also src/Web/src/app/features/insights/insights.page.ts:66-71) provide none of that — all tabs are plain always-tabbable buttons and arrows do nothing, so the promised keyboard interaction is broken and every tab is redundantly Tab-stopped. The app already has a correct implementation in features/beta-ui/segmented-control.ts (keydown handler with ArrowRight/ArrowLeft roving at lines 44,127-128), proving the desktop originals simply were not brought up to standard.

Independent verifier — confirmation
meds.page.ts:76-81  <div class="md-head__windows" role="tablist" aria-label="Window"> @for (w of windows; track w.value) { <button type="button" class="md-chip" role="tab" [class.is-on]="window() === w.value" [attr.aria-selected]="window() === w.value" [disabled]="loading()" (click)="setWindow(w.value)">{{ w.label }}</button> } </div>  — no (keydown) handler, no [tabindex] roving. Contrast beta-ui/segmented-control.ts:44 (keydown)="onKeydown($event, i)" and 124-132 onKeydown handling ArrowRight/ArrowLeft.

Verified. meds.page.ts:76-81 and insights.page.ts:66-71 both emit role="tablist"/role="tab"/aria-selected on plain <button> elements with only a (click) handler — no arrow-key keydown handler and no roving tabindex, so the ARIA tab-widget keyboard contract (Left/Right to move, single tab stop) is unimplemented and every tab is redundantly Tab-stopped. The app demonstrably knows how to do this: beta-ui/segmented-control.ts binds (keydown) at line 44 and implements ArrowRight/ArrowLeft/ArrowUp/ArrowDown roving at lines 124-132 (though it uses radiogroup/radio semantics). Location, evidence, and the cross-reference are all accurate. This is a genuine WCAG 2.1.1/4.1.2 keyboard-interaction gap on a filter control — real but self-contained accessibility defect, no security/PII impact; Medium is defensible (arguably Low).

Remediation (approach)

Either drop the role=tablist/role=tab semantics and let them be a plain group of buttons, or complete the ARIA tabs pattern: add a keydown handler that moves selection on Arrow keys with wrap-around, set roving tabindex (0 on the selected tab, -1 on the rest), and associate the shown content region via aria-controls/role=tabpanel. Simplest fix is to reuse the beta-ui segmented-control primitive on the desktop pages.

Medium Accessibility & Robustness Frontend accessibility CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

App-wide toaster puts aria-live on the per-toast element inserted with its text, so screen readers likely do not announce toasts

src/Web/src/app/features/beta-ui/toast.ts:109-121 Frontend accessibility
Evidence
<div class="bs-toaster" role="region" aria-label="Notifications">
  @for (t of ctrl.toasts(); track t.id) {
    <div class="bs-toast" ...
         [attr.role]="t.tone === 'warn' ? 'alert' : 'status'"
         [attr.aria-live]="t.tone === 'warn' ? 'assertive' : 'polite'">
      ...
      <span class="bs-toast-text">{{ t.text }}</span>
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

This ToastController/app-bs-toaster is the shared toast mechanism used across both desktop and mobile feature pages (bills, ai-usage, users-mobile, etc.), so it is the app's primary async-feedback announcer for save/error/undo results. A live region only announces mutations that occur inside a region that already existed in the accessibility tree. Here the element carrying aria-live/role=status is itself created by the @for at the same moment its text appears, so many screen readers treat it as initial content and stay silent — the persistent container .bs-toaster is only role=region, not a live region. Result: success/error confirmations for operations touching PII, payments, and health data are silently missed by SR users.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/src/app/features/beta-ui/toast.ts:109-115
<div class="bs-toaster" role="region" aria-label="Notifications">
  @for (t of ctrl.toasts(); track t.id) {
    <div class="bs-toast" ... [attr.role]="t.tone === 'warn' ? 'alert' : 'status'"
         [attr.aria-live]="t.tone === 'warn' ? 'assertive' : 'polite'">
      <span class="bs-toast-stripe" aria-hidden="true"></span>
      <span class="bs-toast-text">{{ t.text }}</span>

Confirmed by reading toast.ts. The aria-live/role attributes sit on the per-toast .bs-toast element that the @for block creates at the same moment its text ({{ t.text }}) is inserted (show() pushes the msg into the signal, then the template materializes element+content together — lines 57 / 110-115). The persistent container .bs-toaster (line 109) carries only role="region", not a live region. WAI-ARIA authoring guidance requires the live region to be present and empty in the accessibility tree BEFORE content is injected; a live-region element inserted already-populated is commonly treated as initial content and not announced, so announcement is unreliable across screen readers. This is the app's shared toast primitive used across desktop/mobile feature pages for save/error/undo feedback, so SR users can miss confirmations for PII/payment/health operations. Correctly located; Medium is appropriate. One caveat: role=alert/status also carry implicit live semantics and some modern SR+browser combos may still announce, so it is not universally silent — but the pattern is genuinely non-robust, so the finding stands.

Remediation (approach)

Move the aria-live (polite) / role=status onto the persistent outer .bs-toaster container that is always present in the DOM, and let the individual toast rows be plain children whose insertion the live container announces. For assertive/warn toasts, keep a second always-present role=alert / aria-live=assertive container and route warn toasts into it, rather than toggling politeness on freshly-inserted nodes.

Medium Accessibility & Robustness Frontend accessibility CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

No route-change focus management: SPA navigation never moves focus to <main>, so keyboard/SR users are stranded and the new page is not announced

src/Web/src/app/app.ts:503-509 Frontend accessibility
Evidence
.subscribe(() => {
  this.bareLayout.set(App.isBare(this.router.url));
  this.currentPath.set(this.router.url.split('?')[0]);
  this.closeMobileNav();
  this.maybeApplyPwaHome();
  this.maybeStartTour();
});
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The shell subscribes to NavigationEnd but never moves focus to the `<main id="main-content" tabindex="-1">` region (app.html:704) it deliberately made focusable for exactly this purpose, and there is no scrollPositionRestoration/anchorScrolling focus shim anywhere (grep for 'main-content' finds only the skip-link + the element). On every in-app navigation, keyboard focus remains on the just-clicked nav link (or is lost to <body> when the old component is destroyed), and screen readers get no cue that the page changed — they keep reading the stale context. This is a WCAG 2.4.3 (Focus Order) / SPA-focus-management failure affecting 100% of navigations across an app handling PII, health, payments, and location. It is the classic 'SPA route change is silent to AT' defect.

Independent verifier — confirmation
app.ts:503-509 .subscribe(() => { this.bareLayout.set(App.isBare(this.router.url)); this.currentPath.set(this.router.url.split('?')[0]); this.closeMobileNav(); this.maybeApplyPwaHome(); this.maybeStartTour(); });  |  app.html:704 <main id="main-content" tabindex="-1" class="content" ...>  — grep for 'main-content' across src/Web/src returns ONLY app.html:3 (skip-link) and app.html:704 (the element); no .focus() on it anywhere and no scrollPositionRestoration/anchorScrolling focus shim.

Verified by reading app.ts:498-509 and app.html:3/704. The NavigationEnd subscriber never moves focus to <main id="main-content" tabindex="-1">, and codebase-wide grep confirms nothing focuses it and there is no anchorScrolling/scrollPositionRestoration focus shim. The tabindex="-1" was clearly added to make it focusable (its only non-skip-link purpose) yet is never used on route change — a genuine SPA route-change focus-management gap (WCAG 2.4.3 / 4.1.3): keyboard focus stays on the clicked link or falls to body, and SR users get no page-change cue. Real and correctly located. Downgrading severity to Medium: it is an accessibility/usability defect with no data-integrity, security, or crash impact and is not a hard blocker for keyboard users (the skip-link still lets them jump to content); High overstates it. Location refined to the subscribe body (503) where the missing focus call belongs.

Remediation (approach)

On each settled NavigationEnd, programmatically move focus to the main landmark (the element already carries tabindex=-1) — e.g. query #main-content and call focus() after the outlet has rendered the new component, optionally after resetting scroll. Consider setting a transient aria-live announcement of the new page title as a secondary cue. Ensure the focus move is skipped for in-page fragment navigations so anchor links still work.

Medium Accessibility & Robustness Frontend accessibility CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Closed desktop responsive nav drawer (#mobile-nav) keeps ~25 links keyboard-focusable and screen-reader-exposed while invisible

src/Web/src/app/app.scss:355-366 Frontend accessibility
Evidence
transform: translateY(-12px);
  opacity: 0;
  pointer-events: none;
  ...
  &.open {
    transform: translateY(0);
    opacity: 1;
    pointer-events: auto;
  }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

At <=960px the media query flips `.mobile-nav` to `display: flex` (app.scss:1258-1261). When the drawer is CLOSED it is hidden only via `opacity:0` + `pointer-events:none` — neither of which removes elements from the tab order or the accessibility tree. The `<nav id="mobile-nav">` (app.html:473) carries no `aria-hidden`/`inert` binding (the scrim's aria-hidden is static and separate), and the Tab-trap `onMobileNavKeydown` returns early while closed (app.ts:812). Result: on any narrow desktop/tablet viewport, a keyboard user tabbing across the toolbar falls into up to ~25 invisible, permission-gated navigation links, and a screen reader announces the entire hidden nav. This is distinct from the mobile-shell `app-mobile-sidebar` flagged earlier — this is the desktop shell's own hamburger drawer.

Independent verifier — confirmation
app.scss:355-366 `transform: translateY(-12px); opacity: 0; pointer-events: none; ... &.open { transform: translateY(0); opacity: 1; pointer-events: auto; }`  — combined with app.scss:337 `.mobile-nav { display: none }` base and app.scss:1258-1261 `@media (max-width: 960px){ .mobile-nav, .mobile-nav-scrim { display: flex } }`; app.html:473 `<nav id="mobile-nav" class="mobile-nav" [class.open]="mobileNavOpen()" aria-label="Primary">` (no aria-hidden/inert/[hidden]); app.ts:812 `if (!this.mobileNavOpen()) return;`

Verified against the actual code. At >960px the base rule `.mobile-nav { display:none }` (app.scss:337) correctly removes the drawer from the tab order and a11y tree. But the @media(max-width:960px) block (app.scss:1258-1261) flips it to `display:flex`, and while CLOSED the drawer is hidden ONLY via `opacity:0` + `pointer-events:none` (app.scss:356-357). `opacity:0` still leaves elements focusable and in the accessibility tree, and `pointer-events:none` blocks mouse but NOT keyboard Tab focus. The `<nav id="mobile-nav">` (app.html:473) carries no `aria-hidden`/`inert`/`[hidden]` binding — only a static `aria-label`; the only static `aria-hidden="true"` nearby is on the separate scrim (app.html:469). The Tab-trap `onMobileNavKeydown` returns early while closed (app.ts:812), so it provides no mitigation. There are 26 `.mobile-nav__item` links (matches the ~25 claimed), all permission-gated but still rendered when authenticated. Net effect on any ≤960px viewport (narrow desktop/tablet) with the drawer closed: keyboard users Tab into invisible nav links and screen readers announce the hidden nav — a genuine WCAG 2.4.3/4.1.2 focus/visibility failure, distinct from the mobile-shell sidebar. Finding is real and correctly located. Corrected severity to Medium: it is a real accessibility/compliance defect but purely presentation-layer (no data or security exposure — links remain server-side permission-gated), which for enterprise a11y obligations is best rated Medium rather than High.

Remediation (approach)

Gate the closed drawer out of the a11y tree and tab order: bind `inert` (or `aria-hidden="true"` plus a mechanism that also removes focusability, e.g. `visibility:hidden`/`display:none` when not `.open`) to the `#mobile-nav` element driven by `mobileNavOpen()`. `opacity:0`+`pointer-events:none` alone is insufficient because both keep the element focusable and in the accessibility tree.

Medium Accessibility & Robustness Frontend accessibility CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Home-page picker submenu is a radio group but exposes no role=menuitemradio/aria-checked; the current selection is conveyed only by an aria-hidden icon

src/Web/src/app/app.html:689-701 Frontend accessibility
Evidence
<button mat-menu-item class="acct-item acct-item--radio" (click)="setHomeRoute(null)"
        [class.acct-item--checked]="!auth.session()?.homeRoute">
  <mat-icon class="acct-item__radio" aria-hidden="true">{{ !auth.session()?.homeRoute ? 'radio_button_checked' : 'radio_button_unchecked' }}</mat-icon>
  <span class="acct-item__label">Default (first available)</span>
</button>
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The home-route rows are visually a single-select radio group (checked ring icon + `acct-item--checked` class), but they render as plain MatMenu items (implicit role=menuitem) and the only selected-state indicator is a `mat-icon` marked `aria-hidden="true"`. Grep confirms zero role=menuitemradio / aria-checked / role=radio anywhere in app.html. A screen-reader user opening the Home-page menu cannot tell which route is currently their home, nor perceive that these are mutually-exclusive choices — they hear a flat list of buttons. This is a WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) gap on a persisted user-preference control.

Independent verifier — confirmation
app.html:689-693
<button mat-menu-item class="acct-item acct-item--radio" (click)="setHomeRoute(null)"
        [class.acct-item--checked]="!auth.session()?.homeRoute">
  <mat-icon class="acct-item__radio" aria-hidden="true">{{ !auth.session()?.homeRoute ? 'radio_button_checked' : 'radio_button_unchecked' }}</mat-icon>
  <span class="acct-item__label">Default (first available)</span>
</button>
(and the @for rows at 696-700 identical pattern). Grep for menuitemradio|aria-checked|role=radio in app.html: No matches found.

Verified by reading lines 685-702. The Home-page picker submenu renders a single-select radio group visually (radio_button_checked/unchecked icon + acct-item--checked class) but uses plain mat-menu-item buttons (implicit role=menuitem) and marks the selected-state mat-icon aria-hidden=true. Grep confirms zero role=menuitemradio / aria-checked / role=radio anywhere in app.html, so nothing elsewhere mitigates this. A screen-reader user hears a flat list of buttons with no indication of which route is their current home nor that the choices are mutually exclusive — a genuine WCAG 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value) gap on a persisted preference control. Medium severity is fair: real but non-blocking (labels are still announced and activatable), and low-impact relative to the app's auth/PII/health-data surface. Location, code, and severity as claimed are all correct.

Remediation (approach)

Give each row role=menuitemradio (MatMenu items accept a role override) and bind aria-checked to the same expression that drives `acct-item--checked`, so the selected state and the radio semantics are exposed to AT rather than living only in an aria-hidden icon and a CSS class.

Medium Accessibility & Robustness Frontend accessibility CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Desktop period/window tablists (Wrapped, Insights) declare role=tablist/tab but omit the arrow-key roving-tabindex contract and tabpanel wiring

src/Web/src/app/features/wrapped/wrapped.page.ts:44-50 Frontend accessibility
Evidence
<div class="wr-hero__periods" role="tablist" aria-label="Wrapped period">
  @for (p of periods; track p.key) {
    <button type="button" class="wr-chip" role="tab"
            [class.is-on]="period() === p.key" [attr.aria-selected]="period() === p.key"
            [disabled]="loading()" (click)="setPeriod(p.key)">{{ p.label }}</button>
  }
</div>
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

These are the same ARIA-tab-pattern violations previously flagged on meds, but on two additional desktop pages the earlier round did not name (Wrapped here, and Insights at insights.page.ts:66-71 with the identical shape). Declaring role=tablist + role=tab commits to the WAI-ARIA tabs keyboard contract: exactly one tab tabbable (roving tabindex) and Left/Right/Home/End arrow navigation between tabs. Neither page implements a keydown handler or tabindex management (no keydown/ArrowRight/tabindex in either file), and the tabs carry no aria-controls pointing at a role=tabpanel. A screen-reader/keyboard user is told 'tab, 1 of N' but the arrow keys they are trained to use do nothing, and there is no associated tabpanel, so the announced role misleads.

Independent verifier — confirmation
<div class="wr-hero__periods" role="tablist" aria-label="Wrapped period">
  @for (p of periods; track p.key) {
    <button type="button" class="wr-chip" role="tab"
            [class.is-on]="period() === p.key" [attr.aria-selected]="period() === p.key"
            [disabled]="loading()" (click)="setPeriod(p.key)">{{ p.label }}</button>
  }
</div>  (wrapped.page.ts:44-50; identical shape in insights.page.ts:66-71)

Verified by reading both files and grepping for keyboard/ARIA wiring. wrapped.page.ts:44-50 and insights.page.ts:66-71 both declare role=tablist + role=tab with aria-selected but implement none of the WAI-ARIA tabs keyboard contract: no keydown handler, no Arrow/Home/End navigation, no roving tabindex, and no aria-controls pointing to a role=tabpanel (grep for keydown|Arrow|tabindex|aria-controls|tabpanel returned zero matches in both). The announced "tab, N of M" semantics thus mislead AT/keyboard users about arrow-key navigation. Correctly located and correctly a distinct instance from the previously-flagged meds page. Impact is bounded: each control is a real <button>, so it is still Tab-reachable and Enter/Space-activatable — nothing is functionally unreachable — which is why this sits at the Low/Medium boundary; Medium is acceptable for an enterprise a11y bar. Remediation (prose): either drop the role=tab/tablist/aria-selected and use a plain button group / aria-pressed segmented control (simplest, since there is no real tabpanel), or fully implement the tabs pattern — single roving tabindex with Left/Right/Home/End key handling and aria-controls linking each tab to a role=tabpanel region.

Remediation (approach)

Either implement the full roving-tabindex + arrow-key contract these desktop tablists advertise (one active tab tabindex=0, others -1; Arrow/Home/End move selection; add aria-controls + a role=tabpanel with matching aria-labelledby), or drop the tab/tablist roles and expose the chips as a labelled group of toggle buttons (e.g. role=group with aria-pressed) so the announced semantics match the actual keyboard behaviour.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Backend architecture & debt CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

TrackerEndpoints embeds business logic and data access directly in endpoint handlers (2522-line endpoint file)

src/Api/Endpoints/TrackerEndpoints.cs:203-279 Layering violations
Evidence
g.MapPost("/food/from-meal", async (
    AddFoodFromMealRequest req, CurrentUserAccessor me, UsageDbContext db, CancellationToken ct) =>
{
    ... var meal = await db.FamilyMeals.AsNoTracking().FirstOrDefaultAsync(...);
    var isMember = await db.HouseholdMembers.AsNoTracking().AnyAsync(...);
    ... var perServingCal = (double)meal.Calories / servings;
    var calories = Math.Max(0, (int)Math.Round(perServingCal * s, MidpointRounding.AwayFromZero));
    ... db.FoodEntries.Add(entry); await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The endpoint lambda performs cross-aggregate authorization (household membership lookups), domain math (per-serving macro derivation, clamping/rounding), target-user resolution, and direct DbContext writes inline. This 2522-line file is the largest in the backend and mixes HTTP concerns with data/business logic, so the rules (who may write another user's tracker, how servings scale, macro rounding) cannot be unit-tested without spinning up the HTTP pipeline, cannot be reused by the AI/family callers that need the same math, and drift silently from the parallel copies in AiEndpoints/HardChallengeEndpoints. For a system handling health/PII data, authorization logic living inline in every handler is also easy to get subtly wrong per-endpoint.

Independent verifier — confirmation
TrackerEndpoints.cs:214-216  var isMember = await db.HouseholdMembers.AsNoTracking()\n    .AnyAsync(hm => hm.HouseholdId == meal.HouseholdId && hm.UserId == caller.Id, ct);\n    if (!isMember) return Results.NotFound();\n...:243-249  var perServingCal = (double)meal.Calories / servings;\n  var calories = Math.Max(0, (int)Math.Round(perServingCal * s, MidpointRounding.AwayFromZero));\n...:275-276  db.FoodEntries.Add(entry);\n  await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);  (file confirmed 2522 lines)

Read confirms the core structural claim: the /food/from-meal handler (lines 203-279) inlines cross-aggregate household-membership authorization (214-216, 228-230), target-user email resolution (232-235), per-serving macro derivation with clamp/round (239-252), and direct DbContext writes (275-276), all in a 2522-line endpoint file (verified via wc -l). The finding's remediation direction matches the repo's OWN conventions: a Services layer exists and already extracts parallel tracker logic (Services/TrackerVisibility.cs — whose doc-comment explicitly says it was factored out so HardChallengeEndpoints and TrackerEndpoints share ONE gate rather than duplicating; Services/TrackerStats.cs holds tracker math). So the layering concern is legitimate and testability/reuse arguments hold. HOWEVER two things are overstated: (1) the claimed 'parallel copies in AiEndpoints/HardChallengeEndpoints' of THIS per-serving macro math is unsubstantiated — grep for perServing / '/ servings' math in AiEndpoints returned no matches, so the 'silent drift' rationale is speculative; (2) no actual correctness or security bug is demonstrated — the inline auth is careful and correct (404-not-403, same membership predicate applied to the target, disabled/unknown target -> 404). This is a maintainability/testability/layering smell, not a defect with runtime impact, so High is inflated; Medium is appropriate. Confirmed as real but severity corrected to Medium.

Remediation (approach)

Extract a TrackerService (and a TrackerAuthorization/visibility helper it and HardChallenge share) that owns day aggregation (BuildDayAsync), food/exercise logging, target resolution, and the meal-to-tracker macro derivation. Endpoints should validate input shape, call the service, and map the result; DbContext access and domain math should not appear in the MapPost/MapGet lambdas. This also shrinks the God-file and lets the family/AI paths reuse one implementation.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Backend architecture & debt CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Display-timezone resolution is copy-pasted across four endpoint modules despite a shared resolver existing

src/Api/Endpoints/AiEndpoints.cs:1977-1982 Duplicated logic across endpoints/services
Evidence
private static async Task<TimeZoneInfo> ResolveTimeZoneAsync(UsageDbContext db, CancellationToken ct)
{
    var id = (await db.AppConfigs.AsNoTracking().FirstOrDefaultAsync(ct))?.DisplayTimeZone;
    if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(id)) return TimeZoneInfo.Utc;
    try { return TimeZoneInfo.FindSystemTimeZoneById(id); } catch { return TimeZoneInfo.Utc; }
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

An identical AppConfig->timezone resolver appears in AiEndpoints.cs (ResolveTimeZoneAsync, L1977), DayRecapEndpoints.cs (ResolveTimeZoneAsync, L124-129), and a near-identical ResolveTz(string) in NotificationsEndpoints.cs (L292-297) — while TrackerEndpoints.cs (L2295) correctly delegates to the shared Services.TrackerVisibility.DisplayTzAsync. The canonical resolver already exists but is not consistently used, so a change to timezone handling (e.g. per-household timezone, a fallback other than UTC, or a fix to invalid-id behavior) must be found and edited in 4+ places and WILL drift. Timezone bugs manifest as wrong 'today' boundaries on health/tracker/recap data.

Independent verifier — confirmation
AiEndpoints.cs L1977-1982: private static async Task<TimeZoneInfo> ResolveTimeZoneAsync(UsageDbContext db, CancellationToken ct) { var id = (await db.AppConfigs.AsNoTracking().FirstOrDefaultAsync(ct))?.DisplayTimeZone; if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(id)) return TimeZoneInfo.Utc; try { return TimeZoneInfo.FindSystemTimeZoneById(id); } catch { return TimeZoneInfo.Utc; } } | DayRecapEndpoints.cs L124-129: identical body | NotificationsEndpoints.cs L292-297: private static TimeZoneInfo ResolveTz(string? tzId){ if(string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(tzId)) return TimeZoneInfo.Utc; try{ return TimeZoneInfo.FindSystemTimeZoneById(tzId);} catch{ return TimeZoneInfo.Utc; } } | TrackerEndpoints.cs L2295-2297: private static Task<TimeZoneInfo> DisplayTzAsync(...) => Services.TrackerVisibility.DisplayTzAsync(db, ct);

Verified by reading all four files. AiEndpoints.ResolveTimeZoneAsync (L1977-1982) and DayRecapEndpoints.ResolveTimeZoneAsync (L124-129) are byte-identical AppConfig-fetch-and-resolve implementations; NotificationsEndpoints.ResolveTz (L292-297) is the same fallback logic on a passed-in id. Meanwhile TrackerEndpoints.DisplayTzAsync (L2295-2297) explicitly delegates to the canonical Services.TrackerVisibility.DisplayTzAsync, proving a shared resolver exists and the pattern of consolidation is already established but not applied consistently. This is a legitimate duplicated-logic / DRY finding with real drift risk for date-boundary correctness on health/tracker/recap data. Not currently a live bug (all copies share identical UTC-fallback semantics), so Medium is the correct severity — maintainability, not correctness. Line and file as cited are accurate.

Remediation (approach)

Route every display-timezone lookup and every 'today in display tz' computation through the single existing resolver (Services.TrackerVisibility.DisplayTzAsync or, better, a dedicated IClock/timezone service). Delete the per-file ResolveTimeZoneAsync/ResolveTz/TodayAsync copies in AiEndpoints, DayRecapEndpoints, and NotificationsEndpoints and have them call the shared one.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Backend architecture & debt CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Household membership/role authorization helpers are duplicated across family endpoint modules

src/Api/Endpoints/FamilyRemindersTimersEndpoints.cs:350-353 Duplicated logic across endpoints/services
Evidence
private static async Task<bool> IsHouseholdMemberAsync(
    UsageDbContext db, int householdId, int userId, CancellationToken ct) =>
    await db.HouseholdMembers.AsNoTracking()
        .AnyAsync(m => m.HouseholdId == householdId && m.UserId == userId, ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The exact same IsHouseholdMemberAsync is defined in both FamilyRemindersTimersEndpoints.cs (L350) and FamilyMealsChoresEndpoints.cs (L1981), and raw inline db.HouseholdMembers.AsNoTracking().AnyAsync(...) membership/role predicates are scattered through FamilyEndpoints, FamilyCalendarEndpoints, FamilyLocationsEndpoints, FamilyNotesListsEndpoints, NudgeEndpoints, PresenceEndpoints, SearchEndpoints and others — even though CurrentHouseholdAccessor already exists as the intended single owner of household resolution. Because these are AUTHORIZATION checks guarding private family data (locations, chores, notes, health-adjacent info), duplicating them per-file means an inconsistency or a missed check in one module is a data-leak/authorization defect, and the household model can never evolve (e.g. multi-household membership) without touching every copy.

Independent verifier — confirmation
FamilyRemindersTimersEndpoints.cs:350-353 and FamilyMealsChoresEndpoints.cs:1981-1984 are byte-identical:
private static async Task<bool> IsHouseholdMemberAsync(
    UsageDbContext db, int householdId, int userId, CancellationToken ct) =>
    await db.HouseholdMembers.AsNoTracking()
        .AnyAsync(m => m.HouseholdId == householdId && m.UserId == userId, ct);
CurrentHouseholdAccessor.cs:9-13 documents itself as the single household-resolution owner ("A user belongs to at most one household for now ... only ever resolves the CALLER's own household"). Inline HouseholdMembers.AsNoTracking().AnyAsync membership predicates recur across 7 endpoint files (FamilyToday/Family/FamilyNotesLists/FamilyRemindersTimers/FamilyMealsChores/Nudge/Tracker).

Verified by reading both files: the IsHouseholdMemberAsync helper is duplicated verbatim in FamilyRemindersTimersEndpoints.cs (L350-353) and FamilyMealsChoresEndpoints.cs (L1981-1984), and inline HouseholdMembers.AsNoTracking().AnyAsync membership checks are scattered across 7 endpoint modules. CurrentHouseholdAccessor is confirmed to exist and self-documents as the intended single owner of household resolution/membership, yet these gates bypass it. Each copy is an authorization check guarding private family data (chores, notes, reminders, locations), so a missed or inconsistent update in one copy is a real authorization/data-leak risk, and the "one household per user" model cannot evolve without touching every copy. This is a genuine duplicated-logic defect. Medium severity is appropriate: it is a maintainability/consistency/authorization-drift risk rather than a currently-exploitable vulnerability (all present copies use the identical correct predicate). Location and severity as claimed are accurate.

Remediation (approach)

Centralize membership and role checks (IsMember, RoleInHousehold, IsParent/IsChild, member-id/email listing) on CurrentHouseholdAccessor (or a dedicated HouseholdAuthorization service) and have every family endpoint call it. Remove the per-file IsHouseholdMemberAsync/RoleInHouseholdAsync copies and the inline AnyAsync membership predicates so there is one audited authorization path.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Insights parsing/domain helpers are copy-pasted verbatim between desktop and mobile twins

src/Web/src/app/features/insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:427-444 Duplication between desktop and mobile twins
Evidence
private parseR(c: InsightCard): number {
    const m = `${c.stat} ${c.detail}`.match(/r\s*=\s*(-?\d*\.?\d+)/i);
    ...
  private axisX(c: InsightCard): string {
    const parts = c.title.split(/\bvs\b|→|↔/i);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

parseR, axisX and axisY are byte-identical between features/insights/insights.page.ts (lines 383-414) and features/insights-mobile/insights.page.ts (lines 427-444), and the per-kind metadata (labels/icons/blurbs at insights lines ~382-410 vs insights-mobile ~449-463) is duplicated. There is no shared helper module (grep confirms these symbols exist ONLY in the two insights files). parseR interprets correlation strengths on the Insights page which the UI frames around health/behaviour data; a fix to the regex or the negative-magnitude handling in one twin will not reach the other, producing inconsistent statistical labelling across desktop and mobile for the same user data.

Independent verifier — confirmation
insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:427-433 `private parseR(c: InsightCard): number { const m = `${c.stat} ${c.detail}`.match(/r\s*=\s*(-?\d*\.?\d+)/i); let r = m ? Number(m[1]) : 0.5; ... if (r > 0 && (c.magnitude ?? '').toLowerCase().includes('negative')) r = -r; return Math.max(-1, Math.min(1, r)); }` — byte-identical to insights/insights.page.ts:381-388. axisX/axisY identical (mobile 437-444 vs desktop 391-398). Grep confirms parseR/axisX/axisY exist ONLY in these two files (no shared helper).

Verified by reading both files. parseR/axisX/axisY are byte-identical between the desktop twin (insights/insights.page.ts:381-398) and mobile twin (insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:427-444), and grep confirms these symbols exist only in these two files — no shared helper module exists. Per-kind metadata is also duplicated (desktop GROUP_META 402-413 vs mobile KIND_META 451-462, same labels/icons/blurbs). A regex or negative-magnitude fix in one twin will not reach the other, risking inconsistent correlation-strength labelling. This is a legitimate maintainability/consistency duplication finding. Minor correction: the finding cited desktop range 383-414, but parseR actually begins at line 381 and the metadata block is 402-413. Severity Medium is appropriate — the helpers are illustrative charting/label utilities (axis labels, donut magnitude), not core statistical computation, so the impact is consistency/maintenance rather than a correctness bug.

Remediation (approach)

Move parseR/axisX/axisY and the shared kind metadata into a single insights helper module imported by both components. Keep only the platform-specific rendering (grid layout vs feed/donut) separate.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Mobile twins reach across feature boundaries into desktop feature folders for shared logic instead of a shared/core module

src/Web/src/app/features/family-finance-mobile/family-finance-mobile.page.ts:33 Core-service coupling / inconsistent shared-code placement
Evidence
import { FINANCE_DEFAULT_CATEGORIES } from '../family/finance';
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Shared, reusable logic lives inside desktop feature folders and is imported cross-feature by mobile twins: family-finance-mobile imports FINANCE_DEFAULT_CATEGORIES from ../family/finance (line 33); family-locations-mobile imports ReplayEngine from ../family/replay-engine; family-notes-mobile imports renderMarkdown from ../family/markdown; tracker-profile-mobile imports computeStats/ageFrom/StatsInputs from ../tracker/units; bills-beta and resume-mobile import pickImage/readFileAsBase64 from ../tracker/ai-image. This is inconsistent with the stated architecture where shared services live in core/. It couples the mobile feature's build to the desktop feature's internals, blocks deleting/refactoring a desktop feature without breaking a sibling, and defeats lazy-loading boundaries (importing one symbol can drag the desktop chunk's module graph in).

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/src/app/features/family-finance-mobile/family-finance-mobile.page.ts:33: import { FINANCE_DEFAULT_CATEGORIES } from '../family/finance';
--- source symbol lives inside a desktop @Component module ---
src/Web/src/app/features/family/finance.ts:52: export const FINANCE_DEFAULT_CATEGORIES: readonly string[] = [
src/Web/src/app/features/family/finance.ts:8: ChangeDetectionStrategy, (file is an Angular component)
--- other cited cross-feature imports all verified present ---
family-notes-mobile.page.ts:13: import { renderMarkdown } from '../family/markdown';
family-locations-mobile.page.ts:14: import { ReplayEngine } from '../family/replay-engine';
tracker-profile-mobile.page.ts:25: import { StatsInputs, ageFrom, computeStats } from '../tracker/units';
resume-mobile.page.ts:14: import { readFileAsBase64 } from '../tracker/ai-image';
bills-beta.page.ts:10: import { pickImage, confirmPhotoNotice } from '../tracker/ai-image';

Verified by reading both files. Line 33 imports FINANCE_DEFAULT_CATEGORIES from '../family/finance', which is a desktop feature folder. finance.ts is itself an Angular @Component file (imports ChangeDetectionStrategy at line 8; the constant is declared at line 52 alongside the component), so a mobile twin importing this symbol couples its build/lazy-chunk graph to the desktop component's module. All five other cited cross-feature imports (family/markdown, family/replay-engine, tracker/units, tracker/ai-image x2) are confirmed present in the mobile twins. This is inconsistent with the documented architecture where shared code lives in core/ (page-registry, ViewportService, api, models all under core/). Genuine maintainability/coupling defect: blocks independent refactor or deletion of a desktop feature without breaking a sibling mobile twin, and risks dragging the desktop chunk's module graph into the mobile lazy bundle. Correctly classified Medium — architecture/maintainability debt, no runtime correctness, security, PII, auth, or payment-data impact. Line and file citations are accurate.

Remediation (approach)

Relocate genuinely shared, framework-light utilities (markdown renderer, replay engine, image helpers, finance category constants, the tracker stats/units math) into core/ or a dedicated shared/ library, and update both desktop and mobile importers to reference that neutral location. Enforce with a lint rule forbidding imports that cross from one features/<x>/ folder into another.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Several page components exceed 1500-2200 lines, mixing markup, DTO shaping, and business logic

src/Web/src/app/features/tracker/tracker.ts:238-1 Oversized components / logic in the component layer
Evidence
2205 features/tracker/tracker.ts
2087 features/users-mobile/users-mobile.page.ts
1623 features/users/users.ts
1623 features/family/calendar.ts
1550 features/resume-mobile/resume-mobile.page.ts
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

tracker.ts (2205), users-mobile (2087), users (1623), family/calendar (1623), resume-mobile (1550), chat-beta (1484), family/finance (1431), chat (1336) each bundle template, in-component derivation, and domain logic in a single class/file. Components this large are hard to test in isolation, concentrate merge conflicts, and — because domain logic sits in the component rather than a service — that logic cannot be reused by the sibling twin (which is precisely why users/users-mobile duplicate it). This raises maintenance cost and defect risk on pages that handle permissions, payments (bills/finance), and health data.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/src/app/features/tracker/tracker.ts:234-238 `templateUrl: './tracker.html', ... styleUrl: './tracker.scss', export class Tracker {` — then the class runs to line 2030 with in-component async domain logic e.g. L1106 `private async logCoffees(...)`, L1624 `private async commitBuiltDay(...)`, L1857 `private async runCopyFood(...)`, L2079 `async repullMacros(...)`, L2126 `async saveEditFood(f: FoodEntryDto)`, plus DTO shaping/derivation via dozens of computed() signals.

The oversized-component maintainability concern is real and correctly located. tracker.ts's class (L238-2030) concentrates business logic (day-move, copy-food, AI day-builder commit/undo, macro re-pull, coffee/hydration logging), DTO shaping, and many derived computed() signals in one component, not a service — so a sibling twin (e.g. users vs users-mobile) cannot reuse it, which matches the observed duplication. Two inaccuracies to correct: (1) the claim that files 'mix markup' in the same file is wrong — templates/styles are external (templateUrl/styleUrl), so the counted lines are pure TS logic, which actually strengthens the 'logic-in-component' point rather than weakening it; (2) the cited line counts are inflated — actual: tracker 2030 (not 2205), users-mobile 1887 (not 2087), users 1488 (not 1623), family/calendar 1488 (not 1623), resume-mobile 1428 (not 1550), family/finance 1293 (not 1431), chat 1213 (not 1336), and chat-beta/chat-beta.ts does not exist at that path. Despite the numeric drift, every existing file still substantially exceeds a healthy component size and the design defect holds. Medium is appropriate: it is a maintainability/testability/merge-risk concern on sensitive pages (permissions, finance, health), not a runtime bug.

Remediation (approach)

Decompose the largest pages by extracting domain/derivation logic into injectable services or plain helper modules and splitting the template into smaller presentational child components. Prioritise the users and finance pages given their sensitivity. Consider a soft component-size lint budget to prevent regrowth.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Backend architecture & debt CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

StatsAsync CurrentStreakDays is computed from the tail of active dates without anchoring to today, so a lapsed user still shows an ongoing streak

src/Api/Services/UsageQueries.cs:176-198 Business-logic defect in a shared analytics service
Evidence
var current = 1;
for (var i = activeDates.Count - 1; i > 0; i--)
{
    if (activeDates[i] == activeDates[i - 1].AddDays(1)) current++;
    else break;
}
// ...
CurrentStreakDays = current,
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

activeDates is just the distinct LocalDates present in the (filtered) data, ordered ascending. current starts at 1 and counts consecutive days ending at the MOST RECENT active date — it is never compared to 'today' in the display timezone. A user whose last activity was three weeks ago still reports CurrentStreakDays >= 1 (and possibly a long streak) as if it were live. The headline 'current streak' on the dashboard is therefore wrong for any user who is not active today, overstating engagement. (LongestStreakDays is correct; only the 'current' anchor is missing.)

Independent verifier — confirmation
// UsageQueries.cs:168 activeDates = rows.Select(r => r.LocalDate).Distinct().OrderBy(d => d)
// UsageQueries.cs:176-181
var current = 1;
for (var i = activeDates.Count - 1; i > 0; i--)
{
    if (activeDates[i] == activeDates[i - 1].AddDays(1)) current++;
    else break;
}
// UsageQueries.cs:198  CurrentStreakDays = current,

Verified by reading lines 110-202. activeDates is the distinct LocalDates from the filtered rows, ordered ascending (line 168). The current-streak loop (176-181) initializes current=1 and counts consecutive days ending at the MOST RECENT active date in the data — it never compares activeDates[^1] to 'today' in the display tz. rows come from FilteredCapped(f), a range filter with no today-anchor, and the cap retains the most-recent window but does not tie it to the current date. Therefore a lapsed user (last activity weeks ago) still reports CurrentStreakDays >= 1 as if live, overstating the dashboard's headline streak. LongestStreakDays (169-175) is computed independently and is correct; only the current anchor is missing. Business-logic defect, correctly located, Medium severity appropriate (engagement metric accuracy, not security/PII).
