One nuance: if the filter window itself excludes today, the metric is arguably 'current within range', but the display presents it as a live streak, so the overstatement stands.

Remediation (approach)

Anchor the current streak to the display-timezone 'today' (the same today the tracker/DisplayTz helpers already compute): if the most recent active date is neither today nor yesterday, the current streak is 0; otherwise count backward from that anchor. Compute today once via the shared DisplayTz resolver rather than re-deriving it.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Backend architecture & debt CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

A user disabled (or whose chat permission is revoked) while holding a live ChatHub connection keeps receiving channel broadcasts, because group membership is bound only at connect time with no periodic re-check

src/Api/Hubs/ChatHub.cs:39-46 Inconsistent auth pattern / second-order authorization gap (SignalR)
Evidence
public override async Task OnConnectedAsync()
{
    var email = Email;
    ...
    if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(email) || !await HasReadAsync(scope, email)) { Context.Abort(); return; }
    var channelIds = await db.ChatChannelMembers.AsNoTracking().Where(m => m.UserEmail == email).Select(m => m.ChannelId).ToListAsync(...);
    foreach (var id in channelIds)
        await Groups.AddToGroupAsync(Context.ConnectionId, ChatNotificationService.GroupFor(id));
    await base.OnConnectedAsync();
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

OnConnectedAsync checks IsEnabled+chat.read once and subscribes the connection to its channel SignalR groups. Send/react paths re-check HasSendAsync, but there is no re-check on the RECEIVE side and no server-initiated abort when an admin later disables the account or revokes chat.read WITHOUT bumping SessionVersion. The existing connection stays in the groups and continues to receive every fan-out message until it happens to disconnect. Prior rounds flagged that a SessionVersion bump does not tear down an existing hub connection; this is the adjacent case where disabling/permission-revocation (which does not necessarily bump sv) also fails to stop message delivery to an already-connected client.

Independent verifier — confirmation
ChatHub.cs:39-44 var channelIds = await db.ChatChannelMembers... foreach (var id in channelIds) await Groups.AddToGroupAsync(Context.ConnectionId, ChatNotificationService.GroupFor(id)); | ChatNotificationService.cs:63 await hub.Clients.Group(GroupFor(channel.Id)).SendAsync("ReceiveMessage", dto, ct); | UsersEndpoints.cs:177-184 user.IsEnabled = req.IsEnabled; db.UserPermissions.RemoveRange(user.Permissions); ... await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct); await tx.CommitAsync(ct); (no SessionVersion bump, no SessionRevoked push, no hub abort) — contrast UsersEndpoints.cs:220,229 user.SessionVersion += 1; ... hub.Clients.User(user.Email).SendAsync("SessionRevoked", ct)

Verified real. OnConnectedAsync (ChatHub.cs 26-46) checks IsEnabled+chat.read once and binds the connection to its channel SignalR groups. Fan-out uses Clients.Group with no per-recipient re-check (ChatNotificationService.cs:63). Send/react/mark-read/typing/join DO re-check HasReadAsync/HasSendAsync (which filter on u.IsEnabled), but there is no receive-side re-check and no server-initiated teardown. The admin Update endpoint (UsersEndpoints.cs:176-184) sets IsEnabled=false / strips permissions WITHOUT bumping SessionVersion, WITHOUT pushing SessionRevoked, and WITHOUT aborting the hub connection or removing group membership — only the separate /logout endpoint (220-234) does the real-time teardown. So a disabled/deauthorized user holding a live ChatHub connection keeps receiving every channel broadcast until it happens to disconnect. Correctly located; group binding originates at ChatHub.cs:39-44 (anchored line 39). Medium is fair: it leaks ongoing chat content (potential PII) to a just-revoked user, but requires a pre-existing live connection and an admin action that (by product design distinction) does not itself force logout.

Remediation (approach)

On any account disable or chat-permission revocation, force the affected user's live connections closed (e.g. bump SessionVersion as part of disable, and/or track connectionIds per email and call Context.Abort on them), and/or periodically re-validate IsEnabled/chat.read on long-lived connections before continuing to deliver. Treat disable and revoke as connection-terminating events consistently with the REST permission filter.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Two parallel home-route lists: PageDef.home in the registry is dead code and has already drifted from HOME_OPTIONS

src/Web/src/app/core/page-registry.ts:57 Frontend architecture & duplication
Evidence
  /** Shown in the home-route picker when the caller can reach it. Label defaults to the nav label. */
  readonly home?: { readonly label: string; readonly icon?: string };
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The page-registry was built to be 'the single source of truth' and declares a `home` entry on 37 pages (Dashboard, Meds, Habits, Journal, Pacts, Wrapped, Insights, Your Day, Resume, Agents, Inbox, Search, tracker-profile, settings-health, ...). But NOTHING reads `PageDef.home` — a repo-wide grep shows all four home pickers (app.ts:449-451, beta-settings.page.ts:607-609, users-mobile.page.ts:1219, preferences-mobile.page.ts:488) and the honor-check `AuthService.canAccessHome` (auth.ts:42-43 via HOME_PERMS) read the SEPARATE hand-maintained list in core/home-options.ts. The two lists have already diverged: the registry advertises Meds/Journal/Wrapped/Insights/Your Day/Resume/Agents/Inbox/Search/Habits/Pacts/Trophies as home options, but HOME_OPTIONS omits every one of them — so a user CANNOT actually pick those pages as their home despite the registry docstring promising 'Shown in the home-route picker.' This is exactly the drift the registry claims to eliminate, plus 37 lines of dead metadata that will keep drifting.

Independent verifier — confirmation
page-registry.ts:56-57  `/** Shown in the home-route picker when the caller can reach it. Label defaults to the nav label. */  readonly home?: { readonly label: string; readonly icon?: string };` — declared on ~35 pages (grep: 35 `home:` entries) yet grep for `.home` across src/app shows the field is NEVER read: nav-model.ts (the sole PageDef consumer) reads only p.nav/p.path/p.perm/p.anyPerm/p.id, and all 4 pickers + canAccessHome use HOME_OPTIONS/HOME_PERMS from home-options.ts (app.ts:451, beta-settings.page.ts:609, preferences-mobile.page.ts:490, auth.ts:43).

Verified true. PageDef.home is dead metadata: no code path reads it. nav-model.ts is the only other importer of PageDef and touches nav/path/perm/anyPerm/id only. The four home pickers and AuthService.canAccessHome all consume the separate hand-maintained HOME_OPTIONS/HOME_PERMS in core/home-options.ts. Drift is confirmed: registry advertises home for Meds(148), Journal(169), Wrapped(269), Insights(276), Your Day(262), Resume(255), Agents(213), Inbox(220), Search(283), Habits(162), Pacts(190) — none appear in HOME_OPTIONS, so the docstring 'Shown in the home-route picker' is false for them. This contradicts the registry's own 'single source of truth' claim and is exactly the drift it was built to eliminate. One correction to the finding text: Trophies IS present in HOME_OPTIONS (home-options.ts:37 `/trophies`), so 'omits every one of them' is slightly overstated — the drift covers ~11 of the 12 named pages, not all 12. No runtime/user-facing bug (the actual picker is correct via HOME_OPTIONS); impact is dead code + misleading metadata that will keep drifting. Medium severity is appropriate for an architecture/duplication finding.

Remediation (approach)

Pick ONE source of truth. Either delete the `home` field from PageDef and every registry entry (and keep home-options.ts as the sole list), or derive HOME_OPTIONS and HOME_PERMS from the registry's `home` field (mapping each PageDef's perm/anyPerm into the option's perms) and delete core/home-options.ts. Whichever is chosen, ensure the picker options, the persisted-home honor check, and the backend HomeRoutes allowlist all read from that one derived list so they cannot drift.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Generic file/util helpers are trapped inside the tracker feature and pulled cross-boundary by resume, resume-mobile, and family-calendar

src/Web/src/app/features/resume-mobile/resume-mobile.page.ts:14 Frontend architecture & duplication
Evidence
import { readFileAsBase64 } from '../tracker/ai-image';
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

`readFileAsBase64` (a completely domain-agnostic Blob->base64 reader) is defined in features/tracker/ai-image.ts:213 and imported by three unrelated features: features/resume/resume.ts:35, features/resume-mobile/resume-mobile.page.ts:14, and features/family/calendar.ts:41 (which also grabs downscaleToJpeg). This is a concrete instance of the mobile-and-desktop-twins-reach-into-sibling-feature-folders anti-pattern, and it is systemic: admin-locations-mobile/locations-mobile/family-locations-mobile import from ../location, family-finance-mobile imports FINANCE_DEFAULT_CATEGORIES from ../family/finance, tracker-profile-mobile imports computeStats/ageFrom/StatsInputs/OnboardingCard from ../tracker, feed-mobile from ../feed, family-notes-mobile from ../family/markdown, settings-health-mobile from ../settings-health. Deleting or refactoring the tracker feature would break resume, resume-mobile, and family calendar image upload — a lazy-loaded chunk boundary is violated and the dependency graph is a tangle rather than a tree.

Independent verifier — confirmation
resume-mobile/resume-mobile.page.ts:14: `import { readFileAsBase64 } from '../tracker/ai-image';`; also confirmed resume/resume.ts:35 `import { readFileAsBase64 } from '../tracker/ai-image';` and family/calendar.ts:41 `import { downscaleToJpeg, readFileAsBase64 } from '../tracker/ai-image';`. Source: features/tracker/ai-image.ts:213 `export function readFileAsBase64(file: Blob): Promise<string>` — a fully domain-agnostic Blob→base64 reader.

Verified all cited lines by reading the files. resume-mobile.page.ts:14, resume/resume.ts:35, and family/calendar.ts:41 all import the generic readFileAsBase64 (and calendar also downscaleToJpeg) from ../tracker/ai-image. The function at tracker/ai-image.ts:213 is a completely domain-agnostic Blob→base64 reader with no tracker-specific logic. Three unrelated features (resume desktop, resume mobile, family calendar) reach cross-feature-boundary into the tracker feature folder for a generic file util. This is a real coupling/duplication-of-ownership smell that violates lazy-loaded chunk boundaries and makes the dependency graph a tangle rather than a tree; deleting/refactoring tracker would break resume and calendar image upload. Not a correctness or security bug, so Medium (maintainability/architecture) is the correct severity. Fix in prose: extract readFileAsBase64/downscaleToJpeg into a shared core/util (e.g. core/file.ts or shared/image util) and have tracker, resume, resume-mobile, and family/calendar all import from that neutral location.

Remediation (approach)

Move genuinely shared, domain-agnostic helpers (file readers, image downscaling, markdown rendering, the LocationMap component, the computeStats/units math) into src/Web/src/app/shared/ or core/ modules and have BOTH the desktop feature and its mobile twin import from there. No feature folder should import from another feature folder; a mobile twin should depend only on core/shared and its own folder. Enforce with an ESLint import-boundary rule (e.g. no-restricted-imports on '../<other-feature>/*').

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

users-mobile re-implements the sensitive email-reveal/permission-grant UI (2087 lines — larger than the desktop original) and its docstring falsely claims emails are never revealed

src/Web/src/app/features/users-mobile/users-mobile.page.ts:82-116 Frontend architecture & duplication
Evidence
 * PRIVACY: emails are NEVER revealed here (the live page's key-gated reveal is desktop-only); other users'
 * rows show name + masked identity only, in line with the app's email-privacy rule.
 ...
            <button type="button" class="um-reveal-toggle" [class.is-on]="emailsRevealed()"
 ...
              {{ emailsRevealed() ? 'Hide emails' : 'Show emails' }}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The component's own PRIVACY docstring asserts the key-gated email reveal is 'desktop-only' and emails are 'NEVER revealed here', yet the template (lines 108-117) and TS (revealKey/emailsRevealed/toggleEmails/hideEmails at 950-1394, re-fetch with the key at 1257 and 1296) fully re-implement the reveal — a verbatim copy of features/users/users.ts:732-789. The reveal itself stays server-gated (the key is passed to Api.users), so it is not an authorization hole, but a false security docstring on the most sensitive admin surface is dangerous: a future maintainer trusting the comment could relax the mobile gate believing no PII is exposed. The whole 2087-line file (the app's second-largest component, bigger than the 1623-line desktop page it mirrors) is a parallel copy of the grant-matrix, role presets, access-policy, and landing-page logic — every future change to the users admin flow must be made twice and can silently diverge.

Independent verifier — confirmation
Docstring (users-mobile.page.ts:82-83): " * PRIVACY: emails are NEVER revealed here (the live page's key-gated reveal is desktop-only); other users' / * rows show name + masked identity only, in line with the app's email-privacy rule."
CONTRADICTED by same file:
L112 (click)="toggleEmails()"  L115 {{ emailsRevealed() ? 'Hide emails' : 'Show emails' }}
L242 @if (emailsRevealed() && u.email) {  L313 @if (emailsRevealed() && u.email) { <span class="ud__email">{{ u.email }}</span> }
L1257 firstValueFrom(this.api.users(this.revealKey ?? undefined)), // pass the key when revealed
L1296 this.api.auditLog(this.revealKey ?? undefined).subscribe({ // pass the key when revealed
L1342 toggleEmails(): void { ... L1365 this.emailsRevealed.set(true);

Verified by reading the file. The class docstring at lines 82-83 asserts "emails are NEVER revealed here" and that the key-gated reveal is "desktop-only", but the same component fully implements it: a "Show emails" toggle (L109-116), revealKey passed to api.users()/api.auditLog() (L1257, L1296), toggleEmails/hideEmails/emailsRevealed state (L1342-1394), and template rendering of u.email in both the list and audit rows (L242, L286, L313). The docstring is factually false on the app's most sensitive admin/PII surface. The reveal remains server-gated by the key (no authorization bypass), so this is not a Critical/High auth hole — correctly rated Medium as a security-documentation + duplication defect (2087-line mobile twin vs 1623-line desktop original, a maintenance-divergence hazard). Core claim, location, and severity all hold. corrected line set to 82 (start of the false PRIVACY assertion).

Remediation (approach)

Extract the shared users-admin logic (reveal-key state machine, upsert-body builder, PERM_GROUP_ORDER grouping, nonDefaultable set, home-option computation) into a shared injectable/service or presentational component consumed by both the desktop and mobile pages, leaving each page only its layout. At minimum, immediately correct the mobile docstring to state that the key-gated reveal IS implemented and server-enforced, so the comment cannot mislead a reviewer into loosening the gate.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Backend architecture & debt CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Open-signup default set can auto-grant privileged CONFIG-WRITE keys (settings.manage, sources.manage, pricing.manage, reporter.manage, notifications.manage, shares.manage) to every self-provisioning Google account

src/Api/Auth/Permissions.cs:355-362 Inconsistent auth patterns / privilege escalation surface
Evidence
public static bool IsDefaultable(string key) =>
    IsValid(key) && key != UsersManage && key != ChatModerate && key != ChatContactsManage
    && key != TrackerViewAll && key != FamilyUse && key != FamilyFinance && key != CycleTrack
    && key != ChoreClaim && key != AllowanceManage && key != IdentityMap
    && key != AiUsageView && key != BillsUse && key != PlatformMobile && key != AutomationsUse
    && key != AgentsUse && key != HealthSync
    && key != RecipesUse && key != GroceryUse && key != MealsUse && key != ResumeUse
    && !AiKeys.Contains(key) && !LocationKeys.Contains(key);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

IsDefaultable is the ONLY gate on what an admin may put in the open-signup default set (GoogleAuthService.ParseDefaultPermissions filters by it, Permissions.cs L355; the policy is applied verbatim to every auto-provisioned account, GoogleAuthService.cs L66-76). Prior rounds flagged the READ keys users.view/activity.view. But IsDefaultable ALSO returns true for the privileged WRITE keys settings.manage, sources.manage, pricing.manage, reporter.manage, notifications.manage, shares.manage (none are excluded). With open sign-up ON by default (Program.cs L427) an admin who adds any of these to the default CSV grants EVERY anonymous Google account the ability to edit ingestion sources / global settings / pricing, MINT and revoke system ingest keys (reporter.manage — a data-ingestion credential), rewrite the Discord/notification config, and create/revoke public share links — a config-tampering and credential-minting escalation, materially worse than the read-only leak already reported. These are Admin-group capabilities that should never be inheritable by default.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Auth/Permissions.cs:355-362
    public static bool IsDefaultable(string key) =>
        IsValid(key) && key != UsersManage && key != ChatModerate && key != ChatContactsManage
        && key != TrackerViewAll && key != FamilyUse && key != FamilyFinance && key != CycleTrack
        && key != ChoreClaim && key != AllowanceManage && key != IdentityMap
        && key != AiUsageView && key != BillsUse && key != PlatformMobile && key != AutomationsUse
        && key != AgentsUse && key != HealthSync
        && key != RecipesUse && key != GroceryUse && key != MealsUse && key != ResumeUse
        && !AiKeys.Contains(key) && !LocationKeys.Contains(key);
--- src/Api/Services/GoogleAuthService.cs:179-181 (only IsDefaultable filters the default set) ---
    private static string[] ParseDefaultPermissions(string? csv) => ... .Where(Auth.Permissions.IsDefaultable).Distinct().ToArray();
--- src/Api/Services/GoogleAuthService.cs:66,76 (applied verbatim to auto-provisioned account) ---
    var defaultPerms = ParseDefaultPermissions(cfg.DefaultPermissionsCsv); ... Permissions = defaultPerms.Select(p => new UserPermission { Permission = p }).ToList(),
--- src/Api/Program.cs:427 (open signup on by default) ---
    OpenSignupEnabled = builder.Configuration.GetValue("Auth:OpenSignupEnabled", true),
--- src/Api/Endpoints/IngestEndpoints.cs:48,136 (reporter.manage = system-wide ingest-key mint/revoke) ---
    var canManage = caller.Permissions.Contains(Permissions.ReporterManage);

Defect is real and correctly located. IsDefaultable (L355-362) is the sole gate on what an admin may place in the open-signup default set (GoogleAuthService.ParseDefaultPermissions L179-181 filters by it; the result is applied verbatim to every auto-provisioned Google account, GoogleAuthService L66/76). The exclusion list does NOT contain settings.manage, sources.manage, pricing.manage, reporter.manage, notifications.manage, or shares.manage (verified against constants L23-59 and the catalog L187-243), and none are in AiKeys/LocationKeys — so IsDefaultable returns true for all six privileged config-write/credential-minting capabilities. reporter.manage in particular mints/revokes ANY ingest key system-wide (IngestEndpoints L48/136). This contradicts the documented intent of IsDefaultable's doc comment (L340-353), which excludes users.manage, bills.use, automations.use, etc. precisely because they must never be inherited by every new account — a genuine allowlist-completeness gap in a security guardrail. Two corrections to the finding's framing that lower severity from High to Medium: (1) exploitation is NOT unconditional — the shipped default is dashboard.view only (Program.cs L428, AppConfig L34); the escalation requires a privileged admin to deliberately add one of these keys to the default CSV, so IsDefaultable is a defense-in-depth foot-gun guardrail that fails to catch an admin mistake, not a directly reachable vuln. (2) Only settings.manage and sources.manage are literally in the 'Admin' group (L242-243); the other four are 'Usage'-group manage keys — the 'Admin-group capabilities' phrasing overstates. Same allowlist prior rounds flagged for the read keys users.view/activity.view; this is the write-key extension. Fix: extend the IsDefaultable exclusion list (or invert to an explicit safe-defaults allowlist) so no *.manage / config-write / credential-minting key can ever enter the auto-provision default set.

Remediation (approach)

Extend the IsDefaultable exclusion list to also reject every *.manage / privileged administration write key (settings.manage, sources.manage, pricing.manage, reporter.manage, notifications.manage, shares.manage) — ideally by deriving the defaultable set from an explicit allowlist of genuinely low-risk keys rather than a denylist, so newly added privileged keys are non-defaultable by default. Reuse the same predicate everywhere the default CSV is parsed/saved.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Command palette is a 4th hand-maintained page list that has already drifted — 12 real pages are unreachable via ⌘K

src/Web/src/app/core/command-registry.ts:40-64 Duplication / inconsistent state patterns
Evidence
export const COMMAND_DEFS: readonly CommandDef[] = [
  // ---- Go to (navigation) — mirrors homeOptionDefs route/label/perms + icons from the nav ----
  { id: 'nav-dashboard', label: 'Dashboard', ... route: '/', perm: [PERM.dashboardView], ... },
  ...
  { id: 'nav-settings', label: 'Settings', ... route: '/settings', perm: [PERM.settingsView], ... },
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

COMMAND_DEFS is a FOURTH parallel copy of the page/route/permission taxonomy (alongside PAGE_REGISTRY, HOME_OPTIONS, and nav-model), maintained by hand and self-described as a 'SUPERSET of homeOptionDefs'. It has already drifted: PAGE_REGISTRY registers /grocery, /recipes, /meal-planner, /resume, /today, /wrapped, /insights, /inbox, /meds, /habits, /journal, and /pacts as first-class navigable, permissioned pages, but NONE of them appear in the palette's 'Go to' list (verified: 0 occurrences of each route). The ⌘K command palette — a primary navigation affordance — silently cannot reach 12 shipped pages, and each future page added to the registry must be remembered here too or it vanishes from search. This is the exact drift the registry was built to eliminate, re-introduced.

Independent verifier — confirmation
command-registry.ts:35 "NAV entries are a SUPERSET of the shell's `homeOptionDefs`" + COMMAND_DEFS (lines 40-64) contains no /grocery,/recipes,/meal-planner,/resume,/today,/wrapped,/insights,/inbox,/meds,/habits,/journal,/pacts (grep = 0 occurrences), yet page-registry.ts registers each as a first-class permissioned nav page, e.g. page-registry.ts:230 "{ id: 'grocery', path: 'grocery', ... perm: PERM.groceryUse, ... nav: { group: 'Tools', label: 'Grocery' ... } }" and :144 meds, :158 habits, :165 journal, :186 pacts, :216 inbox, :237 recipes, :244 meal-planner, :251 resume, :258 today, :265 wrapped, :272 insights. command-palette.ts:90 "return COMMAND_DEFS.filter((c) => {" — the palette is driven solely by the static list, no PAGE_REGISTRY augmentation.

Verified against source. COMMAND_DEFS (command-registry.ts:40+) is a fourth hand-maintained copy of the page/route/perm taxonomy, self-described as a SUPERSET of homeOptionDefs (line 35). PAGE_REGISTRY (page-registry.ts) — which was explicitly created to END this class of drift (see its lines 10-12) — registers 12 named routes (grocery, recipes, meal-planner, resume, today, wrapped, insights, inbox, meds, habits, journal, pacts) as first-class permissioned nav pages; grep confirms ZERO of them appear in command-registry.ts. command-palette.ts:88-98 filters only COMMAND_DEFS with no dynamic derivation from PAGE_REGISTRY, so those 12 shipped pages are unreachable via the ⌘K palette, and every future registry page must be re-copied here or it silently vanishes from search. The defect is real and correctly located. I lowered severity High→Medium: this is a UX/maintainability drift in a SECONDARY navigation affordance — permissions are still correctly enforced on the commands that exist, and all 12 pages remain reachable via the primary nav menus/home picker, so there is no auth/PII/data-integrity break. Remediation (prose): derive the 'Go to' commands from PAGE_REGISTRY (mapping each navigable/home page's path→route, its perm/anyPerm→perm, and family children→requireAll double-gate) so the palette shares the single source of truth, leaving only the non-registry Actions/Account commands hand-authored; alternatively add a build/unit test asserting every PAGE_REGISTRY page with a nav or home entry has a corresponding COMMAND_DEFS route.

Remediation (approach)

Derive the palette's navigation ('Go to') commands from PAGE_REGISTRY / nav-model instead of a hand-copied array: generate one CommandDef per registry page (route, label, perm/anyPerm, icon) and keep only the ACTION and ACCOUNT commands hand-written. If keywords must stay curated, attach them via a side map keyed by page id rather than re-declaring the whole route list, so a page can never exist in the registry yet be missing from the palette.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Mobile twins deep-link to the legacy /tracker-beta tree, sending mobile users to a parallel (non-canonical) rendering

src/Web/src/app/features/insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:96 Platform-split correctness / dead routes
Evidence
            ctaLabel="Open the tracker"
            ctaLink="/tracker-beta" />
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The mobile insights/trophies/wrapped twins hard-code ctaLink/routerLink to '/tracker-beta' (also in trophies-beta.page.ts:109,147 and wrapped-beta.page.ts:134). '/tracker-beta' is served by the legacy BETA_ROUTES tree (app.routes.ts:33), which is a SEPARATE component instance from the canonical '/tracker' mobile twin that the platform-split canMatch now serves to the same user. A mobile user tapping 'Open the tracker' is bounced out of the canonical route space into the parallel beta tree — a different URL, different back-stack, and (once /tracker-beta is eventually turned into a redirect, per the file's own TODO) a broken link. This couples the graduated mobile pages to routes the architecture intends to retire.

Independent verifier — confirmation
insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:95-96  `ctaLabel="Open the tracker"` / `ctaLink="/tracker-beta"`. Cross-checked: page-registry.ts:124-126 registers canonical `id:'tracker', path:'tracker'` whose mobile twin is `tracker-beta/tracker-beta.page → TrackerBetaPage` — the SAME component `/tracker-beta` loads (app.routes.ts:33). BETA_ROUTES doc (app.routes.ts:29-30): "Once a canonical page renders the same component per-device (Wave 1+), these can become redirects to the canonical path." Also trophies-beta.page.ts:109 `routerLink="/tracker-beta"` and :147 `routerLink="/tracker-beta"`, and wrapped-beta.page.ts:134 `routerLink="/tracker-beta"`.

Verified all three mobile twins are the registry-served twins (insights.page mobile @ registry:274; wrapped-beta @ :267; trophies twin also served) and all hard-code '/tracker-beta'. The canonical '/tracker' route (registry:124-126) renders the identical TrackerBetaPage component per-device, so these CTAs push mobile users out of the canonical URL space into the parallel BETA_ROUTES tree (app.routes.ts:33) — a different URL/back-stack and, per the file's own stated intent to convert these beta routes to redirects, a link that will break or double-redirect. Real platform-split correctness defect, correctly located; Medium severity is right (navigation/architecture coupling, no data/security impact). Fix in prose: repoint these CTA/routerLink targets from '/tracker-beta' to the canonical '/tracker' so the graduated mobile twins stay within the platform-split route space.

Remediation (approach)

Point these CTAs at the canonical registry path ('/tracker'); on a mobile device the platform canMatch already renders the mobile twin there, so the beta URL is never needed. Audit all -mobile/-beta twins for hard-coded '/tracker-beta' and '/beta/*' links and repoint them to canonical paths, then the BETA_ROUTES tree can safely collapse to redirects without breaking in-app navigation.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

homeRoute() fallback scans a hand-picked 13-route subset, dumping users whose only permission is on an omitted page to /welcome

src/Web/src/app/core/auth.ts:60-68 Inconsistent state patterns / reachability
Evidence
    const order = [
      '/', '/calendar', '/pricing', '/reporter', '/fleet',
      '/tracker', '/feed', '/family', '/chat', '/locations',
      '/users', '/activity', '/settings',
    ];
    for (const route of order) {
      if (this.canAccessHome(route)) return route;
    }
    return '/welcome';
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

canAccessHome() is now backed by the full HOME_OPTIONS list, but homeRoute()'s fallback still iterates a hand-curated 13-entry subset. A user provisioned with ONLY (for example) grocery.use, recipes.use, meals.use, bills.use, automations.use, or agents.use satisfies none of the 13 listed routes — even though /grocery, /recipes, /bills, /automations, /agents are all valid HOME_OPTIONS they can access — so homeRoute() returns '/welcome' as if they had no accessible pages. The comment claims 'every route is represented — no dead landings', which is false; the list drifted from HOME_OPTIONS. Post-login and brand-link navigation strand these users on the welcome/awaiting-approval screen despite having usable pages.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/src/app/core/auth.ts:60-68:
    const order = [
      '/', '/calendar', '/pricing', '/reporter', '/fleet',
      '/tracker', '/feed', '/family', '/chat', '/locations',
      '/users', '/activity', '/settings',
    ];
    for (const route of order) {
      if (this.canAccessHome(route)) return route;
    }
    return '/welcome';
— vs home-options.ts:74-75 where HOME_PERMS is derived from the FULL HOME_OPTIONS list including /grocery(grocery.use), /recipes, /meal-planner, /bills, /automations, /agents, /ask, /challenge, /trophies, /people — none of which appear in `order`.

Verified against both files. canAccessHome() is backed by HOME_PERMS, which home-options.ts:74-75 derives from the complete HOME_OPTIONS list. homeRoute()'s fallback (auth.ts:60-64) iterates only a hand-curated 13-route subset and omits genuinely accessible routes such as /grocery, /recipes, /meal-planner, /bills, /automations, /agents, /ask, /challenge, /trophies, /people. models.ts:6226-6249 confirms grocery.use, recipes.use, meals.use, bills.use, automations.use, agents.use are distinct, separately grantable permissions. A user provisioned with ONLY one of those satisfies none of the 13 order routes, so homeRoute() returns '/welcome' — stranding them on the welcome/awaiting-approval screen on post-login and brand-link navigation despite holding a usable page. The line-59 comment ('every route is represented — no dead landings') is factually false; the array drifted from HOME_OPTIONS. Not a security/over-grant issue (the honored home path via saved preference still works, and route guards still enforce access), so it is a Medium reachability/state-consistency defect. Fix in prose: derive the fallback ordering from HOME_OPTIONS (or otherwise cover every HOME_PERMS route) rather than a separate hand-maintained list, so the loop can never omit an accessible route.

Remediation (approach)

Replace the hard-coded order array with an iteration over HOME_OPTIONS (or the nav-model group order) in declaration order, returning the first route whose perms canAccessHome() satisfies. That keeps the fallback in lock-step with the picker and guarantees any single-permission user lands on a page they can actually open, only reaching '/welcome' when they truly hold no page-view permission.

Medium Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Six mobile/beta twins reach across feature boundaries into desktop feature folders for shared logic

src/Web/src/app/features/family-finance-mobile/family-finance-mobile.page.ts:33 Core-service coupling / duplication
Evidence
import { FINANCE_DEFAULT_CATEGORIES } from '../family/finance';
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Multiple mobile twins import runtime logic directly out of sibling DESKTOP feature folders: family-finance-mobile ← ../family/finance, family-locations-mobile ← ../family/replay-engine, family-notes-mobile ← ../family/markdown, resume-mobile ← ../tracker/ai-image (also imported by resume desktop), tracker-profile-mobile ← ../tracker/units + ../tracker/onboarding-card, bills-beta ← ../tracker/ai-image. This defeats the platform-split's stated isolation contract ('No live page is imported or modified'): editing or lazy-splitting a desktop feature can now silently change or break a mobile page, the desktop chunk's exports get pulled into the mobile lazy bundle, and there is no shared/core seam that both twins depend on symmetrically. It is copy-paste avoided by illegal cross-imports rather than by real sharing.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/src/app/features/family-finance-mobile/family-finance-mobile.page.ts:33: import { FINANCE_DEFAULT_CATEGORIES } from '../family/finance';
src/Web/src/app/features/family-locations-mobile/family-locations-mobile.page.ts:14: import { ReplayEngine } from '../family/replay-engine';
src/Web/src/app/features/family-notes-mobile/family-notes-mobile.page.ts:13: import { renderMarkdown } from '../family/markdown';
src/Web/src/app/features/resume-mobile/resume-mobile.page.ts:14: import { readFileAsBase64 } from '../tracker/ai-image';
src/Web/src/app/features/tracker-profile-mobile/tracker-profile-mobile.page.ts:25: import { StatsInputs, ageFrom, computeStats } from '../tracker/units';
src/Web/src/app/features/tracker-profile-mobile/tracker-profile-mobile.page.ts:26: import { OnboardingCard, OnboardingResult } from '../tracker/onboarding-card';
src/Web/src/app/features/bills-beta/bills-beta.page.ts:10: import { pickImage, confirmPhotoNotice } from '../tracker/ai-image';

Verified by reading all six cited files. The primary evidence at family-finance-mobile.page.ts:33 matches exactly. All five other claimed cross-imports also exist at the stated locations: family-locations-mobile:14 (ReplayEngine), family-notes-mobile:13 (renderMarkdown), resume-mobile:14 (readFileAsBase64 from ../tracker/ai-image), tracker-profile-mobile:25-26 (../tracker/units + ../tracker/onboarding-card), bills-beta:10 (../tracker/ai-image). Confirmed the imported symbols are RUNTIME logic, not just types: FINANCE_DEFAULT_CATEGORIES is a const, renderMarkdown is a function, ReplayEngine is a class, computeStats/pickImage/readFileAsBase64 are functions. Confirmed the desktop targets are separately lazy-loaded chunks (page-registry.ts:384 lazy-imports family/finance), so a mobile twin's lazy bundle now pulls runtime exports out of a sibling desktop feature. This genuinely defeats the documented platform-split isolation contract ('No live page is imported or modified') with no shared/core seam. Medium severity is correct: this is architectural coupling / maintainability risk (editing or restructuring a desktop feature can silently break a mobile page; bundle bloat), not a runtime security/PII/data-corruption defect. Cited file and line are exact. CONFIRMED.

Remediation (approach)

Promote the genuinely shared, framework-light helpers (finance category defaults, markdown renderer, replay engine, ai-image file utils, unit math, onboarding card) into src/app/shared or src/app/core modules that BOTH the desktop and mobile twins import from, and forbid feature-to-feature imports via an ESLint boundary rule (e.g. no-restricted-imports on '../<other-feature>/'). This removes the hidden coupling and lets either twin's feature folder be refactored independently.

Medium CI/CD & Supply Chain CI/CD pipelines & containers CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Both production container images run as root (no USER directive)

src/Api/Dockerfile:18-29 Docker - running as root
Evidence
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:9.0
WORKDIR /app
...
COPY --from=build /app ./
ENV ASPNETCORE_URLS=http://+:8080
EXPOSE 8080
HEALTHCHECK ...
ENTRYPOINT ["dotnet", "Ccusage.Api.dll"]
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Neither the API image (mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:9.0 runtime stage) nor the web image (nginx:alpine, src/Web/Dockerfile) drops privileges with a USER directive, so the app process runs as UID 0 inside the container. If the API (which processes auth tokens, PII, health and payment data) or nginx is compromised via an app-level RCE or a container-escape-enabling kernel/runtime bug, root-in-container materially widens the blast radius on the shared EC2 host. Defense-in-depth for an internet-facing production service calls for a non-root runtime user.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Dockerfile:18-29 — "FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:9.0 / WORKDIR /app ... COPY --from=build /app ./ / ENV ASPNETCORE_URLS=http://+:8080 / EXPOSE 8080 / HEALTHCHECK ... / ENTRYPOINT [\"dotnet\", \"Ccusage.Api.dll\"]" (no USER directive). src/Web/Dockerfile:10-13 — "FROM nginx:alpine / COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf / COPY --from=build /app/dist/ccusage-web/browser /usr/share/nginx/html / EXPOSE 80" (no USER directive).

Verified both cited Dockerfiles. The API runtime stage (src/Api/Dockerfile lines 18-29) defines no USER instruction, so the dotnet process runs as UID 0. The web serve stage (src/Web/Dockerfile lines 10-13, nginx:alpine) likewise has no USER. I also checked deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml and docker-compose.yml for a compose-level `user:` override — neither service specifies one, so there is no mitigation; containers run as root in production on the shared EC2 host. For an internet-facing service handling auth tokens, PII, health and payment data, dropping to a non-root runtime user is standard defense-in-depth. Finding is accurate as located. Medium severity is appropriate: this is a hardening/blast-radius gap requiring a chained app-RCE or container-escape to be exploited, not a directly exploitable vulnerability, so it is not High. The primary anchor line is 18 (the aspnet runtime FROM where a USER should be added after the COPY).

Remediation (approach)

Run the runtime stage as a dedicated unprivileged user: create/allocate a non-root UID, ensure it owns the app directory and can bind the chosen port (use an unprivileged high port like 8080, which the API already uses, and configure nginx to listen unprivileged), and add a USER directive before ENTRYPOINT in both Dockerfiles. Consider read-only root filesystem plus tmpfs for writable paths.

Medium CI/CD & Supply Chain CI/CD pipelines & containers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

No concurrency control on the Deploy workflow — overlapping runs can push :latest and roll out out-of-order

.github/workflows/deploy.yml:16-18 CI/CD - deploy race / concurrency
Evidence
name: Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:
...
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

There is no concurrency: block. Two commits merged to main in quick succession (or a push plus a manual workflow_dispatch) launch two Deploy runs in parallel. Because both build and push the same mutable :latest tag and then trigger an SSM 'git reset --hard origin/main + compose pull' on the host, the pushes and the host-side pulls can interleave: the host may pull an image from run A while checking out the commit from run B, leaving the running stack in an inconsistent state or effectively deploying the older commit. Combined with the :latest scheme there is no way to detect or recover deterministically.

Independent verifier — confirmation
.github/workflows/deploy.yml:1-18 — "name: Deploy\n\non:\n  push:\n    branches: [main]\n  workflow_dispatch:\n\npermissions:\n  id-token: write\n  contents: read\n\nenv:\n  AWS_REGION: us-west-2\n  ECR_REGISTRY: 398152420190.dkr.ecr.us-west-2.amazonaws.com\n\njobs:\n  deploy:\n    runs-on: ubuntu-latest" — no `concurrency:` key anywhere; lines 33-39 push mutable `:latest`; line 44 does `git reset --hard origin/main` + `remote-deploy.sh` which (deploy/remote-deploy.sh) `docker compose ... pull` of `:latest`.

Verified against the actual file. There is no `concurrency:` block at workflow or job level, and both `push: branches:[main]` and `workflow_dispatch` triggers exist, so two runs can execute in parallel. Both jobs build and push the same mutable `:latest` tags (lines 33-39) and then invoke an SSM command that does `git reset --hard origin/main` plus `remote-deploy.sh`, which runs `docker compose ... pull` on `:latest` (deploy/remote-deploy.sh). Nothing pins a per-commit image digest, so a host pull from run A can be paired with the checkout of run B — the interleave described is real and unmitigated. Standard fix is a `concurrency:` group (e.g. keyed on the workflow/ref) with cancel-in-progress or serialization; ideally combined with immutable per-SHA image tags. Severity Medium is appropriate: single prod host, self-inflicted only on near-simultaneous merges, recoverable by re-running, but can transiently leave an inconsistent/older stack. Line anchor best placed at the `jobs:` boundary (line 16) where the concurrency block would go.

Remediation (approach)

Add a concurrency group for the deploy workflow (keyed to the environment/branch) so only one production rollout runs at a time and superseded runs are cancelled or queued. Pair this with immutable per-commit image tags so the host always pulls the exact image for the commit it checked out.

Medium CI/CD & Supply Chain CI/CD pipelines & containers CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

workflow_dispatch always deploys origin/main HEAD regardless of the invoking ref

.github/workflows/deploy.yml:44 Deploy correctness
Evidence
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:
...
git fetch origin main -q && git reset --hard origin/main -q
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The workflow is manually triggerable (workflow_dispatch) from any ref, and the checkout/build steps use that ref, but the host-side roll-out hard-codes `git reset --hard origin/main`. A maintainer who dispatches the workflow from a release branch or an older tag to build/push those images will nonetheless have the EC2 host reset itself to current main HEAD and run main's compose/scripts. The operator's intent (deploy this ref) is silently overridden, and the artifacts and the on-host code diverge. For an app handling PII/health/payments, a 'deploy the fix branch' action that instead deploys main is a real change-management hazard.

Independent verifier — confirmation
.github/workflows/deploy.yml:6  workflow_dispatch:
.github/workflows/deploy.yml:20        - uses: actions/checkout@v4
.github/workflows/deploy.yml:44          {"commands":["dnf install -y git >/dev/null 2>&1 || true; cd /opt/usage-iq && (if [ -d .git ]; then git fetch origin main -q && git reset --hard origin/main -q; else git clone -q https://github.com/itdept-ops/usage-iq .; fi) && bash deploy/remote-deploy.sh"]}

Confirmed. The workflow is manually dispatchable from any ref (line 6). The checkout step (line 20) has no pinned ref, so on a workflow_dispatch it checks out and builds/pushes images from the invoking ref. But the on-host SSM roll-out (line 44) hardcodes `git reset --hard origin/main`, so the EC2 instance always resets to main HEAD and runs main's deploy/remote-deploy.sh and compose files. Result: when a maintainer dispatches from a release branch/tag, the built images and the on-host code/scripts diverge, and the operator's intended ref is silently ignored. No guard or ref-parameterization mitigates this in the file. Real change-management hazard for an app handling PII/health/payments. Severity Medium is appropriate — the exploit path requires a maintainer to deliberately dispatch from a non-main ref (uncommon), so impact is bounded; note also that images are pushed to a fixed :latest tag regardless of ref, which independently makes even normal deploys non-ref-traceable. Location corrected to line 44 (the hardcoded reset), which is the precise offending line.

Remediation (approach)

Parameterize the deployed revision from the triggering context (github.sha / an input) and reset the host to that revision, so a dispatched deploy ships exactly what was requested rather than unconditionally main.

Medium CI/CD & Supply Chain CI/CD pipelines & containers CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Optional-secret fetch failures are silently swallowed and written as empty values, degrading auth on redeploy

deploy/remote-deploy.sh:13-37 Shell error handling / availability
Evidence
ssm() { aws ssm get-parameter --region "$REGION" --name "$1" "${@:2}" --query Parameter.Value --output text 2>/dev/null || true; }
...
GOOGLE_SECRET=$(ssm /usage-iq/google-client-secret --with-decryption)
...
if [ -z "$JWT" ] || [ -z "$DBPW" ]; then
  echo "FATAL: ..."; exit 1
fi
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

ssm() discards both stderr (2>/dev/null) and the exit code (|| true), so a transient SSM throttle / KMS Decrypt hiccup / IAM propagation delay returns an empty string that is indistinguishable from 'not configured'. Only JWT and DB password are guarded; GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID/SECRET, USDA, FatSecret, WorkoutX, and Gemini are not. A momentary failure reading google-client-secret during a routine redeploy writes an empty GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET into .env and `docker compose up -d` brings the API up with sign-in broken for the whole site, with the script still printing 'Deploy OK.' Because the value was previously present, this is a silent regression, not a first-time-setup gap.

Independent verifier — confirmation
deploy/remote-deploy.sh:13  ssm() { aws ssm get-parameter --region "$REGION" --name "$1" "${@:2}" --query Parameter.Value --output text 2>/dev/null || true; }
deploy/remote-deploy.sh:20  GOOGLE_SECRET=$(ssm /usage-iq/google-client-secret --with-decryption)
deploy/remote-deploy.sh:35-37  if [ -z "$JWT" ] || [ -z "$DBPW" ]; then echo "FATAL: ..."; exit 1; fi
deploy/remote-deploy.sh:48  GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET=$GOOGLE_SECRET
deploy/remote-deploy.sh:67  echo "Deploy OK."

Verified against the actual file. ssm() at line 13 discards stderr (2>/dev/null) and the exit code (|| true), so any transient SSM throttle, KMS Decrypt failure, or IAM propagation delay yields an empty string that is indistinguishable from 'not configured'. Only JWT and DBPW are guarded (lines 35-37); GOOGLE_SECRET (line 20) and the other optional secrets are not, and every value is written into .env unconditionally (line 48). On a routine redeploy a momentary failure reading google-client-secret would write an empty GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET while the script still prints 'Deploy OK.' (line 67), silently regressing Google sign-in. Real defect, correctly located; Medium is appropriate because it requires a transient AWS-side failure to coincide with a deploy and the blast radius is limited to Google sign-in (core app auth via JWT/DB stays guarded). Remediation in prose: capture the ssm() exit status separately from empty output (do not mask it with || true), distinguish 'ParameterNotFound' from other AWS errors, and abort the deploy on any non-not-found failure for previously-configured secrets rather than overwriting .env with blanks.

Remediation (approach)

Distinguish 'parameter missing' from 'call failed': let get-parameter errors other than ParameterNotFound be fatal (do not blanket `|| true`), and after populating .env assert that every value that was non-empty on the previous deploy is still non-empty before restarting the stack, aborting the roll-out otherwise.

Medium CI/CD & Supply Chain CI/CD pipelines & containers CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

docker-compose plugin binary is curl-installed as root with no checksum or signature verification

deploy/cloudformation.yaml:103-105 Supply chain / provisioning
Evidence
curl -fsSL https://github.com/docker/compose/releases/download/v2.29.7/docker-compose-linux-x86_64 \
  -o /usr/local/lib/docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose
chmod +x /usr/local/lib/docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The EC2 UserData runs as root and fetches the docker-compose binary over the network, then marks it executable and later uses it for every production deploy. The version tag is pinned but the artifact is never verified against a published SHA256/signature. A compromised or MITM'd/tampered release asset (or a supply-chain compromise of the release) yields arbitrary root-level code that orchestrates the container that holds PII/health/payment data and can read the SSM secrets. Enterprise provisioning of a root-run binary should be integrity-checked.

Independent verifier — confirmation
deploy/cloudformation.yaml:103-105
          curl -fsSL https://github.com/docker/compose/releases/download/v2.29.7/docker-compose-linux-x86_64 \
            -o /usr/local/lib/docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose
          chmod +x /usr/local/lib/docker/cli-plugins/docker-compose

Confirmed by reading lines 96-106. The EC2 UserData runs as root (#!/bin/bash, set -e) and fetches the docker-compose plugin binary via curl from GitHub with -fsSL (which follows redirects and fails on HTTP error, but performs NO integrity check), writes it into the docker cli-plugins dir, and chmods it +x. The version is pinned (v2.29.7) but there is no SHA256 checksum comparison or GPG/cosign signature verification of the downloaded artifact. This binary orchestrates the production container holding PII/health/payment data and has access to the instance's IAM role (which can read SSM secrets — see DeployPolicy at line 121+). A tampered/compromised release asset or MITM would yield root-level code execution. Note TLS does provide transport integrity (reducing casual MITM risk), but supply-chain compromise of the release asset itself is not mitigated. Medium severity is appropriate for enterprise provisioning standards. Correctly located; the actual curl line is 103.

Remediation (approach)

Download the corresponding checksum file for the pinned release, verify the binary's SHA256 (and ideally the cosign/GPG signature) before chmod +x, and fail provisioning if it does not match; consider installing compose from the distro/Docker package repo with signature verification instead of a raw release URL.

Medium CI/CD & Supply Chain CI/CD pipelines & containers CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Developer's real secrets file (appsettings.Local.json) is not excluded by any .dockerignore, so a local API image build bakes live Google/JWT/Gemini/FatSecret/WorkoutX secrets into an image layer

src/.dockerignore:2-8 Docker: secrets baked into layers
Evidence
Web/
Reporter/
**/bin/
**/obj/
**/*.user
**/Dockerfile
**/.dockerignore
# (no exclusion for appsettings.Local.json)
# src/Api/Dockerfile line 14:  COPY Api/ Api/
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

src/Api/appsettings.Local.json is gitignored (verified: git check-ignore reports IGNORED) and holds real secrets (verified keys: Google, Jwt, Usda, FatSecret, WorkoutX, Gemini, Auth). Neither the context-root src/.dockerignore nor src/Api/.dockerignore lists it, so when a developer runs 'docker compose --profile full up --build' the API Dockerfile's 'COPY Api/ Api/' copies that file into a build layer of usage-iq-api. That layer persistently contains plaintext OAuth client secret, JWT signing key, and paid-API keys. If the developer ever 'docker push'es, exports, or shares that local image (or the layer cache leaks), all of those long-lived credentials — including the JWT signing key that mints auth tokens for a PII/health/payments app — are exfiltrated. Because the secrets never rotate (provision.sh generates the prod JWT key once), a leaked signing key is a permanent account-takeover primitive.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/.dockerignore lines 2-8: "Web/\nReporter/\n**/bin/\n**/obj/\n**/*.user\n**/Dockerfile\n**/.dockerignore" (no appsettings.Local.json entry); src/Api/.dockerignore lines 1-5: "bin/\nobj/\n*.user\nDockerfile\n.dockerignore" (no appsettings.Local.json entry); src/Api/Dockerfile line 14: "COPY Api/ Api/"; docker-compose.yml lines 28-30: "context: ./src / dockerfile: Api/Dockerfile"; src/Api/appsettings.Local.json line 4: "\"ClientSecret\": \"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\"" and line 7: "\"Key\": \"F/8KhNkg82ixBw/R9Ez4EEPr8qxtHhvIsN2ZJVZvVNZOSksKEPuJ3JGFySZaGveN\"". git check-ignore confirms appsettings.Local.json is IGNORED.

All claimed facts verified by reading the files. src/Api/appsettings.Local.json exists, is git-ignored, and contains real long-lived secrets (Google OAuth ClientSecret, JWT signing Key, FatSecret, WorkoutX, Gemini, USDA keys). The compose build context is ./src (lines 28-30) and Api/Dockerfile line 14 does `COPY Api/ Api/`, which copies the entire Api/ tree — including appsettings.Local.json — into a build layer of usage-iq-api. Neither the context-root src/.dockerignore (lines 2-8) nor src/Api/.dockerignore (lines 1-5) excludes appsettings.Local.json; both only exclude bin/obj/user/Dockerfile/.dockerignore. So a developer running `docker compose --profile full up --build` bakes plaintext secrets (notably the JWT signing key that mints auth tokens for this PII/health app) into a persistent image layer. Impact is real but gated on the developer pushing/exporting/sharing the local image or a layer-cache leak; the prod AWS image is built in CI from a clean checkout where the gitignored file is absent, so this is a local-build exposure. Medium severity is appropriate. Anchored the finding at src/.dockerignore line 2 (the exclusion list) where the missing entry belongs.

Remediation (approach)

Add an explicit exclusion for appsettings.Local.json (and any appsettings.*.Local.json / *.secrets.json convention) to src/.dockerignore and src/Api/.dockerignore so it can never enter the build context, mirroring how node_modules/dist are excluded on the web side. Additionally have the API Dockerfile COPY only the specific build inputs it needs rather than the whole Api/ tree, and treat any local-secrets file as build-context-forbidden by convention.

Medium Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

ActivityEmitter.EmitOnceAsync at-most-once is a cross-scope check-then-insert with no unique index — concurrent duplicates slip through

src/Api/Services/ActivityEmitter.cs:116-127 Idempotency / duplicate-fire (TOCTOU, no DB constraint)
Evidence
var already = await db.ActivityEvents.AsNoTracking()
    .AnyAsync(e => e.ActorEmail == actor && e.Kind == trimmedKind && e.IntValue == intValue, ct);
if (already) return;
...
await EmitAsync(actorEmail ?? "", kind, intValue, label, ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

EmitOnceAsync guarantees "at most once per (actor, kind, intValue)" (e.g. a 75-Hard / habit day-complete crossing) purely by an AnyAsync check that runs in one DI scope, then inserts in a SEPARATE scope opened inside EmitAsync. There is no unique index on ActivityEvents(ActorEmail, Kind, IntValue) (confirmed in UsageDbContext line 1432+: only a non-unique {ActorEmail, Id} index exists). Two near-simultaneous requests for the same crossing (double-click 'complete day', a retried request, or the workout/hydration endpoints firing twice) can both observe already=false and both insert. Because EmitAsync's tail also runs the actor's automation rules (RuleEvaluator.EvaluateAsync), a duplicate emit can double-fire rule-driven notifications/webhooks, not just show a duplicate feed card.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Services/ActivityEmitter.cs:116-126 — `var already = await db.ActivityEvents.AsNoTracking().AnyAsync(e => e.ActorEmail == actor && e.Kind == trimmedKind && e.IntValue == intValue, ct); if (already) return; } ... await EmitAsync(actorEmail ?? "", kind, intValue, label, ct);` The check runs in one scope (disposed at the `}` on line 119); EmitAsync opens a SEPARATE scope+DbContext (line 61-62) and inserts. UsageDbContext.cs:1440-1442 defines only `HasIndex(x => new { x.ActorEmail, x.Id })` and `HasIndex(x => x.Id)` — no unique index on (ActorEmail, Kind, IntValue).

Verified against actual code. EmitOnceAsync performs a check-then-insert with the AnyAsync de-dupe check in a scope that is disposed before EmitAsync opens its own separate scope to insert — a classic TOCTOU with no DB-level uniqueness backstop. Confirmed at UsageDbContext.cs:1440-1442 that ActivityEvent has only two non-unique indexes ({ActorEmail,Id}, {Id}); nothing enforces uniqueness on (ActorEmail, Kind, IntValue). Two near-simultaneous calls (double-click complete-day, retried request, workout/hydration endpoints firing twice) can both observe already=false and both insert. Impact is real beyond a duplicate feed card: EmitAsync tails RuleEvaluator.EvaluateAsync (ActivityEmitter.cs:90), which fires in-app SystemAutomation notifications and Discord DM/webhook posts per matching automation rule (RuleEvaluator.cs:58-87), so a duplicate emit double-fires those side effects. Medium is correct: side effects are self-directed/opt-in-gated (no cross-user amplification), the intValue discriminator (day number/streak) narrows the race window, and the whole path is fire-and-forget behind ShareActivity — but double notifications/webhooks on double-click or retry is a genuine idempotency defect for an app touching health/notification integrations. Fix in prose: add a unique index/constraint on (ActorEmail, Kind, IntValue) and let a duplicate-key insert exception be caught and treated as no-op, rather than relying on the pre-check.

Remediation (approach)

Enforce the invariant in the database rather than in application code: add a unique index covering the de-dupe discriminator (actor + kind + the intValue/day used as the 'once' key) for the once-emitted kinds, and have EmitAsync tolerate the resulting unique-violation on insert (catch it, clear the change tracker, and treat it as an already-emitted no-op) so the race converges to exactly one row. Alternatively perform the existence check and the insert in the SAME scope/transaction with an appropriate isolation level, but the unique-index approach is the robust fix.

Medium Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Every scheduler/forwarder BackgroundService runs on each API instance with no leader-election or DB advisory lock

src/Api/Services/AgentScheduler.cs:44-63 Race conditions across processes (no distributed coordination)
Evidence
protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)
{
    using var timer = new PeriodicTimer(Interval);
    do { ... await TickAsync(...); ... }
    while (await timer.WaitForNextTickAsync(stoppingToken));
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

AgentScheduler, NotificationBackgroundService, FamilyReminderService, HealthSyncScheduler, MachineGeoBackfillService and AutoSyncBackgroundService all tick on a plain in-process PeriodicTimer with no cross-instance guard. The stamp-first idempotency guards (LastFiredLocalDate / LastBriefingLocalDate / LastSyncCursorDate / LastDailySent) are read-then-write races that are only safe because exactly one process runs (confirmed single-instance today via deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml: one 'api' service on one EC2 host). The moment the deployment is scaled horizontally (a second replica / blue-green overlap / an accidental second container), two instances read the same 'not fired yet' state within the same minute and BOTH deliver the agent nudge, the daily/weekly Discord digest, the family briefing, and the wearable sync — duplicate PII/health notifications and duplicate token spend, with no code-level protection.

Independent verifier — confirmation
AgentScheduler.cs:44-46,62 `protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(...) { using var timer = new PeriodicTimer(Interval); ... } while (await timer.WaitForNextTickAsync(stoppingToken));` — plus AgentScheduler.cs:115 `if (agent.LastFiredLocalDate == localDate) continue;` then AgentScheduler.cs:167-170 `db.ScheduledAgents.Where(a => a.Id == agent.Id).ExecuteUpdateAsync(...)` (unconditional by-Id write = read-then-write, no cross-instance guard). Siblings confirmed via grep: HealthSyncScheduler.cs:49, FamilyReminderService.cs:32, NotificationBackgroundService.cs:16, MachineGeoBackfillService.cs:32 all use PeriodicTimer; no pg_advisory/IDistributedLock/leader-election exists anywhere.

Verified against actual code. ExecuteAsync (lines 44-63) is a plain in-process PeriodicTimer BackgroundService. The idempotency is stamp-first but is a non-atomic read-then-write: line 115 reads LastFiredLocalDate in memory, line 135/167 writes it via ExecuteUpdate filtered only on a.Id (no conditional WHERE LastFiredLocalDate <> today), so two instances that both read 'not fired' in the same minute would both compose+notify before either stamps. Grep confirms all five named siblings use the same PeriodicTimer pattern and the repo contains zero distributed-lock / advisory-lock / leader-election primitives. deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml confirms the mitigation is purely topological: a single 'api' service, restart: unless-stopped, no replicas/scaling — so no duplication occurs today. The defect is therefore a real, correctly-located LATENT race that produces duplicate PII/health/Discord notifications and duplicate AI token spend on any horizontal scale-out or blue-green overlap. Medium is appropriate: correct technical claim, but currently masked by the provably single-instance deployment (impact is conditional on a future topology change rather than active).

Remediation (approach)

Add a cross-instance coordination primitive before these can be scaled: a Postgres advisory lock (pg_try_advisory_lock) acquired per tick, or an atomic conditional UPDATE ... WHERE guard-not-yet-advanced RETURNING to claim each due row, or a single 'scheduler leader' election. At minimum, document and gate the hosted services so they only run on one designated instance. Treat the current single-instance assumption as a hard operational invariant until this is in place.

Medium Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

A scheduled agent whose DeliverHourLocal falls inside its own quiet window is stamped-and-dropped every day and never fires

src/Api/Services/AgentScheduler.cs:123-129 Services & background workers correctness
Evidence
if (InQuietHours(localNow.Hour, agent.QuietStartLocalHour, agent.QuietEndLocalHour))
{
    if (agent.Kind is ScheduledAgentKind.StreakRescue) continue; // defer; try again next tick
    // else fall through to STAMP-and-skip below (drop-for-the-day) so we don't reconsider it.
    await StampFiredAsync(db, agent, localDate, FireKey(agent.Kind, localDate), ct);
    continue;
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

For non-StreakRescue kinds (MorningBriefing, BudgetAlert, LowStaples, MedicationDue) the tick only reaches the quiet-hours check once localNow.Hour >= DeliverHourLocal. If the user configured DeliverHourLocal to a value that lies INSIDE their quiet window (e.g. deliver at 07:00 with quiet hours 22:00→08:00), then on the very first tick at/after 07:00 the hour is still quiet, so the agent is stamped for today and skipped — every single day, forever. The nudge NEVER fires. AgentsEndpoints.Validate (lines 220-229) checks each hour is 0-23 and that both quiet bounds are set together, but performs NO cross-check that DeliverHourLocal falls OUTSIDE [QuietStart, QuietEnd). The user gets no error and no notification, with no indication why.

Independent verifier — confirmation
AgentScheduler.cs:118,123-128:
if (localNow.Hour < agent.DeliverHourLocal) continue;
...
if (InQuietHours(localNow.Hour, agent.QuietStartLocalHour, agent.QuietEndLocalHour))
{
    if (agent.Kind is ScheduledAgentKind.StreakRescue) continue;
    await StampFiredAsync(db, agent, localDate, FireKey(agent.Kind, localDate), ct);
    continue;
}
AgentsEndpoints.cs:221-229 Validate has hour-range + paired-bounds checks only, no DeliverHour∉quiet cross-check.

Verified against the actual code. Flow: line 115 skips if already stamped today; line 118 gates on hour >= DeliverHourLocal; line 123 checks quiet hours. If DeliverHourLocal lies inside the quiet window (e.g. deliver 7, quiet 22->8: InQuietHours(7,22,8) -> 7<8 -> true), then the FIRST eligible tick (hour == DeliverHour) is by definition quiet, so a non-StreakRescue agent (MorningBriefing/BudgetAlert/LowStaples/MedicationDue) is stamped-for-today at line 127 before ever reaching a non-quiet hour. The stamp makes line 115 short-circuit all remaining ticks that day. This repeats every day, so the nudge NEVER fires. InQuietHours math (lines 178-187) is correct and confirms 7 is quiet. AgentsEndpoints.Validate (lines 221-229) only range-checks each hour (0-23) and requires both quiet bounds together; it performs NO cross-check that DeliverHourLocal falls outside [QuietStart,QuietEnd), so the misconfiguration is accepted silently with no error and no notification. Defect is real, correctly located, and not mitigated elsewhere. Severity Medium is fair: it is a silent-drop misconfiguration a user can create via the UI, not a data-integrity/security issue, but it defeats the feature permanently with zero feedback.

Remediation (approach)

Add a cross-field validation in AgentsEndpoints.Validate rejecting (or auto-adjusting) a DeliverHourLocal that falls within the configured quiet window, reusing AgentScheduler.InQuietHours. Alternatively, in the scheduler, treat 'deliver hour is inside quiet hours' as a misconfiguration that delivers at the quiet-window end rather than dropping-for-the-day silently.

Medium Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Security/signup Discord forwarding stalls permanently on a dead webhook or one poison audit entry (high-water mark never advances)

src/Api/Services/NotificationBackgroundService.cs:165-173 Services & background workers correctness
Evidence
var advanced = false;
foreach (var e in newEntries)
{
    ...
    if (securityOn || (signupOn && isSignup))
    {
        ...
        if (!await notifier.SendSecurityAsync(url, e.Action, e.ActorEmail, e.TargetEmail, e.Detail, mention, ct))
            break; // stop on failure; retry from here next tick
    }
    s.LastAuditAlertId = e.Id;
    advanced = true;
}
if (advanced) await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

SendSecurityAsync -> PostAsync returns Ok=false for ANY non-2xx, including PERMANENT failures: a deleted/rotated admin webhook returns HTTP 404, and a payload Discord rejects returns HTTP 400 (see DiscordNotifier.PostWithResultAsync lines 225-227 which return IsSuccessStatusCode=false for these). When the FIRST entry in the batch hits such a permanent error, the loop breaks before advancing s.LastAuditAlertId, so advanced stays false and the high-water mark is never persisted. Every subsequent minute-tick re-reads the same Take(20) batch (line 150-151) and re-fails on the same entry forever. Unlike the daily/weekly/threshold digests (each guarded by its own Last*Sent date so a failure only blocks that one item for a day), the security stream has no per-entry skip: a single unforwardable entry or a dead webhook wedges ALL future security-alert and new-user-signup forwarding indefinitely — a silent loss of the security-monitoring channel for a PII/health/payments app.

Independent verifier — confirmation
NotificationBackgroundService.cs:165-166  `if (!await notifier.SendSecurityAsync(url, e.Action, e.ActorEmail, e.TargetEmail, e.Detail, mention, ct)) break; // stop on failure; retry from here next tick` — advance (s.LastAuditAlertId = e.Id; advanced = true; lines 170-171) is skipped, and DiscordNotifier.cs:225-227 `if (!res.IsSuccessStatusCode) ... return new DiscordPostResult(res.IsSuccessStatusCode, (int)res.StatusCode, retryAfter);` returns Ok=false for any non-2xx (404/400), which PostAsync (line 169 `.Ok`) and SendSecurityAsync (line 100-101) propagate as false.

Verified the full chain. SendSecurityAsync -> PostAsync -> PostWithResultAsync returns Ok = res.IsSuccessStatusCode, which is false for permanent errors like HTTP 404 (deleted/rotated webhook) and HTTP 400 (payload Discord rejects). In ForwardSecurityAsync, when the FIRST forwardable entry in the Take(20) batch fails, the loop breaks before executing s.LastAuditAlertId = e.Id / advanced = true, so advanced stays false and no SaveChanges occurs. The PeriodicTimer (FromMinutes(1)) re-runs each minute, re-reads the identical Where(a => a.Id > s.LastAuditAlertId).Take(20) batch, and re-fails on the same poison entry indefinitely. There is no per-entry skip and no per-item date guard (unlike the daily/weekly/threshold digests which each have their own Last*Sent marker), so one unforwardable entry or a dead webhook permanently wedges ALL future security-alert AND new-user-signup forwarding — a silent loss of the security-monitoring channel for a PII/health/payments app. Retry-on-transient-failure is intended, but there is no distinction between transient and permanent failures, making the stall permanent for permanent errors. Location and Medium severity are correct.

Remediation (approach)

Differentiate transient failures (429, 5xx, network) from permanent ones (4xx other than 429). On a permanent per-entry rejection, log and advance past that entry rather than breaking, so one poison entry cannot wedge the stream. On a dead-webhook (repeated 404), stop the whole route and surface an admin warning instead of silently retrying the same batch forever. Consider a bounded consecutive-failure counter that disables the route and alerts.

Medium Correctness Endpoints correctness & validation PLAUSIBLE conf: high 2 verifiers

MedicationLog dose 'upsert' is not backed by a unique index — concurrent taps create duplicate rows that double-count adherence

src/Api/Endpoints/MedsEndpoints.cs:231-250 Missing uniqueness guard / data integrity
Evidence
// Upsert the slot's log for the day so re-tapping a dose toggles its status rather than piling up rows.
var row = await db.MedicationLogs.FirstOrDefaultAsync(
    l => l.UserEmail == caller.Email && l.MedicationId == id
        && l.LocalDate == req.Date && l.ScheduledSlot == slot, ct);
if (row is null) { ... db.MedicationLogs.Add(row); }
row.Status = req.Status; ...
await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The MedicationLog entity (UsageDbContext.cs:1257-1270) has only NON-unique indexes on (UserEmail, LocalDate) and (MedicationId, LocalDate) — there is NO unique constraint on (UserEmail, MedicationId, LocalDate, ScheduledSlot). The comment claims this is an upsert 'rather than piling up rows', but nothing enforces one-row-per-slot. Two concurrent taps on the same dose slot both read null and both INSERT, producing two rows for the same (med, slot, day). AggregateAdherence then counts BOTH as taken, inflating the adherence percentage, and BuildTodaySlots silently masks the duplication by selecting only the max-Id row. The RepeatDoses idempotency (lines 304-315) likewise relies purely on an in-memory 'present' set, so it too can race into duplicates. This corrupts the health-adherence metric with no error surfaced.

Independent verifier — confirmation
MedsEndpoints.cs:231-234 — "// Upsert the slot's log for the day..." var row = await db.MedicationLogs.FirstOrDefaultAsync(l => l.UserEmail == caller.Email && l.MedicationId == id && l.LocalDate == req.Date && l.ScheduledSlot == slot, ct); if (row is null) { ... db.MedicationLogs.Add(row); }  ||  UsageDbContext.cs:1267-1269 — e.HasIndex(x => new { x.UserEmail, x.LocalDate }); e.HasIndex(x => new { x.MedicationId, x.LocalDate }); (both NON-unique; migration 20260628104007 confirms no unique constraint)  ||  MedsVitalsMath.cs:61-62 — foreach (var g in byDay) taken += Math.Min(g.Count(), DosesDueOn(med, g.Key));

Core claim verified: the log endpoint does a read-then-insert "upsert" (MedsEndpoints.cs:231-250) with NO unique index backing the (UserEmail, MedicationId, LocalDate, ScheduledSlot) tuple — the model (UsageDbContext.cs:1267-1269) and migration 20260628104007 define only two NON-unique indexes. Two concurrent taps on the same slot both read null and both INSERT, producing duplicate rows; BuildTodaySlots (line 513) masks this by taking OrderByDescending(Id).First() per slot; RepeatDoses idempotency (lines 304-315) relies purely on an in-memory HashSet and races the same way. All factual claims hold. However the impact is PARTIALLY over-stated: AggregateAdherence→MedsVitalsMath.Adherence does NOT blindly count both duplicates. It groups by LocalDate and caps taken per day at Math.Min(count, DosesDueOn) (MedsVitalsMath.cs:62), so duplicates can never push adherence past 100%. The corruption is nonetheless real for multi-dose meds: e.g. TimesPerDay=2, user duplicates slot 0 twice while slot 1 is genuinely missed → 2 Taken rows, Min(2,2)=2 taken / 2 scheduled = 100% reported vs true 50%. So the metric IS inflated, just bounded at 100%. Given the per-day cap limits blast radius and the race requires a narrow concurrent-tap window, severity is corrected High→Medium. The data-integrity defect (missing unique guard, unguarded upsert) itself is genuine and correctly located.

Remediation (approach)

Add a unique index on MedicationLog (UserEmail, MedicationId, LocalDate, ScheduledSlot) (treating the null slot as a distinct key, e.g. a filtered/normalized-slot index) and handle the resulting unique violation in the log and repeat handlers by reloading + updating the winner. This makes the documented upsert actually atomic and stops adherence double-counting.

Medium Correctness Endpoints correctness & validation CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Client-supplied TakenAt/MeasuredAt are written to timestamptz columns without UTC-kind normalization (Npgsql throws on Unspecified/Local kind)

src/Api/Endpoints/MedsEndpoints.cs:248 DateTime UTC/local confusion
Evidence
row.TakenAtUtc = req.Status == MedicationLogStatus.Taken ? (req.TakenAt ?? now) : null;
... (VitalReading) row.MeasuredAtUtc = req.MeasuredAt;   // lines 390 & 415
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

TakenAtUtc and MeasuredAtUtc map to 'timestamp with time zone' (UsageDbContext.cs:1262 and 1280). There is no custom JSON DateTime converter registered (Program.cs has no ConfigureHttpJsonOptions), so System.Text.Json deserializes an offset-less ISO string like "2026-07-01T08:00:00" as DateTimeKind.Unspecified. Npgsql requires DateTimeKind.Utc for timestamptz and throws (ArgumentException/InvalidCastException) at SaveChangesAsync, yielding an unhandled 500. The req.TakenAt / req.MeasuredAt values are passed through verbatim with no SpecifyKind/ToUniversalTime coercion. Contrast FamilyCalendarEndpoints.cs:363-364, which defensively normalizes inbound client DateTimes via DateTime.SpecifyKind(r, DateTimeKind.Utc) precisely to avoid this. A client that omits the trailing 'Z' when logging a dose time or a vital measurement time triggers the 500.

Independent verifier — confirmation
MedsEndpoints.cs:248  row.TakenAtUtc = req.Status == MedicationLogStatus.Taken ? (req.TakenAt ?? now) : null;  | MedsEndpoints.cs:390  MeasuredAtUtc = req.MeasuredAt,  | MedsEndpoints.cs:415  row.MeasuredAtUtc = req.MeasuredAt;  | DTOs: MedsEndpoints.cs:50 record LogDoseInput(... DateTime? TakenAt ...) / :59 DateTime? MeasuredAt  | UsageDbContext.cs:1262 e.Property(x => x.TakenAtUtc).HasColumnType("timestamp with time zone"); / :1280 MeasuredAtUtc same. No ConfigureHttpJsonOptions/AddJsonOptions and no EnableLegacyTimestampBehavior found in src. Contrast FamilyCalendarEndpoints.cs:363-364 DateTime.SpecifyKind(r, DateTimeKind.Utc).

Verified all claim elements. TakenAt (line 248) and MeasuredAt (lines 390, 415) are client-supplied DateTime? values written verbatim to timestamptz columns (UsageDbContext.cs:1262, 1280). No custom JSON DateTime converter is registered (no ConfigureHttpJsonOptions/AddJsonOptions anywhere) and Npgsql's legacy timestamp behavior is NOT enabled, so an offset-less ISO string ("2026-07-01T08:00:00") deserializes as DateTimeKind.Unspecified and Npgsql 6+ throws at SaveChangesAsync -> unhandled 500. No SpecifyKind/ToUniversalTime coercion, unlike the codebase's own defensive FamilyCalendarEndpoints.cs:363-364. Real correctness/robustness defect. Impact depends on the client omitting the trailing Z; first-party JS clients using Date.toISOString() send Z and would avoid it, but the API accepts offset-less input with no guard, so any such client causes a 500. Medium severity is appropriate; anchor line 248 is correct.

Remediation (approach)

Before persisting, coerce any client-supplied DateTime to UTC: if Kind is Unspecified assume UTC (SpecifyKind Utc) and if Local convert via ToUniversalTime, mirroring the FamilyCalendar handling. Ideally register a global System.Text.Json DateTime converter that normalizes all inbound timestamps so every timestamptz-bound endpoint is protected uniformly.

Medium Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Health sleep/workout sync inserts the tracker row BEFORE its de-dup log, so a concurrent sync on a second API instance persists a duplicate SleepEntry/ExerciseEntry

src/Api/Services/Health/HealthSyncMapper.cs:181-225 Idempotency / duplicate-fire (concurrent workers, no leader election)
Evidence
db.SleepEntries.Add(entry);
await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);   // (line 181-182) entry COMMITTED here

return await RecordNewImportAsync(email, provider, date, HealthSignalKind.Sleep, s.LogId, entry.Id, ct);
...
db.HealthImportLogs.Add(new HealthImportLog { ... SourceRef = sourceRef ... });
try { await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct); return new SignalResult(1, 0, 0); }
catch (DbUpdateException ex) when (IsUniqueViolation(ex)) { db.ChangeTracker.Clear(); return new SignalResult(0, 1, 0); }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The unique de-dup constraint exists ONLY on HealthImportLog (UsageDbContext.cs:1160 unique on UserEmail,Provider,SignalKind,SourceRef); SleepEntry (line 780) and ExerciseEntry (line 718) have only NON-unique (UserEmail,LocalDate) indexes. HealthSyncScheduler's BackgroundService runs on every API instance with no leader election / advisory lock (prior-round finding). Two instances can both select the same due HealthConnection, both pass the 'log is null' probe, both Add+commit a Watch SleepEntry/ExerciseEntry (which has no unique key to reject it), and only THEN race on the HealthImportLog insert.
