The losing instance's ChangeTracker.Clear() discards nothing useful — its duplicate entry is already committed and is silently reclassified as an 'update'. Result: duplicated watch sleep/workout rows that double-count hours, workout minutes, and calories-out in the tracker roll-ups and the weekly recap. The manual sync-now endpoint hitting the same connection concurrently with the scheduler produces the same duplicate.

Independent verifier — confirmation
HealthSyncMapper.cs:181-184  db.SleepEntries.Add(entry); await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct); [commit] return await RecordNewImportAsync(...);  || :219-225 db.HealthImportLogs.Add(...); try { await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct); return new SignalResult(1,0,0);} catch (DbUpdateException ex) when (IsUniqueViolation(ex)) { db.ChangeTracker.Clear(); return new SignalResult(0,1,0);}  || UsageDbContext.cs:780 SleepEntry HasIndex(UserEmail,LocalDate) [non-unique]; :768 ExerciseEntry [non-unique]; :1160-1161 HealthImportLog HasIndex(UserEmail,Provider,SignalKind,SourceRef).IsUnique() [only unique de-dup]  || HealthSyncScheduler.cs:32-33 BackgroundService; :82-86 candidate read is bare filtered Take with no lock/lease/SKIP LOCKED

Confirmed on read. MapSleepAsync/MapWorkoutAsync commit the Watch SleepEntry/ExerciseEntry (lines 182, 208) BEFORE inserting the de-dup HealthImportLog (RecordNewImportAsync, 219-225). The tracker tables carry only non-unique (UserEmail,LocalDate) indexes (UsageDbContext.cs:780,768); the sole unique de-dup key is on HealthImportLog (1160-1161). HealthSyncScheduler is a BackgroundService running on every instance, and its due-connection scan (82-86) has no leader election, advisory lock, lease flag, or SELECT...FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED. Two instances (or the manual sync-now endpoint overlapping the scheduler) can both pass the 'log is null' probe (157-160 / 190-193), both commit a duplicate Watch entry, then race on the unique HealthImportLog insert; the loser's ChangeTracker.Clear() discards nothing because its duplicate entry was already committed in the earlier SaveChangesAsync — it is silently reclassified as an 'update' (SignalResult(0,1,0)). Net effect: duplicated watch sleep/workout rows that double-count hours, workout minutes, and calories-out in tracker roll-ups and the weekly recap. Notably the XML doc at line 43 claims 'Saves within its own transaction' but MapDayAsync opens NO transaction (no BeginTransaction/execution-strategy wrapping the multiple SaveChangesAsync calls), so even that stated mitigation is absent. Correct fix in prose: insert the HealthImportLog and the tracker entry inside a single transaction (or insert the log FIRST and let its unique violation short-circuit before adding the entry), or add a partial-unique index on the Watch tracker rows keyed to the vendor SourceRef, and/or serialize the scheduler via an advisory lock / SKIP LOCKED lease. Severity Medium is accurate: accuracy defect in health roll-ups, not corruption/security, and it requires concurrent sync.

Remediation (approach)

Make the de-dup structural rather than ordering-dependent: add a unique index on the tracker entity's natural sync key (e.g. a Watch-scoped unique on UserEmail+Provider+vendor LogId, or reuse the import-log key) so a duplicate Watch row is rejected at the DB. Alternatively insert the HealthImportLog row FIRST (letting its unique index arbitrate the race) and only add the SleepEntry/ExerciseEntry after that insert succeeds, so a losing concurrent writer never commits a second entry. Either way the entry insert must be gated by the same unique constraint that guards the log.

Medium Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

CompleteFinishedTimersAsync has no per-item try/catch — one NotifyFamily failure aborts the rest of the finished-timer batch after the timer was already stamped Done

src/Api/Services/FamilyReminderService.cs:412-431 Unobserved exception aborts remaining work in the tick
Evidence
foreach (var t in finished)
{
    // Mark done FIRST so a retry never re-notifies, then ping the whole household.
    t.Done = true;
    await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);

    var memberIds = await db.HouseholdMembers.AsNoTracking()...ToListAsync(ct);

    await notifier.NotifyFamily(memberIds, NotificationType.FamilyTimer, $"⏰ {t.Label} is up!", "/family/timers", ct);
    completed++;
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Prior rounds flagged the reminder loop's missing per-item guard; the sibling timer loop has the identical defect and is a distinct method. Because each timer is stamped Done via SaveChanges BEFORE NotifyFamily, and there is no try/catch inside the loop, a single NotifyFamily throw (SignalR/DB hiccup, a member's notification write failing) bubbles to ExecuteAsync, which logs and moves on. The just-stamped timer's 'is up!' notification is permanently lost (it will never be reconsidered, since Done is already true), AND every not-yet-processed finished timer in the same tick is skipped for this cycle. A family member's cooking/laundry timer silently never announces.

Independent verifier — confirmation
FamilyReminderService.cs:412-430 — foreach (var t in finished) { t.Done = true; await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct); var memberIds = await db.HouseholdMembers... .ToListAsync(ct); await notifier.NotifyFamily(memberIds, NotificationType.FamilyTimer, $"⏰ {t.Label} is up!", "/family/timers", ct); completed++; }  (no try/catch inside the loop). Contrast: DeliverDueBriefingsAsync:94-102 and AnnounceUpcomingEventsAsync:134 wrap each item in try/catch "so one bad household can't block the others." ExecuteAsync:45 only logs the tick and moves on.

Verified against the actual code. Timer is stamped Done and persisted (lines 415-416) BEFORE NotifyFamily (lines 423-428), and the loop (412-430) has no per-item try/catch — unlike its sibling loops in the same file (DeliverDueBriefingsAsync 94-102, AnnounceUpcomingEventsAsync 134) which are explicitly guarded per-item. A NotifyFamily throw bubbles to ExecuteAsync (line 45), which only logs; the tick is not retried. Because the query at line 406 filters !t.Done, the just-completed timer's 'is up!' ping is permanently lost and every unprocessed finished timer in the batch is skipped this cycle. Correctly located, real impact (background notification reliability), Medium severity is appropriate — no data/security harm.

Remediation (approach)

Wrap the body of the finished-timer foreach in a per-item try/catch that logs and continues to the next timer, mirroring the per-agent/per-household swallow already used in AgentScheduler and DeliverDueBriefingsAsync. Consider stamping Done only AFTER a successful notify (or recording a 'notified' flag separately) so a transient notify failure can be retried rather than lost.

Medium Correctness Endpoints correctness & validation CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Identity auto/apply idempotency key ignores window START, so a second apply over a different window ending the same day is silently dropped and undercounts

src/Api/Endpoints/IdentityEndpoints.cs:198-214 Off-by-one / idempotency key collision
Evidence
var sourceId = $"auto:{key}:{toDate:yyyy-MM-dd}";
if (seen.Contains(sourceId) || !batchSeen.Add(sourceId)) { skipped++; continue; }
...
Minutes = Math.Clamp(minutes, 1, 1440),
Source = IdentityEntrySource.Auto,
SourceEventId = sourceId,
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The re-derived server-authoritative minutes depend on BOTH from and to (Window(req.FromUtc, req.ToUtc)), but the synthetic idempotency/uniqueness key encodes only the window END date (toDate). A user who applies the 'workouts' signal over a 7-day window (e.g. 90 min), then applies the SAME signal over a 30-day window ending on the same day (e.g. 500 min), produces the identical sourceId 'auto:workouts:2026-07-01'. The second, larger/correct total is treated as an already-imported duplicate and skipped, so the identity map permanently under-credits time. This is distinct from the previously-reported missing absolute-date guard: here the DATE is in range but the KEY is under-specified, causing lost writes.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Endpoints/IdentityEndpoints.cs:197-199: "// One synthetic id per (signal, window-end) → idempotent re-apply via the unique index.\n                var sourceId = $\"auto:{key}:{toDate:yyyy-MM-dd}\";\n                if (seen.Contains(sourceId) || !batchSeen.Add(sourceId)) { skipped++; continue; }"  — and line 207: "Minutes = Math.Clamp(minutes, 1, 1440)," with server-authoritative minutes re-derived over Window(req.FromUtc, req.ToUtc) at line 180 (depends on BOTH from and to).

Confirmed by reading lines 161-224. The synthetic idempotency key sourceId = "auto:{key}:{toDate:yyyy-MM-dd}" encodes ONLY the window END date, while the authoritative Minutes (line 207) are re-derived from DeriveSignalsAsync over Window(req.FromUtc, req.ToUtc) at line 180, which depends on BOTH from and to. The seen/batchSeen dedup at line 199 skips any sourceId already present, and the write path only INSERTS (line 201) — the one-by-one fallback (ApplyAutoOneByOneAsync) also does not UPDATE an existing row's minutes. Therefore two applies over different-length windows that end on the same calendar day (e.g. a 7-day window = 90 min, then a 30-day window = 500 min, both ending 2026-07-01) collide on the identical sourceId; the second, larger/corrected total is silently dropped (skipped++), and the first written value persists — a real lost-write undercount in the identity time map. This is genuinely distinct from a missing absolute-date-range guard: the date is in range, but the key is under-specified. Severity Medium is appropriate — the feature is user-facing self-scoped time tracking (not auth/PII/payments/health), the collision requires overlapping windows ending the same day, and it degrades accuracy rather than causing data corruption or security exposure. Corrected line set to 198 (the sourceId construction), which is the root of the defect.

Remediation (approach)

Include the window START (fromUtc/fromDate) — or the actual measured minutes/a content hash of (key, from, to, minutes) — in the synthetic SourceEventId so distinct windows that end on the same day produce distinct keys, or change the apply semantics to overwrite (upsert) the existing auto row for that (key, toDate) with the newly re-derived minutes rather than skipping it as a duplicate.

Medium Correctness Endpoints correctness & validation CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Poll re-vote does ExecuteDeleteAsync (auto-committed) then a separate SaveChanges Add, with no wrapping transaction — a failure between them permanently loses the caller's prior votes with nothing re-added

src/Api/Endpoints/FamilyPollsEndpoints.cs:166-176 Non-atomic transaction vs EF retry execution strategy
Evidence
await db.FamilyPlanPollVotes
    .Where(v => optionIds.Contains(v.OptionId) && v.UserId == caller.Id)
    .ExecuteDeleteAsync(ct);

var now = DateTime.UtcNow;
foreach (var optionId in chosen)
    db.FamilyPlanPollVotes.Add(new FamilyPlanPollVote { ... });
await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

ExecuteDeleteAsync issues an immediate, self-committing DELETE (it is not buffered into the later SaveChanges unit of work), and the handler wraps neither call in db.Database.CreateExecutionStrategy()/BeginTransaction. If the subsequent SaveChangesAsync throws (a transient Npgsql error the retry strategy would otherwise absorb, a cancellation, or a constraint issue), the caller's previous votes are already gone and the new votes are never persisted — the vote silently vanishes, corrupting the poll tally. Elsewhere in this codebase (TrackerEndpoints, FamilyMealsChoresEndpoints, UsersEndpoints) analogous delete+write pairs are correctly wrapped in the execution strategy's transaction; this replace path is not.

Independent verifier — confirmation
FamilyPollsEndpoints.cs:166-176:
            await db.FamilyPlanPollVotes
                .Where(v => optionIds.Contains(v.OptionId) && v.UserId == caller.Id)
                .ExecuteDeleteAsync(ct);
            var now = DateTime.UtcNow;
            foreach (var optionId in chosen)
                db.FamilyPlanPollVotes.Add(new FamilyPlanPollVote { OptionId = optionId, UserId = caller.Id, CreatedUtc = now });
            await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
-- vs Program.cs:37: npgsql.EnableRetryOnFailure(maxRetryCount: 5, ...)
-- vs TrackerEndpoints.cs:1436-1450 / UsersEndpoints.cs:162-166 / FamilyMealsChoresEndpoints.cs:1085-1089 which wrap delete+write in CreateExecutionStrategy()+BeginTransactionAsync

Verified against actual code. ExecuteDeleteAsync (line 166-168) issues an immediate self-committing DELETE that is NOT part of the later SaveChangesAsync unit of work (line 176). The handler wraps neither call in a transaction/execution strategy. EnableRetryOnFailure is active (Program.cs:37), and the same repo documents (TrackerEndpoints.cs:1436-1437) that these replace paths must be wrapped in CreateExecutionStrategy()+BeginTransactionAsync — done correctly in TrackerEndpoints, UsersEndpoints, and FamilyMealsChoresEndpoints, but omitted here. A failure between the delete and the save (transient Npgsql error, cancellation, or constraint) permanently loses the caller's prior votes with nothing re-added, corrupting the poll tally. File, lines, mechanism, and mitigation-comparison in the claim are all accurate. Medium is appropriate: real data-loss/atomicity defect but scoped to family poll votes (no PII/health/payment/auth data), low frequency, requiring a failure in a narrow window.

Remediation (approach)

Wrap the delete-then-insert as one atomic unit inside db.Database.CreateExecutionStrategy().ExecuteAsync with an explicit BeginTransactionAsync/CommitAsync (mirroring the weight/activity re-date paths in TrackerEndpoints), so the old votes are only removed if the new set commits; the retry strategy then re-runs the whole replacement on a transient failure.

Medium Correctness Endpoints correctness & validation CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Location admin map fetches only the 2000 globally-most-recent fixes then groups by user, so users are silently omitted from oversight when a few users dominate recent activity

src/Api/Endpoints/LocationEndpoints.cs:170-197 Unbounded/incomplete result set (pagination bounds)
Evidence
var recentRows = await db.UserLocations.AsNoTracking()
    .OrderByDescending(x => x.CapturedUtc)
    .Take(HistoryCap * 4)
    .ToListAsync(ct);
...
var result = recentRows
    .GroupBy(r => r.UserEmail, StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase)
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The admin 'where is everyone' map takes the 2000 (HistoryCap*4) newest rows across ALL users and then groups in memory. If a handful of users log fixes frequently (periodic capture), those 2000 rows can be entirely consumed by a few emails, so other users who have recorded locations but whose most recent fix is older than the 2000th global row NEVER appear on the admin map at all. For a safety/oversight feature this is a correctness gap: an admin can be shown an incomplete roster and wrongly conclude a user has no location. The comment claims it is 'bounded on large datasets' but the boundedness sacrifices per-user completeness.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Endpoints/LocationEndpoints.cs:170-183
var recentRows = await db.UserLocations.AsNoTracking()
    .OrderByDescending(x => x.CapturedUtc)
    .Take(HistoryCap * 4)
    .ToListAsync(ct);
...
var result = recentRows
    .GroupBy(r => r.UserEmail, StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase)
[HistoryCap = 500 at line 30, so Take(HistoryCap*4) = 2000 global rows]

Confirmed. The admin map endpoint (RequirePermission LocationViewAll) takes the 2000 globally-most-recent UserLocations rows (HistoryCap=500 * 4) ordered by CapturedUtc descending across ALL users, THEN groups by email in memory. Grouping after a global take means users whose most recent fix is older than the 2000th global row are entirely absent from the roster — no per-user query or fallback exists downstream. Periodic/high-frequency capture by a few users can consume the whole window, silently omitting others from admin oversight. The line-169 comment itself concedes it trades completeness for boundedness. Real correctness gap for a safety/oversight feature; Medium is fair (admin-gated, data-volume-dependent, not a security/PII breach). Correct fix in prose: fetch the latest fix per distinct user (e.g., a grouped/windowed query keyed by user with a per-user cap) rather than a single global top-N take, so every user with a recorded fix appears.

Remediation (approach)

Resolve the latest fix per user with a per-user query (e.g. a grouped max(CapturedUtc) join, or a window-function/lateral 'top-N-per-user' query) rather than a single global TOP-N, so every user with any recorded fix appears with their most-recent point (and a bounded recent tail), independent of how active other users are.

Medium Correctness Frontend correctness CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

ChatRealtime.loadHistory documents "replaces the channel's message list" on initial open but actually merges, so tombstoned/removed messages and stale bodies survive a channel re-open

src/Web/src/app/core/chat-realtime.ts:491-502 race conditions on rapid navigation / signal state
Evidence
async loadHistory(channelId: number, before?: number, limit = 50): Promise<number> {
    const page = await firstValueFrom(this.api.chatMessages(channelId, { before, limit }));
    const ascending = [...page].reverse(); // newest-first -> oldest-first
    this._messages.update(map => {
      const existing = map[channelId] ?? [];
      const merged = before == null
        ? mergeById(ascending, existing)          // initial load (still dedupe against any live msgs)
        : mergeById(ascending, existing);         // prepend older; mergeById keeps order + dedupes
      return { ...map, [channelId]: merged };
    });
    return page.length;
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The docstring says the no-`before` path "replaces the channel's message list (initial open)", but both branches call mergeById(ascending, existing), which unions by id. mergeById lets the SECOND argument (existing) win on id collisions (it sets a then overwrites with b). So on a channel re-open, any locally-cached message that the server has since edited or that fell out of the returned window is NOT reconciled to the server's authoritative copy: a stale edited body or a message the server no longer returns lingers in the UI indefinitely, and an edited message keeps the pre-edit body because the older cached copy overwrites the fresh page copy. On rapid channel switching this means the chat can display outdated message content that never self-heals until a full app reload.

Independent verifier — confirmation
loadHistory (chat-realtime.ts:496-498): const merged = before == null ? mergeById(ascending, existing) : mergeById(ascending, existing);  // both branches identical. mergeById (line 660-664): for (const m of a) byId.set(m.id, m); for (const m of b) byId.set(m.id, m); — the second arg (existing/cached) overwrites the first (fresh server page) on id collision. Docstring (line 487): "With no `before`, replaces the channel's message list".

Verified directly. The initial-open branch (before == null) does NOT replace the list as documented; both ternary branches call mergeById(ascending, existing), a union. In mergeById (lines 660-664) the second argument wins on id collision, so cached (existing) entries overwrite the fresh server page. Consequence: a message edited server-side while the client was disconnected keeps its stale pre-edit body on channel re-open, and a message that has since fallen out of the returned window (e.g. deleted/tombstoned server-side without a live delete event reaching this client) lingers indefinitely until a full reload. This is a genuine data-freshness/consistency defect for a chat surface. Note it partially self-heals: while connected, live edit/delete hub events reconcile the cache, so the window of harm is edits/deletes that occurred during disconnect. That, plus it being a UI-consistency issue rather than a security/crash/data-loss bug, keeps it at Medium. Fix in prose: for the initial-open (before == null) path, replace the channel's cached list with the server page (optionally re-folding only messages newer than the newest returned id to preserve genuinely-live messages), or make mergeById let the first (server) argument win so authoritative server bodies take precedence over cached copies.

Remediation (approach)

Make the initial-load branch (before == null) actually replace the channel's list with the server page (optionally re-merging only messages strictly newer than the page's newest id to preserve just-arrived live messages), rather than unioning with the whole existing cache. Alternatively fix mergeById so the freshly-fetched page wins on id collisions, and drop cached ids that are absent from a full initial page. Align the code with the docstring either way.

Medium Data & Correctness EF entities & migrations CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

No optimistic-concurrency token on any entity; money read-modify-write drops updates

src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:1087-1099 Concurrency tokens
Evidence
b.Entity<FinanceSavingsGoal>(e =>
{
    e.Property(x => x.TargetAmount).HasPrecision(18, 2);
    e.Property(x => x.SavedAmount).HasPrecision(18, 2);
    ...
    e.HasIndex(x => x.HouseholdId);
});  // no IsRowVersion / IsConcurrencyToken anywhere in the file
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

A repo-wide search (IsRowVersion|IsConcurrencyToken|xmin|RowVersion) returns zero matches, so NO entity carries an optimistic-concurrency token. Money and health mutations are done as read-modify-write: FamilyFinanceEndpoints.cs:2052-2056 computes `goal.SavedAmount + amount` in app memory then SaveChanges, and BillEndpoints.cs:307-313 checks `item.ClaimedByName is not null` then writes the claim. Two concurrent household members contributing to the same savings goal (or two guests claiming the same public bill item via the shared token link) both read the same starting row and the second SaveChanges silently overwrites the first — a lost update / double-claim with no conflict detected. On shared financial + bill-splitting data this produces incorrect balances that reconcile to nothing.

Independent verifier — confirmation
UsageDbContext.cs:1087-1099 FinanceSavingsGoal config has NO IsRowVersion/IsConcurrencyToken (repo-wide grep for IsRowVersion|IsConcurrencyToken|RowVersion|xmin|ConcurrencyCheck → "No files found"). FamilyFinanceEndpoints.cs:2052-2058 `var newSaved = Math.Max(0m, decimal.Round(goal.SavedAmount + amount, 2)); ... goal.SavedAmount = newSaved; ... await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);` — read-modify-write, no guard. BillEndpoints.cs:307-313 `if (item.AssignedToUserId is not null || item.ClaimedByName is not null || ...) return ...409; item.ClaimedByName = name; ... await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);` — check-then-act under `.AllowAnonymous()` shared-token access.

Verified all three cited locations. Grep for concurrency tokens returns zero matches — no entity carries a RowVersion/ConcurrencyToken. Savings contribute (FamilyFinanceEndpoints.cs:2044-2058) reads SavedAmount, adds in memory, saves — classic lost-update race between two household members. Bill claim (BillEndpoints.cs:304-313) is check-then-act under AllowAnonymous shared-token access with no unique constraint or FOR UPDATE, so two anonymous guests can both pass the ClaimedByName-is-null check and the second SaveChanges silently overwrites the first (double-claim). EF Core default ReadCommitted isolation does not prevent this. No mitigation found (no DB-level uniqueness on claim, no transaction with locking, no token). Impact is real but bounded: requires precise concurrent timing, corrupts financial/split data only (no auth/PII/health compromise). Medium is appropriate; line corrected to 1087 (start of the FinanceSavingsGoal entity block).

Remediation (approach)

Add a concurrency token to the mutable financial/claim entities (FinanceSavingsGoal, FinanceBudget, BillItem, FinanceBalanceSnapshot) — on PostgreSQL the idiomatic choice is the system `xmin` column mapped via a rowversion/concurrency-token property so EF issues an optimistic-concurrency WHERE clause and throws DbUpdateConcurrencyException on a stale write. Handle that exception in the contribute/claim endpoints by reloading and retrying or returning 409. Alternatively perform the mutation as an atomic ExecuteUpdate with a guard predicate (e.g. update ... where Id = @id and ClaimedByName is null) and treat a zero-row result as the conflict.

Medium Data & Correctness EF entities & migrations CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

UsageRecord cascades from IngestedFile via a required FK — purging a file destroys its billing rows

src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:140-141 Cascade-delete behavior
Evidence
e.HasOne(x => x.IngestedFile).WithMany()
    .HasForeignKey(x => x.IngestedFileId).OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

UsageRecord.IngestedFileId is a non-nullable int (UsageRecord.cs:76) with OnDelete(Cascade). UsageRecord is the denormalized billing/spend spine (CostUsd, token counts). If an IngestedFile row is ever deleted — a housekeeping purge of stale/rotated file-tracking rows, an admin cleanup, or a future retention job — every de-duplicated billing record first ingested from that file is silently cascade-deleted, irreversibly losing historical spend. The sibling FK to Project is intentionally Cascade (deleting a project drops its records), but a file-tracking row is metadata whose lifecycle should not be coupled to the authoritative usage ledger. The risk is latent today (the sync path upserts IngestedFile rows rather than deleting them), which is why this is Medium not High.

Independent verifier — confirmation
UsageDbContext.cs:140-141 — e.HasOne(x => x.IngestedFile).WithMany().HasForeignKey(x => x.IngestedFileId).OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade);  |  UsageRecord.cs:76 — public int IngestedFileId { get; set; } (non-nullable, comment: "The file this row was first ingested from (informational).")  |  IngestedFile.cs:3-6 — tracking metadata for re-sync skip decisions ("informational / diagnostics").

Verified all three load-bearing facts: (1) FK IngestedFileId is a non-nullable int (UsageRecord.cs:76); (2) the relationship is configured OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade) at UsageDbContext.cs:140-141; (3) IngestedFile is explicitly diagnostic/tracking metadata (IngestedFile.cs and inline comment "informational"), whose lifecycle is architecturally decoupled from the denormalized billing ledger (CostUsd/token counts on UsageRecord). Grep confirms NO code path deletes IngestedFile rows today — both ingestion services (IngestWriteService.cs, JsonlIngestionService.cs) only Add/upsert via ExecuteUpdateAsync, never Remove — so the data-loss is latent, correctly justifying Medium rather than High. The defect is a real design hazard: any future retention/purge/cleanup job that removes a stale file-tracking row would silently cascade-delete every historical spend record first ingested from it, with no recovery. Severity Medium and location (lines 140-141) are accurate. The sibling Project cascade (line 138-139) is intentional and correctly distinguished. Appropriate fix in prose: make the FK nullable and use DeleteBehavior.SetNull, or use Restrict, so deleting file-tracking metadata never destroys authoritative usage/billing rows.

Remediation (approach)

Decouple the billing ledger from the file-tracking table: either make IngestedFileId nullable and use DeleteBehavior.SetNull (mirroring the IngestKey.UserId 'orphan instead of cascade' decision at lines 391-393, so the informational file reference is dropped but the usage/cost row survives), or DeleteBehavior.Restrict so a file row cannot be deleted while records reference it. Since the comment on IngestedFileId calls it 'informational', SetNull is the natural fit.

Medium Data & Correctness EF entities & migrations CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

HouseholdMember.UserId has no FK to AppUser — deleting a user leaves a dangling membership row

src/Api/Data/Entities/HouseholdMember.cs:18 EF entities & migrations integrity
Evidence
/// <summary>AppUser id of the member (identity is by id, never email).</summary>
public int UserId { get; set; }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

HouseholdMember.UserId is only backed by a UNIQUE index (UsageDbContext.cs ln 845-847), not a foreign key to AppUser. When an admin deletes a user (UsersEndpoints.cs ln 202), the membership row is NOT cascaded or cleaned up, so it survives with a UserId pointing at a now-nonexistent AppUser. Because the app resolves member display names/pictures via a Users join at read time, the orphaned member renders as a blank/ghost person in household rosters and 'Where's everyone' maps, and household finance/family features that count members can miscount. The UNIQUE(UserId) constraint also means the ghost row permanently reserves that (stale) id.

Independent verifier — confirmation
HouseholdMember.cs:17-18 `/// <summary>AppUser id of the member (identity is by id, never email).</summary>\n    public int UserId { get; set; }`  — UsageDbContext.cs:840-847 configures only `e.HasIndex(x => new { x.HouseholdId, x.UserId }).IsUnique();` and `e.HasIndex(x => x.UserId).IsUnique();` (no HasOne/HasForeignKey to AppUser). UsersEndpoints.cs:202-203 `db.Users.Remove(user); await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);` removes the user without touching HouseholdMembers.

Verified by reading all three cited locations. HouseholdMember.UserId has no navigation property and no FK mapping to AppUser in UsageDbContext (only a HouseholdId FK at ln 836-837 and two unique indexes at ln 845-847). The DELETE /users/{id} endpoint (UsersEndpoints.cs ln 196-203) removes only the AppUser and never deletes or nulls the membership row, so deleting a user leaves an orphaned HouseholdMember whose UserId points at a nonexistent AppUser. The entity's own doc-comment plus CurrentHouseholdAccessor/FamilyMealsChoresEndpoints confirm member display name/picture are resolved via a Users join at read time, so the orphan renders as a blank/ghost member and inflates member counts. The UNIQUE(UserId) index does not free the stale id. Correctly located and Medium is appropriate (data-integrity/UX defect, no crash, PII join simply returns nothing). One caveat on the claim: because AppUser ids are DB-assigned and never reused, the 'permanently reserves the id' concern is largely inert — but the ghost-row and miscount impact stands, so the finding holds.

Remediation (approach)

Either add a real FK from HouseholdMember.UserId to AppUser with an appropriate delete behavior (cascade the membership, or restrict deletion until the user is removed from households), or have the user-delete handler explicitly remove the user's HouseholdMember row (and reassign/close the household if they were the owner) inside the delete transaction.

Medium Data & Correctness EF entities & migrations CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

FinanceAccount find-or-create backstop unique index is defeated by NULL Institution (code stores null, not the documented "")

src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:996 EF entities & migrations integrity
Evidence
// One account per distinct (household, name, institution); the find-or-create import keys on this.
// ... the importer normalizes a missing institution to "" to keep the key stable.
e.HasIndex(x => new { x.HouseholdId, x.Name, x.Institution }).IsUnique();
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The model comment claims the importer normalizes a missing institution to empty string so the unique index is a stable DB backstop for the find-or-create. The actual commit code does the opposite: it stores Institution = string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(...) ? null : ... (FamilyFinanceEndpoints.cs ln 645 and ln 717). In Postgres a UNIQUE index treats NULLs as distinct, so the (HouseholdId, Name, Institution) constraint provides NO protection for the common CSV case of an account with no institution. App-level dedup via the in-memory byKey dictionary (which normalizes null→"", ln 683-684) covers the single-batch case, but two concurrent commits for the same household introducing the same new no-institution account can both pass the in-memory check and both INSERT, and the unique index will not reject either. Result: duplicate FinanceAccount rows for one real bank account, splitting the transaction ledger and net-worth/savings math across the duplicates for household-private financial data.

Independent verifier — confirmation
UsageDbContext.cs:993-996: "// One account per distinct (household, name, institution)... the importer normalizes a missing institution to \"\" to keep the key stable. e.HasIndex(x => new { x.HouseholdId, x.Name, x.Institution }).IsUnique();"  ||  FamilyFinanceEndpoints.cs:717: "Institution = string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(s.Institution) ? null : Clamp(s.Institution, 200),"  ||  FamilyFinanceEndpoints.cs:683-684: "a => RocketMoneyCsv.AccountKey(a.Name, a.Institution ?? \"\"), a => a);"

Verified across all three files. The model comment (UsageDbContext.cs 993-996) documents that the importer normalizes a missing institution to "" so the unique index (HouseholdId, Name, Institution) is a stable DB backstop for find-or-create. The actual commit code stores null instead: FamilyFinanceEndpoints.cs ln 645 (stage) and ln 717 (account create) both use `string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(...) ? null : ...`. In Postgres a default B-tree UNIQUE index treats NULLs as distinct, so (HouseholdId, Name, NULL) never collides with another (HouseholdId, Name, NULL) — no protection for the common no-institution CSV case. The in-memory byKey dict (ln 683-684) normalizes null→"" and covers single-batch dedup, but CommitStagedAsync reads existingAccounts up front (ln 681-682), so two concurrent commits for the same household each introducing the same new no-institution account both miss byKey and both INSERT; the unique index rejects neither. The whole commit path (comments ln 662-666, 2301-2307) is explicitly designed to lean on this index as the race backstop and convert violations to a retryable 409 — that safety net is defeated precisely for no-institution accounts. Impact: duplicate FinanceAccount rows for one real bank account, splitting the ledger and net-worth/savings math for household-private financial data. Location/severity correct; anchor line 996 (index definition) in UsageDbContext.cs, root-cause null-store at FamilyFinanceEndpoints.cs 645/717. Requires a concurrency race, so Medium is appropriate.

Remediation (approach)

Make the stored value match the constraint: persist Institution as "" (empty string) instead of null on both create paths so the unique index actually enforces one-account-per-(household,name,institution), OR change the index to be filtered/expression-based over COALESCE(Institution,'') so NULL and '' collapse to the same key. Reconcile the code with the model comment either way, and consider a data cleanup to merge any duplicate null-institution accounts already created.

Medium Data & Correctness EF entities & migrations CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

The 'one OVERALL budget per household' invariant is unenforced: the overall budget is stored with Category = NULL, and the (HouseholdId, Category) unique index treats each NULL as distinct in Postgres, so duplicate overall budgets can be created and the endpoint's only guard never fires

src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:1084 Missing unique constraint / NULL-distinct uniqueness
Evidence
// One budget per (household, category); a null Category is the single OVERALL budget (Postgres
// treats nulls as distinct, so the importer/endpoint normalizes a missing category to "" — the
// overall budget is stored with Category == null and guarded in code, see the budgets endpoint).
e.HasIndex(x => new { x.HouseholdId, x.Category }).IsUnique();
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The comment claims the overall budget is 'guarded in code', but POST /budgets (src/Api/Endpoints/FamilyFinanceEndpoints.cs:1585-1620) does NOT pre-check for an existing overall budget — it relies solely on catching the DB unique violation (catch DbUpdateException when IsUniqueViolation). NormalizeBudgetCategory (line 1746-1750) returns null for a blank category, and a standard Postgres unique index treats every NULL as distinct, so inserting a second (HouseholdId, NULL) row never violates the index and never throws. A household can therefore accumulate unlimited duplicate 'overall' whole-month budgets; the budgets read (which puts 'overall budget first when present') and the spend-vs-budget pace math then operate on an ambiguous/duplicated overall row, corrupting the deterministic budget status the AI budget-check floor depends on. This is distinct from the previously-reported FinanceAccount NULL-Institution case — it is a separate table where, unlike FinanceAccount, the code deliberately stores NULL (not "") for the singleton row.

Independent verifier — confirmation
UsageDbContext.cs:1084 `e.HasIndex(x => new { x.HouseholdId, x.Category }).IsUnique();`; FamilyFinanceEndpoints.cs:1748-1749 `var c = (category ?? "").Trim(); return c.Length == 0 ? null : Clamp(c, 120);` (overall budget stored as Category==null); POST /budgets 1609-1616 only `catch (DbUpdateException ex) when (IsUniqueViolation(ex))` with no pre-check for an existing overall budget.

Verified by reading all cited code. NormalizeBudgetCategory (FamilyFinanceEndpoints.cs:1748-1749) returns null for a blank category, so the OVERALL budget is stored with Category==null. The unique index (HouseholdId, Category) at UsageDbContext.cs:1084 is a standard Postgres unique index (no NULLS NOT DISTINCT), so it treats each NULL as distinct and never blocks a second (HouseholdId, null) row. POST /budgets (1585-1620) has NO pre-existence check for an overall budget — it relies solely on the DB unique violation (catch at 1613), which cannot fire for NULL rows. I grep-confirmed there is no other guard (no Category==null existence check anywhere on the write path). Thus duplicate overall budgets can accumulate. ComputeBudgetsAsync orders "overall budget first" (1700) and sums whole-month spend against the overall row (1704-1706), and the deterministic status feeds the AI budget-check floor (comment line 224) — duplicated overall rows corrupt that read. The DbContext comment (1081-1083) is itself inaccurate: it claims the missing category normalizes to "" and is "guarded in code," but the actual code normalizes to null and no guard exists. Correct anchor is UsageDbContext.cs:1084 (the index), within the cited 1081-1084 range. Medium severity is appropriate: data-integrity/correctness defect on financial-budget data, not a security bypass. Distinct from the FinanceAccount NULL-Institution case since here NULL is stored deliberately for the singleton.

Remediation (approach)

Enforce the singleton overall budget at the DB level with a partial unique index over HouseholdId filtered to Category IS NULL (mirroring the filtered-unique pattern already used for GoogleSubject/ShareTokenHash/Fitid), OR store the overall budget with a sentinel non-null category value so the existing composite unique index catches duplicates. Additionally, have POST /budgets pre-check for an existing overall budget rather than depending only on the unique-violation catch, since that catch cannot fire while Category is NULL.

Medium Data & Privacy Logging / PII / DTO exposure CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

LogRedaction only masks a fixed set of secret KEY NAMES, so any PII value (email, coordinates, health text, names) in a body is stored verbatim

src/Api/Infrastructure/LogRedaction.cs:16-44 PII in request/response logs
Evidence
private const string SecretKeys =
    "token|id_token|access_token|refresh_token|code|password|secret|clientSecret|client_secret|apiKey|api_key|authorization|webhookUrl|webhook_url|discordWebhookUrl|DiscordWebhookUrl";
...
var cleaned = SecretJsonFieldRegex().Replace(body, ...);
cleaned = SecretPairRegex().Replace(cleaned, ...);
return Truncate(cleaned);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Redaction is purely key-name based. Any request/response field whose key is not in SecretKeys is stored in full — this includes email addresses, precise lat/lng, medication/symptom text, nicknames and notes. Combined with the broad capture surface (RequestLoggingMiddleware logs all non-excluded /api routes) and /api/logs replay to activity.view holders, this means the log becomes a de-facto secondary store of unredacted PII with only key-name-based masking. It also silently misses secret-bearing keys that don't happen to appear in the list (e.g. a future 'privateKey', 'sessionToken', 'otp', 'backupCode', 'tokenHash' field), so new secrets leak until someone remembers to extend the constant.

Independent verifier — confirmation
LogRedaction.cs:16-17  private const string SecretKeys = "token|id_token|access_token|refresh_token|code|password|secret|clientSecret|client_secret|apiKey|api_key|authorization|webhookUrl|webhook_url|discordWebhookUrl|DiscordWebhookUrl";  and LogRedaction.cs:41-43  var cleaned = SecretJsonFieldRegex().Replace(body, m => $"\"{m.Groups[\"k\"].Value}\":\"[redacted]\""); cleaned = SecretPairRegex().Replace(cleaned, ...); return Truncate(cleaned);  Corroborated by RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:55/85 (captures request+response bodies for all non-excluded /api routes) and ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:76-83 (RequestBody/ResponseBody returned verbatim to activity.view holders).

Verified by reading the file and the two related flows. Redact() is purely key-name based: only values whose JSON key or urlencoded key matches the fixed SecretKeys list are masked; every other field value passes through (then truncated at 4096). The broad capture surface is confirmed in RequestLoggingMiddleware — request and response bodies are stored for all /api routes except a health/probe/poll Excluded list and an email-only BodyExcluded admin set. So bodies for tracker/health, family (non-calendar), journal, GPS/location, notes/nicknames are stored verbatim with only key-name masking. IsSensitivePath whole-value redaction covers only auth/notifications/shares/ingest-keys/family-calendar, not this PII surface. /api/logs replays RequestBody/ResponseBody to any activity.view holder — and notably scrubs the raw request-user email for privacy but does nothing to body content, confirming the asymmetry. Location (lines 16-44) and Medium severity are correct; admin/activity.view gating narrows but does not eliminate the data-minimization violation for health/location PII. The secondary observation (future secret keys like otp/backupCode/tokenHash silently unmasked) is also valid. CONFIRMED.

Remediation (approach)

Do not rely on capturing sensitive bodies at all — prefer the body-suppression approach above for anything carrying PII/health/location. Where bodies ARE captured, augment key-name redaction with value-pattern redaction (mask strings matching email, JWT, and coordinate patterns) and default-deny: capture only fields on a per-endpoint safe allowlist rather than persisting everything except a denylist of key names. Document that the SecretKeys constant must be reviewed whenever a new secret-bearing field is introduced.

Medium Data & Privacy Logging / PII / DTO exposure CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

BodyExcluded (email-bearing admin paths) is a denylist that must be manually kept in lockstep with every endpoint that emits emails; endpoints that return raw emails outside this list would be captured and replayed to activity.view holders

src/Api/Infrastructure/RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:36-40 PII / secrets in request/response logs
Evidence
private static readonly string[] BodyExcluded =
{
    "/api/users", "/api/audit", "/api/access-policy",
    "/api/chat/contacts", "/api/chat/directory",
};
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Body capture is on by default and only these five prefixes suppress it. The safety of every other route depends on that route independently never putting a raw email (or other special-category value) in a body — a property enforced nowhere centrally. Today most email-returning endpoints happen to project to UserId/DisplayName, but nothing structurally prevents a new or existing endpoint (e.g. a future admin/reporting/export JSON that echoes an email, or an error body like the '{email} already exists' 409s) from landing in RequestLog and being served to any activity.view holder who cannot see emails elsewhere. This is the same denylist-fragility class the prior round flagged for special-category PII, here specifically for the email-privacy invariant that the rest of the codebase spends significant effort upholding via DTO masking.

Independent verifier — confirmation
RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:36-40 —
private static readonly string[] BodyExcluded =
{
    "/api/users", "/api/audit", "/api/access-policy",
    "/api/chat/contacts", "/api/chat/directory",
};
...and line 53: var captureBodies = !SkipBodyCapture(path);  // capture ON by default.
LogRedaction.cs:16-17 SecretKeys has NO email token; IsSensitivePath (28-33) lists only auth/notifications/shares/ingest-keys/family calendar — Redact (36-44) never masks emails.

Verified against both files. Body capture is default-on and suppressed only for the 5 BodyExcluded prefixes (lines 36-40, 53). The central sanitizer LogRedaction.Redact does NOT detect or mask emails: SecretKeys (LogRedaction.cs:16-17) covers only auth secrets (token/password/apiKey/webhook), and IsSensitivePath (28-33) whole-value-redacts only auth/notifications/shares/ingest-keys/family-calendar — none of which target the email-privacy invariant. So any other /api route that emits a raw other-user email in its request/response body (e.g. a future admin/export/reporting JSON, or a 409 error body echoing '{email} already exists') would be captured into RequestLog verbatim and replayed to any activity.view holder, defeating the DTO-masking effort the rest of the codebase spends on the same invariant. This is a genuine denylist-fragility defect: the safety of every non-listed route depends on a property enforced nowhere centrally. Correctly located; Medium is appropriate because the exposure is conditional on a route emitting a raw email (most current endpoints project to UserId/DisplayName), so it is a latent gap rather than a confirmed active leak. Line corrected to 36 (start of the BodyExcluded denylist).

Remediation (approach)

Invert the model for body capture: default to NOT storing response bodies and opt specific diagnostic routes in, or run every captured body through the same email-masking used for audit Detail (MaskEmailsInText) before persistence so an address can never survive into RequestLog regardless of which endpoint produced it. At minimum, add an automated test asserting no captured body matches an email-shaped regex.

Medium Dependencies & Supply Chain Dependency hygiene CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

All backend NuGet packages use floating 9.0.* versions with no lock file — non-reproducible, mutable-at-restore supply chain

src/Api/Ccusage.Api.csproj:12-15 Dependency hygiene
Evidence
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.JwtBearer" Version="9.0.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore" Version="9.0.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL" Version="9.0.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design" Version="9.0.*">
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The `9.0.*` wildcard resolves to whatever is the latest 9.0.x patch on NuGet at restore time. The API Dockerfile runs `dotnet restore` during every image build (src/Api/Dockerfile line 10) and there is NO packages.lock.json anywhere in the repo and no RestorePackagesWithLockFile/RestoreLockedMode set (Glob for packages.lock.json returned nothing; Grep for RestoreLockedMode returned nothing). Consequently two builds of the same commit can ship different dependency binaries, a compromised/yanked patch is pulled automatically with no review gate, and there is no reproducibility or provenance trail. For an app handling auth (JwtBearer), PII/health data, and payments running on AWS, an unreviewed transitive change to the JWT or Npgsql stack is a direct security-supply-chain exposure. The same `9.0.*` pattern is used across every backend project: src/ReporterCore (Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration.* and System.Management, lines 13-17 and 31) and tests/Ccusage.Api.Tests (Mvc.Testing / SignalR.Client, lines 14-15).

Independent verifier — confirmation
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.JwtBearer" Version="9.0.*" /> (src/Api/Ccusage.Api.csproj:12); Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL Version="9.0.*" (line 14); Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design Version="9.0.*" (line 15). Glob for **/packages.lock.json returned no files; Grep for RestoreLockedMode|RestorePackagesWithLockFile returned no files.

Verified: lines 12-15 use floating `9.0.*` wildcards on security-sensitive packages (JwtBearer, Npgsql/EF, HealthChecks). Confirmed no packages.lock.json exists anywhere in the repo and no RestoreLockedMode/RestorePackagesWithLockFile is set, so restores are non-deterministic and pull the latest 9.0.x patch at build time with no review gate or provenance — a real reproducibility/supply-chain hygiene gap for an auth/PII/payments app. Two mitigating factors lower it from High to Medium: (1) the wildcard is pinned to the 9.0.x band (major/minor fixed), so blast radius is limited to Microsoft/Npgsql official patch releases rather than arbitrary versions, and these come from first-party signed feeds; (2) it is a hygiene/best-practice defect, not an exploitable code path — no attacker-controlled input flows through it. It is a legitimate defect worth fixing (add lock files + RestoreLockedMode), but 'High' overstates immediate impact; Medium is accurate. Same pattern also present in src/ReporterCore and tests/Ccusage.Api.Tests as claimed.

Remediation (approach)

Pin every PackageReference to an exact, reviewed version instead of a wildcard, and enable NuGet lock files by setting RestorePackagesWithLockFile to true (ideally in a shared Directory.Build.props) so a committed packages.lock.json captures the full transitive graph. Make CI and the Dockerfile restore in locked mode (dotnet restore --locked-mode) so a build fails if the resolved graph drifts from the lock file. Adopt central package management via a Directory.Packages.props so versions are declared once and reviewed together. Treat dependency upgrades as explicit, reviewed commits.

Medium Dependencies & Supply Chain Dependency hygiene CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

ReporterCore (and the whole Agent/Reporter chain) also floats every dependency on 9.0.* with no lock file — a distinct, PII/GPS-collecting binary the API-only finding missed

src/ReporterCore/Ccusage.Reporter.Core.csproj:13-31 Dependency hygiene
Evidence
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration" Version="9.0.*" />
... 
<PackageReference Include="System.Management" Version="9.0.*" />
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The prior round flagged floating 9.0.* only for src/Api. ReporterCore is a separately-shipped artifact: it is referenced by the distributable Windows desktop Agent (src/Agent) that collects WMI hardware inventory and GPS/geolocation and phones home to the ingest endpoint. Every one of its config packages plus System.Management floats on `9.0.*` with no packages.lock.json anywhere in the repo (confirmed: no Directory.Packages.props, no packages.lock.json, no nuget.config), so two developers or two CI runs can build a signed agent .exe from different transitive closures. For a binary distributed to end-user machines that harvests location and hardware data, non-reproducible, mutable-at-restore builds defeat any hope of build attestation / supply-chain provenance for the shipped executable.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/ReporterCore/Ccusage.Reporter.Core.csproj:13-17,31 — <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration" Version="9.0.*" /> ... <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration.Binder" Version="9.0.*" /> ... <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration.Json" Version="9.0.*" /> ... <PackageReference Include="System.Management" Version="9.0.*" />. Repo-wide Glob for packages.lock.json, Directory.Packages.props, and nuget.config all returned "No files found".

Verified by reading the file: six PackageReferences (five Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration.* on lines 13-17 plus System.Management on line 31) all pin Version="9.0.*", a floating wildcard. Confirmed no packages.lock.json, Directory.Packages.props, or nuget.config exist anywhere in the repo (three separate globs each returned nothing), so restores are not pinned and NuGet resolves the newest available 9.0.x at restore time. This is a genuinely distinct project from src/Api: the file's own comment (lines 24-28) documents that System.Management drives WMI hardware probes in MachineInfo.Collect (CPU/RAM/GPU/manufacturer/model/domain), corroborating that this is a data-collecting binary. Non-reproducible, mutable-at-restore builds for an artifact of this nature undermine build attestation/supply-chain provenance. Defect is real, correctly located, and Medium severity is appropriate. Set correctedLine to 13 (first occurrence) since the finding spans lines 13-31.

Remediation (approach)

Adopt Central Package Management (Directory.Packages.props) across the whole solution so Api, Agent, Reporter, ReporterCore and Ingestion share one pinned version set, and enable RestorePackagesWithLockFile so a committed packages.lock.json makes every artifact — especially the distributed agent — bit-for-bit reproducible. Replace the 9.0.* wildcards with exact versions.

Medium Dependencies & Supply Chain Dependency hygiene CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

CI restores floating NuGet ranges at build time with no lock file or --locked-mode, mutating the JWT-auth supply chain per-run

.github/workflows/ci.yml:21-22 Dependency hygiene
Evidence
- run: dotnet restore
- run: dotnet build --no-restore -c Release
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Prior rounds noted the csproj floats every backend package on 9.0.* with no lock file, but the concrete exploitable mechanism is here: every CI (and by extension every deploy) run performs a fresh, unconstrained `dotnet restore` that resolves `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.JwtBearer 9.0.*`, `Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL 9.0.*`, etc. to whatever the newest matching patch is at that instant. There is no `packages.lock.json`, no `RestorePackagesWithLockFile`, and no `--locked-mode`/`--use-lock-file`, so a yanked, hijacked, or malicious patch of the JWT bearer auth package (the code path that validates the app-issued token gating all PII/health/payment/GPS endpoints) is pulled straight into the Release artifact with no gate and no reproducibility. Two builds of the same commit can ship different binaries.

Independent verifier — confirmation
.github/workflows/ci.yml:21-22 — "- run: dotnet restore" / "- run: dotnet build --no-restore -c Release"; corroborated by src/Api/Ccusage.Api.csproj:12 "<PackageReference Include=\"Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.JwtBearer\" Version=\"9.0.*\" />" and :14 Npgsql...PostgreSQL Version="9.0.*". Repo-wide glob for packages.lock.json, Directory.*.props, and NuGet.config all returned no files.

Verified by direct reads. ci.yml lines 21-22 do an unconstrained `dotnet restore` feeding a `--no-restore -c Release` build. No packages.lock.json exists anywhere; no Directory.Build.props/Directory.Packages.props/NuGet.config exists, so there is no RestorePackagesWithLockFile, and the CI passes no --locked-mode/--use-lock-file. The csproj floats security-critical backend packages on 9.0.* including the JwtBearer auth package. So every CI/deploy run resolves patches non-deterministically with no reproducibility or integrity gate — a real supply-chain hygiene defect, correctly located. Severity corrected from High to Medium: the float is patch-only within a pinned major.minor (9.0.*), not a fully open range, and this is a latent risk (no evidence of a currently-published malicious patch), which lowers the practical blast radius versus the High framing.

Remediation (approach)

Enable NuGet lock files repo-wide (set RestorePackagesWithLockFile so a packages.lock.json is generated and committed for the Api and every buildable project), and change the CI/deploy restore step to run in locked mode so restore fails if the committed lock hashes do not match. Additionally replace the floating 9.0.* ranges with exact pinned versions so the lock file has a stable, reviewable target. Turn on NuGet audit (NuGetAudit) to surface advisories at restore time.

Medium Dependencies & Supply Chain Dependency hygiene PLAUSIBLE conf: high 2 verifiers

Desktop Agent (PII/GPS binary with floating System.Management) is never restored, built, or tested in any CI/CD pipeline

src/Agent/UsageIq.Agent.csproj:45-46 Dependency hygiene
Evidence
<ItemGroup>
    <ProjectReference Include="..\ReporterCore\Ccusage.Reporter.Core.csproj" />
  </ItemGroup>
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The Agent transitively floats System.Management 9.0.* (via ReporterCore) and collects hardware/PII plus consented GPS, and ships as a distributable exe to end-user machines. Yet neither ci.yml nor deploy.yml builds or publishes this project: ci.yml only builds src/Reporter and the Api, and deploy.yml contains no reference to the Agent or ReporterCore at all. So the one binary whose supply chain reaches user desktops (and whose floating deps are resolved on some developer's laptop, off-lock, at an unknown time) receives zero pipeline restore verification, no reproducible build, and no dependency/advisory scanning. A compromised or drifted transitive dependency in the Agent would never be caught by the gates that (partially) cover the server.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Agent/UsageIq.Agent.csproj:44-46: `<ItemGroup>\n    <ProjectReference Include="..\ReporterCore\Ccusage.Reporter.Core.csproj" />\n  </ItemGroup>` (Agent is <OutputType>WinExe</OutputType>, line 10). src/ReporterCore/Ccusage.Reporter.Core.csproj:31: `<PackageReference Include="System.Management" Version="9.0.*" />` (floating). ci.yml only builds src/Api (l.22), src/Reporter (l.32), tests (l.45), src/Web (l.61) — no Agent/ReporterCore. deploy.yml (l.31-39) builds only api + web images — no Agent. Grep for Agent|ReporterCore|dependabot|.sln across .github: "No matches found".

All technical claims verified by reading the files. The Agent (a WinExe distributed to end-user desktops that collects WMI hardware/PII via ReporterCore's MachineInfo and consented GPS via GpsLocator) transitively floats System.Management 9.0.* (ReporterCore csproj line 31) plus the Config packages (lines 13-17). ci.yml builds only Api, Reporter, tests, and Web on ubuntu-latest; neither Agent nor ReporterCore is restored/built/tested/published in ci.yml or deploy.yml, and there is no dependabot or dependency/advisory scanning anywhere in .github (grep returned no matches). This is a genuine dependency-hygiene / supply-chain gap: the one binary reaching user machines gets zero reproducible-build or advisory verification, and the Agent's net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0 (WPF/WinForms) target cannot even build on the ubuntu runners CI uses, so it is entirely uncovered. Anchored line corrected to 45 (the actual ProjectReference; 44/46 are the ItemGroup tags). Severity lowered from High to Medium: the floating versions are Microsoft-published and band-constrained to 9.0.* (not fully unpinned), and this is a desktop-side tool rather than the internet-facing server, so immediate exploitable impact is lower than a server supply-chain gap — but the defect is real and correctly located.

Remediation (approach)

Add the Agent (and ReporterCore) to CI as an explicit build/restore job, and to a signed, reproducible publish pipeline for distribution. Once lock files are enabled repo-wide, ensure the Agent's lock file is restored in locked mode in that job so its transitive graph (System.Management and the rest of the 9.0.* set) is pinned and verifiable before any artifact is handed to users.

Medium Infrastructure & Operations CloudFormation / Caddy / TLS CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Single-instance Postgres with no backups or EBS snapshot policy; volume deleted on termination

deploy/cloudformation.yaml:95 No backups / disaster recovery
Evidence
BlockDeviceMappings:
        - DeviceName: /dev/xvda
          Ebs: { VolumeSize: !Ref DiskSizeGb, VolumeType: gp3, DeleteOnTermination: true, Encrypted: true }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Per the README the runtime stack is 'Caddy -> web -> api -> Postgres' all on this one EC2 box, so the production database (PII, health/cycle+meds, payments, GPS history) lives on this single gp3 volume. The volume is encrypted (good) but DeleteOnTermination is true and there is no automated snapshot, AWS Backup plan, or DeletionPolicy anywhere in the template. A stack delete, instance termination, or disk failure permanently destroys all customer data with no recovery point — an enterprise-unacceptable RPO of total loss.

Independent verifier — confirmation
deploy/cloudformation.yaml:93-95
      BlockDeviceMappings:
        - DeviceName: /dev/xvda
          Ebs: { VolumeSize: !Ref DiskSizeGb, VolumeType: gp3, DeleteOnTermination: true, Encrypted: true }
--- corroborating deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:6-13: image: postgres:16-alpine ... volumes: - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data (DB lives on this single host's root EBS volume)

Verified. Postgres 16 runs as a Docker service (docker-compose.prod.yml:6) persisting to the pgdata volume (line 13), which sits on the single EC2 instance's only EBS volume (/dev/xvda). That volume is encrypted (good) but has DeleteOnTermination: true and the Instance resource carries no DeletionPolicy. Grep across the entire deploy/ directory shows no EBS snapshot schedule, AWS Backup plan, pg_dump cron, or any offsite/replica store. README confirms the whole runtime stack (Caddy->web->api->Postgres) is on this one box. A stack delete, instance termination, or disk failure permanently destroys the production DB (PII, health/cycle, meds, payments, GPS history) with total-loss RPO and no recovery path. Located correctly at line 95; Medium severity is appropriate (data-loss risk but requires a destructive/failure event to trigger). Not mitigated anywhere in the reviewed code.

Remediation (approach)

Introduce a durable, backed-up data tier: either move Postgres to RDS with automated backups/PITR and a DeletionPolicy of Snapshot, or if keeping Postgres on-box, attach a dedicated data EBS volume with DeleteOnTermination false, an AWS Backup plan (or DLM snapshot schedule) with tested restores, and off-box copies. Document the RPO/RTO.

Medium Infrastructure & Operations CloudFormation / Caddy / TLS CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Caddy enables gzip response compression over HTTPS on auth/secret-bearing responses (BREACH exposure)

deploy/Caddyfile:7-9 TLS / reverse-proxy (BREACH via compression)
Evidence
{$DOMAIN} {
	encode gzip
	reverse_proxy web:80
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

encode gzip compresses all proxied responses, including JSON from /api that reflects request-controlled input alongside secrets (this app returns JWTs, CSRF/anti-forgery values, PII and health data). Compression + TLS + a request-reflected value is the precondition for a BREACH-style side-channel that lets a network-positioned attacker recover secrets (e.g. tokens) from ciphertext length. Because the proxy blanket-compresses everything with no content-type restriction and no length-masking, sensitive API responses are compressed the same as static SPA assets.

Independent verifier — confirmation
deploy/Caddyfile:6-9
{$DOMAIN} {
	encode gzip
	reverse_proxy web:80
}

Verified the Caddyfile directly: line 7 `encode gzip` sits inside the `{$DOMAIN}` block above `reverse_proxy web:80` (lines 6-9), so Caddy blanket-compresses every proxied response with no content-type/path restriction — dynamic authenticated /api JSON is compressed identically to static SPA assets. For an app fronting auth tokens, PII, and health data over HTTPS, enabling HTTP-level response compression on secret-bearing dynamic responses is the recognized BREACH precondition and a legitimate reverse-proxy hardening gap; best practice is to scope `encode` to static/non-sensitive content types or exclude the API. Two caveats keep this at Medium rather than higher: (1) the specific reflection precondition (attacker-controlled request input echoed into the same response as a secret) is asserted by the finding but not provable from this file alone, and (2) practical BREACH exploitation additionally needs attacker-driven request volume and length measurement with no masking/rate-limit. The config is real, correctly located, and unmitigated (no `match`/content-type filter on the encode directive), so the hardening defect stands. correctedLine set to 7 (the actual `encode gzip` line) within the cited 6-9 range.

Remediation (approach)

Restrict compression to static/asset content types (or disable it for the /api path) rather than blanket gzip on all proxied responses, and/or ensure secret-bearing responses are excluded from compression. Confirm no attacker-reflected input shares a compression context with tokens/secrets. Static SPA assets can still be compressed safely.

Medium Infrastructure & Operations CloudFormation / Caddy / TLS CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

ECR repositories are tag-mutable and deploy pins :latest, allowing silent image overwrite with no provenance

deploy/cloudformation.yaml:30-42 Supply-chain / image integrity
Evidence
EcrApi:
    Type: AWS::ECR::Repository
    Properties:
      RepositoryName: usage-iq-api
      ImageScanningConfiguration: { ScanOnPush: true }
      Tags: [{ Key: Project, Value: usage-iq }]
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Neither repo sets ImageTagMutability, so both default to MUTABLE, and the runtime pulls the floating :latest tag (docker-compose.prod.yml uses ${ECR}/usage-iq-api:latest). Any identity able to push (the deploy role, or an attacker who obtains it) can overwrite :latest so the next restart silently runs a different image, and there is no immutable digest to audit which build actually served PII/health/payment traffic. ScanOnPush does not prevent overwrite. For a regulated-data workload this defeats build provenance and rollback integrity.

Independent verifier — confirmation
deploy/cloudformation.yaml:30-42
  EcrApi:
    Type: AWS::ECR::Repository
    Properties:
      RepositoryName: usage-iq-api
      ImageScanningConfiguration: { ScanOnPush: true }
      Tags: [{ Key: Project, Value: usage-iq }]
  EcrWeb:
    ...
      RepositoryName: usage-iq-web
      ImageScanningConfiguration: { ScanOnPush: true }
--- and deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:21 `image: ${ECR}/usage-iq-api:latest` and :44 `image: ${ECR}/usage-iq-web:latest`

Verified. Neither EcrApi (lines 30-35) nor EcrWeb (37-42) sets ImageTagMutability, so both default to MUTABLE per AWS. docker-compose.prod.yml pins the floating :latest tag for both images (lines 21, 44) with no digest, and services use restart: unless-stopped, so any restart re-pulls whatever :latest currently points to. Any push-capable identity (the deploy role or a compromised one) can silently overwrite :latest, defeating build provenance/rollback integrity for a PII/health/payment workload. ScanOnPush does not prevent overwrite. This is a real defense-in-depth/supply-chain hardening gap; Medium is correct (not directly exploitable absent deploy-role compromise, but genuinely reduces auditability of which image served regulated traffic). Fix in prose: set ImageTagMutability: IMMUTABLE on both repos and deploy by immutable digest (or immutable version tags) rather than :latest.

Remediation (approach)

Set ImageTagMutability to IMMUTABLE on both repositories and deploy by immutable digest (or unique per-commit tags) rather than the mutable :latest, so each release is content-addressed and auditable. Optionally add an ECR repository policy restricting push to the deploy role only.

Medium Observability & Operations Observability & ops readiness CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Startup database migration is unguarded, so any transient DB blip or bad migration crash-loops the only API instance

src/Api/Program.cs:410 Config/env validation at startup + single-instance failure modes
Evidence
using (var scope = app.Services.CreateScope())
{
    var db = scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<UsageDbContext>();
    await db.Database.MigrateAsync();
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

MigrateAsync runs synchronously in the boot path with no try/catch and no retry, and app.Run() is never reached if it throws. Although the DbContext is configured with EnableRetryOnFailure for request-time queries (Program.cs:37), the boot-time MigrateAsync plus the seeding SaveChanges calls that follow are executed on a fresh scope during startup; a DB connection drop, failover, lock, or a migration that fails to apply throws out of Main and the container exits. Under restart: unless-stopped on a single EC2 host (deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml) this becomes a crash-loop and a full outage with no second instance to serve traffic. Coupling schema migration to every process start also means a slow/held migration blocks the whole service from coming up.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Program.cs:407-410:
using (var scope = app.Services.CreateScope())
{
    var db = scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<UsageDbContext>();
    await db.Database.MigrateAsync();

Verified against the actual code. Lines 407-410 match the quoted evidence exactly: a startup scope calls `await db.Database.MigrateAsync()` with no try/catch and no explicit retry, and the seeding SaveChangesAsync calls (lines 430/443/451) follow in the same boot scope before app.Run(). If any of these throw, the exception propagates out of Main and the process exits. The `EnableRetryOnFailure` at line 37 is the request-time EF Core execution strategy; a bare `MigrateAsync()` is NOT automatically wrapped by it (well-known EF Core behavior — you must invoke it via the execution strategy explicitly), so the boot path genuinely has no resilience. deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml confirms the single-instance topology (one `api` service, no replicas) and `restart: unless-stopped` (line 22), so a repeatedly-failing migration/seed crash-loops the only instance = full outage. The claim is real and correctly located. I downgrade severity High→Medium: the `depends_on: db condition: service_healthy` gate (lines 23-25) removes the most common transient case (DB-not-ready-at-boot), narrowing the exposure to mid-startup connection drops/failover/locks or a genuinely bad migration; a bad migration is normally caught at deploy time, and startup-coupled migrations on a small single-instance deployment are a conventional, accepted pattern rather than a severe defect. Remediation (prose): wrap the boot-time MigrateAsync and seeding in a bounded retry loop using the context's execution strategy (CreateExecutionStrategy) or a Polly-style backoff so transient DB blips are tolerated; ensure migrations are validated/gated before deploy; and consider decoupling schema migration from process start (a separate migration job/step) so a slow or held migration cannot block the API from coming up.

Remediation (approach)

Separate schema migration from application startup (run migrations as an explicit deploy step / one-shot job rather than in every container boot), or wrap the startup migrate+seed in a bounded retry with clear fatal-vs-transient handling so a transient DB blip does not crash-loop the instance. Given the single-instance topology, also ensure a failed migrate leaves the previous image running rather than taking the service down.

Medium Observability & Operations Observability & ops readiness CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

No metrics of any kind, and the two observability queues silently drop entries with no counter

src/Api/Infrastructure/RequestLogQueue.cs:13-21 Missing metrics
Evidence
new BoundedChannelOptions(2000)
{
    FullMode = BoundedChannelFullMode.DropWrite,
    SingleReader = true,
});
...
public bool TryEnqueue(RequestLog entry) => _channel.Writer.TryWrite(entry);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

There are no request-rate, latency, or error-rate metrics and no queue-depth/utilization gauges anywhere (no Metrics/Prometheus/OpenTelemetry usage in the codebase). The request-log and AI-usage-log queues (RequestLogQueue, AiUsageLogQueue) are bounded at 2000 with FullMode=DropWrite; under a sustained burst they discard the newest entries — the RequestLoggingMiddleware even comments 'dropped if the buffer is saturated' (RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:88). Because the discard increments no counter and emits no log, the very telemetry an operator would rely on during an incident (the action log / AI-usage log) is silently and invisibly lossy exactly when traffic is highest. There is likewise no signal for how full the queues run or how often the background writers fail a batch.

Independent verifier — confirmation
RequestLogQueue.cs:13-21 `Channel.CreateBounded<RequestLog>(new BoundedChannelOptions(2000){ FullMode = BoundedChannelFullMode.DropWrite, SingleReader = true }); ... public bool TryEnqueue(RequestLog entry) => _channel.Writer.TryWrite(entry);` and RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:88 `queue.TryEnqueue(entry); // non-blocking; dropped if the buffer is saturated` (return value ignored). AiUsageLogQueue.cs:13-21 is identical. Grep for AddOpenTelemetry|AddPrometheus|new Meter(|CreateCounter|CreateHistogram across src/Api returned no matches.

Verified: both RequestLogQueue and AiUsageLogQueue are bounded at 2000 with FullMode=DropWrite; the middleware (line 88) discards TryEnqueue's boolean result, so saturation-drops of the action log and AI-usage log increment no counter and emit no log — silently lossy exactly under peak load. No metrics infrastructure (OpenTelemetry/Prometheus/System.Diagnostics.Metrics/Meter/Counter/Histogram) exists anywhere in src/Api, confirming no request-rate/latency/error-rate or queue-depth telemetry. The claim is accurate and correctly located. Medium is appropriate: this is an observability/operability gap (blind spot during incidents) rather than a direct security or data-corruption vuln — the drop policy itself (never block the request path, cap memory) is a deliberate, defensible design; the defect is purely the absence of any drop-count/queue-depth signal.

Remediation (approach)

Add lightweight instrumentation: increment a counter (and periodically log) whenever TryEnqueue returns false so dropped-entry volume is visible, and expose a queue-depth gauge. More broadly, adopt a metrics pipeline (e.g. OpenTelemetry/prometheus-net or the built-in System.Diagnostics.Metrics) emitting request count, latency histogram, and error rate so operational health is observable without reading raw logs.

Medium Observability & Operations Observability & ops readiness CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

JWT signing key and DB password are generated once and explicitly never rotated; no rotation mechanism exists

deploy/provision.sh:11-22 Secret rotation story
Evidence
# 1. App secrets live in SSM Parameter Store (encrypted). Generate once; never rotate on re-run.
ensure_secret() {
  local name=$1
  if aws ssm get-parameter --region "$REGION" --name "$name" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "    $name already set — keeping it"
  else
    local val
    val=$(openssl rand -base64 48 | tr -dc 'A-Za-z0-9'); val=${val:0:44}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The JWT signing key (/usage-iq/jwt-key) and database password (/usage-iq/db-password) are minted once by provision.sh and, by explicit design ('Generate once; never rotate on re-run'), are never rotated. There is no rotation runbook, no scheduled rotation, and no key-versioning: the JWT validation in Program.cs (255-271) accepts a single symmetric key, so rotating it would instantly invalidate every outstanding token with no overlap window. Long-lived, never-rotated signing keys are a standard enterprise finding — if the key ever leaks (it is materialized into the container's .env on the host, remote-deploy.sh:40-54), an attacker can forge JWTs for any user indefinitely, and there is no operational path to cut over cleanly.

Independent verifier — confirmation
deploy/provision.sh:11 "# 1. App secrets live in SSM Parameter Store (encrypted). Generate once; never rotate on re-run."; provision.sh:14-15 "if aws ssm get-parameter ... >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo \"    $name already set — keeping it\""; provision.sh:24-25 ensure_secret /usage-iq/jwt-key + /usage-iq/db-password; src/Api/Program.cs:271 "IssuerSigningKey = new SymmetricSecurityKey(Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(jwtKey))" with a single ValidAlgorithms HmacSha256 (no kid/key-ring); remote-deploy.sh:43 "JWT_KEY=$JWT" written into host .env.

Verified all cited files. ensure_secret explicitly generates each secret once and keeps it on re-run ("never rotate on re-run"), covering both jwt-key and db-password. Program.cs validates a single symmetric HMAC key with no key-id/keyring resolver, so any rotation instantly invalidates all outstanding tokens with no overlap window. remote-deploy.sh materializes the JWT into a host .env. There is no rotation runbook, schedule, or versioning anywhere in the flow. Real long-lived-key hardening gap. Medium is correct: mitigating factors (SSM SecureString encryption at rest, fail-fast on weak/missing key at Program.cs:255-258) mean this is an operational-maturity/blast-radius finding, not an active vuln — so it does not warrant High. Line anchored at provision.sh:11 where the design decision is stated.

Remediation (approach)

Establish a secret-rotation process for the JWT key and DB password (documented runbook plus, ideally, automation). Support key rollover for JWT validation (accept the previous key during an overlap window / key-id-based selection) so rotation does not force a mass logout, and rotate the DB credential in coordination with the connection string. Add periodic rotation rather than generate-once.

Medium Observability & Operations Observability & ops readiness CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Entire wearable/health-sync action surface (/api/health/connect, /sync-now, /disconnect) is silently excluded from the RequestLog audit trail

src/Api/Infrastructure/RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:25-28 Structured logging coverage on critical paths
Evidence
private static readonly string[] Excluded =
{
    "/api/health", "/api/auth/me", "/api/auth/config", "/api/sync/status", "/api/presence", "/api/logs", "/api/share",
    ...
};
// ShouldLog: foreach (var ex in Excluded) if (req.Path.StartsWithSegments(ex)) return false;
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The exclusion for the anonymous DB-free liveness probe "/api/health" is matched with StartsWithSegments, which also matches the wearable Health-Sync group mapped at /api/health (HealthEndpoints.cs:71): /api/health/connect (OAuth code exchange that establishes access to a user's Fitbit health data), /api/health/sync-now, /api/health/disconnect, and the toggle endpoints.
