Correctly located, correctly rated Low, and not mitigated elsewhere in the flow. Impact is a cosmetic/data-integrity papercut in exported CSV, not a security or system-of-record issue, hence Low. Remediation: add a check for '\r' (or match any of \r, \n, comma, quote) to the quoting predicate.

Remediation (approach)

Extend the quoting predicate to also trigger on '\r' (and ideally any control character), matching RFC 4180. Consider also neutralizing spreadsheet formula-injection prefixes (=,+,-,@) on the free-text name columns since the CSV is opened in Excel.

Low Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

OptimisticFamily is a self-admitted hand-copy of OptimisticTracker's bump-then-reconcile pattern

src/Web/src/app/features/family-beta/state/optimistic-family.ts:8-19 Frontend architecture & duplication
Evidence
 * It mirrors the bump-then-reconcile shape of tracker-beta's `OptimisticTracker` (patch the local
 * signal first so the count moves sub-second, fire the API, reconcile from the server response, roll back
 * + offer Retry on failure) but is a SELF-CONTAINED copy — it injects only the root {@link Api} and
 * MatSnackBar and imports NO live-page internals.
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The optimistic-write contract (patch local signal -> firstValueFrom(api) -> reconcile-or-rollback+Retry-toast) is duplicated between tracker-beta's OptimisticTracker and family-beta's OptimisticFamily by explicit copy rather than a shared abstraction. Each new optimistic surface re-copies the same error-handling and retry-toast logic, so a fix to the reconcile/rollback semantics (e.g. handling the offline-queue's 202 optimistic-ack, or a race between rollback and a late server response) must be applied in every copy and will drift. The 'imports NO live-page internals' note shows the author consciously chose copy-paste over extraction.

Independent verifier — confirmation
optimistic-family.ts:8-13: "It mirrors the bump-then-reconcile shape of tracker-beta's `OptimisticTracker` (patch the local signal first ... fire the API, reconcile ..., roll back + offer Retry on failure) but is a SELF-CONTAINED copy". Confirmed against optimistic-tracker.ts:400-404 rollback()/snack.open(...,'Retry').onAction() vs optimistic-family.ts:85-88 fail() which duplicates the same snackbar-Retry pattern.

Both files read directly. The docstring (lines 8-19) self-declares OptimisticFamily as a SELF-CONTAINED copy of OptimisticTracker's optimistic-write contract, and the code confirms it: the fire→reconcile/rollback+Retry-toast shape (each catch block calls rollback() then this.fail(msg, retry)) and the fail() snackbar-Retry helper (lines 85-88) duplicate OptimisticTracker's rollback() (lines 400-404). This is genuine cross-feature duplication of the error-handling/retry contract; a change to reconcile/rollback semantics must be applied in both copies and will drift. It is a maintainability/duplication finding with no correctness, security, or data-integrity impact — Low severity is correct. Confirmed as a real, accurately-located, appropriately-severity architectural observation. Remediation (prose): extract the shared patch→firstValueFrom→reconcile-or-rollback+Retry-toast primitive into a common beta-ui/core helper that both OptimisticTracker and OptimisticFamily depend on, so reconcile/rollback fixes live in one place.

Remediation (approach)

Extract a generic optimistic-mutation helper (a small function/service taking patch, apiCall, reconcile, rollback, and a retry-toast presenter) into shared/ and have both OptimisticTracker and OptimisticFamily and any future optimistic surface call it. The domain-specific methods stay thin wrappers that supply the Api call and the signal patch.

Low Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Insights domain metadata (KIND_META blurbs/labels) is copy-pasted verbatim between the desktop and mobile twins and can silently drift

src/Web/src/app/features/insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:451-461 Frontend architecture & duplication
Evidence
  private static readonly KIND_META: Record<InsightKind, { label: string; icon: string; blurb: string }> = {
    ... blurb: 'Paired-day associations across domains (≥10 days). Association, not causation.' },
    ... blurb: 'Where a metric is drifting — with a bounded estimate, not a prediction.' },
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The mobile KIND_META (insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:451-461) and the desktop GROUP_META (insights/insights.page.ts:402-412) carry byte-identical blurb strings, including the load-bearing compliance disclaimers 'Association, not causation' and 'a bounded estimate, not a prediction' and the '(≥10 days)' / '|z| ≥ 2' thresholds. These are health/insight honesty statements. Because they live in two class-static tables, an edit to the desktop disclaimer (e.g. a legal/medical wording change) will not reach the mobile twin, so the two platforms can present different compliance language for the same feature. Prior rounds noted the helpers are copy-pasted; the specific risk here is divergence of user-facing medical-adjacent disclaimer text.

Independent verifier — confirmation
Mobile insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:451-461 KIND_META: correlation blurb: 'Paired-day associations across domains (≥10 days). Association, not causation.'; trend blurb: 'Where a metric is drifting — with a bounded estimate, not a prediction.'; anomaly blurb: 'Statistical outlier days (|z| ≥ 2).' — byte-identical to desktop insights/insights.page.ts:402-413 GROUP_META (correlation blurb '...Association, not causation.'; trend '...a bounded estimate, not a prediction.'; anomaly 'Statistical outlier days (|z| ≥ 2).').

Verified both files at cited lines. The disclaimer/threshold blurb strings are truly duplicated verbatim across two independent class-static tables (mobile KIND_META vs desktop GROUP_META) with no shared constant, so a legal/medical wording change to one platform silently will not reach the other, producing divergent compliance language for the same feature. Finding is real, correctly located, and severity Low is appropriate (a drift/maintainability risk, not a current runtime defect — the strings presently match). Remediation: extract the shared label/icon/blurb metadata into a single common module imported by both twins so the honesty/disclaimer text has one source of truth.

Remediation (approach)

Move the InsightKind metadata (label, icon, blurb, color, order) into a single shared constant in core/ or a shared insights module and have both the desktop and mobile pages import it, so the disclaimers and thresholds are authored once.

Low Architecture & Maintainability Backend architecture & debt CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Two incompatible error envelopes across endpoints — RFC7807 ProblemDetails vs ad-hoc { message } JSON

src/Api/Endpoints/BillEndpoints.cs:220-233 API contract inconsistency (error shapes)
Evidence
if (!gemini.IsConfigured)
    return Results.Problem(
        title: "AI assistance is not configured.",
        detail: "AI assistance is not configured.",
        statusCode: StatusCodes.Status503ServiceUnavailable);
...
    ? Results.Problem(
        title: "AI receipt read unavailable, enter manually.", ... )
    : Results.Ok(result);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Across the endpoint layer, error bodies come in at least two mutually incompatible shapes: RFC7807 ProblemDetails via Results.Problem (fields title/detail/status — 12 files) and ad-hoc anonymous { message } JSON via Results.Json/Results.BadRequest/Results.Conflict (39+ files). A single feature (e.g. Bills receipt-AI) even mixes both: validation errors return { message } while the 503 returns ProblemDetails. A frontend/consumer cannot parse errors uniformly — it must special-case both envelopes per route, and a missed case surfaces a blank/wrong error to the user. This is a maintainability and integration-contract defect for an app that is meant to be an enterprise product surface.

Independent verifier — confirmation
BillEndpoints.cs:220-234:
if (!gemini.IsConfigured)
    return Results.Problem(
        title: "AI assistance is not configured.",
        detail: "AI assistance is not configured.",
        statusCode: StatusCodes.Status503ServiceUnavailable);
...
return result is null
    ? Results.Problem(
        title: "AI receipt read unavailable, enter manually.", ...)
    : Results.Ok(result);
Meanwhile grep shows BillEndpoints.cs also has 4x `new { message = }` and the endpoint layer splits: Results.Problem in 12 files vs `new { message =}` in 39 files (343 occurrences).

Verified by reading BillEndpoints.cs lines 200-234: the ProblemDetails (RFC7807) 503 envelopes are exactly as quoted. Confirmed the app-wide inconsistency: Results.Problem appears in 12 endpoint files while ad-hoc `new { message = }` JSON appears across 39 files (343 total). BillEndpoints.cs itself mixes both (4 `new { message = }` plus these Results.Problem calls), so a single feature returns two incompatible error shapes — validation/400 as {message}, 503 as ProblemDetails. This is a genuine API-contract/maintainability defect forcing consumers to special-case per route. No guard/framework default normalizes these shapes. Severity Low is correct: no security/data impact, purely integration-contract consistency. Line corrected to 220 (start of the Results.Problem block).

Remediation (approach)

Standardize one error envelope for the whole API (either ProblemDetails everywhere via a shared helper, or the { message } shape everywhere) and route all handlers through that helper; add an analyzer/test asserting no handler returns the other shape. At minimum, make each feature module internally consistent so a client never sees two envelopes from the same endpoint.

Low Architecture & Maintainability Backend architecture & debt CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

WebPushSender fans out to ALL of a recipient's push subscriptions in a sequential awaited loop with no per-recipient subscription cap

src/Api/Services/WebPushSender.cs:143-173 High coupling / God class (perf second-order)
Evidence
foreach (var s in subs)
{
    try
    {
        var libSub = new LibPushSubscription(s.Endpoint, s.P256dh, s.Auth);
        await _client.SendNotificationAsync(libSub, payload, vapid, ct);
        nowSent.Add(s.Endpoint);
    }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

PushEndpoints /subscribe upserts by Endpoint (globally unique) but places no cap on how many DISTINCT endpoints one OwnerEmail may register — every browser/profile/device a user ever subscribes from is a new row, and dead rows are only pruned on a 404/410 from the push service. WebPushSender then loops over every subscription sequentially with an awaited network POST each. A user who has accumulated many stale subscriptions makes every notification to them slow (N sequential HTTP round-trips, each up to the WebPush client timeout), and this runs on the notification-dispatch path. It is a self-inflicted latency/resource issue that grows unbounded over a user's lifetime.

Independent verifier — confirmation
WebPushSender.cs:143-150
foreach (var s in subs)
{
    try
    {
        var libSub = new LibPushSubscription(s.Endpoint, s.P256dh, s.Auth);
        await _client.SendNotificationAsync(libSub, payload, vapid, ct);
        nowSent.Add(s.Endpoint);
    }
// PushEndpoints.cs:61-72 — upsert by globally-unique Endpoint, NO per-OwnerEmail cap:
var existing = await db.PushSubscriptions.FirstOrDefaultAsync(s => s.Endpoint == endpoint, ct);
if (existing is null) { db.PushSubscriptions.Add(new Data.Entities.PushSubscription { OwnerEmail = user.Email, ... }); }

Every factual claim verified against the code. PushEndpoints (lines 61-80) upserts subscriptions by globally-unique Endpoint with zero per-OwnerEmail cap; dead rows are pruned only on a 404/410 (WebPushSender lines 155-188); SendToUserAsync loops over ALL of a recipient's subscriptions sequentially with an awaited network POST each (lines 143-150). So an unbounded set of stale-but-not-yet-gone subscriptions does turn each notification into N sequential HTTP round-trips. The defect is real and correctly located. Impact is genuinely minor, matching the claimed Low: (a) this runs off the request path as a fire-and-forget background mirror (class docstring lines 13-14, 20-22) — it does NOT block the user's request or the in-app notification delivery; (b) it is self-pruning — any endpoint the push service reports gone is reaped on its very next send, so only stale-but-still-live endpoints accumulate; (c) per project memory this is a small personal/family app, so realistic devices-per-user is single-digit. A per-owner cap or parallelized/limited fan-out would be a reasonable hardening, but this is a Low-severity latency/resource nit, not a correctness or security defect. Confirmed at claimed Low severity.

Remediation (approach)

Cap the number of active push subscriptions per owner (evict the oldest / least-recently-used on subscribe), and fan the sends out concurrently with a bounded degree of parallelism instead of a strict sequential await, so one recipient with many stale endpoints cannot serialize the whole dispatch.

Low Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Two unit-conversion factor tables feeding health goal math have already drifted (LB_PER_KG and oz differ)

src/Web/src/app/features/tracker/units.ts:4 Duplication of health-critical math
Evidence
export const LB_PER_KG = 2.20462;   // core/units.ts declares 2.2046226; ML_PER_OZ=29.5735 vs core 1/0.0338140≈29.5744
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

core/units.ts documents that features/tracker/units.ts keeps its own independent factor table and 'the two factor tables can drift'. They already HAVE drifted: tracker's LB_PER_KG=2.20462 vs core's 2.2046226, and tracker's ML_PER_OZ=29.5735 vs core's implied 1/0.0338140≈29.5744. Both tables feed body-weight, hydration and calorie/macro display for the SAME health data, computed by computeStats() here which is explicitly required to stay 'numerically identical to' the backend. Divergent factors mean the weight shown on the desktop tracker vs. a core/UnitService-driven surface (or the imperial round-trip on save) can disagree by fractions of a pound/ounce, and each future edit to one table silently desyncs the two — a correctness liability for a page that also drives goal calorie math.

Independent verifier — confirmation
features/tracker/units.ts:4  `export const LB_PER_KG = 2.20462;` and :37 `export const ML_PER_OZ = 29.5735;`  vs  core/units.ts:29 `export const LB_PER_KG = 2.2046226;` and :37/:39 `FLOZ_PER_ML = 0.0338140` → `ML_PER_FLOZ = 1 / FLOZ_PER_ML` (≈29.5744). core/units.ts:11-13 documents: "the older tracker helpers ... keep their own factor table (e.g. their own LB_PER_KG=2.20462) ... so the two factor tables can drift."

The two factor tables genuinely have drifted exactly as claimed (LB_PER_KG 2.20462 vs 2.2046226; ML_PER_OZ 29.5735 vs core's ~29.5744), and core/units.ts explicitly warns about this. So the finding is factually real and a legitimate maintainability liability (silent desync on any future edit). However the claimed impact is overstated: (1) the weight drift is ~1.2e-6 relative (≈0.0002 lb at 200 lb) and the volume drift ~3e-5 relative (≈0.0008 oz at 750 ml), both erased by the display rounding actually used (formatWeight .toFixed(1), formatVolume/mlToOz Math.round) — no user-visible discrepancy or round-trip corruption occurs at real precision. (2) The 'numerically identical to backend' computeStats() function operates entirely in metric (kg/cm/kcal) and never references LB_PER_KG or ML_PER_OZ, so the drift does NOT touch calorie/macro/goal math at all — that part of the rationale is a misread. Real defect, correctly located, but a code-quality/consistency nit, not a Medium health-math correctness bug; severity reduced to Low. Remediation: have features/tracker/units.ts re-export LB_PER_KG and the oz factor from core/units.ts (single source of truth) instead of redeclaring literals, so the tables cannot diverge on future edits.

Remediation (approach)

Delete tracker/units.ts's private LB_PER_KG / ML_PER_OZ / M_PER_MI constants and re-export the conversion primitives from core/units.ts, keeping only the tracker-specific goal-math (computeStats, reshapeForDiet, ACTIVITY_FACTOR) local. One canonical factor table then feeds every surface, and the 'keep in sync by hand' hazard is removed.

Low Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

PageDef.home is declared and populated on ~35 pages but never read anywhere — dead metadata drifting from HOME_OPTIONS

src/Web/src/app/core/page-registry.ts:57 Dead code / page-registry correctness
Evidence
    /** Shown in the home-route picker when the caller can reach it. Label defaults to the nav label. */
    readonly home?: { readonly label: string; readonly icon?: string };
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Every registry page carries a `home: { label, icon }` object (e.g. dashboard, calendar, tracker…), documented as driving the home-route picker. But the picker and canAccessHome() are actually fed by the SEPARATE home-options.ts (HOME_OPTIONS) list; a search for reads of the PageDef.home field finds none in the app. So this is ~35 lines of populated-but-unread metadata that (a) misleads maintainers into editing the wrong list and (b) has already diverged from HOME_OPTIONS (e.g. registry marks /meds, /habits, /journal, /insights as home options while HOME_OPTIONS does not list them). Dead, drift-prone duplication of the home taxonomy.

Independent verifier — confirmation
page-registry.ts:56-57 declares `readonly home?: { readonly label: string; readonly icon?: string };` and it is populated on 35 pages (e.g. line 89 `home: { label: 'Dashboard', icon: 'space_dashboard' }`). Its only consumer is `toRoutes()` (line 457) which never reads `.home`. All home-picker logic instead reads the separate HOME_OPTIONS: app.ts:451 `return HOME_OPTIONS.filter(...)`, beta-settings.page.ts:609, preferences-mobile.page.ts:490, auth.ts:5 `import { HOME_PERMS }`. The lists have diverged — home-options.ts:27-68 omits /meds, /habits, /journal, /insights, /search, /wrapped that the registry marks as home.

Verified: PageDef.home is declared (lines 56-57) and populated on 35 registry entries but never read anywhere. The sole consumer of PAGE_REGISTRY, toRoutes(), ignores it; every home-route picker (app.ts, beta-settings, preferences-mobile) and canAccessHome/HOME_PERMS (auth.ts) are fed by the separate HOME_OPTIONS list in home-options.ts. The two taxonomies have already diverged (registry lists /meds,/habits,/journal,/insights,/search,/wrapped as home; HOME_OPTIONS does not; /ask perms also differ). The JSDoc on line 56 falsely claims it drives the picker, actively misleading maintainers. Dead, drift-prone duplication. No runtime, security, or PII impact — pure maintainability. Low severity confirmed; line corrected to 57 (the field declaration) though 56-57 range is accurate.

Remediation (approach)

Either delete the PageDef.home field and its per-page objects entirely (relying on HOME_OPTIONS as the single source), or invert the dependency so HOME_OPTIONS is DERIVED from PageDef.home — but not both. Whichever is kept must be the one the picker and canAccessHome() actually read, so there is exactly one home-route list.

Low Architecture & Maintainability Frontend architecture & duplication CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Insights domain metadata (KIND_META labels/blurbs) copy-pasted verbatim between desktop and mobile twins

src/Web/src/app/features/insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:451-462 Duplication between twins
Evidence
  private static readonly KIND_META: Record<InsightKind, { label: string; icon: string; blurb: string }> = {
    correlation: { label: 'Correlations', icon: 'sync_alt',
      blurb: 'Paired-day associations across domains (≥10 days). Association, not causation.' },
    ...
    bestworst: { label: 'Best & worst', icon: 'emoji_events',
      blurb: 'Your standout high & low days per metric.' },
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The InsightKind label/icon/blurb table and KIND_ORDER live as a private static inside the mobile page, hand-duplicated from the desktop insights page's equivalent metadata. The blurbs carry the deliberate 'Association, not causation' / non-medical framing on HEALTH insights; if the desktop copy is reworded for accuracy or compliance and the mobile copy is missed (or vice-versa), the two surfaces present different statistical/medical disclaimers for the same data — a silent, compliance-sensitive drift with no compile-time link between them.

Independent verifier — confirmation
Mobile src/Web/src/app/features/insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:451-453
  private static readonly KIND_META: Record<InsightKind, { label: string; icon: string; blurb: string }> = {
    correlation: { label: 'Correlations', icon: 'sync_alt',
      blurb: 'Paired-day associations across domains (≥10 days). Association, not causation.' },
Desktop src/Web/src/app/features/insights/insights.page.ts:402-404
  private static readonly GROUP_META: { kind: InsightKind; label: string; icon: string; blurb: string }[] = [
    { kind: 'correlation', label: 'Correlations', icon: 'sync_alt',
      blurb: 'Paired-day associations across domains (≥10 days). Association, not causation.' },

Verified by reading both files. Mobile KIND_META (insights-mobile/insights.page.ts:451-462) duplicates verbatim the label/icon/blurb strings held in desktop GROUP_META (insights/insights.page.ts:402-412), including the compliance-sensitive 'Association, not causation' and 'not a prediction' medical/statistical framing on the correlation/trend blurbs. No shared constant, module, or type links the two; each is a private static in its own component, so reword-one-miss-the-other drift is possible with zero compile-time signal. The desktop file explicitly calls out 'STATISTICAL HONESTY' as a deliberate invariant (line 40), making the divergence genuinely compliance-relevant. Location and Low severity (duplication/maintainability, not a live bug) are both correct. Finding confirmed.

Remediation (approach)

Move the InsightKind metadata (order + label/icon/blurb, and the KIND_COLORS if shared) into a small shared module under core/ or shared/ alongside the InsightKind type, and have both the desktop and mobile insights pages import it. This makes the disclaimer wording single-sourced so it cannot diverge between twins.

Low CI/CD & Supply Chain CI/CD pipelines & containers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Web (nginx) production image has no HEALTHCHECK, yet prod compose gates other startup on service health

src/Web/Dockerfile:9-13 Docker - no healthcheck / web serve image
Evidence
# --- serve ---
FROM nginx:alpine
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
COPY --from=build /app/dist/ccusage-web/browser /usr/share/nginx/html
EXPOSE 80
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The API image defines a HEALTHCHECK (src/Api/Dockerfile) and prod compose relies on health conditions (caddy depends_on web; web depends_on api service_healthy). The web image defines no HEALTHCHECK, so 'web' is considered up as soon as the container starts even if nginx failed to load config or the SPA assets are missing, and Caddy will start proxying to a not-actually-serving upstream. This can surface as user-facing 502s during rollout rather than a controlled, health-gated start.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/Dockerfile:9-13:
# --- serve ---
FROM nginx:alpine
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
COPY --from=build /app/dist/ccusage-web/browser /usr/share/nginx/html
EXPOSE 80
(no HEALTHCHECK). Contrast src/Api/Dockerfile:27-28: HEALTHCHECK --interval=15s ... CMD curl -fsS http://localhost:8080/api/health || exit 1. deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:43-48 web has no compose-level healthcheck and depends_on api service_healthy; caddy depends_on: - web (line 53-54).

Confirmed and correctly located: the nginx web image (src/Web/Dockerfile lines 9-13) defines no HEALTHCHECK, whereas the API image (src/Api/Dockerfile:27-28) does and even installs curl for it. The prod stack does health-gate elsewhere (api depends_on db service_healthy; web depends_on api service_healthy). The missing web healthcheck means the web container is considered 'up' the instant it starts, so a bad nginx.conf or missing SPA assets would not be caught by container health. Impact is genuinely Low, and the finding's specific rollout-502 mechanism is somewhat overstated: caddy's dependency on web (docker-compose.prod.yml:53-54) uses short-form `depends_on: - web`, i.e. service_started, so even if web HAD a healthcheck Caddy would not gate on it — the gating would only take effect if both a web HEALTHCHECK and a `condition: service_healthy` on caddy were added. Also nginx serves 404 (not 502) for missing assets, and `restart: unless-stopped` plus nginx failing to start on bad config mitigate partially. Still a legitimate consistency/observability gap worth a Low. Severity Low is correct.

Remediation (approach)

Add a HEALTHCHECK to the web image that probes a real served endpoint (e.g. the SPA index or an nginx status/health location) so orchestration only treats the container as healthy once nginx is actually serving, and dependent services wait on it.

Low CI/CD & Supply Chain CI/CD pipelines & containers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

All GitHub Actions (including AWS OIDC/credential actions) are pinned to mutable major-version tags, not commit SHAs

.github/workflows/deploy.yml:20-29 CI/CD - un-pinned actions (tags vs SHA)
Evidence
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
...
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
...
      - name: Log in to Amazon ECR
        uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v2
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Every action in deploy.yml and ci.yml is referenced by a floating tag (@v4, @v2). Tags are mutable: if any of these third-party action repos (notably aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials, which mints AWS credentials via OIDC in a workflow with id-token: write) is compromised and the tag is re-pointed, the next Deploy run silently executes attacker-controlled code with the ability to assume the production AWS deploy role. Supply-chain hardening standards for privileged pipelines require immutable pinning.

Independent verifier — confirmation
.github/workflows/deploy.yml:8-9 permissions: id-token: write   # OIDC; :20 - uses: actions/checkout@v4; :23 uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4; :25 role-to-assume: ${{ vars.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}; :29 uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v2

Verified by reading both workflow files. Every action is pinned to a mutable major-version tag (@v4/@v2), never a commit SHA. Critically, deploy.yml grants id-token: write (line 9) and uses aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4 (line 23) to assume the production AWS deploy role (line 25), then aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v2 (line 29). ci.yml likewise uses @v4 tags. If any upstream action repo/tag is re-pointed to malicious code, the next Deploy run executes it with the ability to mint prod AWS credentials — a legitimate supply-chain hardening gap for a privileged pipeline. Evidence matches the cited lines exactly. Low severity is correct: exploitation requires upstream compromise of reputable/AWS-published actions; it is a hardening recommendation (pin to full-length commit SHAs) rather than an active exploitable defect. Line/file/severity all accurate.

Remediation (approach)

Pin every third-party action to a full commit SHA (optionally with the version as a trailing comment) and adopt a tool/process (e.g. Dependabot or a pinning action) to update those SHAs deliberately. Prioritize the AWS credential/ECR actions and checkout since they run in the privileged, OIDC-enabled deploy workflow.

Low CI/CD & Supply Chain CI/CD pipelines & containers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Schema migrations auto-apply at API startup on every deploy with no gate, backup, or ordering barrier

deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:20-25 Deploy correctness / migrations
Evidence
api:
    image: ${ECR}/usage-iq-api:latest
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The API calls db.Database.MigrateAsync() unconditionally at boot (src/Api/Program.cs:410). Combined with `docker compose up -d` on every push-to-main and the mutable :latest tag (no test gate, per the known findings), any destructive or slow EF migration is applied to the single production Postgres automatically the instant the new API container starts — before any human verification and with the data living only in a local volume with no snapshot (known finding). A bad migration on health/payment tables is effectively unrecoverable here, and `restart: unless-stopped` will re-run a partially-failed migration on crash-loop.

Independent verifier — confirmation
deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:20-25:
  api:
    image: ${ECR}/usage-iq-api:latest
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
--- src/Api/Program.cs:410: `await db.Database.MigrateAsync();` (unconditional, no gate)
--- .github/workflows/deploy.yml:4-5 `push: branches: [main]` + remote-deploy.sh:60-61 `docker compose ... pull` / `up -d`
--- docker-compose.prod.yml:12-13 `volumes: - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data` (single local volume, no snapshot step anywhere in deploy path)

All claimed facts verified against the actual code. Program.cs:410 calls MigrateAsync() unconditionally at API startup with no environment gate, feature flag, or manual approval. deploy.yml triggers on every push to main, rebuilds the mutable :latest tag, and runs remote-deploy.sh which does `docker compose pull` + `up -d` (lines 60-61) — so a new API container starts and applies EF migrations against production the instant it boots, before any human verification. Postgres data lives only in a local named volume `pgdata` (compose lines 12-13) with no snapshot/backup anywhere in the deploy scripts. `restart: unless-stopped` (line 22) means a container that crash-loops after a bad/partial migration will re-invoke MigrateAsync on each restart. Partial mitigation exists (EF applies each migration in its own transaction on Postgres, so a mid-migration failure rolls that step back), but the core defect — automatic, ungated, un-backed-up schema application to the single prod DB on every push — is real and correctly located. Low severity is appropriate and conservative: it is an operational/recoverability risk, not a guaranteed exploit or data loss. Reasonable remediation: gate migrations behind an explicit run-migrations step or startup flag, take an automated DB snapshot before migrating, and pin/verify images (immutable tag + test gate) rather than mutable :latest.

Remediation (approach)

Run migrations as a discrete, gated deploy step (a one-shot job that must succeed before the API rolls out), take a database snapshot immediately before applying, and stop coupling schema changes to container auto-restart so a failed migration cannot loop against production data.

Low CI/CD & Supply Chain CI/CD pipelines & containers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Decrypted secrets are written to a persistent .env file and passed as container environment on a shared root host

deploy/remote-deploy.sh:40-61 Secret handling on host
Evidence
umask 077
cat > .env <<EOF
ECR=$ECR
DB_PASSWORD=$DBPW
JWT_KEY=$JWT
...
GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET=$GOOGLE_SECRET
...
EOF
...
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml --env-file .env up -d
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

All plaintext secrets (JWT signing key, DB password, Google client secret, provider keys) are decrypted from SSM and persisted to /opt/usage-iq/.env on disk and injected as container environment variables. umask 077 protects the file mode, but the file lingers between deploys, and env vars are readable via `docker inspect` / /proc to anyone with root/docker on the box (and every deploy runs as root via SSM). The JWT key never rotates (known finding), so a single host read of this file yields a forge-any-user token indefinitely. The persistence and env-var exposure amplify the no-rotation finding into a durable at-rest exposure.

Independent verifier — confirmation
umask 077
cat > .env <<EOF
ECR=$ECR
DB_PASSWORD=$DBPW
JWT_KEY=$JWT
...
GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET=$GOOGLE_SECRET
...
EOF
...
docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml --env-file .env up -d --remove-orphans   (deploy/remote-deploy.sh lines 39-61)

Verified lines 15-61. Decrypted SSM secrets (JWT_KEY, DB_PASSWORD, GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET, provider keys) are written to a persistent /opt/usage-iq/.env (cat > .env, line 40) and injected as container environment via --env-file (lines 60-61). The file is never deleted after deploy, so it lingers at rest; env vars are readable via docker inspect and /proc to anyone with root/docker on the host. umask 077 (line 39) correctly restricts the file mode to owner-only, which is why this is genuinely Low, not higher — it does mitigate the primary file-read vector for non-root local users. Residual risk is durable at-rest exposure to root/docker principals, amplifying the no-rotation JWT finding. The claim is accurate, correctly located, and not fully mitigated. Severity Low is appropriate.

Remediation (approach)

Avoid persisting decrypted secrets to disk (feed them to compose via a transient mechanism or docker/compose secrets mounted as files, not environment), remove/shred the .env after `up`, and prefer file-based secret injection over environment variables so they are not visible in docker inspect/proc.

Low CI/CD & Supply Chain CI/CD pipelines & containers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Caddy (the internet-facing TLS terminator) only 'depends_on: web' with no health condition and web has no healthcheck, so Caddy can start proxying before the SPA is ready

deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:53-54 Docker / compose: health-gating correctness
Evidence
  caddy:
    image: caddy:2-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      - web
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

web hard-depends on api service_healthy, but caddy — the only component exposed on 80/443 — uses the plain (start-order-only) depends_on form against web, and the web/nginx image defines no HEALTHCHECK. During a rollout, Caddy therefore begins serving and can request/renew Let's Encrypt certs and proxy to web:80 the instant the web container process starts, before nginx has actually loaded config or web's own api dependency is truly serving. The result is user-visible 502s during every deploy window and unnecessary ACME churn. This compounds the already-reported 'web has no healthcheck' and 'api liveness is DB-free' gaps: the outermost hop has the weakest readiness contract of the whole chain.

Independent verifier — confirmation
deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:50-54
  caddy:
    image: caddy:2-alpine
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      - web
--- web has no healthcheck (lines 43-48) and src/Web/Dockerfile:13 defines only `EXPOSE 80` with no HEALTHCHECK; Caddyfile:8 `reverse_proxy web:80`.

Structural claim is accurate: caddy (the only service on 80/443, lines 55-57) uses the plain start-order-only `depends_on: - web` (lines 53-54), while web itself gates on `api: service_healthy` (lines 47-48) and defines no healthcheck; the Web Dockerfile (src/Web/Dockerfile:13) has no HEALTHCHECK. So caddy can start proxying to web:80 the moment the container process starts, before nginx binds — producing transient 502s during the deploy window. Correctly located and impactful, hence CONFIRMED. Severity Low is right: it is a brief rollout-window annoyance mitigated in practice by Caddy's dial retries and near-instant nginx static startup, not a persistent outage or security defect. One part of the stated rationale is inaccurate and should not be relied on: Caddy's Let's Encrypt cert acquisition/renewal is driven by ACME challenges and the caddy_data cert cache, independent of the web upstream, so this gap does NOT cause 'unnecessary ACME churn'. The remediation (add a HEALTHCHECK to the nginx web image and gate caddy with `depends_on: web: condition: service_healthy`) is valid.

Remediation (approach)

Give the nginx web image a real HEALTHCHECK (e.g. an internal readiness endpoint) and change caddy's dependency to the long-form depends_on with condition: service_healthy on web, so TLS/proxy only comes up after the SPA is actually serving. Ensure the readiness signal reflects nginx being able to reach the api upstream, not just the process being alive.

Low Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

MachineGeoBackfill OrderBy(GeoUpdatedUtc) claims 'nulls first' but Postgres sorts NULLS LAST ascending — never-resolved machines can be starved

src/Api/Services/MachineGeoBackfillService.cs:61-63 Query correctness / starvation ordering
Evidence
.Where(m => m.PublicIp != null && m.PublicIp != ""
            && m.GeoSource != "agent"
            && (m.GeoUpdatedUtc == null || m.GeoUpdatedUtc < staleBefore))
    .OrderBy(m => m.GeoUpdatedUtc) // nulls first, then oldest
    .Take(BatchSize)
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The comment asserts 'nulls first, then oldest', but PostgreSQL orders NULLs LAST in an ascending sort by default. Rows with GeoUpdatedUtc == null are brand-new machines that have never been geo-resolved; with NULLS LAST they sort AFTER every stale-but-resolved row. If the backlog of rows matching the WHERE clause ever exceeds BatchSize (20), the newly-added, never-resolved machines are pushed to the tail and can be starved indefinitely while older resolved rows churn — so a new desktop's Fleet map location stays blank longer than intended. Impact is limited (batch is small, re-resolution needs the 7-day staleness window), but the code does not do what its comment claims.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Services/MachineGeoBackfillService.cs:60-62
                        && (m.GeoUpdatedUtc == null || m.GeoUpdatedUtc < staleBefore))
            .OrderBy(m => m.GeoUpdatedUtc) // nulls first, then oldest
            .Take(BatchSize)  — provider is Npgsql (Ccusage.Api.csproj:14 Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL; Program.cs:34 UseNpgsql)

Verified: the DbContext runs on PostgreSQL via Npgsql (Ccusage.Api.csproj line 14; Program.cs UseNpgsql). EF Core's Npgsql provider translates .OrderBy(m => m.GeoUpdatedUtc) to `ORDER BY geo_updated_utc ASC`, and PostgreSQL's default for ascending sorts is NULLS LAST — the exact opposite of the inline comment "nulls first, then oldest". So brand-new machines (GeoUpdatedUtc == null, never geo-resolved) actually sort AFTER every stale-but-resolved row, not before. The comment/code mismatch is real and correctly located. The impact is legitimately Low: because processing a stale row stamps GeoUpdatedUtc = now (line 81), it leaves the candidate set and cannot re-enter for 7 days, so the stale-resolved backlog drains rather than replenishing — nulls are delayed until that finite backlog clears, not starved "indefinitely" as the write-up implies. Bounded delay for a new desktop's Fleet map dot, batch is only 20 every 5 min. Confirming the defect (wrong ordering vs. documented intent) at Low severity; the "indefinite starvation" framing is somewhat overstated but the underlying correctness/comment bug is genuine.

Remediation (approach)

Order with NULLS FIRST explicitly (EF Core Npgsql supports an ordering that emits NULLS FIRST, or order by a computed 'GeoUpdatedUtc IS NULL' descending then by GeoUpdatedUtc) so never-resolved machines are prioritized as the comment intends. Alternatively split into two passes (unresolved first, then stale) to guarantee new machines are always picked up.

Low Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

DiscordForwarder per-user TokenBucket dictionary is never evicted — grows with distinct recipients for the process lifetime

src/Api/Services/DiscordForwarder.cs:85 Unbounded resource growth (singleton state)
Evidence
private readonly ConcurrentDictionary<string, TokenBucket> _buckets = new(StringComparer.Ordinal);
...
var bucket = _buckets.GetOrAdd(item.RecipientEmail, _ => new TokenBucket(BucketCapacity, RefillSeconds));
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

DiscordForwarder is a singleton hosted service; its _buckets dictionary gains one entry per distinct recipient email that ever has a Discord forward enqueued and never removes them. In a long-running process serving many users this is a slow, monotonic memory leak. It is bounded by the total user population so it is not catastrophic, but there is no reclamation of idle buckets, which is below enterprise standard for an unbounded-key cache on a process-lifetime singleton.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Services/DiscordForwarder.cs:55  private readonly ConcurrentDictionary<string, TokenBucket> _buckets = new(StringComparer.Ordinal);
src/Api/Services/DiscordForwarder.cs:85  var bucket = _buckets.GetOrAdd(item.RecipientEmail, _ => new TokenBucket(BucketCapacity, RefillSeconds));

Confirmed. DiscordForwarder is a singleton BackgroundService (line 40-41). Its _buckets ConcurrentDictionary (line 55) is populated only via GetOrAdd keyed on RecipientEmail (line 85). I read the entire file (1-182): there is no TryRemove, no expiry sweep, no idle reclamation anywhere. Each distinct recipient that ever has a forward enqueued leaves a permanent TokenBucket entry for the process lifetime — a slow monotonic leak. It is bounded by the total distinct-user population and each entry is tiny (a double, a DateTime, a lock), so it is not catastrophic; Low severity is appropriate. The bounded Channel (line 45-50) caps the queue but not this per-key map. Remediation (prose): give each bucket a last-access timestamp and periodically sweep/TryRemove buckets idle beyond a threshold (e.g., a timer or opportunistic prune), or cap the dictionary size with LRU eviction, so idle recipients' buckets are reclaimed.

Remediation (approach)

Periodically evict buckets that are full (i.e. fully refilled and idle beyond some TTL), or store a last-touched timestamp and prune stale entries on a low-frequency sweep, so the dictionary size tracks active senders rather than the all-time distinct recipient set.

Low Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

RequestLogWriter/AiUsageLogWriter Task.Delay in the catch-all is unguarded — shutdown cancellation escapes as an uncaught OperationCanceledException

src/Api/Infrastructure/RequestLogWriter.cs:56-58 Unobserved-exception / shutdown handling
Evidence
catch (Exception ex)
{
    logger.LogError(ex, "RequestLogWriter failed to persist a batch; dropping it.");
    await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(2), stoppingToken);
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

After a batch-persist failure the generic catch backs off with Task.Delay(2s, stoppingToken). If the host begins shutting down during that delay, the delay throws OperationCanceledException, which is NOT caught by this block (the OCE-specific catch is a separate, earlier clause) and propagates out of ExecuteAsync. That surfaces as a faulted hosted service during shutdown and, more importantly, if the failure recurs while healthy, an unexpected non-OCE state could interact poorly with the loop. The same pattern exists in AiUsageLogWriter.cs (lines 53-57). Low impact (occurs only at shutdown) but it is a latent unobserved-cancellation path in a long-lived worker.

Independent verifier — confirmation
RequestLogWriter.cs:49-57
            catch (OperationCanceledException) when (stoppingToken.IsCancellationRequested)
            {
                break;
            }
            catch (Exception ex)
            {
                logger.LogError(ex, "RequestLogWriter failed to persist a batch; dropping it.");
                await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(2), stoppingToken);
            }

Verified in both files. The catch-all (line 53-57) awaits Task.Delay(2s, stoppingToken). If the host begins shutdown during the backoff, Task.Delay throws OperationCanceledException. C# catch clauses are evaluated top-down against the exception from the try body only; an exception thrown *inside* the later catch block does not get routed to the earlier OCE-specific clause (line 49) — it propagates out of ExecuteAsync. So the finding's mechanism and location are correct. The same pattern is confirmed in AiUsageLogWriter.cs line 56. Impact is genuinely Low: this only triggers when (a) a batch persist fails AND (b) shutdown lands within the 2s window; and in .NET the BackgroundService's faulted Task during shutdown is observed by the host at StopAsync rather than crashing a healthy process (StopHost behavior applies to faults while running, and the token is already cancelled here). Real but minor latent unobserved-cancellation path in a long-lived worker. Recommend widening the OCE handling to also short-circuit on cancellation during the backoff delay (e.g., swallow/break when the stopping token is cancelled) rather than letting it escape.

Remediation (approach)

Wrap the back-off delay so cancellation during shutdown is treated as a clean exit (catch the OperationCanceledException around the delay and break, or check stoppingToken.IsCancellationRequested before delaying), mirroring how the main WaitToReadAsync path already handles cancellation.

Low Correctness Endpoints correctness & validation CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Anonymous bill-item claim is a check-then-act with no concurrency guard (lost update)

src/Api/Endpoints/BillEndpoints.cs:304-313 Endpoints correctness & validation
Evidence
var item = bill.Items.FirstOrDefault(i => i.Id == req.ItemId);
if (item is null) return Results.NotFound();
// Only an OPEN item may be claimed (not assigned, not already claimed).
if (item.AssignedToUserId is not null || item.ClaimedByName is not null || item.ClaimedByUserId is not null)
    return Results.Json(new { message = "That item has already been claimed." },
        statusCode: StatusCodes.Status409Conflict);

item.ClaimedByName = name;
item.ClaimedUtc = DateTime.UtcNow;
await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

This is a public, AllowAnonymous, rate-limited claim surface. The 'already claimed' guard reads the item state, then writes ClaimedByName in a separate SaveChangesAsync with no wrapping transaction, no unique/partial index on the claim columns, and no optimistic-concurrency token (BillItem has no RowVersion/xmin — confirmed in src/Api/Data/Entities/BillItem.cs). Two anonymous users who load the same open item and POST /claim near-simultaneously both pass the open-check, and the second SaveChanges silently overwrites the first person's ClaimedByName. The user who believes they claimed (and may pay) the item is quietly displaced, corrupting who-owes-what on a money-splitting bill. The sibling chore-claim path in FamilyMealsChoresEndpoints.cs:1085-1089 deliberately wraps its claim race in the execution-strategy transaction, so the mitigation pattern exists in the codebase but was not applied here.

Independent verifier — confirmation
BillEndpoints.cs:307-313:
if (item.AssignedToUserId is not null || item.ClaimedByName is not null || item.ClaimedByUserId is not null)
    return Results.Json(new { message = "That item has already been claimed." }, statusCode: StatusCodes.Status409Conflict);
item.ClaimedByName = name;
item.ClaimedUtc = DateTime.UtcNow;
await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
--- BillItem.cs (entire entity, no RowVersion/concurrency token; ClaimedByName is plain string?, ClaimedUtc plain DateTime?)

Verified by reading the cited lines. The claim endpoint reads item state, checks the open-guard in memory, then issues an unconditional EF Core UPDATE via a single SaveChangesAsync. BillItem.cs (read in full) has no RowVersion/concurrency token and there is no wrapping transaction or DB-level unique/partial index on the claim columns. So two anonymous callers who both load an open item and POST /claim concurrently both pass the in-memory guard, and the second SaveChanges overwrites the first's ClaimedByName — a classic lost update, silently displacing the first claimer on a money-splitting bill. The endpoint is AllowAnonymous + rate-limited (public surface), which matches the claim. The codebase demonstrably knows the correct pattern: FamilyMealsChoresEndpoints.cs:1086-1101 uses CreateExecutionStrategy + BeginTransaction + a conditional ExecuteUpdateAsync gated on the prior status so exactly one concurrent writer wins — that mitigation was not applied here. Defect is real, correctly located, and impactful, but the impact is a cosmetic display-name overwrite (no direct money movement or auth/PII exposure) and the window is narrow, so Low severity is appropriate.

Remediation (approach)

Make the claim atomic. Either add a database-level guard (a filtered/partial unique index or a conditional UPDATE that only sets the claim columns WHERE they are currently null, then treat a zero-row result as the 409) so the second writer loses deterministically, or add an optimistic-concurrency token to BillItem and re-check/return 409 on DbUpdateConcurrencyException. Wrap the read-check-write in the retrying execution strategy's transaction as the chore-claim endpoint already does. Describe-only: do not rely on the in-memory open-check alone.

Low Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

FamilyReminderService reminder/timer loops lack per-item try/catch: one NotifyFamily failure aborts the rest of the tick and permanently loses the already-stamped occurrence's notification

src/Api/Services/FamilyReminderService.cs:343-366 Services & background workers correctness
Evidence
foreach (var r in due)
{
    // Persist the state advance FIRST (so a crash/retry never re-pings the same occurrence), then notify.
    r.LastFiredUtc = now;
    ...
    await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);

    await notifier.NotifyFamily(
        new[] { r.TargetUserId },
        NotificationType.FamilyReminder,
        $"⏰ Reminder: {r.Text}",
        "/family/reminders",
        ct);
    fired++;
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The reminder-fire and timer-complete loops advance state (Active=false / DueUtc advanced / Done=true) and SaveChanges BEFORE calling NotifyFamily, but there is no per-iteration try/catch (unlike AgentScheduler and the briefing/heads-up sub-passes, which explicitly swallow per-item). If NotifyFamily throws for one recipient (e.g. a transient SignalR hub SendAsync failure or DB hiccup while writing the Notification rows), the exception propagates out of FireDueRemindersAsync/CompleteFinishedTimersAsync and is caught only by the tick-level handler in ExecuteAsync. Consequences: (a) the throwing reminder was already advanced/deactivated, so its notification is permanently lost (stamp-first with no compensation); (b) all remaining due reminders/timers in that batch are skipped this tick (they recover next tick since not yet advanced, but delivery is delayed). Given these drive user-set reminders and shared timers, a silent drop of a fired reminder is a correctness gap.

Independent verifier — confirmation
FamilyReminderService.cs:343-365 foreach (var r in due) { r.LastFiredUtc = now; ... await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct); await notifier.NotifyFamily(new[] { r.TargetUserId }, NotificationType.FamilyReminder, $"⏰ Reminder: {r.Text}", "/family/reminders", ct); fired++; } — no per-item try/catch, while sibling DeliverDueBriefingsAsync (lines 94-102) explicitly swallows per-household. NotifyFamily throws freely: ChatNotificationService.cs:318 await hub.Clients.User(email).SendAsync("ReceiveNotification", ...) with no internal try/catch.

Verified by reading the file. Both FireDueRemindersAsync (343-366) and CompleteFinishedTimersAsync (412-430) advance state (Active=false / DueUtc advanced / Done=true) and SaveChangesAsync BEFORE calling NotifyFamily, with no per-iteration try/catch. NotifyFamily (ChatNotificationService.cs:282-330) can throw: after committing the Notification rows it fans out hub.Clients.User(email).SendAsync(...) at line 318-320 with no internal exception handling, so a transient SignalR/DB failure propagates out. The exception unwinds the loop to the tick-level catch in ExecuteAsync (line 45), which only logs. Because the throwing reminder/timer was already advanced+persisted (stamp-first with no compensation), its notification is permanently lost, and remaining batch items are skipped this tick (they recover next tick since not advanced). Sibling sub-passes (DeliverDueBriefingsAsync 94-102, AnnounceUpcomingEventsAsync per its doc-comment) deliberately swallow per-item, confirming the inconsistency. Low severity is correct: narrow transient window, single silent drop, other items self-heal. Finding accurately located and impactful.

Remediation (approach)

Wrap each reminder/timer iteration in its own try/catch that logs and continues, mirroring the per-agent and per-household swallow pattern already used elsewhere in this file, so one recipient's notification failure neither aborts the batch nor silently loses an occurrence. Consider notifying before or transactionally with the state advance for reminders where a lost ping is worse than a rare double-ping.

Low Correctness Endpoints correctness & validation CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

75 Hard cheat-days: removals are committed before the add-cap validation, so a rejected request still mutates state

src/Api/Endpoints/HardChallengeEndpoints.cs:434-443 Broken error path / non-atomic partial write
Evidence
foreach (var dt in toRemove) { ... r.IsCheatDay = false; r.UpdatedUtc = now; }
await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);   // removals COMMITTED here

var existingFuture = await db.HardChallengeDays.Where(... IsCheatDay && LocalDate > today) ...
var resulting = new HashSet<DateOnly>(existingFuture);
foreach (var dt in toAdd) resulting.Add(dt);
if (resulting.Count > MaxCheatDays)
    return Results.BadRequest(new { message = $"At most {MaxCheatDays} cheat days may be declared." });
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The Remove branch persists its changes (SaveChangesAsync at line 434) BEFORE the MaxCheatDays cap on the Add set is evaluated (lines 442-443). A single request that both removes and adds — where the additions push the total over the cap — returns a 400 to the caller as if nothing happened, yet the removals have already been irreversibly committed. The client sees an error and its local state (still showing the removed days as cheat days) diverges from the server, and the user has silently lost pre-declared cheat days. The add validation and the removal commit are not in one transaction, so the error path leaves the row set half-applied.

Independent verifier — confirmation
// HardChallengeEndpoints.cs lines 426-443
foreach (var dt in toRemove)
{
    var r = await db.HardChallengeDays.FirstOrDefaultAsync(x => x.UserEmail == caller.Email && x.LocalDate == dt, ct);
    if (r is null) continue;
    r.IsCheatDay = false;
    r.UpdatedUtc = now;
}
await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);          // removals COMMITTED here

var existingFuture = await db.HardChallengeDays.Where(x => x.UserEmail == caller.Email && x.IsCheatDay && x.LocalDate > today).Select(x => x.LocalDate).ToListAsync(ct);
var resulting = new HashSet<DateOnly>(existingFuture);
foreach (var dt in toAdd) resulting.Add(dt);
if (resulting.Count > MaxCheatDays)     // validation runs AFTER the commit
    return Results.BadRequest(new { message = $"At most {MaxCheatDays} cheat days may be declared." });

Verified against the actual code. The Remove branch (426-433) mutates HardChallengeDay rows and line 434 SaveChangesAsync commits them BEFORE the MaxCheatDays=10 cap is evaluated at 442-443. A single /cheat-days request containing both Remove and Add, where the additions push the resulting future cheat-day set over 10, returns 400 to the caller yet the removals are already persisted. The two writes are not wrapped in a transaction, so the error path leaves state half-applied and the client (still showing the removed days) diverges from the server, silently losing pre-declared cheat days. Defect, file, and lines are all correct. Severity corrected Medium -> Low: it is self-scoped to the caller's own fitness data (no PII/health-record/security/payment corruption), only fires on an uncommon mixed remove+add request that crosses the cap, and the loss is user-recoverable by re-declaring. Anchor line set to 434 (the premature commit). Fix: reorder so the cap validation runs before any mutation, or wrap the remove and add in a single DB transaction so a rejected request rolls back the removals.

Remediation (approach)

Validate the resulting cheat-day count (existing future set, minus removals, plus additions) up front against MaxCheatDays and the window/future-date rules BEFORE writing anything, then apply removals and additions together in a single SaveChanges so any rejection leaves the persisted state untouched.

Low Correctness Frontend correctness CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

NotificationBell.surfaced Set grows unbounded for the lifetime of the app shell

src/Web/src/app/features/notifications/notification-bell.ts:95-113 Memory leak / unbounded growth
Evidence
private readonly surfaced = new Set<number>();
...
effect(() => {
  const live = this.chat.liveNotification();
  if (!live) return;
  const n = live.notification;
  if (this.surfaced.has(n.id)) return;
  this.surfaced.add(n.id);
  ...
});
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The bell is mounted by the always-on app shell for the entire authenticated session and never destroyed while signed in. Every distinct live notification id is added to `surfaced` and nothing is ever pruned, so on a long-lived tab (the app is a PWA meant to stay open for days) this Set grows without bound in proportion to lifetime notification volume. It is a slow leak, not a correctness bug — but on a heavy-notification account over a multi-day session it is real retained memory that only a reload clears.

Independent verifier — confirmation
notification-bell.ts:95 `private readonly surfaced = new Set<number>();` ... :106-107 `if (this.surfaced.has(n.id)) return; this.surfaced.add(n.id);` — no delete/clear/cap anywhere in the file (only .has and .add references exist).

Confirmed: the `surfaced` Set is a per-instance dedupe set that only ever grows — the sole mutation is `.add(n.id)` at line 107, with no prune, cap, TTL, or clear anywhere in the file. The bell is mounted by the always-on app shell for the entire authenticated session (per the class doc comment: "rendered by the app shell only when authed + chat.read") and is not destroyed while signed in, and this is a long-lived PWA tab. So on a heavy-notification account over a multi-day session the Set retains one number entry per distinct live notification id indefinitely. The finding is real and correctly located. Severity Low is correct and if anything generous: each entry is a JS number (a handful of bytes), so even tens of thousands of notifications is KB-to-low-MB scale, cleared by any reload — a slow, benign leak, not a correctness or DoS issue. A bounded structure (e.g. capped LRU or periodic prune keyed off the currently-loaded notification window) would remove it entirely.

Remediation (approach)

Cap the dedupe set (e.g. keep only the last N ids via an insertion-ordered structure or an LRU, evicting the oldest), or clear it on the same signals that reset chat state (logout/session teardown). A modest cap is safe because a re-surfaced very-old notification is not a realistic concern.

Low Correctness Frontend correctness CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

ChatRealtime.refreshChannels() unconditionally replaces the channel list, dropping optimistic ChannelAdded/unread updates that raced in

src/Web/src/app/core/chat-realtime.ts:456-465 Race conditions on rapid navigation
Evidence
async refreshChannels(): Promise<ChatChannelDto[]> {
  const list = (await firstValueFrom(this.api.chatChannels())) ?? [];
  this._channels.set(list);
  this._unread.update(map => {
    const next = { ...map };
    for (const ch of list) next[ch.id] = ch.unreadCount;
    return next;
  });
  for (const ch of list) void this.joinChannel(ch.id);
  return list;
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

refreshChannels() is invoked from several concurrent paths — start(), onreconnected() (which fires refreshChannels + refreshInbox), createChannel/openDirect indirectly, and the chat page's deep-link resolveDeepLink(). It does a hard _channels.set(list) from a REST snapshot taken at request time. If a hub ChannelAdded (onChannelAdded appends a brand-new channel and seeds its unread) arrives during the in-flight GET, the subsequent .set(list) overwrites it with a list that predates that channel, so the just-added conversation vanishes from the sidebar until the next refresh, and its unreadCount is lost. Two overlapping refreshChannels() calls likewise let the slower response win. It self-heals on the next event/refresh, so impact is transient UI flicker/missing-row rather than data loss.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/src/app/core/chat-realtime.ts:455-461
const list = (await firstValueFrom(this.api.chatChannels())) ?? [];
this._channels.set(list);
this._unread.update(map => {
  const next = { ...map };
  for (const ch of list) next[ch.id] = ch.unreadCount;
  return next;
});
// vs onChannelAdded (l.327-329): this._channels.update(list => list.some(...) ? list : [...list, channel])

Verified against the actual code. refreshChannels() unconditionally does _channels.set(list) from a REST snapshot captured at request start (line 455-456), while onChannelAdded (line 327-329) appends a hub-pushed channel and seeds its unread. If a ChannelAdded fires during the in-flight GET, the trailing .set(list) overwrites the optimistically-added row with a list that predates it, and its unreadCount is dropped from the _unread map (which is fully recomputed only from `list`). refreshChannels is genuinely invoked from concurrent paths (start() l.196, onreconnected() l.187, plus page-level deep-link resolution), so overlapping/racing calls are plausible. Two overlapping refreshChannels() also let the slower response win. Correctly located and correctly rated Low: purely a transient sidebar flicker / momentarily-missing row + lost unread badge that self-heals on the next hub event or refresh; no persistence, security, PII, or health-data impact. The `.set` on _channels (line 456) is the offending line, tightened from the range's start.

Remediation (approach)

Merge rather than replace: fold the server list into the existing _channels by id (upsert, preserving any channel not present in this snapshot only if you can prove it's stale — otherwise at least dedupe-merge), or guard refreshChannels with a request-generation token so only the latest snapshot applies and hub-appended channels since the request are re-reconciled.

Low Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Fitbit token/API reads use the request CancellationToken but the rotated-refresh-token persistence is not atomic with the mint, so a cancel between refresh and re-store loses the rotated token and bricks the connection

src/Api/Services/Health/FitbitHealthProvider.cs:330-339 Missing CancellationToken plumbing on the delivery path
Evidence
var rotated = GetStr(root, "refresh_token");
var now = DateTime.UtcNow;
if (!string.IsNullOrEmpty(rotated))
{
    var enc = protector.Protect(rotated);
    conn.EncryptedRefreshToken = enc;
    await db.HealthConnections.Where(c => c.Id == conn.Id)
        .ExecuteUpdateAsync(s => s
            .SetProperty(c => c.EncryptedRefreshToken, enc)
            .SetProperty(c => c.LastUsedUtc, now), ct);
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Fitbit invalidates the OLD refresh token the moment the refresh HTTP call succeeds (documented at the top of the file as the #1 correctness rule). The successful token response is already consumed, but the NEW refresh token is only durably stored in a SEPARATE ExecuteUpdateAsync that is passed the same request/stopping token. If cancellation fires (host shutdown during a HealthSyncScheduler tick, or the caller's ct is cancelled) after PostTokenAsync returned 200 but before/ during this ExecuteUpdateAsync, the rotated token is never persisted while the old one is already dead server-side. The next sync decrypts the stale old token, Fitbit returns invalid_grant (400), and the connection is permanently marked AuthExpired — surfacing to the user only as a spurious 'reconnect your Fitbit' with no transient cause. Silent health-data loss until manual reconnect.

Independent verifier — confirmation
// src/Api/Services/Health/FitbitHealthProvider.cs:324-333
var rotated = GetStr(root, "refresh_token");
var now = DateTime.UtcNow;
if (!string.IsNullOrEmpty(rotated))
{
    var enc = protector.Protect(rotated);
    conn.EncryptedRefreshToken = enc;
    await db.HealthConnections.Where(c => c.Id == conn.Id)
        .ExecuteUpdateAsync(s => s
            .SetProperty(c => c.EncryptedRefreshToken, enc)
            .SetProperty(c => c.LastUsedUtc, now), ct);
}

Confirmed and correctly located. PostTokenAsync (line 303) returns 200 with the rotated refresh_token already consumed by Fitbit's server (old token dead per the #1 correctness rule documented at lines 20 and 322-323). The NEW token is only durably written by a separate ExecuteUpdateAsync at lines 330-333 which is passed the same request/stopping ct. If cancellation fires (host shutdown mid-HealthSyncScheduler tick, or caller ct cancelled) after the 200 but before/during the ExecuteUpdateAsync, EF surfaces OperationCanceledException and the rotated token is never persisted while the old one is already invalidated server-side. Next sync decrypts the stale token, Fitbit returns 400 invalid_grant, and StampStatusAsync marks the connection AuthExpired (lines 307-310) — a permanent 'reconnect your Fitbit' with no transient cause and silent health-data loss. No mitigation exists: there is no transaction, no CancellationToken.None for the persistence write, and no idempotent re-store on the next attempt (the old token is gone). The race window is genuinely narrow (a few ms per successful refresh) and requires cancellation to land precisely in that gap, so Low severity is accurate. The fix in prose: perform the rotated-token persistence with a non-cancellable token (CancellationToken.None) so a successful mint always commits its rotated token durably, decoupling the durable write from the request/stopping token.

Remediation (approach)

Persist the rotated refresh token with a NON-cancellable token (e.g. CancellationToken.None) for just the re-store write, since the old token is already invalidated and the write must complete to keep the connection alive. More robustly, treat the token refresh + re-store as a single committed unit before returning the access token, and never abandon the re-store on cancellation once the refresh HTTP call has succeeded.

Low Correctness Services & background workers CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Weekly recap guard is not advanced when the user's preference row vanishes between send and re-load, allowing a duplicate Discord recap on the next tick

src/Api/Services/NotificationBackgroundService.cs:136-138 Idempotency / duplicate-fire under mid-loop deletion
Evidence
foreach (var email in candidates)
{
    if (!await recap.SendRecapAsync(email, from, to, today, ct)) continue;

    // Advance the guard ONLY on a confirmed send. Re-load the tracked row so the marker persists.
    var pref = await db.NotificationPreferences.FirstOrDefaultAsync(p => p.UserEmail == email, ct);
    if (pref is not null) { pref.LastRecapSent = today; await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct); }
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

SendRecapAsync posts to Discord and returns true on a confirmed 2xx, but the LastRecapSent guard is advanced in a SEPARATE re-load. If the NotificationPreference row is deleted or its email changes (account disable, email-privacy refactor, re-provision) between the send and the re-load, pref is null and the guard is never stamped. On the next minute-tick it is still Sunday >= RecapHourLocal and the candidate query (which reads the row again if it reappears, or a differently-cased email) can re-select and RE-SEND the same weekly recap. The idempotency here relies on the guard write succeeding, which is not guaranteed after the irreversible external send.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Services/NotificationBackgroundService.cs:133-137
    if (!await recap.SendRecapAsync(email, from, to, today, ct)) continue;
    // Advance the guard ONLY on a confirmed send. Re-load the tracked row so the marker persists.
    var pref = await db.NotificationPreferences.FirstOrDefaultAsync(p => p.UserEmail == email, ct);
    if (pref is not null) { pref.LastRecapSent = today; await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct); }

Confirmed as described. SendRecapAsync (WeeklyRecapComposer.cs:158-181) performs an irreversible Discord POST and returns true on a 2xx, but the LastRecapSent idempotency guard is stamped in a SEPARATE, non-atomic re-load (line 136). Because the re-load is guarded by `if (pref is not null)`, if the NotificationPreference row is deleted or its keyed UserEmail changes between the send and the re-load, the guard is silently never advanced after the external send has already gone out. For an actual DUPLICATE recap to fire, the row must then be re-selected by the candidate query (line 125-126: WeeklyRecapEnabled && DiscordWebhookEnc != null && LastRecapSent != today) on a subsequent minute-tick while it is still Sunday and Hour >= RecapHourLocal (9). A permanently-deleted row won't re-qualify, and emails are normalized lower-case (per LoadStatsAsync comment at line 237), so the practical trigger is narrow: the pref row reappearing/being re-provisioned the SAME Sunday with the same email. The idempotency correctly relies on the guard write succeeding, which is not guaranteed post-send — a genuine, correctly-located gap, but very low probability, matching the claimed Low severity. Fix (prose): make the send-and-guard atomic per user — e.g. load and track the pref row BEFORE sending and stamp/commit LastRecapSent in the same unit as (or transactionally around) the send, or pre-mark the guard optimistically and roll it back only on a confirmed non-send, so an irreversible Discord post can never leave the guard unadvanced.

Remediation (approach)

Advance the LastRecapSent guard BEFORE the send (stamp-first, matching the AgentScheduler/FamilyBriefing pattern) so a confirmed-but-unrecorded send can't repeat, accepting that a rare send failure after stamping simply skips that week; or make SendRecapAsync itself stamp the guard inside the same scope/transaction as the send-success determination rather than relying on a second best-effort re-load in the caller.

Low Correctness Endpoints correctness & validation CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

AI-usage 'to' filter treats a date-only value as an inclusive midnight bound, silently excluding same-day rows

src/Api/Endpoints/ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:106 DateTime UTC/local confusion + inclusive-bound off-by-one
Evidence
if (from is { } f)
    window = window.Where(r => r.WhenUtc >= DateTime.SpecifyKind(f, DateTimeKind.Utc));
if (to is { } t)
    window = window.Where(r => r.WhenUtc <= DateTime.SpecifyKind(t, DateTimeKind.Utc));
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The 'to' bound is applied as an inclusive <= against a client-supplied DateTime that is force-kinded to UTC. When the admin filters by a calendar day (e.g. to=2026-07-01, which binds to 2026-07-01T00:00:00Z), every AI-usage row logged later that same day (00:00:01 onward) is excluded from both the page and the window-wide summary/cost totals. Because SpecifyKind only relabels the kind (it never converts from the admin's local zone), a value the admin intends as 'through July 1' silently drops nearly a full day of usage/cost, producing an under-count on an audit/billing surface.

Independent verifier — confirmation
ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:105-106
if (to is { } t)
    window = window.Where(r => r.WhenUtc <= DateTime.SpecifyKind(t, DateTimeKind.Utc));

Confirmed by reading lines 89-177. The `to` filter uses an inclusive `<=` against DateTime.SpecifyKind(t, Utc). SpecifyKind only relabels the Kind (no zone conversion), so a date-only value like to=2026-07-01 binds to 2026-07-01T00:00:00Z. With `<=`, any row logged after midnight that same day is excluded. This `window` is reused for the keyset row page AND every summary aggregate (totalCalls line 133, tokenTotals line 140, costByModel line 168), so the under-count propagates to the audit/billing cost totals. No guard/normalization mitigates it anywhere in the flow (contrast with the `from` filter which correctly uses >=). Severity Low is appropriate: admin-gated (ai.usage.view) observability filter, triggered only when an admin supplies a date-only `to` bound; impact is an under-count, not corruption or a security exposure. Location, claim, and evidence all verified. Standard fix is prose-only: treat a date-only `to` as end-of-day (exclusive next-midnight or +1 day) and/or convert from the admin's local zone rather than relabeling.

Remediation (approach)

Treat the 'to' filter as an exclusive next-day (or end-of-day) upper bound for date-only inputs (e.g. compare < to.Date.AddDays(1)), and document/convert the expected timezone of from/to rather than blindly SpecifyKind-ing to UTC, so the reported window matches the admin's intent and the summary totals stay consistent with the rows.

Low Data & Correctness EF entities & migrations CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

ShareAccess 'exactly one of the two FKs set' invariant is unenforced (no CHECK constraint)

src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:340-350 EF entities & migrations integrity
Evidence
e.HasOne(x => x.ShareLink).WithMany()
    .HasForeignKey(x => x.ShareLinkId).OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade);
e.HasOne(x => x.WrappedShareLink).WithMany()
    .HasForeignKey(x => x.WrappedShareLinkId).OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

ShareAccess reuses one table for two link types and its entity comment asserts 'exactly one FK is set per row (the other is null)', but both ShareLinkId and WrappedShareLinkId are nullable with no CHECK constraint (and no partial-unique guard) enforcing the XOR. A bug or manual insert can produce a row with BOTH null (an access attributed to nothing) or BOTH set (double-counted). This is analytics/audit data so impact is low, but the asserted invariant is silently unguaranteed, so share-view counts can drift and orphan rows can accumulate.

Independent verifier — confirmation
ShareAccess.cs:3-5 comment: "exactly one FK is set per row (the other is null)". Entity: `public int? ShareLinkId` (line 11), `public int? WrappedShareLinkId` (line 15) — both nullable. UsageDbContext.cs:346-349:
e.HasOne(x => x.ShareLink).WithMany()
    .HasForeignKey(x => x.ShareLinkId).OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade);
e.HasOne(x => x.WrappedShareLink).WithMany()
    .HasForeignKey(x => x.WrappedShareLinkId).OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade);
No HasCheckConstraint / no filtered unique index enforcing the XOR.

Confirmed. The ShareAccess entity (src/Api/Data/Entities/ShareAccess.cs lines 3-5) documents the invariant "exactly one FK is set per row (the other is null)" but both ShareLinkId and WrappedShareLinkId are nullable ints, and the model config (UsageDbContext.cs 340-350) adds only two non-unique composite indexes plus two nullable FK relationships — no HasCheckConstraint(...) XOR guard and no partial/filtered unique index. A defect or manual insert can leave both null (unattributed) or both set (double-counted), and DeleteBehavior.Cascade on both FKs would not prevent it. Impact is genuinely Low: this is anonymous view-count analytics/audit data (WhenUtc + Ip only), not auth/PII/payment data; worst case is skewed share-view counts or orphan-ish rows. Finding is real, correctly located, and honestly scoped. Fix in prose: add a Postgres CHECK constraint asserting exactly one of the two FKs is non-null (an XOR / num_nonnulls(...) = 1 check) so the DB enforces the documented invariant. Anchored correctedLine to 340 (the ShareAccess entity block start) since the missing guard belongs in that configuration block; cited 346-349 evidence range is also valid.

Remediation (approach)

Add a table CHECK constraint enforcing exactly one of (ShareLinkId, WrappedShareLinkId) is non-null (num_nonnulls(...) = 1) via a migration, so the database rejects violating rows rather than relying on caller discipline.

Low Data & Correctness EF entities & migrations CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

HardChallengeDayTask.ChallengeId is a bare integer column with no foreign key, despite the entity doc asserting it is 'cascade-deleted with the owning challenge'

src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:1418-1430 Cascade-delete behavior / unenforced FK
Evidence
b.Entity<HardChallengeDayTask>(e =>
{
    ...
    e.HasIndex(x => new { x.UserEmail, x.LocalDate, x.TaskId }).IsUnique();
    e.HasIndex(x => x.TaskId);
    // Cascade with the owning task (and thus the challenge).
    e.HasOne(x => x.Task).WithMany()
        .HasForeignKey(x => x.TaskId).OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade);
});
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

HardChallengeDayTask.ChallengeId (documented in the entity as 'FK to the owning HardChallenge, cascade-deleted with it') has no navigation and no HasOne mapping, so the model snapshot renders it as a plain 'integer' with no FK constraint (confirmed in UsageDbContextModelSnapshot.cs lines 2825-2826 — Property<int>("ChallengeId") with no HasOne in the relationship block at 5670-5679). The row is only actually deleted transitively via TaskId→HardChallengeTask→HardChallenge, so cascade still works, but ChallengeId itself has zero referential integrity: nothing prevents a row whose ChallengeId points at a non-existent or wrong challenge, and any code that filters day-task rows by ChallengeId (rather than TaskId) can read stale/mismatched rows. This is a latent integrity gap on a denormalized FK the schema treats as authoritative.

Independent verifier — confirmation
DbContext (src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:1418-1429) maps HardChallengeDayTask with ONLY: `e.HasOne(x => x.Task).WithMany().HasForeignKey(x => x.TaskId).OnDelete(DeleteBehavior.Cascade);` — no HasOne for ChallengeId. Entity doc (src/Api/Data/Entities/HardChallengeTask.cs:100-101): `/// <summary>FK to the owning <see cref="HardChallenge"/> (cascade-deleted with it).</summary>  public int ChallengeId { get; set; }`. Snapshot (src/Api/Migrations/UsageDbContextModelSnapshot.cs:2825-2826): `b.Property<int>("ChallengeId").HasColumnType("integer");` and the relationship block for HardChallengeDayTask (5670-5679) declares only the TaskId FK, no ChallengeId FK.

Verified against the actual source. HardChallengeDayTask.ChallengeId is documented (entity line 100-101) as an FK cascade-deleted with the owning HardChallenge, but the fluent config (UsageDbContext.cs 1418-1429) maps only the TaskId relationship — there is no HasOne/HasForeignKey for ChallengeId and no Challenge navigation on this entity. The model snapshot confirms ChallengeId renders as a bare `Property<int>` "integer" (2825-2826) with no FK in the entity's relationship block (5670-5679); only TaskId has an FK.
