src/Web/src/app/shared/chart.ts:252-257 Frontend performance
Evidence
effect(() => {
  const opt = this.option();
  this.theme.resolved();
  this.theme.scheme();
  this.chart?.setOption(withAxonTheme(opt), true);
});
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The chart re-render effect takes a dependency on both theme.resolved() (light/dark) and theme.scheme() (one of 9 FiMobile accent schemes) and re-applies with notMerge=true (a full option replace/repaint) rather than a lightweight recolor. Only the two accent series colors and axis/tooltip chrome actually change with a scheme, yet every mounted ChartComponent instance on the page tears down and rebuilds its entire option on each scheme pick. On chart-dense pages (dashboard, finance with 4 charts, calendar) a user cycling schemes triggers a burst of full canvas rebuilds. Correctness is fine; it is avoidable repaint cost.

Independent verifier — confirmation
effect(() => {
  const opt = this.option();
  this.theme.resolved();
  this.theme.scheme();
  this.chart?.setOption(withAxonTheme(opt), true);
}); // src/Web/src/app/shared/chart.ts:252-257

Verified against the actual file. Lines 252-257 match the quoted evidence exactly. The effect takes signal dependencies on both theme.resolved() and theme.scheme(), and re-applies via setOption(withAxonTheme(opt), true) where the second arg `true` is ECharts' notMerge flag — a full option replacement/repaint, not a lightweight merge/recolor. So on any light/dark toggle or accent-scheme pick, every mounted ChartComponent tears down and rebuilds its entire option. On chart-dense pages a user cycling schemes triggers a burst of full canvas rebuilds. No mitigation exists in the file (no debounce, no scheme-only fast path, no untracked read). Correctness is fine; this is avoidable repaint cost only on interactive theme changes, so Low severity is accurate. Real but genuinely minor.

Remediation (approach)

Split the effect so a pure theme/scheme change applies only the chrome + series-color delta via a merged setOption (notMerge=false) or a targeted setOption of color/textStyle/axis, reserving the notMerge=true full replace for an actual [option] input change. Alternatively debounce/coalesce scheme changes. This keeps a scheme switch from repainting the full dataset of every chart on the page.

Low Security Backend auth core CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Open sign-up defaults ON with no hosted-domain restriction — any Google account self-provisions

src/Api/Program.cs:427-428 Admin bootstrap / open sign-up default
Evidence
OpenSignupEnabled = builder.Configuration.GetValue("Auth:OpenSignupEnabled", true),
            DefaultPermissionsCsv = builder.Configuration["Auth:DefaultPermissionsCsv"] ?? Permissions.DashboardView,
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

AppConfig seeds OpenSignupEnabled=true (also the entity/DB default), and GoogleTokenValidator (src/Api/Services/IGoogleTokenValidator.cs) validates only the audience/signature/expiry with no HostedDomain constraint. Combined, a fresh deployment of a product that handles PII, health, payments, and GPS accepts ANY Google account with a verified email and auto-provisions it. Blast radius is limited because ParseDefaultPermissions filters to IsDefaultable keys (default is only dashboard.view, and all sensitive/AI/location/family/admin keys are non-defaultable), so a self-registered stranger cannot reach private data, but they still obtain a valid session, appear in presence/user lists, and consume the auth surface. This is a deliberate posture but an open-by-default one.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Program.cs:427-428: `OpenSignupEnabled = builder.Configuration.GetValue("Auth:OpenSignupEnabled", true), DefaultPermissionsCsv = builder.Configuration["Auth:DefaultPermissionsCsv"] ?? Permissions.DashboardView,` | src/Api/Services/IGoogleTokenValidator.cs:17-21: `GoogleJsonWebSignature.ValidateAsync(idToken, new GoogleJsonWebSignature.ValidationSettings { Audience = new[] { clientId } });` (no HostedDomain) | src/Api/Services/GoogleAuthService.cs:60 `if (cfg is null || !cfg.OpenSignupEnabled)` ... :66 `var defaultPerms = ParseDefaultPermissions(cfg.DefaultPermissionsCsv);` auto-provisions the account.

Confirmed on all points. OpenSignupEnabled defaults ON at three layers (Program.cs:427 seed default true, AppConfig.cs:28 entity default, UsageDbContext.cs:179 DB HasDefaultValue(true)). GoogleTokenValidator (IGoogleTokenValidator.cs:17-21) validates only audience/signature/issuer/expiry with NO HostedDomain constraint. GoogleAuthService.cs:53-77 auto-provisions ANY verified Google account when open signup is on. Blast radius is genuinely limited as claimed: ParseDefaultPermissions (GoogleAuthService.cs:179-181) filters the stored CSV through Permissions.IsDefaultable (Permissions.cs:355-362), which excludes all admin/AI/location/family/health/private keys; the seeded default CSV is only dashboard.view. So a self-provisioned stranger obtains a valid session and appears in presence/user lists but cannot reach sensitive data. This is a deliberate but open-by-default posture on a PII/health/payments/GPS product on AWS. Severity Low is correct given the permission mitigation. Evidence quote matches the file exactly; location correct.

Remediation (approach)

Default OpenSignupEnabled to false so a new deployment is closed until an operator opts in, or gate auto-provisioning behind an allowed-domain / allowlist check (e.g. validate payload.HostedDomain or the email domain) so only intended users can self-register. At minimum ensure prod explicitly sets Auth:OpenSignupEnabled=false when the allowlist model is intended.

Low Security Backend auth core CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

XFF trust (ForwardLimit=null + broad private KnownNetworks) is safe only while Kestrel stays unpublished

src/Api/Program.cs:343-347 ForwardedHeaders / X-Forwarded-For trust
Evidence
o.ForwardedHeaders = ForwardedHeaders.XForwardedFor | ForwardedHeaders.XForwardedProto;
    o.ForwardLimit = null;
    o.KnownNetworks.Add(new ...IPNetwork(IPAddress.Parse("10.0.0.0"), 8));
    o.KnownNetworks.Add(new ...IPNetwork(IPAddress.Parse("172.16.0.0"), 12));
    o.KnownNetworks.Add(new ...IPNetwork(IPAddress.Parse("192.168.0.0"), 16));
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

ForwardLimit=null unwinds the entire XFF chain, trusting every hop inside all RFC1918 ranges. This is only sound because the api container publishes no host port (verified in deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml — the api service has no ports mapping), so Kestrel is reachable only via the Docker network and a client cannot inject a spoofed private hop into the middle of the chain. The safety therefore depends entirely on that deployment invariant, which is not enforced in code. If the api container is ever published (a debug port map, a different compose, local ASPNETCORE_URLS bound to a host interface) or another internal service can reach Kestrel directly, a caller can forge X-Forwarded-For to set RemoteIpAddress to any value, spoofing the per-IP rate-limit partitions (auth/share/ingest) and poisoning logged IPs (login history, activity log, fleet machine IP, ingest LastUsedIp).

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Program.cs:342-346
    o.ForwardedHeaders = ForwardedHeaders.XForwardedFor | ForwardedHeaders.XForwardedProto;
    o.ForwardLimit = null;
    o.KnownNetworks.Add(new Microsoft.AspNetCore.HttpOverrides.IPNetwork(IPAddress.Parse("10.0.0.0"), 8));
    o.KnownNetworks.Add(new Microsoft.AspNetCore.HttpOverrides.IPNetwork(IPAddress.Parse("172.16.0.0"), 12));
    o.KnownNetworks.Add(new Microsoft.AspNetCore.HttpOverrides.IPNetwork(IPAddress.Parse("192.168.0.0"), 16));
--- deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:20-41: the `api` service has NO `ports:` mapping (only caddy publishes 80/443); ASPNETCORE_URLS: "http://+:8080" is container-network only.
--- src/Api/Program.cs:360: partitionKey: http.Connection.RemoteIpAddress?.ToString() ?? "unknown"

Evidence matches exactly. ForwardLimit=null unwinds the entire XFF chain, trusting every hop inside the broad /8, /12, /16 RFC1918 ranges. I confirmed in deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml that the api service publishes no host port and binds ASPNETCORE_URLS to http://+:8080 (Docker-network-only), so a public client cannot currently inject a spoofed private hop — the safety holds today. I also confirmed RemoteIpAddress feeds the rate-limit partition keys (line 360), so a spoof would poison per-IP limits and logged IPs. The defect is real but conditional on a deployment invariant not enforced in code (a future published debug port, alternate compose, or any internal service reaching Kestrel directly would enable XFF spoofing). The code comment (lines 337-339) documents the invariant but does not enforce it. Correctly rated Low as a latent hardening gap, not an active exploit. Suggested prose remediation: replace the broad KnownNetworks with the actual proxy subnet(s), or set a fixed ForwardLimit equal to the known number of trusted hops, so trust is bounded independent of network reachability.

Remediation (approach)

Reduce reliance on the unpublished-port invariant: set a bounded ForwardLimit matching the known proxy hop count (Caddy -> nginx -> Kestrel) instead of null, and/or restrict KnownProxies to the specific proxy container address rather than trusting entire /8-/16 private ranges. Add a deployment guardrail/test asserting the api container is never host-published.

Low Security Endpoints: chat / share / social CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Location share can be started into an archived conversation (START skips the archived-channel check that message-send enforces)

src/Api/Endpoints/ChatLocationShareEndpoints.cs:43-50 Location share authorization - conversation state
Evidence
g.MapPost("/channels/{id:int}/location-share", async (
    int id, StartLocationShareRequest req, ... ) =>
{
    var user = (await me.GetUserAsync(ct))!;
    if (!await ChatEndpoints.IsMemberAsync(db, id, user.Email, ct)) return Results.NotFound();
    if (double.IsNaN(req.Lat) || double.IsNaN(req.Lng))
        return Results.BadRequest(new { message = "lat and lng are required." });
    var dto = await shares.StartAsync(id, user.Email, req, ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The message-send path explicitly rejects an archived channel (ChatEndpoints.cs line 221-222: "This channel is archived."), but the location-share START only checks membership, not ArchivedUtc. A member of an archived conversation can still start/broadcast a live GPS pin (health/PII-adjacent location data) into a conversation the moderator has retired, and it will fan out over the channel SignalR group. Impact is limited (only members receive it) so this is Low, but it is an authorization-state inconsistency around location data.

Independent verifier — confirmation
ChatLocationShareEndpoints.cs:42-47: var user = (await me.GetUserAsync(ct))!; if (!await ChatEndpoints.IsMemberAsync(db, id, user.Email, ct)) return Results.NotFound(); if (double.IsNaN(req.Lat) || double.IsNaN(req.Lng)) return Results.BadRequest(...); var dto = await shares.StartAsync(id, user.Email, req, ct); — no ArchivedUtc check. Contrast ChatEndpoints.cs:221-222: if (channel.ArchivedUtc is not null) return Results.BadRequest(new { message = "This channel is archived." });

Verified in the endpoint and the service. The START handler (line 42-47) only checks IsMemberAsync + lat/lng NaN; it never inspects ArchivedUtc. The message-send path (ChatEndpoints.cs 221-222) explicitly loads the channel and rejects archived ones. ChatLocationShareService.StartAsync (line 49-64) also has no archived guard — it only dedupes an active share by (channel, sharer). So a member of an archived conversation can start/broadcast a live GPS pin into a channel the moderator retired, and it fans out over the channel SignalR group. Authorization-state inconsistency around location/PII-adjacent data. Impact is genuinely limited to existing members of that channel, so Low is the correct severity. Finding, file, and severity are accurate; anchoring to line 43 (the membership-only check) is precise.

Remediation (approach)

Have the START handler (or ChatLocationShareService.StartAsync) load the channel and refuse to start a share when ArchivedUtc is set, mirroring the archived-channel guard already applied to POST /channels/{id}/messages, so retired conversations cannot receive new live-location broadcasts.

Low Security Endpoints: admin / users / fleet / ingest CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

users.view and activity.view are defaultable, so open-signup defaults can be configured to auto-grant the user list, audit log, and PII-bearing request logs to every new account

src/Api/Auth/Permissions.cs:355-362 Admin-only routes / self-permission-grant
Evidence
public static bool IsDefaultable(string key) =>
    IsValid(key) && key != UsersManage && key != ChatModerate && key != ChatContactsManage
    && key != TrackerViewAll && key != FamilyUse && key != FamilyFinance && key != CycleTrack
    && key != ChoreClaim && key != AllowanceManage && key != IdentityMap
    && key != AiUsageView && key != BillsUse && key != PlatformMobile && key != AutomationsUse
    && key != AgentsUse && key != HealthSync
    && key != RecipesUse && key != GroceryUse && key != MealsUse && key != ResumeUse
    && !AiKeys.Contains(key) && !LocationKeys.Contains(key);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The defaultable exclusion list intentionally blocks users.manage (no auto-admin) and ai.usage.view, but does NOT exclude the other Admin-group read gates users.view (view user list + permission catalog + audit log) or activity.view (view request logs, which under the finding above can include coordinate/PII bodies). PUT /api/access-policy persists whatever an admin puts in DefaultPermissions filtered only by IsDefaultable, and open sign-up auto-provisions every new Google account with those defaults. An admin who carelessly adds users.view or activity.view to the default set would grant every self-registered account read access to administrative oversight surfaces (the user directory and request-log activity feed). This requires an admin misconfiguration to trigger, so impact is bounded, but the two admin read-gates are inconsistent with the surrounding intent that admin oversight capabilities (ai.usage.view, users.manage) must be deliberate grants.

Independent verifier — confirmation
Permissions.cs:355-362 `public static bool IsDefaultable(string key) => IsValid(key) && key != UsersManage && ... && key != AiUsageView && key != BillsUse && ... ;` (excludes UsersManage and AiUsageView but NOT UsersView/ActivityView). Permissions.cs:237 `new PermissionInfo(UsersView, "Admin", "View users", "View the user list, permission catalog, and audit log.")`; :239 `new PermissionInfo(ActivityView, "Admin", "View activity", "View request logs on the Activity page.")`; :261 both listed in Views. UsersEndpoints.cs:80-81 `var perms = (req.DefaultPermissions ?? Array.Empty<string>()).Where(Permissions.IsDefaultable).Distinct().ToArray();` — the only filter on persisted defaults.

Verified accurate and correctly located. UsersView (Admin: user list + permission catalog + audit log) and ActivityView (Admin: request logs) are both Admin-group read gates in the Views candidate set, yet neither is excluded by IsDefaultable (lines 355-362), which does exclude UsersManage and the analogous admin-oversight gate AiUsageView. PUT /api/access-policy (UsersEndpoints.cs:80-81) persists any submitted default filtered only by IsDefaultable, and open sign-up auto-provisions new accounts with those defaults, so an admin could configure every self-registered account to inherit read access to administrative oversight surfaces. This is a real defensive-consistency/hardening gap. Severity Low is correct and not understated: exploitation requires an admin holding users.manage to deliberately misconfigure the default set — there is no unprivileged self-escalation path — so impact is bounded to admin footgun risk. The inconsistency (one admin read-gate excluded, two comparable ones not) is genuine and code-provable.

Remediation (approach)

Reconsider whether the Admin-group read gates (users.view, activity.view) should be excluded from IsDefaultable for the same reason ai.usage.view and users.manage already are — administrative oversight of the user directory and the request-log feed is a privileged capability that arguably should never be inheritable by every auto-provisioned account. If they must remain defaultable for a product reason, document that explicitly; otherwise add them to the exclusion predicate alongside AiUsageView.

Low Security Endpoints: tracker / AI / agents / misc CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

POST /api/tracker/food/from-meal writes food entries onto another household member's private tracker

src/Api/Endpoints/TrackerEndpoints.cs:226-279 Ownership checks on mutate (cross-user write)
Evidence
var ownerEmail = caller.Email;
if (req.TargetUserId is int targetUserId && targetUserId != caller.Id)
{
    var targetIsMember = await db.HouseholdMembers.AsNoTracking()
        .AnyAsync(hm => hm.HouseholdId == meal.HouseholdId && hm.UserId == targetUserId, ct);
    if (!targetIsMember) return Results.NotFound();
    var targetEmail = await db.Users.AsNoTracking()
        .Where(u => u.Id == targetUserId && u.IsEnabled).Select(u => u.Email).FirstOrDefaultAsync(ct);
    if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(targetEmail)) return Results.NotFound();
    ownerEmail = targetEmail.ToLowerInvariant();
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Every other write on this group is strictly OWNER-ONLY (the class doc states "WRITES (add/delete food or exercise) are OWNER-ONLY regardless of viewall"). This endpoint is the one exception: any member of a household can insert FoodEntry rows (health data) onto a co-member's own tracker/day roll-up with no target consent, keyed only on shared household membership. Households can be admin-provisioned, so a member added to a household could silently mutate another member's food diary, skewing their calorie/macro totals and coaching. The write is bounded (same-household, tracker.self-gated) and documented as intentional, so impact is limited, but it deviates from the stated owner-only write invariant.

Independent verifier — confirmation
// TrackerEndpoints.cs:225-236
var ownerEmail = caller.Email;
if (req.TargetUserId is int targetUserId && targetUserId != caller.Id)
{
    var targetIsMember = await db.HouseholdMembers.AsNoTracking()
        .AnyAsync(hm => hm.HouseholdId == meal.HouseholdId && hm.UserId == targetUserId, ct);
    if (!targetIsMember) return Results.NotFound();
    var targetEmail = await db.Users.AsNoTracking()
        .Where(u => u.Id == targetUserId && u.IsEnabled).Select(u => u.Email).FirstOrDefaultAsync(ct);
    if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(targetEmail)) return Results.NotFound();
    ownerEmail = targetEmail.ToLowerInvariant();
}
// then FoodEntry { UserEmail = ownerEmail } is saved (line 263, 275-276)

Confirmed. The class-level invariant (lines 23-24) states "WRITES (add/delete food or exercise) are OWNER-ONLY regardless of viewall." The /food/from-meal endpoint (lines 226-236) is the sole exception: when TargetUserId names another user, it resolves that user's email and sets ownerEmail to it, then saves a FoodEntry owned by the target (lines 263, 275-276). The only trust boundary is shared household membership (same predicate as the caller's own check) plus the tracker.self gate — no target consent. Since households can be admin-provisioned, a co-member can silently insert health/calorie rows onto another member's private diary, skewing their totals/coaching. The write is bounded (same-household, macros-must-exist, clamped servings) and is explicitly documented as intentional in the endpoint comment (lines 198-202), so real enterprise impact is limited — Low severity is correct. Code, path, and lines all match the finding.

Remediation (approach)

Decide explicitly whether cross-member meal logging is a supported product behavior; if so, document it as an intentional exception to the owner-only write rule and consider a per-user opt-in or an audit trail of who logged the entry. If not, restrict this endpoint to log only onto the caller's own tracker (drop the TargetUserId branch) so it matches every other tracker write.

Low Security Endpoints: tracker / AI / agents / misc CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Habit leaderboard viewall path enumerates every enabled user with per-user N+1 stat computation (no roster cap)

src/Api/Endpoints/HabitEndpoints.cs:338-341 Unbounded cost / enumeration
Evidence
private static IQueryable<AppUser> SharingUsersQuery(UsageDbContext db, CurrentUserAccessor.CurrentUser caller)
{
    if (caller.Permissions.Contains(Permissions.TrackerViewAll))
        return db.Users.AsNoTracking().Where(u => u.IsEnabled && u.Email != caller.Email);
    return ContactGraph.SharingUsers(db, caller.Email);
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

GET /api/habits/leaderboard calls ComputeUserStatsAsync for every user in this roster, and each call runs several per-habit day-window queries (LoadAutoInputsAsync issues 2 queries per Water/Workout habit). For a tracker.viewall caller the roster is ALL enabled users with no cap, so the endpoint fans out to O(users x habits) database round-trips per request — a slow, DB-heavy call an admin can trigger repeatedly. The sibling 75-Hard leaderboard deliberately caps this same viewall roster at MaxLeaderboardPeople (200) with an ordered slice; the habit version omits that cap, so the two diverge and the habit one is unbounded.

Independent verifier — confirmation
HabitEndpoints.cs:338-339 (uncapped): `if (caller.Permissions.Contains(Permissions.TrackerViewAll)) return db.Users.AsNoTracking().Where(u => u.IsEnabled && u.Email != caller.Email);` — vs the sibling HardChallengeEndpoints.cs:598-601 which caps the SAME roster: `.Where(u => u.IsEnabled && u.Email != caller.Email).OrderBy(u => u.Email).Take(MaxLeaderboardPeople);`. The habit /leaderboard then loops `foreach (var u in others) await AddRowAsync(...)` (line 280-281) → `ComputeUserStatsAsync` (line 271) → per habit `ComputeFullAsync` runs a HabitDays query + `LoadAutoInputsAsync` (up to 2 queries for Water/Workout, lines 460-483).

Verified against both files. The habit SharingUsersQuery (HabitEndpoints.cs:336-341) returns ALL enabled users for a tracker.viewall caller with no OrderBy/Take cap. GET /leaderboard (line 255-289) iterates every returned user calling ComputeUserStatsAsync, which itself loops per active habit invoking ComputeFullAsync (1 HabitDays query + LoadAutoInputsAsync issuing up to 2 more queries for Water/Workout auto-source habits). This is a genuine O(users x habits) DB round-trip fan-out. The claim that the 75-Hard sibling deliberately caps the identical viewall roster is accurate: HardChallengeEndpoints.cs:594-601 applies .OrderBy(u => u.Email).Take(MaxLeaderboardPeople) (200) with a comment explicitly naming this exact risk ("A viewall caller could otherwise enumerate EVERY enabled user"). The two implementations diverge and the habit one is unbounded. Low severity is correct: the path is gated behind the tracker.viewall admin/coach permission (not anonymous or ordinary users), leaks no PII, and is a performance/DoS-amplification concern rather than a correctness or security bug. Note the finding cited lines 336-341 for the query helper; the actual uncapped statement is line 338-339 — line reference is accurate to the cited range.

Remediation (approach)

Apply the same bounded, ordered slice the 75-Hard SharingUsersQuery uses for the viewall branch (e.g. OrderBy(email).Take(a sane cap)), so the leaderboard roster can never enumerate every enabled user, and prefer the existing batched stat load pattern over per-user N+1 computation.

Low Security Endpoints: tracker / AI / agents / misc CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Anonymous bill-share item claim is a check-then-set with no uniqueness guard (double-claim race)

src/Api/Endpoints/BillEndpoints.cs:304-313 Ownership checks on mutate (concurrency)
Evidence
var item = bill.Items.FirstOrDefault(i => i.Id == req.ItemId);
if (item is null) return Results.NotFound();
// Only an OPEN item may be claimed (not assigned, not already claimed).
if (item.AssignedToUserId is not null || item.ClaimedByName is not null || item.ClaimedByUserId is not null)
    return Results.Json(new { message = "That item has already been claimed." },
        statusCode: StatusCodes.Status409Conflict);
item.ClaimedByName = name;
item.ClaimedUtc = DateTime.UtcNow;
await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The public, anonymous /api/bill-share/{token}/claim endpoint reads the item, checks it is unclaimed, then writes the claim without a DB unique constraint, row lock, or optimistic-concurrency token on the claim fields. Two concurrent anonymous requests (the surface is only IP-rate-limited, not per-item serialized) can both pass the unclaimed check and the second write silently overwrites the first, so the earlier claimer loses attribution and their share silently shifts. Impact is limited to bill-split attribution (no cross-bill/user data exposure), hence low severity, but the guard is not race-safe.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Endpoints/BillEndpoints.cs:304-313
var item = bill.Items.FirstOrDefault(i => i.Id == req.ItemId);
if (item is null) return Results.NotFound();
if (item.AssignedToUserId is not null || item.ClaimedByName is not null || item.ClaimedByUserId is not null)
    return Results.Json(new { message = "That item has already been claimed." }, statusCode: StatusCodes.Status409Conflict);
item.ClaimedByName = name;
item.ClaimedUtc = DateTime.UtcNow;
await db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
--- No concurrency token on claim fields: UsageDbContext.cs:372 e.Property(x => x.ClaimedByName).HasMaxLength(80);  (no IsConcurrencyToken/RowVersion/unique index anywhere per repo-wide grep)

Verified by reading lines 288-316. The anonymous /api/bill-share/{token}/claim endpoint loads the item into a tracked context, checks it is unclaimed (AssignedToUserId/ClaimedByName/ClaimedByUserId all null), then writes ClaimedByName and SaveChangesAsync with no serialization. Repo-wide grep confirms no IsConcurrencyToken, RowVersion, unique index, or row lock on the claim fields — UsageDbContext.cs:372 only sets HasMaxLength(80). The endpoint is AllowAnonymous and only IP-rate-limited (PublicRateLimitPolicy), so two concurrent requests can both pass the unclaimed check and the second silently overwrites the first (last-write-wins), losing the earlier claimer's attribution. This is a genuine check-then-set race. Impact is correctly scoped to bill-split attribution only — no cross-bill/cross-user data exposure, no money movement, no auth bypass — so Low severity is appropriate. Finding confirmed as-is.

Remediation (approach)

Make the claim atomic: perform the claim as a single conditional UPDATE that only writes when the claim columns are still null (e.g. ExecuteUpdate with the unclaimed predicate in the WHERE) and treat a zero-row result as the 409, or add optimistic concurrency on the item; do not rely on a read-then-write gap on a publicly reachable endpoint.

Low Security Injection / committed secrets / config CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Dev Postgres password committed in appsettings.json and as hardcoded fallbacks

src/Api/appsettings.json:3 committed secrets / config
Evidence
"ConnectionStrings": {
    "Default": "Host=localhost;Port=5433;Database=ccusage;Username=ccusage;Password=ccusage_dev_pw"
  }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The development database password 'ccusage_dev_pw' is committed here and duplicated as a hardcoded fallback in Program.cs line 31 and DesignTimeDbContextFactory.cs line 15. Production overrides it via ConnectionStrings__Default (docker-compose.prod.yml line 27 uses ${DB_PASSWORD} from SSM), so the live database is not exposed. Impact is limited to a local/dev credential, but committing any credential (even a dev one) trains the codebase to accept committed passwords and risks reuse; a developer who binds the dev DB to a non-loopback interface would expose a known-password database.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/appsettings.json:3  "Default": "Host=localhost;Port=5433;Database=ccusage;Username=ccusage;Password=ccusage_dev_pw"
src/Api/Program.cs:31  ?? "Host=localhost;Port=5433;Database=ccusage;Username=ccusage;Password=ccusage_dev_pw";
src/Api/Data/DesignTimeDbContextFactory.cs:15  ?? "Host=localhost;Port=5433;Database=ccusage;Username=ccusage;Password=ccusage_dev_pw";
deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml:27  ConnectionStrings__Default: "Host=db;Port=5432;Database=ccusage;Username=ccusage;Password=${DB_PASSWORD}"

Verified all three cited locations verbatim: appsettings.json line 3 commits Password=ccusage_dev_pw, and the identical string appears as a hardcoded fallback in Program.cs line 31 and DesignTimeDbContextFactory.cs line 15. Production is not exposed: docker-compose.prod.yml line 27 sets ConnectionStrings__Default with Password=${DB_PASSWORD} (injected from SSM at deploy time), which overrides both the committed value and the code fallbacks. Impact is genuinely limited to a local/dev credential bound to localhost:5433, so Low severity is correct. Still a real committed-credential hygiene issue (normalizes committed passwords; would expose a known-password DB if a dev bound it to a non-loopback interface). Finding is accurate, correctly located, and correctly rated. The cited "lines 2-3" is more precisely line 3; corrected to line 3.

Remediation (approach)

Keep the connection string out of committed config: leave the ConnectionStrings:Default value blank in appsettings.json and source the local value from the git-ignored appsettings.Local.json or a User Secrets store, matching how Jwt:Key and the Google secrets are already handled. Prefer a fail-fast (or clearly-throwaway, randomly generated) local password rather than a memorable committed one, and avoid duplicating the literal in code fallbacks.

Low Security Injection / committed secrets / config CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

AllowedHosts set to wildcard "*" (no Host-header allowlist)

src/Api/appsettings.json:72 config hardening
Evidence
"AllowedHosts": "*"
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

AllowedHosts:* disables ASP.NET Core Host-header filtering, so the API answers to any Host value. Behind the Caddy reverse proxy that terminates HTTPS for the fixed ${DOMAIN} (deploy/Caddyfile, docker-compose.prod.yml), the practical exposure is reduced because Caddy is the only ingress, and CORS is separately locked to https://${DOMAIN} (Program.cs line 250 WithOrigins(corsOrigin)). It remains below enterprise hardening standard: Host-header attacks (cache poisoning, misrouted absolute-URL generation, password-reset-style link poisoning) rely on an unfiltered Host, and defense-in-depth calls for pinning it to the known domain rather than a wildcard.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/appsettings.json:72  "AllowedHosts": "*"  — confirmed also deploy/Caddyfile: `{$DOMAIN} { reverse_proxy web:80 }` and Program.cs:250 `p.WithOrigins(corsOrigin).AllowAnyHeader().AllowAnyMethod()`

Verified by reading the file: line 72 is literally "AllowedHosts": "*", which disables ASP.NET Core Host-header filtering so the API accepts any Host value. The finding's mitigations are also accurate: deploy/Caddyfile shows Caddy is the sole ingress terminating HTTPS for the fixed {$DOMAIN} and reverse-proxying to web:80, and Program.cs:250 pins CORS to a single origin (corsOrigin from Cors:AllowedOrigin). These substantially reduce practical exposure, so Low severity is correct. It remains a legitimate defense-in-depth gap below enterprise hardening standard (Host-header attacks: cache poisoning, misrouted absolute-URL/link generation) — the value should be pinned to the known domain rather than a wildcard. Location, severity, and reasoning all correct.

Remediation (approach)

Set AllowedHosts to the concrete production hostname(s) (the deployment DOMAIN, plus localhost for local dev via a Development-profile override) instead of "*", so the app rejects requests bearing an unexpected Host header even if traffic reaches it directly. Drive the prod value from the same DOMAIN variable already used for CORS and Caddy.

Low Security Backend auth core CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Config-write permissions (settings/sources/reporter/notifications manage) remain defaultable, so open-signup defaults can auto-grant deployment-wide config control

src/Api/Auth/Permissions.cs:356-362 Admin bootstrap / default-permission policy (open sign-up second-order)
Evidence
public static bool IsDefaultable(string key) =>
    IsValid(key) && key != UsersManage && key != ChatModerate && key != ChatContactsManage
    && key != TrackerViewAll && key != FamilyUse && ...
    && !AiKeys.Contains(key) && !LocationKeys.Contains(key);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

IsDefaultable excludes a long list of privileged keys but still permits SettingsManage, SourcesManage, ReporterManage, and NotificationsManage in the open-signup default set (Auth:DefaultPermissionsCsv, applied to every auto-provisioned Google account via ParseDefaultPermissions). These are deployment-global, not per-user: NotificationsManage lets a holder rewrite the shared Discord webhook and fire test messages; SourcesManage/SettingsManage edit ingestion source paths and the timezone/auto-sync for the whole instance; ReporterManage mints/revokes ingest keys for the whole fleet. With open sign-up ON (the default) and a misconfigured DefaultPermissionsCsv, any stranger's Google account would inherit deployment-wide configuration control — a strictly broader blast radius than the users.view/activity.view case already reported, because these keys WRITE global state rather than read PII.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Auth/Permissions.cs:355-362:
public static bool IsDefaultable(string key) =>
    IsValid(key) && key != UsersManage && key != ChatModerate && key != ChatContactsManage
    && key != TrackerViewAll && key != FamilyUse && key != FamilyFinance && key != CycleTrack
    && key != ChoreClaim && key != AllowanceManage && key != IdentityMap
    && key != AiUsageView && key != BillsUse && key != PlatformMobile && key != AutomationsUse
    && key != AgentsUse && key != HealthSync
    && key != RecipesUse && key != GroceryUse && key != MealsUse && key != ResumeUse
    && !AiKeys.Contains(key) && !LocationKeys.Contains(key);
// SettingsManage/SourcesManage/ReporterManage/NotificationsManage are NOT excluded.
// src/Api/Endpoints/UsersEndpoints.cs:80-84 persists any IsDefaultable key into DefaultPermissionsCsv;
// src/Api/Services/GoogleAuthService.cs:179-181 auto-grants those to every open-signup account.

Mechanics confirmed by reading the code. IsDefaultable (lines 355-362) omits SettingsManage, SourcesManage, ReporterManage, and NotificationsManage from its exclusion list, so all four — deployment-global config-WRITE capabilities (Admin-category settings/ingestion-sources edit, ingest-key mint/revoke, notification/webhook edit + test-fire per lines 189/195/242/243) — are persistable into DefaultPermissionsCsv (UsersEndpoints.cs:80-84) and auto-granted to every auto-provisioned Google account (GoogleAuthService.cs:179-181, ParseDefaultPermissions -> Where(IsDefaultable)). This is a genuine gap in the allowlist's stated defense-against-misconfigured-defaults purpose, and the write-global blast radius is broader than the read-PII case. Severity corrected to Low, not Medium: exploitation requires an admin to DELIBERATELY add one of these keys to DefaultPermissionsCsv (out-of-box default is only "dashboard.view", OpenSignupEnabled=true), so it is not a default-state exposure; it is the identical mechanism to the already-reported users.view/activity.view finding (a scope variant, not a new vulnerability class). Correctly located and real, so CONFIRMED with reduced severity.

Remediation (approach)

Treat deployment-global config-write capabilities the same way the catalog already treats the family/AI/location keys: add SettingsManage, SourcesManage, ReporterManage and NotificationsManage (and reconsider ReporterView/SettingsView) to the non-defaultable exclusion list in IsDefaultable so they can never be inherited by an auto-provisioned account and must be granted deliberately on the Users page.

Low Security Endpoints: family / health / identity CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

MedicationLog has no unique constraint, so the dose-log upsert and "repeat yesterday" idempotency can be defeated by concurrent writes

src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:1267-1270 Data integrity / health-data correctness (adherence)
Evidence
b.Entity<MedicationLog>(e =>
{
    ...
    // The day's adherence read scans one user's logs for one local date (many per day for multi-dose meds).
    e.HasIndex(x => new { x.UserEmail, x.LocalDate });
    // The per-med adherence window read.
    e.HasIndex(x => new { x.MedicationId, x.LocalDate });
});
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Unlike CycleDayLog / JournalEntry / HabitDay (all of which have an IsUnique() index on their (owner, date/slot) natural key), MedicationLog has only non-unique indexes. Yet POST /api/meds/{id}/log implements an upsert purely with an application-level FirstOrDefaultAsync-then-Add (MedsEndpoints.cs lines 232-246), and POST /api/meds/doses/repeat relies on an in-memory HashSet plus a pre-read of existing target-day rows for idempotency (lines 305-315). With no DB uniqueness backing them, two concurrent requests for the same (user, med, date, slot) — e.g. a double-tap from a flaky mobile connection, or a 'repeat yesterday' racing a manual log of the same slot — both see no existing row and INSERT, producing duplicate logs for one dose. This corrupts the private adherence record for sensitive medication-tracking data (duplicate Taken/Skipped/Missed rows for the same slot) and silently defeats the documented 're-tapping a dose toggles its status rather than piling up rows' guarantee. Adherence() caps taken-per-day so the percentage can't exceed 100%, which limits the blast radius to log-list/UI inconsistency rather than a miscomputed number, keeping this Low.

Independent verifier — confirmation
UsageDbContext.cs:1266-1269 — e.HasIndex(x => new { x.UserEmail, x.LocalDate }); / e.HasIndex(x => new { x.MedicationId, x.LocalDate }); (neither .IsUnique(), and neither covers ScheduledSlot). Contrast: CycleDayLog:1235 .IsUnique(), JournalEntry:1298 .IsUnique(), HabitDay:1326 .IsUnique(). Upsert MedsEndpoints.cs:232-246 is FirstOrDefaultAsync-then-Add; repeat idempotency MedsEndpoints.cs:305-315 is a pre-read + in-memory HashSet(present).Add(...).

Accurately located and described. MedicationLog's two indexes are both non-unique and neither matches the (UserEmail, MedicationId, LocalDate, ScheduledSlot) natural key used by the upsert (MedsEndpoints.cs 232-246) or the repeat idempotency check (298-315), while its sibling day-log entities (CycleDayLog 1235, JournalEntry 1298, HabitDay 1326) all carry .IsUnique() on their natural key. The app-level read-then-insert pattern is a genuine TOCTOU race unprotected by any DB constraint, so concurrent same-slot requests (double-tap; repeat racing a manual log) can produce duplicate log rows for one dose. Impact is correctly bounded: AggregateAdherence caps taken vs scheduled so the adherence % cannot exceed 100%, leaving the harm at UI/log-list duplication of private medication records rather than a wrong metric. Low severity is appropriate given it requires real concurrency and yields only cosmetic/record duplication. Anchor line adjusted to the first offending index (1267).

Remediation (approach)

Add a unique index on MedicationLog keyed by its natural upsert key — (UserEmail, MedicationId, LocalDate, ScheduledSlot) — mirroring the IsUnique() indexes already used for CycleDayLog, JournalEntry, and HabitDay. Because ScheduledSlot is nullable, decide whether a NULL slot should collapse to a single row (Postgres treats NULLs as distinct, so a coalesced/expression index or a NOT-NULL sentinel slot may be needed) and then wrap the two upsert paths in the same per-row unique-violation catch-and-fallback pattern the Identity import already uses, so a lost race is treated as an idempotent no-op rather than a duplicate insert.

Low Security Endpoints: chat / share / social CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Open-DM endpoint distinguishes 'user exists+enabled' (403) from 'nonexistent/disabled' (400), enabling instance-wide AppUser id enumeration by any chat user

src/Api/Endpoints/ChatEndpoints.cs:94-106 IDOR on contact/person ids (user enumeration oracle)
Evidence
var target = req.UserId <= 0 ? null : await db.Users...Where(u => u.Id == req.UserId && u.IsEnabled).Select(u => u.Email).FirstOrDefaultAsync(ct);
if (target is null) return Results.BadRequest(new { message = "That user doesn't exist." });
...
if (!isChatAdmin && !await ContactGraph.IsContactAsync(db, user.Email, target, ct))
    return Results.Json(new { message = "You can only message your contacts..." }, statusCode: StatusCodes.Status403Forbidden);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

A caller with only chat.send can iterate req.UserId over the (sequential) AppUser id space and read the response code as an oracle: 400 'That user doesn't exist' means the id is absent or disabled, while 403 'You can only message your contacts' means the id maps to a real, enabled account that simply isn't in their circle. This lets any low-privilege user enumerate exactly which user ids exist and are enabled across the whole instance (and the returned message text further confirms it), defeating the email-privacy id-indirection design where ids are supposed to be opaque handles. The 400/403 split leaks the membership of the entire user table to non-admins.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Endpoints/ChatEndpoints.cs:90-106:
var target = req.UserId <= 0 ? null : await db.Users.AsNoTracking().Where(u => u.Id == req.UserId && u.IsEnabled).Select(u => u.Email).FirstOrDefaultAsync(ct);
if (target is null) return Results.BadRequest(new { message = "That user doesn't exist." });
...
var isChatAdmin = user.Permissions.Contains(Permissions.ChatContactsManage);
if (!isChatAdmin && !await ContactGraph.IsContactAsync(db, user.Email, target, ct))
    return Results.Json(new { message = "You can only message your contacts. Ask an admin to add them to your circle." }, statusCode: StatusCodes.Status403Forbidden);

Confirmed by reading lines 84-116. The /direct endpoint (RequirePermission ChatSend, any authenticated chat user) resolves req.UserId only when the row exists AND IsEnabled; otherwise target is null and it returns 400 "That user doesn't exist." The 403 "You can only message your contacts" branch (line 103-106) is only reachable when target != null, i.e., the id maps to a real, enabled AppUser who simply isn't in the caller's contact circle. This 400-vs-403 split is a reliable existence/enabled oracle: a non-admin can iterate the sequential AppUser id space and read the status code to enumerate exactly which ids exist and are enabled instance-wide, defeating the email-privacy id-indirection design (ids meant to be opaque handles). No rate limit or generic-response mitigation is present at these lines. Severity Low is correct: requires an authenticated chat.send user and leaks only existence/enabled membership of the user table (no direct PII/email is returned on the 403/400 paths). Location best anchored at line 94 (the null-collapse of nonexistent+disabled into one 400) with the 403 divergence at 103-106.

Remediation (approach)

Collapse the two negative outcomes into a single indistinguishable response for a non-admin caller: when the target is not a contact (and the caller is not a chat admin), return the SAME status/body whether the id is nonexistent, disabled, or a real non-contact user — e.g. resolve contact-membership first and return the generic 'you can only message your contacts' 403 for every id that is not a resolvable contact, so a nonexistent id is not distinguishable from a valid-but-non-contact id. Do not reveal existence/enabled state to callers who cannot act on that user.

Low Security Endpoints: chat / share / social CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

UpdatePosition/Extend re-broadcast a live location pin into a conversation after it is archived (no archived-channel guard on the sharer-owned mutation paths)

src/Api/Services/ChatLocationShareService.cs:81-99 Location share authorization (archived-conversation, second-order of the reported START gap)
Evidence
public async Task<ChatLocationShareDto?> UpdatePositionAsync(int shareId, string sharerEmail, UpdateLocationShareRequest req, CancellationToken ct = default)
{
    var share = await db.ChatLocationShares.FirstOrDefaultAsync(s => s.Id == shareId, ct);
    if (share is null || !OwnedBy(share, sharerEmail)) return null;
    var now = DateTime.UtcNow;
    if (!IsActive(share, now)) return null;
    ...
    await Broadcast(share.ChannelId, "locationShareUpdated", dto, ct);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Message-send (ChatEndpoints line 221) and the hub (ChatHub line 66) both refuse to write to an archived channel, but the location-share update/extend paths only check share ownership + share activeness, never the channel's ArchivedUtc. A sharer who began a live share can keep pushing new GPS positions (locationShareUpdated) and extend the window into a channel that a moderator has since archived, so a live location feed continues in a conversation the moderator deliberately shut down. This is the same class of gap the prior round flagged for START, but on the mutation paths that keep the pin alive rather than the one that begins it.

Independent verifier — confirmation
ChatLocationShareService.cs:84-97 — UpdatePositionAsync: `var share = await db.ChatLocationShares.FirstOrDefaultAsync(s => s.Id == shareId, ct); if (share is null || !OwnedBy(share, sharerEmail)) return null; var now = DateTime.UtcNow; if (!IsActive(share, now)) return null; ... await Broadcast(share.ChannelId, "locationShareUpdated", dto, ct);` — no ArchivedUtc check. Contrast ChatEndpoints.cs:221-222 message-send: `if (channel.ArchivedUtc is not null) return Results.BadRequest(new { message = "This channel is archived." });`

Confirmed. UpdatePositionAsync (lines 81-99) and ExtendAsync (lines 105-124) gate only on share ownership (OwnedBy) and activeness (IsActive = !Stopped && now < ExpiresUtc); neither loads the ChatChannel nor checks ArchivedUtc. The endpoints (ChatLocationShareEndpoints.cs:53-78) likewise only re-check permissions, not archive state. Meanwhile message-send (ChatEndpoints.cs:221-222) and the hub explicitly reject archived channels, establishing "archived = writes blocked" as an enforced state — so the location-share mutation paths inconsistently allow a sharer to keep broadcasting locationShareUpdated and extend the live pin into a channel a moderator archived. Real authorization-consistency gap on location/PII. Severity Low is correct: it requires an already-live share owned by the actor and members still in the SignalR group; archived channels are filtered out of the member channel list (ChatEndpoints.cs:457 ArchivedUtc == null), limiting practical reach, but the live feed can still flow to any connection still joined to the group. Best-anchored on the UpdatePositionAsync signature at line 81 (ExtendAsync at 105 shares the identical defect).

Remediation (approach)

Apply the archived-channel guard consistently to every write path in ChatLocationShareService (start, update-position, extend), not just parity with send on start: after loading the share, verify its channel is not archived before persisting/broadcasting, returning the same 404/ended result used for an inactive share when the channel is archived. Centralize the archived check so the location-share surface can never outlive an archive that stops normal messaging.

Low Security Endpoints: chat / share / social CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Removing a chat contact edge does not revoke the ability to keep messaging in a pre-existing DM (send checks membership only, not current contact status)

src/Api/Endpoints/ChatEndpoints.cs:219-123 DM/chat membership authorization (contact-gate durability)
Evidence
var channelId = await GetOrCreateDirectAsync(db, user.Email, target, ct); // /direct fast-path returns the existing DM id regardless of current contact status
...
// send:
if (channel is null || channel.Members.All(m => m.UserEmail != user.Email)) return Results.NotFound();  // membership only — never re-checks IsContactAsync
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The DM-open endpoint enforces the mutual-contact gate, but once a Direct channel exists its two members stay members forever (ChatEndpoints has no member-removal route). After an admin removes the contact edge between A and B (ContactsEndpoints DELETE), A can still POST /channels/{dmId}/messages and B still receives them, because message-send only verifies channel membership, never re-evaluates the contact relationship. The 'you can only message your contacts' policy is therefore only enforced at first-DM creation and silently bypassed for any conversation that already exists, so revoking a contact does not actually cut off the DM channel.

Independent verifier — confirmation
ChatEndpoints.cs:108 `var channelId = await GetOrCreateDirectAsync(db, user.Email, target, ct);` — but /direct's contact gate (108 preceded by 103-106 IsContactAsync) is the ONLY contact check. Send at ChatEndpoints.cs:219 `if (channel is null || channel.Members.All(m => m.UserEmail != user.Email)) return Results.NotFound();` checks membership only. ContactsEndpoints.cs:97-100 deletes only ChatContacts rows: `await db.ChatContacts.Where(c => ...).ExecuteDeleteAsync(ct);` — never touches ChatChannelMembers, and ChatEndpoints has no member-removal route.

Verified in code. /direct enforces the mutual-contact gate before returning the DM id (lines 103-106), but GetOrCreateDirectAsync's fast path (lines 367-371) returns an existing DM regardless of current contact status, and the message-send endpoint (lines 217-220) only verifies channel membership — it never re-checks IsContactAsync. ContactsEndpoints DELETE (lines 97-100) removes only ChatContacts rows and never removes ChatChannelMembers; there is no member-removal route in ChatEndpoints. So once a DM exists, removing the contact edge does not stop either party from continuing to message in that DM. The contact policy is only enforced at first-DM open. Severity Low is correct: this is internal team chat, requires an admin contact-removal plus a pre-existing DM, and leaks no PII/auth — it is a policy-durability gap, not a privilege escalation. Location best anchored at the send membership check (line 219), which is where the missing re-evaluation lives.

Remediation (approach)

Decide and enforce the intended lifetime of the contact gate: if contact removal is meant to sever messaging, re-check the mutual-contact relationship (or channel membership derived from it) on the DM send/read paths for Direct channels and treat a now-non-contact pair as no-longer-authorized; alternatively, have the contacts-remove path also remove/close the corresponding DM membership. If continued messaging after contact removal is intentional, document it so the gate is not mistaken for a durable access control.

Low Security Endpoints: admin / users / fleet / ingest CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Client-controlled source="login" bypasses the LocationEnabled opt-in gate on the server

src/Api/Endpoints/LocationEndpoints.cs:55-62 Location/GPS privacy
Evidence
if (source != "login")
{
    var enabled = await db.Users.AsNoTracking()
        .Where(u => u.Id == caller.Id).Select(u => u.LocationEnabled).FirstOrDefaultAsync(ct);
    if (!enabled)
        return Results.Json(new { message = "Enable location first." },
            statusCode: StatusCodes.Status409Conflict);
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The opt-in enforcement is skipped entirely when the request body sets source=="login". The privacy justification (the SPA only fires the login grab when the browser permission is already granted) is a CLIENT-side guarantee the server does not verify: any location.self holder can POST {lat,lng,source:"login"} to force a recorded fix even though they never toggled LocationEnabled on. Impact is bounded because the data is the caller's OWN self-scoped location and location.self is still required, but it defeats the server-side meaning of the LocationEnabled toggle (a user who deliberately turned capture OFF can still have coordinates written and reflected into presence via the login path). This is a documented/tested design choice, so it is a hardening gap rather than a clear vulnerability.

Independent verifier — confirmation
if (source != "login")
{
    var enabled = await db.Users.AsNoTracking()
        .Where(u => u.Id == caller.Id).Select(u => u.LocationEnabled).FirstOrDefaultAsync(ct);
    if (!enabled)
        return Results.Json(new { message = "Enable location first." },
            statusCode: StatusCodes.Status409Conflict);
}
// (src/Api/Endpoints/LocationEndpoints.cs:55-62; endpoint gated only by .RequirePermission(Permissions.LocationSelf) at line 94)

Verified against the actual code. Lines 55-62 skip the LocationEnabled opt-in check whenever the client-supplied req.Source normalizes to "login". The endpoint (line 94) is gated only by Permissions.LocationSelf, so any location.self holder can POST {lat,lng,source:"login"} to force a recorded fix and reflect a coarse city into presence (line 91) even with capture toggled OFF. The privacy justification (SPA only fires the login grab when browser permission is already granted, never prompting) is documented in the XML comment (lines 48-54) as an intentional design choice but is a CLIENT-side guarantee the server does not verify. Impact is genuinely bounded: the data is the caller's own self-scoped location, location.self is still required, and household members only see a coarse city — so this is a hardening gap, not a real vulnerability. Severity Low is correct. Corrected line to 55 (start of the bypass block).

Remediation (approach)

Do not treat a client-supplied source string as the authorization to bypass the opt-in. Either drop the login-source exception and gate all recording on LocationEnabled, or derive the trusted 'this is a genuine post-login grab' signal server-side (e.g. a short-lived server-issued marker tied to the just-completed sign-in) rather than trusting a mutable request field.

Low Security Endpoints: tracker / AI / agents / misc CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

HardChallenge PUT /day accepts an unbounded, out-of-window date with no range check

src/Api/Endpoints/HardChallengeEndpoints.cs:214-255 Input validation / resource abuse (self-scoped data integrity)
Evidence
if (!TryParseDate(req?.Date, out var localDate))
    return Results.BadRequest(new { message = "A valid date (yyyy-MM-dd) is required." });
var challenge = await db.HardChallenges
    .FirstOrDefaultAsync(c => c.UserEmail == caller.Email && c.Status == HardChallengeStatus.Active, ct);
if (challenge is null) return Results.NotFound();
...
var row = await db.HardChallengeDays
    .FirstOrDefaultAsync(x => x.UserEmail == caller.Email && x.LocalDate == localDate, ct);
...
if (row is null) { row = new HardChallengeDay { ChallengeId = challenge.Id, UserEmail = caller.Email, LocalDate = localDate, ... }; db.HardChallengeDays.Add(row); }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Every other day/date write in this family bounds the date: POST / (start) rejects a start outside today +/- 1 year (lines 147-148), POST /cheat-days rejects dates outside the challenge window (lines 420-422), and HabitEndpoints rejects dates outside today +/- 5..1 years (DateOutOfRange). PUT /day alone parses any yyyy-MM-dd and upserts a HardChallengeDay with NO lower/upper bound and no check that the date falls within the active challenge's 75-day window. A caller can therefore create effectively unlimited HardChallengeDay rows for their own account at arbitrary far-past/far-future dates (a bounded storage-abuse / row-growth vector), and can set a Confession on any such row which increments challenge.ConfessionsUsed (lines 266-271), polluting their own confession count. Impact is confined to the caller's own data (strictly UserEmail == caller.Email — not an IDOR), so severity is low, but it is an unenforced invariant that the sibling endpoints all enforce.

Independent verifier — confirmation
HardChallengeEndpoints.cs:214-238 — `if (!TryParseDate(req?.Date, out var localDate)) return Results.BadRequest(...);` ... `var row = await db.HardChallengeDays.FirstOrDefaultAsync(x => x.UserEmail == caller.Email && x.LocalDate == localDate, ct);` ... `if (row is null) { row = new HardChallengeDay { ChallengeId = challenge.Id, UserEmail = caller.Email, LocalDate = localDate, ... }; db.HardChallengeDays.Add(row); }` — no lower/upper bound or window check. Contrast POST / at 147: `if (start < today.AddYears(-1) || start > today.AddYears(1)) return Results.BadRequest(...)`, POST /cheat-days at 421: `if (dt <= today || dt < challenge.StartDate || dt > windowEnd) return Results.BadRequest(...)`, and HabitEndpoints.cs:561-562 `DateOutOfRange = date < today.AddYears(-5) || date > today.AddYears(1)`.

Verified by reading the handler (lines 209-284), ApplyDayManual (1221-1227), and the three cited sibling bounds (POST / line 147-148, POST /cheat-days 420-422, HabitEndpoints DateOutOfRange 561-562). PUT /day validates only that req.Date parses as yyyy-MM-dd; it applies no lower/upper bound and no check that the date falls within the active challenge's window (StartDate..StartDate+TotalDays-1). It then upserts a HardChallengeDay at that arbitrary date, and a first-time confession increments challenge.ConfessionsUsed (266-271). All queries filter UserEmail == caller.Email, so impact is strictly self-scoped (not an IDOR) — a bounded row-growth / invariant-violation vector, one row per distinct date. Low severity is correct; the sibling endpoints all enforce the bound this one omits.

Remediation (approach)

Before upserting the HardChallengeDay, validate that the parsed localDate falls within the active challenge's window (challenge.StartDate .. StartDate.AddDays(TotalDays-1)), returning a 400 otherwise, mirroring the bound POST /cheat-days already applies; at minimum apply the same today +/- 1 year sanity clamp used by POST / so a single caller cannot create arbitrarily-dated day rows.

Low Security Services: SSRF / secrets / HTTP CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

WorkoutX GIF proxy streams the upstream-chosen Content-Type back to the browser verbatim with no image-type allowlist and no X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (no global security-header middleware exists)

src/Api/Endpoints/TrackerEndpoints.cs:1136 GIF/image proxy abuse: reflected upstream Content-Type
Evidence
var gif = await workoutx.GetGifAsync(id, ct);
if (gif is not { } g) return Results.NotFound();
http.Response.Headers.CacheControl = "private, max-age=86400";
return Results.Bytes(g.Bytes, g.ContentType);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

GetGifAsync returns `res.Content.Headers.ContentType?.MediaType ?? "image/gif"` (WorkoutXService.cs:129) — an upstream-controlled value — and the endpoint reflects it directly. A grep for nosniff/X-Content-Type-Options/CSP across src/Api returns no matches, so there is no global security-header middleware to backstop it. If the WorkoutX host were ever compromised or MITM'd (compounded by the missing redirect suppression), it could return `text/html`/`application/javascript` content that the proxy would serve from the app's own JWT-authorized origin under an id-only URL, enabling stored-XSS-style content injection on the API origin. The blast radius is limited because the host is fixed and the id is digit-validated, hence Low.

Independent verifier — confirmation
TrackerEndpoints.cs:1136 `return Results.Bytes(g.Bytes, g.ContentType);` where ContentType originates at WorkoutXService.cs:129 `var contentType = res.Content.Headers.ContentType?.MediaType ?? "image/gif";` (upstream-controlled). Grep for nosniff/X-Content-Type-Options/CSP/X-Frame-Options across src/Api returns "No matches found" — no global security-header middleware.

Verified as real and correctly located. The proxy reflects the upstream-chosen Content-Type (WorkoutXService.cs:129) verbatim with no image/* allowlist, and grep confirms no X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff or CSP middleware anywhere in src/Api to backstop MIME-sniffing. This is a genuine defense-in-depth gap on a JWT-authorized, same-origin API endpoint. Impact is legitimately limited: the upstream host is fixed, the id is constrained to 1-8 digits at the route (:int:min(0)) and re-validated (IsValidGifId), and content is fetched as a blob via HttpClient rather than navigated to — so exploitation requires the WorkoutX host to be compromised or MITM'd. Low severity is well-calibrated. Recommended fix (prose): enforce an image/* Content-Type allowlist in GetGifAsync (reject or normalize to image/gif otherwise) and set X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on the response, ideally via global security-header middleware. Line reference 1132-1136 is accurate; the reflecting call is at 1136.

Remediation (approach)

Clamp the served Content-Type to a small image allowlist (e.g. only image/gif, image/png, image/webp; otherwise default to image/gif or reject), and add X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on this response (or, better, a global security-headers middleware). Do not trust the upstream media type verbatim.

Low Security Services: SSRF / secrets / HTTP CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

OAuth token/refresh responses are read via ReadAsByteArrayAsync with no size cap in the Fitbit and Google token exchanges, so a compromised/MITM'd token endpoint can force an unbounded allocation

src/Api/Services/Health/FitbitHealthProvider.cs:376-377 Secret handling: unbounded upstream response read
Evidence
var bytes = await res.Content.ReadAsByteArrayAsync(ct);
return (JsonDocument.Parse(bytes), res.StatusCode);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

PostTokenAsync (Fitbit) and GoogleCalendarService.PostTokenAsync/CalendarGetAsync buffer the entire upstream body into memory before parsing, with no MaxResponseContentBufferSize and no length guard. These are the exact requests that also carry the client secret / refresh token, and (per the redirect finding) can be redirected off the fixed host. A hostile or MITM'd token endpoint returning a multi-GB body would drive an unbounded allocation on a background sync thread. Low because the upstreams are normally trusted and there is a per-request timeout, but the buffering has no ceiling.

Independent verifier — confirmation
FitbitHealthProvider.cs:376-377 — `var bytes = await res.Content.ReadAsByteArrayAsync(ct);` / `return (JsonDocument.Parse(bytes), res.StatusCode);`  |  Program.cs:208-212 configures the Fitbit HttpClient with only BaseAddress + 20s Timeout, no MaxResponseContentBufferSize  |  GoogleCalendarService.cs:501 & 567 mirror the same uncapped read.

Verified by reading the cited lines and the HttpClient registration. Both Fitbit PostTokenAsync (376-377) and the parallel Google PostTokenAsync (GoogleCalendarService.cs:501) / CalendarGetAsync (567) buffer the full upstream body via ReadAsByteArrayAsync/ReadAsStringAsync with no length guard, and no MaxResponseContentBufferSize is set on any HttpClient in the codebase (grep returns zero hits) — the registrations set only BaseAddress + Timeout. So an upstream returning a multi-GB body would drive an unbounded allocation, bounded only by the 15-20s timeout. These are exactly the token/refresh requests that carry the client secret and rotating refresh token, on a background sync thread (HealthSyncScheduler). Severity Low is correct: api.fitbit.com/googleapis.com are fixed, TLS-protected, trusted hosts, so the trigger requires a compromised/MITM'd endpoint; the timeout partially caps sustained-throughput attacks but does not cap a single fast large body. Defect is real, correctly located, and impact/severity as stated.

Remediation (approach)

Cap the buffered response size for these outbound calls (set HttpClient.MaxResponseContentBufferSize on the named clients, or read with a bounded stream and abort past a small limit such as 256 KB for token/JSON responses). Combine with redirect suppression so the body can only come from the intended host.

Low Security Injection / committed secrets / config CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

My Data ZIP export Csv() helper is also formula-injection-unsafe across all free-text columns

src/Api/Services/MyDataExportService.cs:412-415 Injection (CSV / formula injection)
Evidence
private static string Row(params string[] cells) => string.Join(',', cells.Select(Csv));

private static string Csv(string s) =>
    s.Contains(',') || s.Contains('"') || s.Contains('\n') || s.Contains('\r')
        ? "\"" + s.Replace("\"", "\"\"") + "\""
        : s;
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The same quote-only escaping feeds food.csv (Description, Brand), exercise.csv (Name), usage_records.csv (Model, Project, MachineName, GitBranch), contacts.csv, and activity_events.csv. All are user-authored free text. A field beginning with =/+/-/@ opens in a spreadsheet as a live formula. Severity is Low rather than Medium because the My Data export is strictly owner-scoped (the victim opening the file is the same user who authored the values), so it is mostly a self-inflicted vector — but a shared household member's authored merchant/contact/exercise text can still land in another member's downloaded archive in the interconnected household model, and the fix is the same one-line change as the records.csv sink.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Services/MyDataExportService.cs:412-415
private static string Csv(string s) =>
    s.Contains(',') || s.Contains('"') || s.Contains('\n') || s.Contains('\r')
        ? "\"" + s.Replace("\"", "\"\"") + "\""
        : s;
(feeds free-text sinks e.g. line 169 Row(f.Description, f.Brand,...), line 176 Row(e.Name,...), line 105 Row(...,r.Model, r.Project,...))

Verified by reading lines 407-423 and grepping the Row/Csv sinks. The Csv() helper only wraps fields containing comma/quote/CR/LF in quotes; it never prefixes or neutralizes a leading =, +, -, or @, so a free-text value like "=cmd|'..." is emitted verbatim and executes as a formula when opened in Excel/Sheets. The sinks are confirmed user-authored free text: food.csv Description/Brand (169), exercise.csv Name (176), usage_records.csv Model/Project (105), activity_events.csv Label (195), plus contacts/name rows (353). No mitigation exists in this file (no sanitizer, no allowlist). Severity Low is correct: the My Data export is owner-scoped, so it is mostly self-inflicted; the household cross-authored-text angle is plausible but secondary. Location and severity as claimed are accurate.

Remediation (approach)

Apply the same leading-metacharacter neutralization described for UsageQueries.Csv here (ideally by extracting one shared CSV-cell encoder used by both services), so every exported cell that starts with a formula trigger is defanged before write.

Low Security Frontend security CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Markdown link sanitizer allows protocol-relative (//host) external links, defeating the intended same-origin/allowed-scheme restriction

src/Web/src/app/features/family/markdown.ts:28-30 Frontend security (XSS / token / redirect)
Evidence
function safeUrl(url: string): string | null {
  const u = url.trim();
  if (/^(https?:|mailto:)/i.test(u)) return u;
  // Allow scheme-relative / relative-looking links that can't be a javascript: payload.
  if (/^(\/|#)/.test(u)) return u;
  return null;
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The comment says the relative branch only permits in-app/anchor links, but the regex ^(\/|#) also matches a protocol-relative URL such as //evil.com, which the browser resolves to https://evil.com. A family member (or a compromised/AI-assisted note draft) can author `[Payroll portal](//evil.example)` in a Family Hub note; it renders as a normal-looking in-app link that silently navigates victims to an attacker origin in a new tab. In a section handling household finance/PII this is a viable phishing/credential-harvest vector. rel=noopener noreferrer prevents reverse-tabnabbing but not the redirect itself.

Independent verifier — confirmation
markdown.ts:27-28: `// Allow scheme-relative / relative-looking links that can't be a javascript: payload.` / `if (/^(\/|#)/.test(u)) return u;`  — and note-editor-dialog.ts:121 / notes.ts:209 bind the result via `sanitizer.bypassSecurityTrustHtml(renderMarkdown(...))`, so Angular does not re-sanitize the href.

Verified by reading the file. The comment on line 27 literally says "scheme-relative" yet claims the branch can't be a javascript: payload — true for javascript:, but the regex ^(\/|#) matches `//evil.com`, a protocol-relative URL the browser resolves to an external origin (https://evil.com). The rendered <a> has target="_blank" (line 41) and the output is bound via bypassSecurityTrustHtml (note-editor-dialog.ts:121, notes.ts:209), so Angular's sanitizer never scrubs the href — no upstream mitigation exists. An authenticated family member or a compromised/AI-generated note draft can author `[text](//evil.example)` that renders as an ordinary in-app-looking link and silently navigates a victim to an attacker origin — a viable open-redirect/phishing vector in a section handling household finance/PII. Not XSS (scheme is still restricted from javascript:/data:), so Low severity is correct. Fix in prose: in the relative branch require the second char after a leading slash to NOT be another slash (i.e., reject when u starts with `//` or its backslash equivalent `/\`), only permitting single-slash root-relative paths and `#` anchors.

Remediation (approach)

In the relative branch, reject protocol-relative URLs: only accept a leading single slash that is NOT immediately followed by a second slash (and similarly reject leading backslashes), or explicitly require the path to start with a single '/' followed by a non-slash character, or '#'. Alternatively, resolve the candidate against a fixed same-origin base and confirm the resulting origin equals the app origin before emitting the anchor.

Low Security Frontend security CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Post-login navigation trusts an attacker-supplied ?returnUrl verbatim without validating it is an in-app path

src/Web/src/app/features/signin/signin.ts:69-70 Frontend security (XSS / token / redirect)
Evidence
  private returnUrl(): string {
    // An explicit ?returnUrl stays verbatim; the homeRoute() fallback is normalized ...
    return this.route.snapshot.queryParamMap.get('returnUrl') || normalizeHome(this.auth.homeRoute());
  }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The value is taken straight from the query string and passed to router.navigateByUrl(this.returnUrl()) on both the constructor short-circuit (line 44) and the successful-login path (line 117). Angular's router keeps navigation same-origin, so this is not a classic cross-origin open redirect; however the app never asserts the value is a leading-slash in-app route. A crafted /login?returnUrl=... link (or a stored value) can force an authenticated user to land on any arbitrary in-app deep link chosen by the attacker immediately after sign-in, which can be abused for UI-redress/pre-seeding of dialogs and undermines the guards' intent of returning users only to the page they were bounced from. Compare PwaService.onNotificationClick and ShareTargetPage which both correctly require target.startsWith('/') before navigating.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/src/app/features/signin/signin.ts:69: return this.route.snapshot.queryParamMap.get('returnUrl') || normalizeHome(this.auth.homeRoute());  // consumed verbatim at line 44 (this.router.navigateByUrl(this.returnUrl())) and line 117 (next: () => this.router.navigateByUrl(this.returnUrl())). Contrast src/Web/src/app/core/pwa.ts:196: if (target.startsWith('/')) { ... } which does validate before navigating.

Verified: the raw ?returnUrl query param is passed straight to router.navigateByUrl() on both the constructor short-circuit (line 44) and the post-login callback (line 117) with no leading-slash / in-app-path assertion. The finding correctly notes this is NOT a classic cross-origin open redirect (Angular's router keeps navigateByUrl same-origin — an absolute external URL like http://evil.com is treated as an in-app path segment and won't leave the app), so severity Low is accurate rather than High. The real, provable defect is a defensive-hardening gap: an attacker-crafted /login?returnUrl=... link forces an authenticated user to land on any arbitrary in-app deep link post-login, which can pre-seed dialogs / enable UI-redress and defeats the guards' intent of returning users only to the page they were bounced from. The cited mitigation in the codebase (pwa.ts:196 requiring target.startsWith('/')) confirms the app has an established pattern that signin.ts fails to apply. Location and severity are correct; anchoring line adjusted to 69 (the actual sink of the untrusted value inside returnUrl()).

Remediation (approach)

Validate returnUrl before using it: require it to begin with a single '/' and not '//' (reject protocol-relative and absolute URLs), and ideally confirm it matches a known in-app route (or fall back to normalizeHome(homeRoute()) otherwise). Apply the same check in login.ts where the value is captured and re-forwarded.

Low Security Endpoints: family / health / identity CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

POST /api/meds/{id}/log accepts any ScheduledSlot in [0,11] without validating it against the medication's actual TimesPerDay/schedule

src/Api/Endpoints/MedsEndpoints.cs:229-246 Household scoping / data integrity on mutation
Evidence
var slot = req.Slot is { } s ? Math.Clamp(s, 0, MaxTimesPerDay - 1) : (int?)null;
// Upsert the slot's log for the day ...
var row = await db.MedicationLogs.FirstOrDefaultAsync(
    l => l.UserEmail == caller.Email && l.MedicationId == id
        && l.LocalDate == req.Date && l.ScheduledSlot == slot, ct);
if (row is null) { row = new MedicationLog { MedicationId = id, ... ScheduledSlot = slot, ... }; db.MedicationLogs.Add(row); }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The med was ownership-verified (so this is not cross-user IDOR), but the slot index is clamped only to the global 0..11 cap, never to the med's own Schedule.TimesPerDay or DosesDueOn for the date. A client can persist adherence logs for phantom slots (e.g. slot 11 on a once-daily med, or on a day the med is not scheduled). BuildTodaySlots only renders slots < due and Adherence caps per-day taken at DosesDueOn, so aggregate math is protected, but the underlying MedicationLog table accumulates orphan rows that never appear in the checklist yet persist as the user's private health record and can skew any future/less-guarded consumer of these rows.

Independent verifier — confirmation
MedsEndpoints.cs:229  var slot = req.Slot is { } s ? Math.Clamp(s, 0, MaxTimesPerDay - 1) : (int?)null;
MedsEndpoints.cs:232-234  var row = await db.MedicationLogs.FirstOrDefaultAsync(l => l.UserEmail == caller.Email && l.MedicationId == id && l.LocalDate == req.Date && l.ScheduledSlot == slot, ct);
MedsEndpoints.cs:507-517  var due = MedsVitalsMath.DosesDueOn(med, today); ... for (var i = 0; i < due; i++)  // only slots < due are rendered

Verified in the actual file. Line 229 clamps req.Slot only to the global 0..11 cap (MaxTimesPerDay-1), never against the med's own Schedule.TimesPerDay or DosesDueOn(med, req.Date). The med is ownership-verified (lines 224-226), so this is not IDOR. BuildTodaySlots (507-517) iterates only i < due, so phantom slots (e.g. slot 11 on a once-daily med, or any slot on a non-scheduled day where due=0) never appear in the checklist, and AggregateAdherence/DosesDueOn cap the per-day denominator — so aggregate adherence math is protected. But the upsert (235-246) still persists orphan MedicationLog rows keyed on those out-of-range slots in the user's private health record. Defect is real, correctly located, and Low severity is accurate (integrity hygiene, not a security/aggregate-correctness breach). Fix in prose: after resolving the med, validate the requested slot against that med's scheduled dose count for req.Date (e.g. reject or ignore slots >= DosesDueOn(med, req.Date), and reject non-null slots when the med isn't due that day) rather than only clamping to the global maximum.

Remediation (approach)

After loading the owning medication, validate that req.Slot is within [0, DosesDueOn(med, req.Date)) (and reject logging on a date the med is not scheduled), returning 400 for out-of-range slots rather than silently clamping and persisting a phantom-slot row.

Low Security Endpoints: family / health / identity CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

POST /api/meds/doses/repeat copies Missed/Skipped statuses verbatim onto the target day, fabricating adherence history the user never recorded for that day

src/Api/Endpoints/MedsEndpoints.cs:322-327 Household scoping / data integrity on mutation
Evidence
foreach (var src in sources)
{
    if (!present.Add((src.MedicationId, src.ScheduledSlot))) continue; // already on toDate → skip
    copies.Add(new MedicationLog
    {
        MedicationId = src.MedicationId,
        UserEmail = caller.Email, // always the caller's own day
        LocalDate = req.ToDate,
        ScheduledSlot = src.ScheduledSlot,
        Status = src.Status,
        TakenAtUtc = src.Status == MedicationLogStatus.Taken ? now : null,
        Notes = src.Notes,
        CreatedUtc = now,
    });
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

This is owner-scoped (no IDOR), but 'repeat yesterday' is documented as pre-filling TODAY's checklist. Copying every source log — including Status=Missed and Status=Skipped — writes negative adherence facts for a day the user has not yet acted on. A slot the user simply hasn't taken yet on toDate becomes a persisted 'Missed'/'Skipped' record, silently degrading the private adherence percentage the user relies on and any med-reminder logic keyed off it. Because the operation is idempotent-by-skip, the fabricated status also can't be re-derived away by re-tapping the same slot until the row is edited.

Independent verifier — confirmation
MedsEndpoints.cs:322-323  Status = src.Status, TakenAtUtc = src.Status == MedicationLogStatus.Taken ? now : null,  — copies Missed/Skipped verbatim onto req.ToDate. Corroborated by AgentComposer.cs:88 which treats Skipped as "resolved" (suppresses the reminder nudge): (l.Status == MedicationLogStatus.Taken || l.Status == MedicationLogStatus.Skipped).

The defect is real and correctly located: /doses/repeat ("repeat yesterday", documented at lines 279-282 as pre-filling today's checklist) copies every source-day log forward including Status=Missed and Status=Skipped (line 322), fabricating a negative adherence status for a target day the user has not yet acted on. Owner-scoped, no IDOR (UserEmail==caller on both read and write), so Low severity stands.
