HOWEVER the finding's PRIMARY stated impact — "silently degrading the private adherence percentage" — is FALSE / already mitigated: Adherence() in MedsVitalsMath.cs (lines 45, 56-62) counts ONLY Status==Taken in the numerator and derives the denominator from DosesDueOn(med,d) (the schedule, not the logs), so a copied Missed/Skipped row is inert to the percentage. The genuine (narrower) impact is on reminder logic: AgentComposer.cs:88 treats a Skipped slot as "resolved," so a fabricated Skipped copied onto today would suppress a legitimate med-reminder nudge for a dose the user never actually skipped — a real health-feature data-integrity concern. Fix in prose: when copying forward, only propagate Status=Taken doses (or reset copied statuses to a neutral/unlogged state), rather than reproducing Missed/Skipped marks the user never recorded for the target day. Confirming on the correctly-located, real underlying defect while noting the primary rationale was overstated.

Remediation (approach)

Copy forward only Status=Taken doses (or reset copied rows to an unlogged/pending state), so 'repeat yesterday' pre-fills a positive checklist without fabricating Missed/Skipped adherence for a day the user has not yet lived, matching the endpoint's documented intent.

Low Security Endpoints: chat / share / social CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Pact progress exposes per-member activity counts to invited-but-not-joined users

src/Api/Endpoints/PactEndpoints.cs:238-245 IDOR on contact/person ids
Evidence
var isOwner = string.Equals(pact.OwnerEmail, callerEmail, StringComparison.Ordinal);
            var isMember = await db.HabitPactMembers.AsNoTracking()
                .AnyAsync(m => m.HabitPactId == id && m.MemberEmail == callerEmail
                               && m.Status != HabitPactMemberStatus.Left, ct);
            if (!isOwner && !isMember) return Results.NotFound();

            var memberEmails = await db.HabitPactMembers.AsNoTracking()
                .Where(m => m.HabitPactId == id && m.Status == HabitPactMemberStatus.Active)
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The progress read admits any member row whose Status != Left, which INCLUDES Status == Invited (a user who was invited but has not accepted). That not-yet-joined user can call GET /api/pacts/{id}/progress and receive every Active participant's matching-event count and met-target flag (each tied to an AppUser id + display name). Because a pact invite only requires being the owner's mutual contact, an invitee who never consents to participate can still read other members' activity-derived counts. The join endpoint deliberately treats Invited as 'not yet a participant', so gating progress on `!= Left` is inconsistent with that model and over-discloses to a pending (possibly-to-be-declined) invitee.

Independent verifier — confirmation
PactEndpoints.cs:236-239: var isMember = await db.HabitPactMembers.AsNoTracking().AnyAsync(m => m.HabitPactId == id && m.MemberEmail == callerEmail && m.Status != HabitPactMemberStatus.Left, ct); if (!isOwner && !isMember) return Results.NotFound();  — contrast PactEndpoints.cs:197 "if (member.Status != HabitPactMemberStatus.Invited)" where join treats Invited as a pending non-participant; enum HabitPactMember.cs:7 "Invited = 0". Disclosed row PactEndpoints.cs:262-263: new PactProgressRowDto(a.Id, ...a.Name, c, c >= pact.TargetIntValue).

Confirmed. The progress authorization gate admits any member whose Status != Left, which includes Status == Invited (enum value 0). The join endpoint (lines 197-200) deliberately treats Invited as a not-yet-accepted pending invite, so gating progress on != Left is inconsistent and lets a pending (possibly-to-decline) invitee read every Active participant's AppUser id, display name, activity count, and metTarget flag (PactProgressRowDto, lines 262-263). Invites only require mutual-contact status of the owner (InviteMembersAsync docstring, lines 278-282), so consent to participate is not required to read the data. Impact is genuinely Low: the invitee is an already-vetted mutual contact and the disclosed data is aggregate activity counts, not raw PII/health records. Fix by tightening the isMember check to Status == Active (with owner still allowed), matching the participant model used by join. Severity Low is correct; corrected anchor line 238 (the Status != Left predicate).

Remediation (approach)

Gate the progress read on active participation the same way the rest of the pact treats membership: require isOwner OR a member row with Status == Active (mirroring the memberEmails selection just below), rather than Status != Left. That keeps an invited-but-unjoined user (and a declined/left user) from reading other participants' counts until they actually accept.

Low Security Endpoints: admin / users / fleet / ingest CONFIRMED conf: low 1 verifier

Request-log body redaction/exclusion is path-allowlisted, so newly added email/secret-bearing routes silently leak into the Activity log

src/Api/Infrastructure/RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:36-40 Observability / request-log access control
Evidence
private static readonly string[] BodyExcluded = { "/api/users", "/api/audit", "/api/access-policy", "/api/chat/contacts", "/api/chat/directory", };
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Whether a request/response body (which the Activity page replays verbatim to any activity.view holder) is stored is decided by a hand-maintained allowlist of known email-bearing paths, and secret redaction (LogRedaction.cs) by a hand-maintained set of secret paths/field names. This is fail-open: any endpoint added later that carries another user's email or PII in its body — e.g. the location endpoints echo a user's raw lat/lng in the POST /api/location request+response, and fleet reassign/delete responses — is captured in full and shown to every activity.view holder unless someone remembers to extend BodyExcluded. The invariant ('other users' PII must not be replayed to activity.view') is not enforced structurally, only by a list that must be kept in sync as endpoints grow.

Independent verifier — confirmation
RequestLoggingMiddleware.cs:36-40 — private static readonly string[] BodyExcluded = { "/api/users", "/api/audit", "/api/access-policy", "/api/chat/contacts", "/api/chat/directory", }; ... LogRedaction.cs:16-17 SecretKeys = "token|id_token|...|DiscordWebhookUrl" (no lat/lng/location fields); LogRedaction.cs:28-33 IsSensitivePath does not include /api/location; LocationEndpoints.cs:40 g.MapPost("/", ...) with body {Lat,Lng} clamped and echoed; ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:76-83 returns RequestBody/ResponseBody gated only by Permissions.ActivityView.

Verified against actual code. Body persistence is gated by a hand-maintained path allowlist (BodyExcluded, lines 36-40); secret redaction (LogRedaction) covers only a fixed set of secret field-names and sensitive path prefixes. Neither /api/location nor its Lat/Lng field names are covered, so POST /api/location's request body (raw coordinates) is captured verbatim (LogRedaction.Redact leaves it intact) and stored in RequestLog. ObservabilityEndpoints returns RequestBody/ResponseBody to any Permissions.ActivityView holder (line 83). The design is fail-open: newly added PII/coordinate-bearing routes leak unless someone remembers to extend BodyExcluded — the invariant is enforced by a list, not structurally. Finding is real and correctly located. Severity Low is appropriate: activity.view is a privileged permission (not general users), and the location body contains the caller's own coordinates rather than a third party's email; the fleet-response example was not independently verified but the core location example holds. Corrected line set to 36 (start of the BodyExcluded declaration).

Remediation (approach)

Move to a fail-closed posture for body capture: default to NOT storing request/response bodies and opt specific diagnostic routes IN, or centrally scrub email-shaped and coordinate-shaped values from every captured body the way the audit Detail masking already does. At minimum add a test that fails when a new /api route returning another user's email/PII is not on the body-exclusion list, so the allowlist can't silently drift out of date.

Low Security Endpoints: tracker / AI / agents / misc CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

75-Hard day confession can never be cleared and ConfessionsUsed only ever increments (monotonic mutation)

src/Api/Endpoints/HardChallengeEndpoints.cs:1225-1227 Ownership checks / data integrity (75-Hard confessions)
Evidence
private static void ApplyDayManual(HardChallengeDay row, UpsertDayRequest req, string? confession, DateTime now)
{
    if (req.NoAlcohol is { } na) row.NoAlcohol = na;
    if (req.DietOverride is { } d) row.DietOverride = d;
    if (confession is not null) row.Confession = confession;   // never assigns null → cannot clear
    row.UpdatedUtc = now;
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

In the PUT /day handler an empty/whitespace confession is normalized to null (line 221-222), and ApplyDayManual only writes the confession when it is non-null. So once a confession is set, the owner can NEVER erase it by submitting an empty value — the private free-text narration is sticky. Combined with lines 266-271, challenge.ConfessionsUsed is only ever incremented (never decremented), so the counter can drift permanently above the true number of confessed days after edits, and the private text remains stored against the owner's wishes. This is the caller's own private data (no cross-user exposure), so impact is confined to data integrity / a privacy-hygiene expectation, not an IDOR.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Endpoints/HardChallengeEndpoints.cs:1225 `if (confession is not null) row.Confession = confession;` — combined with line 222 `if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(confession)) confession = null;` and lines 266-271 `if (!hadConfession && confession is not null) { challenge.ConfessionsUsed += 1; ... }`

Verified against the actual code. Line 222 normalizes an empty/whitespace confession to null; ApplyDayManual (line 1225) only assigns row.Confession when confession is non-null, so once set the owner can NEVER erase the private free-text narration by submitting an empty value — it is sticky, contrary to a reasonable privacy-hygiene expectation. ConfessionsUsed (lines 266-271) is only ever incremented, never decremented, so it is a monotonic counter. All queries are scoped to caller.Email, so this is the caller's own private data — no cross-user IDOR. Impact is confined to data integrity / privacy hygiene, so Low severity is correct. One caveat on the claim's wording: the !hadConfession guard prevents double-counting on re-edits of an already-confessed day, so within this handler the counter does not spuriously drift upward from repeated edits, and since a confession can never be cleared here the counter stays consistent with the stored (sticky) data. The core, unambiguous defect — a confession cannot be cleared — is real, correctly located at line 1225, and impactful. Confirmed.

Remediation (approach)

Distinguish an ABSENT confession field (leave unchanged) from an explicitly-empty one (clear). Carry the raw request nullability into ApplyDayManual so that when the client sends an empty string it sets row.Confession = null, and correspondingly decrement challenge.ConfessionsUsed when a previously-present confession is cleared (guarding against going below zero). Keep the increment path as-is for the first-time set.

Low Security Services: SSRF / secrets / HTTP CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

IpGeoService calls ip-api.com over cleartext http:// with redirects enabled

src/Api/Program.cs:174-176 SSRF / external HTTP
Evidence
builder.Services.AddHttpClient(IpGeoService.HttpClientName, c =>
{
    c.BaseAddress = new Uri("http://ip-api.com");
    c.Timeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(8);
});
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The IP-geo lookup is issued over plaintext HTTP (the free ip-api tier has no TLS), so the target machine's public IP travels in cleartext and the JSON reply (city/region/lat/lon that becomes a machine's fleet-map location) is trivially observable and tamperable by any on-path party. Because this client also keeps the default AllowAutoRedirect=true, an on-path attacker who can spoof the plaintext response can 302 the request to an arbitrary host, turning a fixed-host call into an attacker-directed fetch (a limited SSRF), or simply inject a false location for a machine. It runs unattended on the ingest/backfill path.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Program.cs:172-176
builder.Services.AddHttpClient(IpGeoService.HttpClientName, c =>
{
    c.BaseAddress = new Uri("http://ip-api.com");
    c.Timeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(8);
});

Verified in Program.cs lines 172-176: the ipgeo named HttpClient uses a plaintext http:// base address, and no HttpClientHandler overrides the default AllowAutoRedirect=true, so both claimed conditions (cleartext + redirects enabled) hold. The free ip-api tier genuinely offers no TLS. IpGeoService.cs confirms the finding's own caveats: host is fixed (not user-controlled), IP is validated and Uri-escaped, private/loopback/link-local IPs are skipped, and the response is parsed read-only into rounded lat/lng + short strings with a status check (lines 36-71). Thus no request secrets/credentials transit the cleartext channel and there is no user-directed SSRF. The residual risk is exactly as claimed: an already-positioned on-path (MITM) attacker between the AWS host and ip-api.com could observe the machine's public IP, tamper the coarse fleet-map location, or 302-redirect a no-secret GET to an arbitrary host. This is a genuine but narrow, cosmetic-impact hardening gap requiring a privileged network position. Severity Low is correct. Fix (prose): switch the base address to https:// (paid tier or a TLS-capable geocoder), and construct the client with a handler that sets AllowAutoRedirect=false so a spoofed response cannot redirect the fixed-host call elsewhere.

Remediation (approach)

Prefer the HTTPS ip-api endpoint (or a TLS-capable geo provider) so the request/response can't be observed or forged on-path, and disable auto-redirect on this client so a spoofed 3xx can't redirect the call. At minimum, treat the response as fully untrusted (which the parser already does) and disable redirects.

Low Security Services: SSRF / secrets / HTTP CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

WorkoutX GIF proxy reads unbounded upstream bytes and echoes the upstream Content-Type verbatim to the browser

src/Api/Services/WorkoutXService.cs:129-131 GIF/image proxy / open-proxy abuse
Evidence
var contentType = res.Content.Headers.ContentType?.MediaType ?? "image/gif";
var bytes = await res.Content.ReadAsByteArrayAsync(ct);
return new GifResult(bytes, contentType);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

GetGifAsync buffers the entire upstream response into a byte[] with no size cap and no MaxResponseContentBufferSize on the client, then TrackerEndpoints returns Results.Bytes(g.Bytes, g.ContentType) with the upstream-supplied Content-Type. If the WorkoutX host ever returns an oversized body (or is compromised/MITM'd), a single request allocates the whole payload in process memory (per-request memory-exhaustion vector), and the pass-through Content-Type lets the trusted-origin API response serve a non-image media type (e.g. text/html) that the browser could render in the app's origin. Host is fixed and the id is digits-only, so this is not an arbitrary open proxy, but the unbounded buffer + reflected content-type is below enterprise standard for a media proxy.

Independent verifier — confirmation
WorkoutXService.cs:129-131: `var contentType = res.Content.Headers.ContentType?.MediaType ?? "image/gif"; var bytes = await res.Content.ReadAsByteArrayAsync(ct); return new GifResult(bytes, contentType);` | Program.cs:120-121: `builder.Services.AddHttpClient(WorkoutXService.HttpClientName, c => c.Timeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(15));` (no MaxResponseContentBufferSize) | TrackerEndpoints.cs:1136: `return Results.Bytes(g.Bytes, g.ContentType);`

All technical claims verified against actual code. GetGifAsync calls ReadAsByteArrayAsync with no size cap (line 130) and the named HttpClient (Program.cs:120-121) sets only Timeout, never MaxResponseContentBufferSize, so a misbehaving/compromised/MITM'd upstream can force a full-payload per-request allocation. The upstream Content-Type is echoed verbatim (line 129) and passed to Results.Bytes (TrackerEndpoints.cs:1136), so a non-image media type (e.g. text/html) could be served from the trusted API origin. Not an open proxy: host is fixed (no SSRF), id is digits-only and route-constrained :int, and the endpoint is JWT-authorized + RequirePermission(TrackerSelf) + RequireRateLimiting(GifRateLimitPolicy), with a 15s timeout. These guards keep impact low but do not cap body size nor validate/pin the response content type, which is below enterprise standard for a media proxy. Remediation (prose): set MaxResponseContentBufferSize on the workoutx HttpClient and/or check Content-Length before buffering; and either serve a fixed image/gif content type or allowlist image/* rather than reflecting the upstream value. Finding is real, correctly located, Low severity appropriate.

Remediation (approach)

Cap the download (set MaxResponseContentBufferSize on the workoutx client and/or read with a hard byte ceiling, rejecting anything larger) and do not trust the upstream Content-Type — force a safe, fixed image/gif (or validate it against an image allowlist) before streaming the bytes back, ideally with a nosniff response header.

Low Security Injection / committed secrets / config CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Google Calendar OAuth token exchange reads the response body with no size cap (unbounded allocation on a compromised/MITM'd token endpoint)

src/Api/Services/GoogleCalendarService.cs:501-502 Unsafe deserialization / unbounded allocation
Evidence
var bytes = await res.Content.ReadAsByteArrayAsync(ct);
            return JsonDocument.Parse(bytes);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

This is the auth-code exchange + refresh-token grant against Google's token endpoint. Round 2 reported the identical unbounded-read pattern in FitbitHealthProvider's Fitbit/Google *health* token exchanges, but this Family-Hub Google Calendar provider is a separate class/file that was not covered. ReadAsByteArrayAsync buffers the entire response into memory with no MaxResponseContentBufferSize / no length guard, so a compromised or MITM'd token endpoint (or a proxy that injects a huge body) can force an arbitrarily large allocation and OOM the API process. Same class of DoS as the reported Fitbit finding, distinct location.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Services/GoogleCalendarService.cs:501-502:
            var bytes = await res.Content.ReadAsByteArrayAsync(ct);
            return JsonDocument.Parse(bytes);
--- HttpClient registration (no size cap), src/Api/Program.cs:193-197:
builder.Services.AddHttpClient(GoogleCalendarService.HttpClientName, c =>
{
    c.BaseAddress = new Uri("https://www.googleapis.com");
    c.Timeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(15);
});

Verified by reading the file. PostTokenAsync reads the entire token-endpoint response via ReadAsByteArrayAsync(ct) with no length guard, and the 'google-calendar' HttpClient (Program.cs:193-197) sets only BaseAddress and a 15s Timeout — no MaxResponseContentBufferSize. So an unbounded body from a compromised/MITM'd endpoint (or injecting proxy) buffers fully into memory. This is a real, distinct location from the previously-reported FitbitHealthProvider finding (separate class/file/HttpClient), and the same unbounded-read class of DoS. Low severity is correct: the target is a FIXED Google host (not user-controlled, so no SSRF and normal path is safe), the 15s timeout bounds sustained transfer, and it fires only on low-frequency OAuth code-exchange/refresh. Fix in prose: set a MaxResponseContentBufferSize on the google-calendar HttpClient registration, or stream-parse with a bounded read and reject responses exceeding a small expected token-JSON size — mirroring whatever cap was applied to the Fitbit provider fix.

Remediation (approach)

Cap the buffered token response the same way the other outbound calls should be capped: set a bounded MaxResponseContentBufferSize on the named HttpClient (or read via a length-limited stream and reject bodies over a few KB before parsing). A legitimate OAuth token JSON is well under a kilobyte, so a small ceiling is safe. Apply the same bound uniformly across every ReadAsByteArrayAsync token/GIF read (Google Calendar, Fitbit, WorkoutX) so no single upstream can force an unbounded allocation.

Low Security Frontend security CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Persisted auth session is JSON.parse-cast to AuthSession with no shape validation, so any localStorage tamper/XSS forges identity, permissions, and expiry the client trusts

src/Web/src/app/core/auth.ts:179-183 Frontend security (XSS / token / redirect)
Evidence
private restore(): AuthSession | null {
  try {
    const raw = localStorage.getItem(STORAGE_KEY);
    return raw ? (JSON.parse(raw) as AuthSession) : null;
  } catch {
    return null;
  }
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

restore() blindly casts the parsed blob to AuthSession. isAuthenticated (line 15-18), token (line 138-141), permissions (line 19) and canAccessHome all read fields straight off it, including the client-trusted expiresAtUtc and permissions array. An attacker with any XSS foothold (the token-in-localStorage exposure already reported) — or a shared/kiosk device where localStorage is writable — can plant a session object with an arbitrary far-future expiresAtUtc and a full permissions list. The forged token still fails server RequirePermission checks, but the client will render permission-gated admin UI, reveal locally-cached state, expose the email-reveal/permission-grant surfaces, and route the user to admin homes as if authorized, widening the blast radius of any XSS beyond simple token theft. There is no client-side sanity check (userId/token present, expiry plausible) before the session is trusted.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/src/app/core/auth.ts:176-183
private restore(): AuthSession | null {
  try {
    const raw = localStorage.getItem(STORAGE_KEY);
    return raw ? (JSON.parse(raw) as AuthSession) : null;
  } catch {
    return null;
  }
}
And consumers read fields directly: line 17 `new Date(s.expiresAtUtc).getTime() > Date.now()`; line 19 `this._session()?.permissions ?? []`; line 140 returns `s.token`.

Confirmed and correctly located. restore() blind-casts the parsed localStorage blob to AuthSession with no shape/expiry/signature validation, and isAuthenticated (17), permissions (19), token (140), and canAccessHome (42-45) all read fields straight off it. An actor with an XSS foothold or write access to localStorage can plant a session with a far-future expiresAtUtc and a full permissions array, unlocking client-rendered permission-gated UI and locally-cached state and routing to admin homes. Severity Low is correct: the client permission model is never the security boundary — the forged bearer token is unsigned, so every data-bearing request still fails server-side JWT/RequirePermission validation and no PII/health/payment/location data is actually served. Anyone able to write localStorage already fully controls the real session anyway. This is a legitimate defense-in-depth hardening gap (restore should sanity-check userId/token presence and expiry plausibility), not a protected-data disclosure. Real, Low.

Remediation (approach)

Validate the restored object before trusting it: require the expected fields to be present and correctly typed (string token, ISO expiresAtUtc that parses, array permissions), and discard/clear the session on any mismatch. Treat the stored session as untrusted input rather than an as-cast type. Longer term, moving the bearer out of localStorage (as already recommended) removes both the theft and the forgery vector.

Low Security & Correctness Reporter / agent / ingestion CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

A single newline-free giant file is loaded into one string by ReadLine (potential OOM)

src/ReporterCore/LogScanner.cs:164-169 JSONL parsing robustness — memory/OOM
Evidence
using var stream = new FileStream(path, FileMode.Open, FileAccess.Read, FileShare.ReadWrite);
using var reader = new StreamReader(stream);
return parser.Parse(reader, path).ToList();
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

All three parsers read via reader.ReadLine(). ReadLine has no per-line length cap, so a *.jsonl file with no newline (e.g. a corrupted multi-GB blob, or an unexpected binary file that happens to match the extension) is materialized as a single string, and JsonDocument.Parse then allocates on top of it — an out-of-memory crash of the reporter/agent. Impact is bounded because the files scanned are the user's own local tool logs rather than remote input, but a corrupt log can still take the background agent down.

Independent verifier — confirmation
LogScanner.cs:164-168: using var stream = new FileStream(path, FileMode.Open, FileAccess.Read, FileShare.ReadWrite); using var reader = new StreamReader(stream); return parser.Parse(reader, path).ToList();  — ClaudeParser.cs:24 `while ((line = reader.ReadLine()) is not null)` (same in CodexParser.cs:29, GeminiParser.cs:66), no per-line length cap. LogScanner.cs:96 reads fi.Length but only for change-detection (line 99 IsUnchanged), never as a size guard before parsing.

Verified real and correctly located. All three parsers (ClaudeParser:24, CodexParser:29, GeminiParser:66) consume the reader via StreamReader.ReadLine(), which has no per-line length cap; ParseFile opens the file with no size guard (fi.Length at line 96 is used only for change detection, not to bound parsing). A newline-free giant/corrupt .jsonl would be materialized as one string plus JsonDocument/JsonSerializer allocation, risking OOM in the background reporter. Severity Low is appropriate and honestly scoped by the finding itself: input is the user's own local CLI tool logs, not remote/attacker-controlled data, so the blast radius is a self-inflicted crash of the local agent rather than a security exposure. Remediation in prose: enforce a maximum line-length / file-size threshold (e.g. skip or truncate files above a sane cap, or use a bounded read that abandons a line exceeding N bytes) before feeding content to the JSON parser, and log/skip oversized or non-text files rather than loading them wholesale.

Remediation (approach)

Guard file size before parsing (skip or warn on files far larger than any plausible transcript) and/or read with a bounded line length, treating an over-long line as a skip. Also consider capping total rows accumulated per file so one pathological file cannot exhaust memory via ToList().

Low Security & Correctness Reporter / agent / ingestion CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Family quick-add path sends the ingest key with no http/cleartext guard, unlike every other send path

src/Agent/Views/QuickAddWindow.xaml.cs:66 Reporter ingest-key storage & transport
Evidence
var opt = ReporterConfig.LoadFile();           // non-secret (URL, machine, paths…)
var url = opt.Url?.Trim();
var key = ReporterConfig.ReadKeyFile();        // the secret ingest key
if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(url) || string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(key))
{ ShowStatus("Set your server URL and ingest key in Settings first."); return; }
SetBusy(true);
try
{
    using var client = new QuickAddClient(url, key);
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

ReporterOptions.Validate and the console/PowerShell entry points all warn when Url is http:// to a non-local host because the ingest key travels in cleartext. The quick-add path added for the tray app performs no such scheme check and no warning: if the user's saved Url is http:// (which Validate explicitly permits), the secret X-Ingest-Key is POSTed to /api/family/quick-add over plaintext with no indication. This is a distinct, unguarded egress of the same secret the rest of the code takes pains to protect.

Independent verifier — confirmation
QuickAddClient.cs:36 `_http.DefaultRequestHeaders.Add("X-Ingest-Key", key);` sends the secret with no scheme check; QuickAddWindow.xaml.cs:53-66 resolves url/key and does `using var client = new QuickAddClient(url, key);` with no http guard. Contrast: Reporter/Program.cs:56-57 `if (Uri.TryCreate(opt.Url,...,out var parsed) && parsed.Scheme == "http" && !parsed.IsLoopback) ui.Warn("--url is http:// ... key is sent in cleartext. Use https.");` and Run-UsageIqReporter.ps1:78-79 same warning; ReporterOptions.cs:92-93 explicitly permits http.

Premise verified end-to-end. Validate() (ReporterOptions.cs:92-93) permits http://; the only cleartext warnings for the X-Ingest-Key secret live in the console reporter (Program.cs:55-57) and the PowerShell launcher (Run-UsageIqReporter.ps1:78-79) — exactly the "console/PowerShell entry points" named. The quick-add path (QuickAddWindow.xaml.cs:53-66 → QuickAddClient.cs:36) POSTs the same X-Ingest-Key header to /api/family/quick-add with zero scheme check or warning anywhere in the Agent code (grep for cleartext/IsLoopback/http in Agent returns nothing). So it is a real, correctly-located, unguarded egress of the same secret. Downgrading Medium→Low: the existing warnings are advisory-only (they never block the send), the target URL is user-owned/self-configured, and this exposes no secret the reporter isn't already sending over the same http URL — it is a defense-in-depth/consistency gap, not a new vulnerability. Line corrected to 66 (the actual QuickAddClient construction/send).

Remediation (approach)

Before constructing QuickAddClient, apply the same scheme check used in the console/Program.cs (warn or refuse when the resolved URL is http:// to a non-loopback host). Ideally centralize the cleartext-key guard so any code path that attaches X-Ingest-Key inherits it, rather than re-implementing it per call site.

Low Security & Correctness Reporter / agent / ingestion CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Atomic-write temp files leave the plaintext key/config under a second filename that key-management flows ignore

src/ReporterCore/ReporterConfig.cs:140-143 Reporter ingest-key storage (plaintext at rest)
Evidence
public static void SaveKeyFile(string key)
{
    Directory.CreateDirectory(Dir);
    var tmp = KeyPath + ".tmp";
    File.WriteAllText(tmp, key.Trim());
    File.Move(tmp, KeyPath, overwrite: true);
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The temp file reporter.key.tmp is created with the process's default inherited ACL and contains the full plaintext ingest key. If the process is killed or the disk errors between WriteAllText and File.Move, the plaintext secret persists at ~/.usage-iq/reporter.key.tmp indefinitely. Any operator hygiene that treats only reporter.key as the secret (rotation, deletion, backup exclusion, the docs' 'keep that file private' guidance) misses the .tmp sibling. The same orphan-temp pattern applies to config.json.tmp and the state file, but reporter.key.tmp is the sensitive one.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/ReporterCore/ReporterConfig.cs:137-143
public static void SaveKeyFile(string key)
{
    Directory.CreateDirectory(Dir);
    var tmp = KeyPath + ".tmp";
    File.WriteAllText(tmp, key.Trim());
    File.Move(tmp, KeyPath, overwrite: true);
}

Confirmed exactly as claimed. Lines 140-142 write the full plaintext ingest key to reporter.key.tmp then rename it. If the process dies (crash/kill/power loss/disk error) between WriteAllText (141) and File.Move (142), the plaintext key persists at ~/.usage-iq/reporter.key.tmp. The file's own docs (line 11, 136) and design treat only reporter.key as "the secret," so rotation/deletion/backup-exclusion/hygiene flows keyed on that filename miss the .tmp sibling. Same orphan-temp pattern exists for config.json.tmp (line 116-118) but reporter.key.tmp is the sensitive one. Severity Low is correct: the base reporter.key is already plaintext at rest with the same inherited ACL, so the .tmp adds no new exposure surface beyond unnoticed persistence, and the trigger is a narrow crash window. Corrected the anchor line to 140 (where the .tmp path is constructed) within the cited 137-143 range.

Remediation (approach)

Write the temp file with a restrictive ACL/permission set before writing content (owner-only), and add best-effort cleanup that removes a stale reporter.key.tmp on load/save. Any 'delete/rotate key' flow should also remove the .tmp sibling. Longer term, protect the secret at rest (e.g. Windows DPAPI/CurrentUser) so an orphaned temp is not plaintext.

Low Security & Correctness Reporter / agent / ingestion CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

LogScanner skip-list uses backslash-delimited segments that never match on a non-Windows reporter, so node_modules/.tmp trees are scanned and parsed

src/ReporterCore/LogScanner.cs:51 Ingestion path handling / cross-platform robustness
Evidence
private static readonly string[] SkipSegments = { @"\.tmp\", @"\node_modules\", "plugins-backup" };
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The reporter targets net9.0 with no Windows-only TFM and resolves ~/.claude, ~/.codex, ~/.gemini via Path.Combine, so it runs on macOS/Linux too. There, filesystem paths are '/'-separated, but the first two skip segments are hard-coded with backslashes, so ShouldSkip (line 171, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase) never matches '/node_modules/' or '/.tmp/'. The comment claims this mirrors the server's skip list, yet on any POSIX host the reporter will walk into and parse huge vendored node_modules .jsonl files, wasting CPU/IO and feeding attacker-controlled JSON to the parsers on every pass. It also diverges from the server's intended file set, contradicting the stated invariant.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/ReporterCore/LogScanner.cs:51  private static readonly string[] SkipSegments = { @"\.tmp\", @"\node_modules\", "plugins-backup" };
src/ReporterCore/LogScanner.cs:171-172  private static bool ShouldSkip(string path) => SkipSegments.Any(seg => path.Contains(seg, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase));

Confirmed. The ReporterCore project targets net9.0 with no Windows-only TFM (csproj line 4; comment at lines 24-28 confirms deliberate cross-platform compilation), and roots resolve via Path.Combine(Home, ".claude"/".codex"/".gemini") in ReporterOptions.cs (lines 58-64), so the reporter runs on macOS/Linux. On those hosts filesystem paths are '/'-separated, but two of the three SkipSegments are hard-coded with backslashes (@"\.tmp\", @"\node_modules\"). ShouldSkip (line 172) does path.Contains(seg, OrdinalIgnoreCase), so those backslash segments can NEVER match a POSIX path — only the third bare token "plugins-backup" would. Result: on any POSIX host the walker descends into node_modules/.tmp trees and, for any *.jsonl there that a parser's MatchesFile accepts, opens and parses it (lines 85-102), wasting CPU/IO and feeding vendored/attacker-controlled JSON to the parsers, while diverging from the server's intended skip set (contradicting the "mirror the server's skip list" comment at line 50). Impact is bounded because the walk is still limited to *.jsonl and gated by parser.MatchesFile, so Low severity is correct. Finding is real, correctly located at line 51, mechanism accurately described.

Remediation (approach)

Match path segments in a separator-agnostic way: normalize separators before comparison (or compare against both '/' and '\\' forms, or split on Path.DirectorySeparatorChar/AltDirectorySeparatorChar and compare segment names). Ensure the reporter and server share the same normalized skip logic.

Low Security & Correctness Reporter / agent / ingestion CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Deferred GPS rebuild runs as fire-and-forget Task that can stop/rebuild the engine concurrently with a Settings-triggered Reload, and swallows its own faults

src/Agent/Services/AgentController.cs:228-244 Unobserved exceptions / lifecycle race
Evidence
private void OnGpsFixAcquired() => Task.Run(() =>
{
    ...
    Stop(keepEngine: false);
    ReporterEngine.ResetMachineInfoCache();
    lock (_gate) _engine = BuildEngine(opt);
    if (wasRunning) Start();
    Emit(LogLine.Info("location fix acquired — telemetry now includes precise GPS"));
});
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

OnGpsFixAcquired reads _options/_loopTask under the lock but then performs Stop()/BuildEngine()/Start() outside any single critical section, using a captured opt. If the user saves Settings (AgentController.Reload -> Stop + rebuild) at nearly the same moment the deferred fix lands, the two rebuild paths interleave: the GPS task can dispose/replace an engine the Reload just built (or rebuild with the pre-save opt), leaving the agent watching with stale config or in an inconsistent running state. Any exception thrown inside this fire-and-forget Task (e.g. from Start/BuildEngine) is not awaited and only reaches the AppDomain UnobservedTaskException sink, so the GPS rebuild can fail silently mid-way, leaving the engine stopped while the UI still shows Running.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Agent/Services/AgentController.cs:228-244
private void OnGpsFixAcquired() => Task.Run(() =>
{
    ReporterOptions? opt;
    bool wasRunning;
    lock (_gate) { opt = _options; wasRunning = _loopTask is { IsCompleted: false }; }
    if (opt is null || opt.Validate() is not null) return;
    Stop(keepEngine: false);
    ReporterEngine.ResetMachineInfoCache();
    lock (_gate) _engine = BuildEngine(opt);
    if (wasRunning) Start();
    Emit(LogLine.Info("location fix acquired — telemetry now includes precise GPS"));
});

Confirmed as read. The GPS-fix handler is a discarded Task.Run (arrow body, not awaited, no inner try/catch) — a fault from Stop/BuildEngine/Start faults the orphan Task and only reaches the UnobservedTaskException sink, potentially leaving the engine Stop()'d while _status still reads Running. Separately, the rebuild sequence (Stop → ResetMachineInfoCache → lock BuildEngine → Start) is NOT one atomic critical section; each _gate acquisition is independent, so it can interleave with Reload() (also Stop + rebuild under a separate _gate block). A concurrent Settings save can have the GPS task overwrite _engine with the pre-save captured opt (line 234), yielding a running engine with stale config. Both claimed defects are code-accurate, correctly located (lines 228-244), and Low severity is appropriate: the trigger requires a one-time deferred GPS fix to land within a narrow window of a manual Settings save, and the worst case is stale telemetry config or a silently stopped agent — no corruption/security impact. correctedLine set to 228 (the discarded Task.Run entry point) but the whole 228-244 block is the locus.

Remediation (approach)

Serialize all engine (re)build transitions through a single lock/queue so a GPS-driven rebuild and a Settings reload cannot interleave, re-read _options under the lock at the moment of rebuild rather than using a captured copy, and wrap the deferred work in try/catch that surfaces failures via the Log/StatusChanged channels instead of relying on the global unobserved-exception handler.

Low Security & Correctness Reporter / agent / ingestion CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Reveal toggle leaves the plaintext ingest key in the KeyPlain TextBox even after the user switches back to masked mode

src/Agent/Views/SettingsWindow.xaml.cs:61-66 Secret in-memory exposure
Evidence
else
{
    KeyBox.Password = KeyPlain.Text;
    KeyBox.Visibility = Visibility.Visible;
    KeyPlain.Visibility = Visibility.Collapsed;
    RevealButton.Content = "Show";
}
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Prior rounds noted that revealing copies the secret into a plain TextBox. The subtler second-order defect: pressing Hide copies KeyPlain.Text back into the masked PasswordBox but never CLEARS KeyPlain.Text. The plaintext key therefore remains resident in the collapsed TextBox for the lifetime of the Settings window - still reachable via the UI-automation/accessibility tree and in a control whose contents (unlike PasswordBox) are not afforded any of WPF's secure-string handling - even though the user believes they re-masked it. Screen readers and automation clients can still read it after Hide.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Agent/Views/SettingsWindow.xaml.cs:59-65
        else
        {
            KeyBox.Password = KeyPlain.Text;
            KeyBox.Visibility = Visibility.Visible;
            KeyPlain.Visibility = Visibility.Collapsed;
            RevealButton.Content = "Show";
        }

Verified against the actual code. The Hide branch (lines 59-65) copies KeyPlain.Text back into KeyBox.Password and collapses the TextBox but never resets KeyPlain.Text, so the plaintext ingest key stays resident in the collapsed TextBox for the window's lifetime and remains readable via the UI-automation/accessibility tree, which a masked PasswordBox would not expose. The defect is real and correctly located (the missing clear belongs at line 61). Severity Low is appropriate, not higher: the exposure is confined to the same user's own desktop session and the same process that already holds the plaintext (it was intentionally copied into KeyPlain at line 54 on Reveal, and lives in GC-held managed strings and the PasswordBox regardless). An attacker able to read the automation tree of this window is already inside the user's session and could read the in-process key or the reporter.key file anyway, so there is no cross-boundary escalation. Remediation in prose: in the Hide branch, after copying the value back to the PasswordBox, overwrite KeyPlain.Text with an empty string (and ideally clear it on window close) so no plaintext copy lingers in the accessible TextBox.

Remediation (approach)

On Hide, after copying the value into the PasswordBox, explicitly clear KeyPlain.Text (and clear it again on window close). Better, avoid mirroring the secret into a TextBox at all - drive the reveal from a single backing field and render masked vs. plain without ever persisting the cleartext in an automation-exposed control.

Low Testing & CI/CD Test coverage & quality CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Log-assertion tests rely on a fixed-interval Task.Delay polling loop with a hard timeout (mild flake risk under load)

tests/Ccusage.Api.Tests/Integration/AuthIntegrationTests.cs:573-581 Test quality
Evidence
    private async Task<JsonElement> WaitForLog(HttpClient admin, Func<JsonElement, bool> match)
    {
        for (var i = 0; i < 50; i++) // ~7.5s
        {
            var logs = await (await admin.GetAsync("/api/logs?take=500")).Content.ReadFromJsonAsync<JsonElement>();
            foreach (var e in logs.EnumerateArray())
                if (match(e)) return e;
            await Task.Delay(150);
        }
        throw new Xunit.Sdk.XunitException("Expected log entry did not appear in time.");
    }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Because request logging is asynchronous, ~9 tests (redaction, response-body capture, exclusion rules) depend on this bounded poll. The loop is tolerant (retries, reasonable ceiling) so it is far better than a single Task.Delay, but under a loaded CI runner the async log-writer hosted service can miss the ~7.5s window, producing an intermittent failure that is unrelated to the code under test. This is a classic time-coupled flaky pattern to watch, not a correctness bug.

Independent verifier — confirmation
tests/Ccusage.Api.Tests/Integration/AuthIntegrationTests.cs:573-580
for (var i = 0; i < 50; i++) // ~7.5s
{
    var logs = await (await admin.GetAsync("/api/logs?take=500")).Content.ReadFromJsonAsync<JsonElement>();
    foreach (var e in logs.EnumerateArray())
        if (match(e)) return e;
    await Task.Delay(150);
}
throw new Xunit.Sdk.XunitException("Expected log entry did not appear in time.");

Code matches the evidence exactly: a 50-iteration loop with 150ms Task.Delay (~7.5s ceiling) polling /api/logs, throwing XunitException on timeout. WaitForLog has 8 occurrences (1 definition + 7 call sites), consistent with the ~9-tests claim (async request-logging middleware assertions). The defect is real, accurately located, and correctly rated Low: it is a bounded, tolerant polling helper (the recommended pattern for async work) but is still time-coupled with a fixed wall-clock ceiling, so under a loaded CI runner the async log-writer hosted service could miss the window and produce an intermittent, code-unrelated failure. This is a test-quality/flake-risk observation, not a correctness bug, exactly as the finding characterizes it. Suggested remediation (prose): make the timeout configurable/generous or drive it off a signal from the log-writer (e.g., await a completion or flush hook) rather than a fixed retry-count ceiling, so it scales with runner load.

Remediation (approach)

Prefer a deterministic signal over wall-clock polling: expose a test hook to flush/await the request-log writer, or subscribe to a completion signal, so the assertion proceeds the moment the row is durable rather than racing a timeout. If polling is retained, make the ceiling generous and driven by an overall test timeout rather than a fixed iteration count.

Low Testing & CI/CD Test coverage & quality CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Auth-log polling helper (WaitForLog) uses a fixed 150ms Task.Delay loop with a hard ~7.5s ceiling — mild flake risk under CI load

tests/Ccusage.Api.Tests/Integration/AuthIntegrationTests.cs:573-581 Flaky patterns
Evidence
for (var i = 0; i < 50; i++) // ~7.5s
{
    var logs = await (await admin.GetAsync("/api/logs?take=500")).Content.ReadFromJsonAsync<JsonElement>();
    foreach (var e in logs.EnumerateArray())
        if (match(e)) return e;
    await Task.Delay(150);
}
throw new Xunit.Sdk.XunitException("Expected log entry did not appear in time.");
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The request/response action log is written by an async hosted writer, so these tests depend on wall-clock timing to observe it. The whole integration collection is serialized into ONE xUnit collection sharing a single Postgres container and app host; under a loaded GitHub-hosted runner the ~7.5s ceiling (50 x 150ms plus the /api/logs round-trip cost) can be reached before the drain completes, surfacing as an intermittent XunitException rather than a real defect. Multiple such log-assertion tests (Requests_are_captured, Auth_route_bodies_are_redacted, Query_string_secrets_are_redacted, Health_and_polling_routes_are_not_logged, Response_bodies_are_captured, Share_token_is_redacted, Discord_webhook_url_is_redacted) all share this helper, multiplying the exposure.

Independent verifier — confirmation
tests/Ccusage.Api.Tests/Integration/AuthIntegrationTests.cs:573-580
for (var i = 0; i < 50; i++) // ~7.5s
{
    var logs = await (await admin.GetAsync("/api/logs?take=500")).Content.ReadFromJsonAsync<JsonElement>();
    foreach (var e in logs.EnumerateArray())
        if (match(e)) return e;
    await Task.Delay(150);
}
throw new Xunit.Sdk.XunitException("Expected log entry did not appear in time.");

Verified the cited lines match the evidence exactly. WaitForLog polls /api/logs in a bounded 50-iteration loop with a fixed 150ms Task.Delay and throws XunitException on the hard ~7.5s ceiling. The log entries are produced by an async hosted writer, so the assertion depends on wall-clock drain timing rather than a deterministic signal (e.g. an async barrier or flush). Under a loaded CI runner with the serialized integration collection sharing one Postgres container and host, the ceiling can plausibly be hit before drain completes, surfacing as an intermittent failure that is not a real defect. This is a legitimate Low-severity flaky-test pattern; severity and location are correct. It is test-only code with no production impact, so Low is appropriate.

Remediation (approach)

Prefer a deterministic drain: expose a test hook to flush/await the log-writer channel, or have the writer signal completion, so the assertion runs after the row is guaranteed persisted instead of after a timed poll. If polling must stay, raise the ceiling generously and/or back off exponentially, and make the timeout tunable so a slow runner does not fail a correct system.

Low Testing & CI/CD Test coverage & quality CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Double-post guard relies on a fixed 300ms sleep to 'give a second enqueue a chance to land' — a timing race that can pass while a real double-post exists

tests/Ccusage.Api.Tests/Integration/AutomationsTests.cs:492-496 Flaky patterns
Evidence
(await WaitForDiscord(before)).Should().BeTrue();
// Exactly ONE Discord post, to the RULE's webhook — not a second mirror to the per-user webhook.
await Task.Delay(300); // give any (erroneous) second enqueue a chance to land before asserting "exactly one"
(factory.Discord.Count - before).Should().Be(1, "no double-post: only the rule webhook is used");
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The 'exactly one' assertion is guarding against a duplicate Discord forward, but it depends on an erroneous second enqueue landing within a fixed 300ms window on a shared, serialized test host. Under CI load the duplicate could arrive at 350ms — after the assertion has already passed — so a genuine double-post regression could ship undetected (a false-negative flake, worse than a false failure). Conversely, if the forwarding pipeline is ever made slower, even the first post might not be counted deterministically. Fixed sleeps to bound an asynchronous side effect are inherently order/timing dependent on this single-container collection.

Independent verifier — confirmation
tests/Ccusage.Api.Tests/Integration/AutomationsTests.cs:490-493:
(await WaitForDiscord(before)).Should().BeTrue();
// Exactly ONE Discord post, to the RULE's webhook — not a second mirror to the per-user webhook.
await Task.Delay(300); // give any (erroneous) second enqueue a chance to land before asserting "exactly one"
(factory.Discord.Count - before).Should().Be(1, "no double-post: only the rule webhook is used");
--- WaitForDiscord (line 133-142) returns as soon as `Count - before >= atLeast` with atLeast defaulting to 1.

Confirmed. WaitForDiscord (lines 133-142) polls only until the FIRST post lands (atLeast=1), then the test uses a fixed Task.Delay(300) as the sole window to catch an erroneous SECOND enqueue before asserting exactly-one. This is a classic timing-race: a real double-post regression whose duplicate lands >300ms later (plausible under CI load on a shared serialized test host) passes the assertion silently — a false-negative that lets a genuine regression ship. The pattern and location are exactly as claimed (evidence at lines 490-493). Severity Low is correct: this is test-only code and the effect is a weakened regression guard, not a production defect. A deterministic fix would poll for stability (e.g., assert count stays at 1 across a bounded quiescence window, or make the forwarder's completion observable) rather than sleeping a fixed 300ms.

Remediation (approach)

Replace the fixed delay with a deterministic drain of the Discord forwarding queue (flush-and-await hook), then assert the count. If the intent is to prove no second post EVER occurs, drive the pipeline to quiescence via a signal rather than a guessed duration, so the 'exactly one' invariant does not depend on wall-clock timing.

Low Testing & CI/CD Test coverage & quality CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

'Exactly one Discord post' is asserted after a fixed 300 ms sleep — a slow second enqueue under CI load races the assertion

tests/Ccusage.Api.Tests/Integration/AutomationsTests.cs:492-494 Flaky patterns (fixed-delay race)
Evidence
        (await WaitForDiscord(before)).Should().BeTrue();
        // Exactly ONE Discord post ...
        await Task.Delay(300); // give any (erroneous) second enqueue a chance to land before asserting "exactly one"
        (factory.Discord.Count - before).Should().Be(1, "no double-post: only the rule webhook is used");
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The test polls until the FIRST post lands (good), then sleeps a fixed 300 ms and asserts the count is exactly 1. The negative half ('no second post') is a prove-a-negative bounded only by a hard-coded 300 ms window. Under a loaded CI runner a genuinely-buggy second enqueue that takes >300 ms to reach the capture would slip past the assertion (false pass), and conversely background contention could in principle let a legitimately-async mirror land late and flip a correct run red. Because the whole integration collection is serialized on one Postgres container and one shared DiscordCapture, timing is not isolated. Impact is limited to occasional flake/weakened signal, not production behavior — hence Low.

Independent verifier — confirmation
tests/Ccusage.Api.Tests/Integration/AutomationsTests.cs:490-494
        (await WaitForDiscord(before)).Should().BeTrue();
        // Exactly ONE Discord post, to the RULE's webhook — not a second mirror to the per-user webhook.
        await Task.Delay(300); // give any (erroneous) second enqueue a chance to land before asserting "exactly one"
        (factory.Discord.Count - before).Should().Be(1, "no double-post: only the rule webhook is used");

Verified against the actual code. WaitForDiscord (lines 133-141) polls up to 3000ms for the first post to land, then line 492 sleeps a hard-coded 300ms and line 493 asserts the count delta is exactly 1. This is a genuine fixed-delay race: the positive half (first post) is polled correctly, but the negative half ("no second/mirror post") is bounded only by a 300ms window. A genuinely-buggy second enqueue that reaches DiscordCapture after 300ms would be missed (false pass, weakened signal), and background contention on the serialized single-Postgres/single-DiscordCapture integration collection could in principle let a legitimate late-arriving item flip a correct run red. Impact is test-flake/weakened-signal only — no production behavior is affected — so Low severity is correct. Finding is real, correctly located, and not mitigated elsewhere.

Remediation (approach)

Replace the fixed sleep with a bounded poll that fails fast as soon as the count exceeds 1 (assert-eventually-and-stays), or make the notification pipeline deterministically drainable in tests (as FamilyReminders already is via direct TickAsync) so the 'exactly once' invariant is checked against a quiesced queue rather than a wall-clock guess.

Low Testing & CI/CD Test coverage & quality CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

Non-delivery is proven by a fixed 2 s wait; slow SignalR delivery under load can flip a correct run red

tests/Ccusage.Api.Tests/Integration/ChatIntegrationTests.cs:695-696 Flaky patterns (prove-a-negative timeout)
Evidence
        await outsiderHub.InvokeAsync("JoinChannel", channelId);
        await alice.PostAsJsonAsync($"/api/chat/channels/{channelId}/messages", new { body = "members only" });
        // The outsider must NOT receive the broadcast (join was rejected).
        var delivered = await Task.WhenAny(got.Task, Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(2)));
        (delivered == got.Task).Should().BeFalse();
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

This asserts an outsider does NOT receive a broadcast by racing the TaskCompletionSource against a 2-second timeout. The security invariant under test (a non-member must not receive channel messages) is real and valuable, but the timing model is fragile: if the real hub delivery is merely slow (loaded CI, cold SignalR negotiation over the in-memory TestServer transport) rather than blocked, a message that a correct authorization path would still eventually deliver could arrive within 2 s and produce a confusing failure, while a truly-broken authz that delivers at 2.1 s would pass. It only weakens signal, not production behavior — Low.

Independent verifier — confirmation
// The outsider must NOT receive the broadcast (join was rejected).
var delivered = await Task.WhenAny(got.Task, Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(2)));
(delivered == got.Task).Should().BeFalse();
  (tests/Ccusage.Api.Tests/Integration/ChatIntegrationTests.cs:694-696)

Confirmed: lines 690-696 prove non-delivery by racing a TaskCompletionSource against a fixed 2s Task.Delay (Task.WhenAny + delivered==got.Task assertion). This is a classic prove-a-negative timeout — the absence of the ReceiveMessage event is only ever established probabilistically, so under loaded CI or slow in-memory TestServer SignalR delivery the assertion can behave inconsistently. Contrast the sibling positive-assertion test at line 673 which robustly uses got.Task.WaitAsync(10s). Severity Low is correct: it only weakens test signal, never affecting production authorization behavior. Location and severity are accurate. Minor caveat on rationale: if authz is truly correct the outsider is never added to the SignalR group, so a correct run will not go red from mere slowness; the genuine flakiness is the inherent unreliability of asserting a negative via a short fixed timeout, which is exactly the dimension claimed.

Remediation (approach)

Assert the authorization decision directly and deterministically (e.g. confirm the outsider was never added to the channel's SignalR group / membership set) rather than inferring it from the absence of an event within an arbitrary window; keep a short event-wait only as a secondary check. Where a negative wait is unavoidable, document the chosen timeout as a deliberate flake/latency trade-off.

Info severity 7
Info Data & Correctness EF entities & migrations CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

All DateTime columns are consistently mapped to timestamptz — verified clean

src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:114 DateTime column types (timestamptz)
Evidence
e.Property(x => x.TimestampUtc).HasColumnType("timestamp with time zone");
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

This is a positive verification rather than a defect. Every DateTime/DateTime? property across the entity model is *Utc-suffixed (a grep for a non-Utc `public DateTime` property on entities returned no matches) and each is explicitly mapped to `timestamp with time zone` in OnModelCreating. Under Npgsql this avoids the classic 'Cannot write DateTime with Kind=Unspecified to timestamp with time zone' / silent local-time-drift failure mode, so the time-handling dimension is sound and worth recording as verified-clean.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Api/Data/UsageDbContext.cs:114  e.Property(x => x.TimestampUtc).HasColumnType("timestamp with time zone");  — plus grep of src/Api entities shows every DateTime/DateTime? property is *Utc-suffixed (e.g. Entities/AppUser.cs:122 public DateTime CreatedUtc; ActivityComment.cs:35 public DateTime? EditedUtc), and UsageDbContext.cs contains 150 timestamp-with-time-zone mappings.

The evidence line matches exactly (line 114). The broader claim holds: no non-Utc `public DateTime` property exists on any entity (all are *Utc-suffixed), and OnModelCreating maps DateTime columns to `timestamp with time zone` (150 occurrences). This is a positive verified-clean note, not a defect, so Info severity is correct. Confirmed as an accurate verification of the time-handling dimension.

Remediation (approach)

No change needed. Keep the convention (every persisted instant is *Utc + timestamptz) as new entities are added; consider a model-building convention to enforce it automatically so a future entity can't regress.

Info Dependencies & Supply Chain Dependency hygiene CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

@angular/animations is a framework-deprecated package still declared as a direct dependency

src/Web/package.json:15 Dependency hygiene
Evidence
"@angular/animations": "^22.0.2",
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The lockfile carries an explicit deprecation notice for this package (package-lock.json:331: "@angular/animations is deprecated. Use `animate.enter` and `animate.leave` instead"). Unlike an abandoned third-party package this is a vendor-managed deprecation with a migration path, so risk is low — but it will be removed in a future Angular major, so continuing to depend on it accrues upgrade debt that will block the next framework bump. Flagging so it is on the migration radar, not as an active vulnerability.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/package.json:15  "@angular/animations": "^22.0.2",  |  src/Web/package-lock.json:331  "deprecated": "@angular/animations is deprecated. Use `animate.enter` and `animate.leave` instead. For more information see: https://v22.angular.dev/guide/animations."

Both claims verified against the actual files. package.json line 15 declares @angular/animations ^22.0.2 as a direct runtime dependency, and package-lock.json line 331 carries the vendor deprecation notice with the animate.enter/animate.leave migration path. This is a genuine, correctly-located, vendor-managed deprecation. Impact is accurately characterized as Info-level upgrade debt (not a vulnerability): it will be removed in a future Angular major and accrues migration debt for the next framework bump. Severity Info is appropriate. Note: @angular/material still depends on @angular/animations transitively, so it may not be fully removable until Material also migrates — but flagging the direct declaration for the migration radar is legitimate.

Remediation (approach)

Plan migration to the new `animate.enter`/`animate.leave` APIs per the Angular v22 animations guide before the next major upgrade, and drop the @angular/animations direct dependency once no code imports it.

Info Performance & Scalability Frontend performance CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Root App shell and 100+ desktop components use Default (CheckAlways) change detection while only the mobile/beta twins use OnPush

src/Web/src/app/app.ts:92 Frontend performance
Evidence
changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.Eager,
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

In Angular 22 ChangeDetectionStrategy.Eager is the (renamed) alias of Default/CheckAlways - the component is checked on every change-detection traversal. 108 components carry .Eager including the always-mounted root App shell, which also owns many document-level @HostListeners (pointermove, wheel, scroll, touchstart) and several rxjs polling timers (15s/20s/60s). Every one of those events/ticks triggers a full CheckAlways pass over the shell and its non-OnPush descendants, doing more work than an OnPush + signals design would. This is a deliberate, valid API choice (not a bug), and the app is signal-heavy so correctness is fine; it is flagged only as a scalability observation - the desktop pages forgo the CD-skipping that the newer OnPush mobile twins get.

Independent verifier — confirmation
src/Web/src/app/app.ts:92  `changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.Eager,` (imported from @angular/core, line 12). Root shell has document-level @HostListeners at lines 848-853 (pointerdown/pointermove/keydown/wheel/touchstart/scroll) and polling timers timer(15000,15000)/timer(...,20000)/timer(...,60000) at lines 513-569. Repo-wide: 108 files use ChangeDetectionStrategy.Eager; 184 files use ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush (the mobile/beta twins).

All claims verified against the actual code. Line 92 uses ChangeDetectionStrategy.Eager (Angular 22's renamed alias of Default/CheckAlways), imported from @angular/core. The always-mounted root App shell owns 6 document-level HostListeners (pointermove/wheel/scroll/touchstart at 848-853) plus 15s/20s/60s rxjs polling timers (513-569), each of which triggers a full CheckAlways traversal over the shell and its non-OnPush descendants. Grep confirms 108 files carry .Eager while 184 (the mobile/beta twins) use OnPush. This is a legitimate scalability observation, not a defect: it is a valid deliberate API choice, the app is signal-heavy so correctness is unaffected, and Angular's global CD is largely optimized. Info severity is correct — flagged only as an efficiency note, not a bug. Location and severity are accurate.

Remediation (approach)

Treat as an optimization backlog item, not a correctness fix: since the codebase already reads all view state through signals, migrating the hot always-mounted components (the App shell first, then dashboard/chat/tracker) to ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush would let Angular skip their subtrees except when an input or a template-read signal changes, cutting the per-event/per-tick work. Verify each candidate has no template expression depending on un-tracked mutable state before switching. Angular's own onpush-zoneless-migration tooling (present in node_modules) can assist.

Info Security Endpoints: family / health / identity CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

POST /api/family/identity/auto/apply persists a time-entry Date with no out-of-range guard, unlike every sibling write path

src/Api/Endpoints/IdentityEndpoints.cs:206-212 Input validation / owner-data quality
Evidence
db.IdentityTimeEntries.Add(new IdentityTimeEntry
{
    UserEmail = caller.Email,
    UserId = caller.Id,
    RoleId = it.RoleId,
    Date = toDate,
    Minutes = Math.Clamp(minutes, 1, 1440),
    Source = IdentityEntrySource.Auto,
    SourceEventId = sourceId,
    ...
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The auto-apply write stamps Date = toDate, where toDate = DateOnly.FromDateTime(to) and (from, to) come from Window(req.FromUtc, req.ToUtc) (lines 171-173). Window() only clamps the *span* between from and to (to <= 366 days) — it never bounds the absolute value of to, so a client can pass toUtc far in the future (or, via the from-relative default, a date derived from an arbitrary caller value) and the entry is persisted without the DateOutOfRange(date) check that guards POST /time (line 316), PATCH /time (line 361), and import/commit (line 509, which explicitly skips it.Minutes < 1 || DateOutOfRange(it.Date)). This is strictly the caller's OWN data (RoleId is verified owned; minutes are re-derived server-side), so there is no IDOR/cross-user exposure — the only effect is a self-inflicted out-of-range point poisoning the caller's own radial-web chart math, matching the exact class of 'fat-fingered year' the DateOutOfRange helper exists to reject everywhere else in the file.

Independent verifier — confirmation
IdentityEndpoints.cs:206 `Date = toDate,` (no DateOutOfRange guard) where line 173 `var toDate = DateOnly.FromDateTime(to);` and Window (811-818) only clamps the span: `if (end - start > TimeSpan.FromDays(RangeCapDays)) start = end.AddDays(-RangeCapDays);` — `end = toUtc ?? now` is never absolutely bounded. Siblings guard: line 316 `if (req.Date is not { } date || DateOutOfRange(date))`, line 361 `if (DateOutOfRange(date))`, line 509 `if (it.Minutes < 1 || DateOutOfRange(it.Date))`.

Verified against the actual code. Window() (lines 811-818) caps only the span between start/end (RangeCapDays), never the absolute value of end = toUtc ?? now, so a client can pass a far-future toUtc. toDate = DateOnly.FromDateTime(to) is stamped as Date at line 206 with no DateOutOfRange check, while every sibling write path (POST /time L316, PATCH /time L361, import/commit L509, and L744) applies DateOutOfRange — which exists (L803-807) specifically to reject a fat-fingered year that would poison the chart. Scope is strictly the caller's own data: RoleId is owner-verified (L196) and minutes are re-derived server-side (L195), so no IDOR/cross-user exposure — the only effect is self-inflicted out-of-range point in the caller's own radial chart. Info severity, location, and mitigation-gap reasoning are all correct.

Remediation (approach)

Apply the same DateOutOfRange(toDate) guard used by the other Identity write endpoints before adding the auto row (and in the ApplyAutoOneByOneAsync fallback), skipping/short-circuiting the apply when the resolved window-end date falls outside the accepted calendar range, so the auto-ingest path is consistent with the manual and calendar-import paths.

Info Security Frontend security CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

User-authored pay-me / share links are rendered with rel=noopener but omit noreferrer, leaking the app URL as Referer to arbitrary external payment hosts

src/Web/src/app/features/bills/public-bill.html:123-125 Frontend security (XSS / token / redirect)
Evidence
<a class="pb-pay__link" [href]="l.url" target="_blank" rel="noopener">
  <mat-icon aria-hidden="true">{{ l.icon }}</mat-icon>{{ l.label }}
</a>
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

public-bill.html is an unauthenticated, token-based public page. The pay-me link URL is bill-owner-authored and points to an arbitrary external host. With rel=noopener but no noreferrer, the browser sends the full public-bill URL (which contains the share token in its path/query) as the Referer header to that third-party host, disclosing the capability token to whatever domain the owner pointed the link at. The same pattern (rel=noopener only) is used for l.url in bills.html:204 and the markdown renderer correctly uses noreferrer, so the omission here is inconsistent.

Independent verifier — confirmation
<a class="pb-pay__link" [href]="l.url" target="_blank" rel="noopener">  (public-bill.html:123). URL source: bills.ts:103-105 url = p.cashApp/payPal/venmo (owner-authored arbitrary host). Page is unauthenticated /bill/{token} with token in path (public-bill.ts:22,39). Contrast markdown.ts:41 uses rel="noopener noreferrer".

All premises verified by reading the code. public-bill is an anonymous, unauthenticated view at /bill/{token} (public-bill.ts:22,39) where the share/capability token is a URL path parameter, so it appears in the page URL that becomes the Referer. The pay-me link href (l.url) is the raw owner-configured cashApp/payPal/venmo handle string (bills.ts:103-105) — an arbitrary, owner-authored external host. With rel="noopener" but no noreferrer, the full /bill/{token} URL is sent as Referer to that host, disclosing the token. The inconsistency is real: markdown.ts:41 and two fleet map links use noopener noreferrer, and the sibling bills.html:204 shares the same noopener-only pattern. Fix (prose): add noreferrer to the rel attribute (rel="noopener noreferrer") on the pay link so the Referer header is suppressed for these external navigations; consider applying the same to bills.html:204 for consistency. Severity correctly Info: exploitation requires the bill owner (already a trusted party holding all bill data) to maliciously point their own handle at a host they control to harvest a token that only grants access to the bill they themselves authored — low likelihood and limited impact.

Remediation (approach)

Add noreferrer to the rel attribute on these external, user-URL anchors (rel="noopener noreferrer"), matching the markdown renderer, so the share-token-bearing Referer is not leaked to third-party payment hosts.

Info Security Endpoints: tracker / AI / agents / misc CONFIRMED conf: medium 1 verifier

ask-act tracker_log relies on FRONTEND validation of the model-emitted 'kind', not server validation as the comment claims

src/Api/Endpoints/AiEndpoints.cs:1994-2007 AI prompt-injection into privileged actions (ask-act)
Evidence
// tracker_log's concrete /api/tracker/{kind} is
// re-derived by the FRONTEND from the (server-validated) "kind" param against its own frozen allow-list; the
// chip carries the base /api/tracker path so an unknown type is dropped (returns "").
...
"tracker_log" => "/api/tracker", // concrete /{kind} appended by the frontend from the validated kind param
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

The comment states the tracker_log 'kind' is server-validated, but the handler (lines 827-831) only re-derives the Endpoint from the action Type and filters chips via CallerMayAct — the model-emitted a.Params (including 'kind') is passed through untouched and the concrete /api/tracker/{kind} route is chosen by the frontend against a frontend allow-list. Because every downstream tracker write is itself owner-scoped and re-gated on tracker.self, a poisoned model output cannot escalate privilege or write cross-user; the risk is limited to a misleading in-code security claim (defense-in-depth is one layer thinner than documented). If the frontend allow-list is ever loosened, the server would not be the backstop it is described as.

Independent verifier — confirmation
AiEndpoints.cs:1994 "re-derived by the FRONTEND from the (server-validated) \"kind\" param against its own frozen allow-list" and :2002 "// concrete /{kind} appended by the frontend from the validated kind param" — but the handler at :829 `.Select(a => new ActAskActionDto(a.Type, a.Title, AskActEndpointFor(a.Type), a.Params))` passes the model-emitted `a.Params` (which holds `kind`) through untouched; no server code validates `kind` (grep for "kind" in AiEndpoints shows only comments in this flow). Downstream in TrackerEndpoints.cs every concrete route (`/food`, `/exercise`, `/weight`, ...) is `.RequirePermission(Permissions.TrackerSelf)` and owner-scoped, so the poisoned kind cannot escalate.

Verified against the actual code. The comment (lines 1993-1994, 2002) asserts the tracker_log `kind` is "server-validated"/"validated", but the handler (lines 827-831) only server-issues the Endpoint from `a.Type` (AskActEndpointFor) and gates chips via CallerMayAct on Type; the model-emitted `a.Params` including `kind` is passed through verbatim into ActAskActionDto with no server allow-list check. The concrete /api/tracker/{kind} route is selected client-side. Because every tracker write in TrackerEndpoints.cs is owner-scoped (caller from auth token) and independently re-gated on Permissions.TrackerSelf, a poisoned `kind` cannot escalate privilege or write cross-user. Real defect = an inaccurate in-code security claim (defense-in-depth one layer thinner than documented); no exploitable impact. Info severity and location are correct; anchoring to line 1994 where the "(server-validated)" claim appears (line 2002 repeats it).

Remediation (approach)

Either enforce the tracker_log 'kind' against a server-side frozen allow-list before emitting the chip (so the server, not the SPA, is the authority), or correct the comment to state the kind is validated client-side and that the true trust boundary is the individual tracker write endpoint's own gate/clamps. Prefer the former for genuine defense-in-depth.

Info Security & Correctness Reporter / agent / ingestion CONFIRMED conf: high 1 verifier

Precise GPS consent and graceful-null handling are implemented correctly

src/Agent/Services/GpsLocator.cs:63-95 GpsLocator privacy/consent
Evidence
var access = await RequestAccessWithTimeoutAsync(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(10));
if (access != GeolocationAccessStatus.Allowed) return null;
...
catch (OperationCanceledException) { return null; }
Why it matters (enterprise impact)

Not a defect. The locator requests OS-level consent (honoring the Windows location-privacy setting), degrades to null on any non-Allowed status, timeout, or exception, never throws into the caller, and coordinates are only attached when explicitly granted — the server otherwise falls back to coarse IP-geo. This is the correct privacy posture for opt-in precise location; recorded here as verified-clean for the consent dimension.

Independent verifier — confirmation
GpsLocator.cs:66-67 `var access = await RequestAccessWithTimeoutAsync(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(10)); if (access != GeolocationAccessStatus.Allowed) return null;` and :77-79 `catch (OperationCanceledException) { return null; }` and :52-53 `try { fix = await GetFixAsync().ConfigureAwait(false); } catch { fix = null; }`

The finding is a verified-clean Info note, and the code substantiates every claim. Consent is gated at line 67 (only proceeds on GeolocationAccessStatus.Allowed). Graceful null degradation is present on non-allowed status (67), sensor timeout (71-80 via CancellationTokenSource + OperationCanceledException), unanswered consent prompt timeout (89-95 → Unspecified → null), and any exception (52-53 catch-all; 57 host-callback guard). Coordinates (85-86) are reached only after the Allowed check, and null is returned when the point is unavailable (83). The method returns Task<...?> and never rethrows into the caller; TryGetFix is non-blocking. Evidence quotes match the actual lines read. This is correct opt-in precise-location privacy posture, appropriately recorded at Info severity. No defect.

Remediation (approach)

No change required. Ensure the server side treats agent-supplied coordinates as advisory (never trusts a client to assert another machine's location) and that the fleet UI clearly discloses precise-GPS collection to users, consistent with the opt-in model.

Prioritized remediation roadmap

Quick wins (low effort · high value)

  • Replace the committed "Starbucks" EmailRevealKey with a required, environment-injected secret and fail startup if it is unset in production.
  • Move activity.view, users.view, and all config-write manage permissions out of the open-signup defaultable set so self-provisioned accounts cannot auto-grant audit/PII/config access.
  • Add the missing index on UsageRecord.TimestampUtc (and the CostUsd/token sort columns) to fix the default sort and every records/CSV/calendar/heatmap/stats path.
  • Suppress auto-redirects on all secret-bearing outbound HttpClients (Gemini, Fitbit, Google, FatSecret, USDA, geocoders, webpush) and on the ingest-key clients to stop credential replay across redirects.
  • Switch IpGeoService to https and cap all external OAuth/geo response reads to prevent cleartext location transport and unbounded-allocation MITM.
  • Enable IMDSv2-required on the EC2 instance and add an egress allowlist to the security group to shrink the credential-theft and exfiltration surface.
  • Wire the deploy workflow to depend on the CI test job and add a concurrency guard so out-of-order :latest pushes cannot roll out untested or stale code.
  • Add unique-violation catch/handling and UTC-kind normalization to the cycle day-log and meds-log upsert paths to eliminate the unhandled 500s and Npgsql kind exceptions.

Strategic work (structural)

  • Redesign the observability/logging pipeline: stop capturing sensitive request/response bodies by default, replace the key-name/path allowlist with a positive-allowlist (log only explicitly safe fields) model, and separate audit logging from PII exposure so activity.view never surfaces health/GPS/payment data.
  • Stand up a real data-durability and DR story: managed or snapshotted Postgres (or automated pg_dump to versioned object storage), immutable image digests with tag-mutability disabled, tested rollback, and a decoupled non-DeleteOnTermination data volume.
  • Introduce production observability: centralized log aggregation, error reporting/alerting, metrics, correlation/trace-id propagation, and a real readiness healthcheck that verifies the database (retire the DB-free liveness stub used as the startup gate).
  • Enforce full account/session lifecycle: server-side disabled-account re-checks on every authenticated path, JWT/session-version invalidation that also tears down live SignalR connections, ingest-key revocation on user delete/disable, and a JWT signing-key rotation scheme decoupled from the at-rest token-encryption key.
  • Establish a meaningful test and supply-chain baseline: real frontend test coverage run in CI, negative/authz integration tests (unverified-email rejection, XFF spoofing, disabled-account access), NuGet/npm lock files with pinned SDK and SHA-pinned GitHub Actions.
