  • PresenceTracker (src/Api/Services/PresenceTracker.cs) is bounded: Online() prunes stale entries as a side effect on every read (lines 81-88) keyed by lowercased email, so the ConcurrentDictionary cannot grow beyond the active-user set — no unbounded growth.
  • RequestLogQueue and AiUsageLogQueue use Channel.CreateBounded (capacity 2000, DropWrite) — the request/AI paths never block and memory is hard-capped (src/Api/Infrastructure/RequestLogQueue.cs:13-18, AiUsageLogQueue.cs:13-18).
  • DiscordForwarder and WebPushForwarder queues are Channel.CreateBounded (2048 / 4096, DropOldest) — flood-safe, cannot grow unbounded (DiscordForwarder.cs:45-50, WebPushForwarder.cs:40-45); the per-user token-bucket dictionary is keyed by recipient email and is bounded by the user set.
  • The Filtered() WHERE columns (LocalDate, ProjectId, Model, Source, MachineName, ReportedByUser, IsSidechain) all have matching indexes in UsageDbContext.cs:129-136, so the filter predicates are index-served; only the ORDER BY column (TimestampUtc) and some sort columns are unindexed.
  • SummaryAsync, CacheEfficiencyAsync, FleetAsync, CalendarAsync aggregates are performed DB-side via GroupBy/Sum (translated to SQL), not client-side — the heavy aggregation stays in Postgres (UsageQueries.cs:55-62, 257-352, 382-391, 508-517).
  • FleetAsync resolves owner emails and machine metadata via two batched IN-list queries (db.Users .Where(lowerOwners.Contains) and db.MachineInfos .Where(rawNames.Contains)) rather than per-row lookups — no N+1 (UsageQueries.cs:398-426).
  • Chat channel-list build (ChatEndpoints.cs:455-551) avoids N+1: unread counts, last-message ids, last messages, and sender identities are each resolved in a single batched query keyed by the channel-id/email set, then joined in memory.
  • SearchEndpoints per-domain queries each use Take(perDomain) and the chat search uses correlated subqueries inside one translated query bounded by Take — results are capped, not unbounded (SearchEndpoints.cs:200-262).
  • CostRecomputeService uses set-based ExecuteUpdateAsync per distinct model and a single ExecuteSqlInterpolated for local-date rebucketing — no per-row SaveChanges loop (CostRecomputeService.cs:21-41).
  • ObservabilityEndpoints reads clamp their page size with Take(Math.Clamp(...)) and resolve related users via a single batched IN query — bounded, no N+1 (ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:33-40,49,177-208).
  • WriteRecordsCsvAsync streams rows via AsAsyncEnumerable rather than buffering the whole result set, so CSV export memory is bounded regardless of row count (UsageQueries.cs:600-616).
  • RequestLogQueue and AiUsageLogQueue are both Channel.CreateBounded (2000 cap, DropWrite) — sound, non-blocking, memory-bounded hand-offs; not unbounded.
  • DiscordForwarder and WebPushForwarder queues are bounded channels (2048/4096, DropOldest) — flood-safe. The DiscordForwarder per-user TokenBucket dictionary growth is a separate already-reported item; the channel itself is fine.
  • PresenceTracker._entries is a ConcurrentDictionary keyed by email that self-prunes stale entries in Online() (UsageQueries-style side-effect prune at lines 84-88) and is bounded by the distinct-online-user count — not an unbounded leak.
  • FeedEndpoints (/api/feed) batch-loads clap counts, my-reactions, and comment counts in three grouped queries over the page's event ids — no N+1; actor resolution is a single ResolveActorsAsync call.
  • ChatEndpoints.BuildChannelDtosForMemberAsync computes per-channel unread via one grouped query built from an expression-tree OR predicate (BuildUnreadPredicate) and resolves last-message + participants in batched queries — a well-engineered N+1 avoidance.
  • InsightsEndpoints.ComputeInsightsAsync reads each tracker series once, owner-scoped and bounded by a 30/90/365-day window with (UserEmail, LocalDate) indexes; rollups are in-memory over a bounded window — no unbounded materialization.
  • SearchEndpoints uses leading-wildcard ILIKE (cannot use a b-tree index) but every domain sub-query is owner/household-scoped and Take(perDomain <= 8) capped with an overall cap of 60 — bounded, acceptable.
  • UsageQueries Summary/CacheEfficiency/Projects/Machines/Models push their GROUP BY to the database (no per-record materialization); only the sessionization paths (Calendar/Heatmap/Stats) and the records grid materialize rows, and those go through the RowMaterializationCap.
  • RecordsAsync clamps pageSize to [1,500] and page to >=1, and pages with Skip/Take — pagination is present and bounded (the only gap is the unindexed default TimestampUtc sort, reported).
  • Public share read (/api/share/{token}) uses only SummaryAsync (DB-side GROUP BY, no row materialization), so the token-gated public path is NOT exposed to the 2M-row StatsAsync/FilteredCapped path.
  • WeeklyRecap and security-forward background passes loop per-candidate but run at most weekly / on small opted-in cohorts and advance high-water marks idempotently — acceptable background N+1, not a request-path hot spot.
  • PresenceTracker (src/Api/Services/PresenceTracker.cs) is bounded: the ConcurrentDictionary is keyed by lowercased email so it is capped at the distinct-user count, and stale entries are pruned as a side effect on every Online() read (lines 76-94). No unbounded growth.
  • RequestLogQueue and AiUsageLogQueue are both Channel.CreateBounded(2000) with FullMode=DropWrite (RequestLogQueue.cs:13-18, AiUsageLogQueue.cs:13-18) -- the newest entries are dropped under sustained burst, so neither queue grows without bound.
  • FleetAsync owner/machine resolution (UsageQueries.cs:398-426) batches lookups: db.Users.Where(u => lowerOwners.Contains(u.Email)) and db.MachineInfos.Where(m => rawNames.Contains(m.Name)) are single IN queries over the distinct keys, both backed by unique indexes on Email and Name -- no N+1.
  • InsightsEndpoints.ComputeInsightsAsync (InsightsEndpoints.cs:140-185) issues a fixed number of owner-scoped, date-bounded reads, each backed by a (UserEmail, LocalDate) composite index; the subsequent foreach loops iterate already-materialized date-bounded lists, not per-row DB queries -- no N+1 and no unbounded materialization.
  • SessionAsync (UsageQueries.cs:205-234) filters by SessionId (indexed) and projects into a DTO with a single query; the Project!.Name access is translated into a DB join, not a lazy per-row load.
  • CacheEfficiencyAsync (UsageQueries.cs:503-546) and the day/month SummaryAsync path perform a single DB-side GroupBy(Model)/GroupBy(LocalDate) aggregate and price in memory over a small per-model/per-day set -- the heavy lifting stays in the database, no per-record materialization.
  • RecordsAsync paging (UsageQueries.cs:551-591) correctly clamps pageSize to [1,500] and uses Skip/Take, so the paged read itself is bounded (the only issue is the missing sort index, reported separately).
Reporter / agent / ingestion 28
  • Secret separation is sound: ReporterConfig.Save (ReporterConfig.cs:96-119) always nulls Key before serializing config.json, LoadFile (line 85) discards any key found in config.json, and reporter events/console never emit the raw key — the key lives only in reporter.key.
  • Transport auth is correct: IngestClient/QuickAddClient send the key via the X-Ingest-Key header (not the URL), use a 100s/30s timeout, treat 401/403 as fatal (no retry loop that would hammer with a bad key), and back off exponentially on 429/5xx with a capped attempt count.
  • http-to-non-local-host cleartext-key risk is explicitly warned about in both Program.cs:56-57 and Run-UsageIqReporter.ps1:78-79; https is the default URL.
  • StartupRegistry (StartupRegistry.cs) is per-user HKCU\...\Run (no admin needed, no cross-user effect), quotes the exe path in the launch command (line 24) so a spaced path can't be argument-injected, and swallows policy failures into a soft false rather than crashing.
  • PowerShell key prompt uses Read-Host -AsSecureString (Run-UsageIqReporter.ps1:52) and only persists the key on explicit y/N consent; the key file is deliberately stored outside any repo (~/.usage-iq) to avoid accidental commit.
  • Unobserved/background exceptions are handled: App.xaml.cs wires AppDomain.UnhandledException, DispatcherUnhandledException (marked handled), and TaskScheduler.UnobservedTaskException (SetObserved) to a crash log; ReporterEngine.Emit and GpsLocator both isolate listener/gatherer faults so one bad handler can't tear down the engine.
  • Per-line JSON parse failures are contained: all three parsers wrap Deserialize/JsonDocument.Parse in try/catch and 'continue', so a malformed line is skipped rather than aborting the file or pass; LogScanner.SafeEnumerateFiles tolerates inaccessible directories.
  • Path handling in LogScanner honors a skip-list (.tmp/node_modules/plugins-backup) and only walks *.jsonl under configured roots; state writes (FileStateStore.Save, ReporterConfig.Save) are atomic temp-file+Move so a crash mid-write can't corrupt state, and re-sends are idempotent via server-side dedup.
  • MachineInfo (MachineInfo.cs) deliberately does not collect the public IP (server records observed client IP), and every hardware/registry/WMI probe is best-effort Try()-guarded, degrading to null off Windows or on failure rather than throwing.
  • GpsLocator.cs: precise-GPS acquisition is correctly gated behind Geolocator.RequestAccessAsync (OS consent + Windows location-privacy setting), bounded by 10s access + 15s fix timeouts, caches one fix per process, returns null on any denial/timeout/exception, and never throws or blocks the caller — the console reporter never sets GpsProvider so it never collects GPS. Consent/graceful-null handling is sound.
  • StartupRegistry.cs: uses per-user HKCU\...\Run (no admin, no cross-account impact), quotes the exe path in the registered command, guards read/write in try/catch returning false, and deletes with throwOnMissingValue:false. Registry autostart persistence is implemented safely for its scope.
  • FileStateStore.cs: state persisted via temp-file + File.Move atomic replace; corrupt/unreadable state falls back to a fresh store and the comment correctly notes server-side dedup makes this an optimization, not a correctness dependency — no crash on malformed state JSON.
  • ClaudeParser.cs / CodexParser.cs: both wrap per-line JSON parsing in try/catch and skip malformed lines rather than throwing, honor the ISourceParser contract, and are iterative (ReadLine loop) with no recursion — robust against malformed lines.
  • MachineInfo.cs: public IP is deliberately not client-collected (server records observed IP); every WMI/registry/NIC probe is wrapped in Try<T> degrading to null; Windows-only probes guarded by OperatingSystem.IsWindows so the record builds cross-platform without throwing.
  • App.xaml.cs: unhandled exceptions across AppDomain, Dispatcher, and TaskScheduler.UnobservedTaskException are all captured to ~/.usage-iq/agent-log.txt (UnobservedTaskException calls SetObserved), preventing silent process death — unobserved-exception handling is sound.
  • ReporterEngine.cs / AgentController.cs: engine event fan-out wraps every observer callback in try/catch so a misbehaving listener cannot crash the engine; GPS provider invocation is wrapped so a GPS failure never breaks metadata gathering; pass overlap is guarded by a SemaphoreSlim and cancellation is honored in both pass and idle.
  • Program.cs / ReporterConfig.cs: the raw ingest key is never echoed to the console; CLI bare flags are stripped before the valued-option binder runs; config.json load explicitly nulls any key found in config.json so a secret can never be trusted from the non-secret file.
  • Run-UsageIqReporter.ps1: prompts for the key with -AsSecureString, resolves key from param/env/file, and warns when Url is http:// to a non-local host that the key travels cleartext — the interactive prompt path handles the secret reasonably (the plaintext-arg-to-exe and plaintext-file-at-rest issues were already reported in prior rounds).
  • ReporterConfig.SaveKeyFile/Save use temp-file-then-Move atomic writes (line 116-118, 140-142); LoadFile explicitly nulls any key found in config.json (line 85) so a key can never be trusted from the non-secret file - the key/config separation itself is sound.
  • FileStateStore.Load tolerates corrupt/unreadable state and starts fresh (line 43); Save is atomic (line 59-61). Re-sending is documented as harmless because the server de-dups, so a lost/corrupt state file is an optimization loss only, not a correctness bug.
  • ClaudeParser and CodexParser both parse line-by-line with per-line try/catch that swallows malformed JSON (ClaudeParser line 29-31, CodexParser line 34-36), so a bad line never aborts the file; neither does an unbounded recursive walk.
  • GpsLocator correctly requests OS consent with a timeout, returns null on any non-Allowed status/timeout/exception, never blocks the caller (TryGetFix returns cached-or-null immediately and gathers on the thread pool), and caches the fix per process - the consent + graceful-degradation contract holds.
  • StartupRegistry writes only under HKCU\...\Run (no admin, no other users affected), quotes the exe path in the launch command (line 24), and swallows registry failures into a soft false rather than crashing.
  • IngestClient/QuickAddClient correctly treat 401/403 as fatal (no retry) and 429/5xx as transient with bounded exponential backoff; the raw key is never placed in any ReporterEvent or log line (verified across ReporterEvent.cs and LogLine usage).
  • MachineInfo probes are all wrapped in Try<T> and Windows-only WMI/registry calls are guarded by OperatingSystem.IsWindows(), so a probe failure degrades a single field to null rather than throwing; public IP is deliberately not collected client-side.
  • Run-UsageIqReporter.ps1 reads a saved key file rather than echoing it, warns on plain-http-to-non-local-host (line 78-80), and reads the interactive key as a SecureString; it stores reporter.key outside any repo under ~/.usage-iq. (The plaintext-CLI-arg and plaintext-at-rest issues were already reported in prior rounds and are not re-listed.)
  • App.xaml.cs installs AppDomain/Dispatcher/UnobservedTask handlers before startup and logs+surfaces crashes, and MainWindow bounds the live log to 1000 lines (line 17) so a long backfill can't grow the UI unbounded.
  • LogScanner.FlushAsync records file state only AFTER a successful push (line 154-155), so an interrupted push safely retries next pass - the commit-after-send ordering is correct.
CI/CD pipelines & containers 28
  • Script injection: deploy.yml only interpolates ${{ github.sha }} (a trusted 40-char hex, inside a --comment string) and ${{ vars.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }} (a repo variable, used as an action input, not shell). Deploy runs only on push to main / workflow_dispatch, so no attacker-controlled PR title/branch/body is ever interpolated into a run: step. No script-injection vector found.
  • OIDC token permissions in deploy.yml are correctly minimized: permissions: id-token: write + contents: read only, scoped at the workflow with no broader write scopes granted.
  • Secrets are pulled from SSM Parameter Store with --with-decryption at deploy time and written to a .env created under umask 077 (remote-deploy.sh lines 39-54); no secrets are echoed to logs and no secret values are baked into Docker build args or image layers.
  • Both shell scripts (provision.sh, remote-deploy.sh) begin with 'set -euo pipefail' and quote their variable expansions (e.g. "$REGION", "$VPC", "$ECR"); no unquoted-variable word-splitting or curl|bash pattern was found.
  • TLS is not disabled anywhere: ECR login uses 'aws ecr get-login-password | docker login --password-stdin' over HTTPS, the SSM git clone uses an https:// URL, and Caddy provisions Let's Encrypt certs — no --insecure/-k or curl|sh over http.
  • The API runtime image (src/Api/Dockerfile) defines a proper HEALTHCHECK probing /api/health, which satisfies prod compose's 'web depends_on api: service_healthy' gate even though prod compose does not redeclare a healthcheck — so that dependency is not actually broken.
  • docker-compose.yml (local dev) uses only weak default credentials via ${VAR:-default} fallbacks (ccusage_dev_pw) that are dev-only; production credentials come from SSM, so the dev defaults are not a production exposure.
  • The ng-build-ci.mjs wrapper is a build-reliability shim (guarantees process exit) and does not weaken build integrity — it exits non-zero on compile errors and only exits 0 on the success marker or a verified dist; no security concern.
  • Deploy workflow's SSM roll-out polls invocation status and fails the job (exit 1) when the remote command Status is Failed/Cancelled/TimedOut, so a failed host-side deploy is surfaced rather than silently ignored.
  • deploy.yml script-injection surface is small: the only untrusted-ish interpolations are ${{ github.sha }} (a 40-hex commit id, not attacker-controlled text) used in an SSM --comment and a heredoc; no PR title/branch/body is interpolated into any run: block, so classic GitHub Actions ${{ }} shell-injection is not present.
  • deploy.yml top-level permissions are correctly minimized to id-token: write + contents: read (least privilege for OIDC + checkout); no broad write-all.
  • cloudformation.yaml DeployPolicy scopes the OIDC role tightly: ECR push limited to the two repo ARNs, ssm:SendCommand limited to the single instance ARN + AWS-RunShellScript document; EcrAuth GetAuthorizationToken requiring Resource '*' is an AWS API constraint, not over-broad grant.
  • Both shell scripts correctly use `set -euo pipefail`; provision.sh quotes its variable expansions ($REGION/$VPC/$SUBNET/$name) consistently.
  • ECR login uses `get-login-password | docker login --password-stdin` — the token is not exposed as a CLI argument or echoed to logs.
  • src/.dockerignore excludes Web/, Reporter/, bin/, obj/, *.user, Dockerfiles and .dockerignore, so the known 'API build context is all of src' concern is at least mitigated against pulling the web tree and build outputs into API layers; no secret env files were found in the API context.
  • API Dockerfile uses a multi-stage build with restore-then-copy layer caching and a proper HEALTHCHECK; no secrets are passed via ARG/ENV or baked into layers.
  • docker-compose.prod.yml passes secrets via environment referencing .env variables rather than hardcoding them in the compose file; db has a valid pg_isready healthcheck and api depends on db: service_healthy.
  • nginx.conf sets sound security response headers (X-Frame-Options DENY, X-Content-Type-Options nosniff, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy) and correctly marks index.html no-cache while long-caching fingerprinted assets.
  • Base images are pinned to reasonable minor tags (postgres:16-alpine, node:22-alpine, nginx:alpine, caddy:2-alpine, dotnet 9.0) rather than bare :latest; EBS root volume is Encrypted:true and the security group exposes only 80/443 with no SSH.
  • deploy.yml: the SSM --comment uses ${{ github.sha }}, which is a 40-char hex commit id and not attacker-controllable, so this is not a script-injection sink; no untrusted ${{ }} (issue title, PR body, branch name) is interpolated into any run: block.
  • deploy.yml permissions block is correctly minimized to id-token: write + contents: read at the workflow level; no over-broad write scopes are granted.
  • cloudformation.yaml: the GitHub OIDC DeployPolicy is tightly scoped — ECR push limited to the two named repos, ssm:SendCommand restricted to the AWS-RunShellScript document and the single project instance ARN, and kms:Decrypt gated by kms:ViaService=ssm; no wildcard resource on the write paths.
  • remote-deploy.sh uses set -euo pipefail, umask 077 before writing .env, and correctly fails closed when JWT or DB password is missing.
  • src/.dockerignore (the context root for the API image build) does exclude Web/, Reporter/, **/bin/, **/obj/, and *.user, so the previously-flagged 'entire src tree' concern is partially mitigated for build weight and stray project output — the remaining gap is the un-excluded local secrets file reported above.
  • .env is gitignored (.env and *.env), so the on-host git reset --hard origin/main during deploy does not clobber or expose the decrypted secrets file, and the secrets file is not tracked in git.
  • The API runtime Dockerfile installs curl solely for the HEALTHCHECK and cleans apt lists in the same layer; the API image does define a HEALTHCHECK (the missing-healthcheck problem is specific to the web/nginx image).
  • db service in docker-compose.prod.yml has a proper pg_isready healthcheck and api gates on db service_healthy, so DB start-ordering for the API is sound.
  • TLS: Caddyfile relies on Caddy's automatic Let's Encrypt with a configured ACME email; there is no curl|bash-to-shell or --insecure/-k TLS-bypass anywhere in the deploy scripts (the compose docker-compose binary download in cloudformation UserData uses https with -fsSL against a pinned v2.29.7 release URL).
Logging / PII / DTO exposure 27
  • GlobalExceptionHandler.cs (lines 8-21): returns only {title, status, traceId} with a generic message; exception + stack trace go only to logger.LogError (server-side). No stack trace or exception detail is written to the client. Program.cs confirms UseExceptionHandler() is always registered and Swagger/dev-exception behavior is gated to app.Environment.IsDevelopment() only — no detailed errors to prod clients.
  • AiUsageLog does NOT capture prompt or response content: GeminiService.Record() (lines 5628-5641) writes only email, feature, model, outcome, http status, duration and token COUNTS; the AiUsageLogDto (ApiDtos.cs:904-927) and the /api/ai-usage projection (ObservabilityEndpoints.cs:221-237) carry no prompt text. The XML doc's 'Carries NO prompt or response content' claim matches the code.
  • Email-privacy discipline in DTOs is consistent: response DTOs expose UserId + DisplayName instead of raw emails (PresenceDto, FleetUserDto, MemberDto, ChatMessageDto, ShareDto, RequestLogDto, AiUsageLogDto, NotificationDto, all Location DTOs). The only DTOs carrying another user's email (UserDto.Email, AuditEntryDto.ActorEmail/TargetEmail) are nullable and masked behind the X-Email-Reveal-Key gate, and their endpoints (/api/users, /api/audit) are in RequestLoggingMiddleware.BodyExcluded so those bodies are not persisted to the replayable log.
  • The admin email-bearing paths ARE correctly kept out of the persisted request-log body (RequestLoggingMiddleware BodyExcluded covers /api/users, /api/audit, /api/access-policy, /api/chat/contacts, /api/chat/directory) using segment-based StartsWithSegments matching, and the auth/notifications/shares/ingest-keys/family-calendar secret paths are whole-body redacted via LogRedaction.IsSensitivePath. Those specific mitigations are sound; the gap is the sensitive-but-not-listed health/GPS read paths (see High finding).
  • CapturingResponseStream (lines 15-22) caps captured bytes at maxCapture and never fully buffers large responses; ClientIp uses Connection.RemoteIpAddress (post-forwarded-headers) rather than a spoofable raw header (RequestLoggingMiddleware:142). These are sound.
  • MyDataExportService is owner-scoped end-to-end with secrets/other-user emails stripped (see Info finding) — export does not leak beyond the caller.
  • AiUsageLog captures NO prompt/response content: AiUsageLog.cs fields are metadata-only, and GeminiService.Record (GeminiService.cs:5620-5647) only ever sets ErrorHint to fixed strings ('HTTP {status}', 'timeout', 'non-JSON candidate', etc.) — never model input/output text. This concern in the dimension focus is verified sound.
  • MyDataExportService is rigorously owner-scoped: every query filters by the caller email/id; ShareTokenHash/ShareTokenEnc/OwnerEmail, ingest-key secrets, AES/TokenProtector material and Discord webhooks are never read; other people are reduced to DisplayName only (bills assignee/claimer, contacts) and never to an email or reversible AppUser id. Confirmed sound (consistent with the prior round's note).
  • Auth routes are fully protected from the action log two ways: /api/auth is in the middleware Excluded set (ShouldLog returns false so it is never logged at all) AND LogRedaction.IsSensitivePath whole-body-redacts it — the Google token in and issued JWT out never reach RequestLog.
  • Public share/bill-share token READ paths (/api/share, /api/bill-share) are excluded from logging, and the /api/shares + /api/ingest-keys management paths are whole-body redacted by IsSensitivePath, so live share tokens and raw ingest keys never land in RequestLog.
  • Per-user and system Discord webhook URLs are protected: /api/notifications is an IsSensitivePath (whole-body redacted) and the field names WebhookUrl/DiscordWebhookUrl (ApiDtos.cs:61,94) are also in the SecretKeys field regex; the plaintext webhook is encrypted at rest and never returned.
  • ObservabilityEndpoints /api/logs and /api/ai-usage both resolve raw UserEmail to AppUser id + display name and never emit the raw email in the response (email-privacy), and both are gated behind RequirePermission (ActivityView / AiUsageView).
  • GlobalExceptionHandler returns a generic 500 with no exception message or stack trace to the client; Swagger is dev-only (Program.cs:521) so the API schema is not exposed anonymously in prod.
  • ClientIp is taken from Connection.RemoteIpAddress after UseForwardedHeaders with KnownNetworks-bounded trust (Program.cs:340-347), so a direct caller cannot spoof the logged client IP via a raw X-Forwarded-For header.
  • CapturingResponseStream caps its in-memory copy at maxCapture bytes and forwards all writes to the real body, so large exports are not fully buffered; it disposes only its own buffer, not the server-owned inner stream — resource handling is correct.
  • PresenceMiddleware is best-effort and swallows all exceptions, never short-circuiting the request; it reads only the JWT email/name/picture claims and does not persist or log PII.
  • MyDataExportService is genuinely owner-scoped: every DbSet query filters by caller email/id (r.ReportedByUser == email, f.UserEmail == email, b.OwnerEmail == email, e.ActorEmail == email, etc.), the caller's own email is dropped from the manifest, bills omit ShareTokenHash/ShareTokenEnc/OwnerEmail and reduce assignee/claimer to DisplayName, and contacts expose DisplayName only — no cross-user or secret leakage found.
  • MyDataExportService.Csv() correctly RFC-escapes commas/quotes/newlines; the formula-injection angle was already reported in a prior round and is not re-raised.
  • GlobalExceptionHandler does NOT leak stack traces or exception messages to the client — it returns a fixed generic title/status/traceId body only. Sound on the client-facing side.
  • ObservabilityEndpoints /api/logs and /api/ai-usage both resolve UserEmail to {AppUser.Id, Name} and never emit the raw email in the response; AiUsageLog carries no prompt/response content (only feature/model/outcome/token counts).
  • AuthEndpoints and the whole /api/auth surface are covered by LogRedaction.IsSensitivePath, so login bodies (Google id_token in, JWT + caller email out) are whole-redacted to [redacted] before persistence.
  • The X-Email-Reveal-Key travels only in a request header, which RequestLoggingMiddleware does not capture (it records method/path/status/query/duration/bodies, not headers), so the reveal key itself is not logged.
  • No ResponseCompression middleware is configured, so CapturingResponseStream captures uncompressed plaintext and LogRedaction operates on readable JSON — redaction is not defeated by gzip/br encoding.
  • RequestLog column lengths match the middleware/redaction caps (Path trimmed to 2048 == HasMaxLength(2048); QueryString truncated to 4096 == HasMaxLength(4096); bodies stored as unbounded text), so oversized values will not throw and silently drop whole log rows.
  • AuditLogger stamps the acting admin and stores emails in ActorEmail/TargetEmail/Detail, but the /api/audit read endpoint masks ActorEmail/TargetEmail and runs Detail through MaskEmailsInText when the reveal gate is closed, and /api/audit is in BodyExcluded so its response is never captured into RequestLog.
  • PeopleEndpoints /api/people uses emails only internally for joins/presence and projects PersonDto with UserId + DisplayName (no email), so its capturable response body is email-free.
  • The two observability queues (RequestLogQueue/AiUsageLogQueue) use DropWrite bounded channels; the drop-visibility and Task.Delay-in-catch shutdown concerns were already reported in prior rounds and are not re-raised.
Frontend performance 27
  • Heavy libraries are correctly lazy-loaded. echarts is imported via tree-shaken ESM subpaths (echarts/core, echarts/charts, echarts/components) in src/Web/src/app/shared/chart.ts, and every feature page imports ChartComponent through lazy routes only (no chart import in the eager App shell), so echarts is never in the initial bundle. All the EChartsOption imports across pages are 'import type' (erased at build). Leaflet is dynamically import()ed on first map use via src/Web/src/app/shared/leaflet-loader.ts (memoised promise, CSS injected at runtime), and @microsoft/signalr is dynamically import()ed inside ChatRealtime.start() (src/Web/src/app/core/chat-realtime.ts, type-only top-level import).
  • Route-level lazy loading is thorough: every route in app.routes.ts uses loadComponent/loadChildren (public, beta, tail, and the page-registry-generated desktop/mobile twins). No eagerly-imported feature routes were found.
  • The app uses Angular's modern @for control flow exclusively (898 occurrences; zero *ngFor). @for mandates a track expression, so trackBy is compiler-enforced. Spot-checks of the hottest lists (chat messages track m.id, chat channels track ch.id, users filteredUsers track u.id, dashboard selects track by id) all use stable identity keys, not track $index on identity-bearing objects.
  • Dashboard charts are deferred with @defer (on viewport) with skeleton placeholders (dashboard.html lines 236-253), so echarts only initializes when a chart scrolls into view. The records table is server-paginated via mat-paginator (page size <=100), so it is not an unbounded list.
  • ChartComponent lifecycle is sound: it initializes echarts in afterNextRender, re-applies option/theme via a single effect, uses a rAF-debounced ResizeObserver to avoid 'ResizeObserver loop' churn, and disposes the chart + observer + cancels the rAF in ngOnDestroy (src/Web/src/app/shared/chart.ts lines 237-264). No chart-instance leak.
  • SignalR memory management is correct. ChatRealtime is a root singleton; the app shell owns its lifecycle via a symmetric effect (start on auth+chat.read, stop on logout / revocation / 401-403) in app.ts lines 588-594. stop() awaits connection.stop(), nulls the connection, clears all typing timers, and resets every cached signal (chat-realtime.ts lines 217-236). Hub .on() handlers are bound to the connection object which is discarded on stop, so they do not accumulate across reconnects.
  • Component-level rxjs subscriptions and timers are consistently cleaned up: the App shell and chat page pipe all timer()/router/route streams through takeUntilDestroyed(); the PulseTickerStore ref-counts its poll (attach/detach via DestroyRef) so the interval and notification debounce are cleared when the last widget unmounts (pulse-ticker.store.ts lines 92-125).
  • Bundle-size budgets are configured in angular.json (initial 1.25MB warn / 1.5MB error; per-component-style 44kB/56kB), production build uses outputHashing 'all' and disables source maps. echarts/leaflet are correctly whitelisted in allowedCommonJsDependencies.
  • Route-level lazy loading is comprehensive and correct: every authenticated page in core/page-registry.ts and every public/beta/tail route in app.routes.ts uses loadComponent/loadChildren thunks, so each page (and each per-device desktop/mobile twin) is its own lazy chunk — no eager route bundling.
  • Heavy libraries are lazily/tree-shaken loaded, not eager: Leaflet + its CSS are dynamic-imported on first map use (shared/leaflet-loader.ts), @microsoft/signalr is dynamic-imported only on first hub connect (chat-realtime.ts:163 with type-only top-level import), Google Identity Services is injected on demand (core/gis-loader.ts), and ECharts is registered via a tree-shaken 'echarts/core' + explicit charts/components list (shared/chart.ts) rather than the whole library, with every feature file importing only the erased `type EChartsOption`.
  • All 442 @for blocks across the app templates specify a `track` expression; there are zero legacy *ngFor usages, so the classic missing-trackBy list-diff cost does not exist.
  • angular.json defines production bundle budgets (initial 1.25MB warn / 1.5MB error; anyComponentStyle 44kB/56kB) and allowlists only echarts+leaflet as CommonJS deps, so bundle-size regressions are gated in CI.
  • The shared ChartComponent (shared/chart.ts) memoizes correctly: charts bind to `computed` EChartsOption inputs (e.g. dashboard mainChart()/modelChart()), the input().setOption effect only re-runs on option/theme signal changes, and afterNextRender inits before the effect can touch this.chart, so there is no double-initial-render and no per-CD-pass chart rebuild despite the parent being CheckAlways.
  • ViewportService (core/viewport.ts) drives mobile detection via matchMedia change listeners that only fire when a breakpoint is crossed — no resize-event spam scheduling change detection.
  • The dashboard usage table is server-paged via mat-paginator (pageSize signal, server page/pageIndex), and the family finance import-review rows are server-paged (finance.ts refresh pulls page.items), so those specific large tables are not unbounded DOM renders.
  • ChatRealtime correctly tears down all per-session state on stop()/logout (connection, typing timers, messages, channels, unread, notifications, live-notification, preferences), so the realtime cache does not leak across users — the growth concern is within a single long-lived session, not cross-session.
  • Mobile/beta twin components (184 OnPush files) uniformly use ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush, so the mobile platform is not affected by the desktop CheckAlways cost.
  • angular.json production budgets are sane (initial 1.25MB warn / 1.5MB error; anyComponentStyle 44/56kB) and allowedCommonJsDependencies only lists echarts + leaflet, both of which are handled correctly.
  • ECharts is tree-shaken via echarts/core with an explicit echarts.use([...]) registration (shared/chart.ts) rather than the whole-library import; only type-only EChartsOption imports appear across feature files, so the runtime lib is not pulled eagerly into every route.
  • Leaflet is fully lazy: shared/leaflet-loader.ts dynamic-imports the library + CSS on first map use with a memoised promise; only two map pages consume it and both import via loadLeaflet(). LocationMap disposes the map (map.remove) and disconnects its ResizeObserver on destroy, and reconciles markers with clearLayers rather than leaking.
  • @microsoft/signalr is kept out of the initial bundle: chat-realtime.ts uses a type-only import for HubConnection and dynamic import('@microsoft/signalr') inside start(), guarded so the ~35-40KB lib only loads for chat.read users who actually connect.
  • Route-level lazy loading is comprehensive: every public/beta/tail route and all registry pages use loadComponent/loadChildren; no eagerly-imported feature route components were found.
  • The new @for control-flow syntax is used throughout (776 occurrences) and Angular requires a track expression to compile, so trackBy is universally present; spot-checked templates all carry track keys (e.g. track it.id, track x.route).
  • chat-realtime.ts channel/message caches: the previously-reported unbounded-growth/re-sort issues remain, but the connection lifecycle itself is sound — stop() clears all caches and typing timers and nulls the connection so a new user never reuses the prior socket/token.
  • PulseTickerStore is ref-counted (attach/detach): the poll interval and notify-debounce timer are cleared on the last consumer detach, so an unmounted dashboard leaves nothing ticking; refresh() is throttled to 3s and coalesces notification bursts.
  • Search page (search.ts) debounces input (250ms), guards stale responses with a monotonic runSeq token, and clears results below the min-query threshold — no per-keystroke API storms or stale-overwrite.
  • meds.page.ts, finance.ts (categoryOption/ownerOption/trendOption/netWorthTrendOption), calendar, dashboard (mainChart/modelChart), and weight-trend all feed [option] from memoised computed() signals (weight-trend additionally wraps the chart in @defer on viewport), so those charts do not suffer the method-in-template rebuild problem — the defect is isolated to the two insights pages.
CloudFormation / Caddy / TLS 26
  • Security group ingress (cloudformation.yaml lines 49-52) exposes ONLY tcp/80 and tcp/443 to 0.0.0.0/0 — no SSH (22), no database port (5432), no other management ports are open to the internet. Postgres runs on the internal Docker network and is not exposed by the SG. This is sound.
  • Shell access is via SSM Session Manager (AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore), not inbound SSH — no port 22 rule exists, removing a major brute-force/exposure vector.
  • EBS root volume is Encrypted: true (line 95), so encryption at rest for the on-box data is enabled.
  • No hardcoded credentials or secrets appear in the template or Caddyfile: JWT key and DB password are stored as SSM SecureString parameters (README lines 14, 43) and read at runtime; the instance role's secret access is scoped to arn:.../parameter/usage-iq/* (line 73).
  • kms:Decrypt on Resource '*' (line 76) is meaningfully constrained by a kms:ViaService StringEquals condition limiting it to ssm.<region>.amazonaws.com, so it cannot be used to decrypt arbitrary KMS-protected resources — acceptable.
  • ECR push/pull deploy permissions (lines 135-145) are resource-scoped to exactly the two repos via !GetAtt EcrApi.Arn / EcrWeb.Arn; ecr:GetAuthorizationToken requires '*' by AWS design and is a read-only auth call — acceptable.
  • ECR repositories enable ImageScanningConfiguration ScanOnPush: true (lines 34, 41) — image vulnerability scanning is on.
  • ssm:SendCommand (lines 146-151) is scoped to the AWS-RunShellScript document and this specific instance ARN, not '*', matching the least-privilege intent for the deploy path.
  • No public S3 bucket or public-read resource is defined in the template; there is no S3 resource at all.
  • Caddy uses automatic Let's Encrypt HTTPS with ACME email configured (Caddyfile lines 1-4); TLS provisioning/renewal is handled by Caddy defaults (modern TLS, HTTP->HTTPS redirect) rather than a weak custom config.
  • deploy/cloudformation.yaml lines 74-77: the kms:Decrypt with Resource:'*' is correctly constrained by a StringEquals kms:ViaService condition scoped to ssm.<region>.amazonaws.com, so it cannot be used to decrypt arbitrary KMS keys outside SSM — this is a sound scoping choice, not a wildcard-abuse finding.
  • deploy/cloudformation.yaml line 95: the EBS root volume sets Encrypted:true, so encryption at rest is present for the host disk (including the pgdata docker volume that lives on it).
  • deploy/cloudformation.yaml lines 49-52: security-group ingress is correctly limited to 80/443 only; no port 22/SSH and no database port (5432) is exposed to 0.0.0.0/0 — Postgres is reachable only on the internal docker network.
  • deploy/cloudformation.yaml lines 145,150-151: ECR push/pull actions are resource-scoped to the two specific repo ARNs and SsmSendCommand is scoped to the specific document + this instance ARN, which is appropriately tight.
  • deploy/docker-compose.prod.yml: the db and api services expose no host ports (only caddy publishes 80/443), so Postgres and the API are not directly reachable from the internet.
  • deploy/cloudformation.yaml lines 34,41: ECR repositories enable ScanOnPush image scanning.
  • deploy/cloudformation.yaml: no hardcoded credentials or secrets appear in the template itself; jwt-key and db-password are generated into SSM SecureStrings by provision.sh and pulled at deploy time.
  • deploy/remote-deploy.sh line 39: umask 077 is set before writing the .env file, so the on-host secrets file is created with owner-only permissions.
  • Security group ingress is correctly limited to tcp/80 and tcp/443 from 0.0.0.0/0 only (deploy/cloudformation.yaml:49-51); no SSH/22 and no database port exposed to the internet - Postgres is only reachable on the internal Docker network. This part of the ingress posture is sound.
  • Root EBS volume sets Encrypted: true (deploy/cloudformation.yaml:95), so encryption at rest for the block device is present (the gap is durability/backup, not encryption).
  • ECR repositories enable ScanOnPush image scanning (deploy/cloudformation.yaml:34,41).
  • No hardcoded credentials or secrets are embedded in the CloudFormation template; JWT key and DB password are generated into SSM SecureString by provision.sh and only referenced by path.
  • ecr:GetAuthorizationToken uses Resource '*' (deploy/cloudformation.yaml:131-134), which is required by AWS (the auth token is account-level and does not support resource scoping) - not a least-privilege defect.
  • ECR push/pull actions are correctly resource-scoped to the two repo ARNs via !GetAtt (deploy/cloudformation.yaml:135-145) rather than '*'.
  • SSM GetParameter/GetParameters/GetParametersByPath on the instance role are path-scoped to arn:...:parameter/usage-iq/* (deploy/cloudformation.yaml:72-73), not account-wide.
  • No public S3 buckets or open resource policies are declared in the template; Outputs expose only PublicIp, InstanceId, and ECR URIs - no secret material (deploy/cloudformation.yaml:157-166).
Dependency hygiene 24
  • src/Web front-end lockfile is healthy and current: transitive versions resolved in package-lock.json are all on patched releases — esbuild 0.28.1, vite 7.3.5, postcss 8.5.15, cookie 0.7.2 (post-CVE-2024-47764), send 1.2.1, body-parser 2.3.0, http-cache-semantics 4.2.0, semver 7.7.4, tough-cookie 4.1.4, ws 7.5.11. No obviously-vulnerable old JS transitive stood out.
  • src/Web/Dockerfile uses `npm ci` (not `npm install`), so the front-end build is reproducible and honors package-lock.json integrity hashes — the JS supply chain is deterministic even though the top-level ranges use ^/~.
  • Front-end top-level direct dependencies are on current major lines: Angular 22.x, @microsoft/signalr 10.0.0, echarts 6.1.0, leaflet 1.9.4, rxjs 7.8, typescript ~6.0.3 — no visibly abandoned or years-stale direct JS dependency.
  • src/Ingestion/Ccusage.Ingestion.csproj has zero external PackageReferences (framework-only), so it carries no third-party supply-chain surface.
  • src/Agent/UsageIq.Agent.csproj declares no external NuGet PackageReferences — the WinForms tray dependency ships with the WindowsDesktop runtime, so the agent adds no third-party package risk.
  • src/Reporter/Ccusage.Reporter.csproj has no PackageReferences of its own (relies on the ReporterCore project reference), so no direct package risk there.
  • System.Management usage in ReporterCore is correctly guarded (OperatingSystem.IsWindows() per the csproj comment) and referenced for cross-platform compile — an appropriate, non-abandoned in-box package choice.
  • Test project correctly marks IsPackable=false, so its test-only dependencies (FluentAssertions, xunit, Testcontainers.PostgreSql 4.12.0, Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk 17.12.0) are not shipped to production.
  • src/Web/package-lock.json is present and uses lockfileVersion 3 with resolved URLs + SRI integrity hashes for every package (e.g. leaflet 1.9.4, echarts 6.1.0, @microsoft/signalr 10.0.0, jsdom 29.1.1, vitest 4.1.9), so the JS dependency graph itself is pinned and reproducible — the JS supply chain is materially healthier than the NuGet side.
  • Scanned the entire lockfile for `deprecated` markers: only one hit, and it is Angular's own vendor-managed deprecation of @angular/animations (migration path provided) — no abandoned/hijacked third-party packages surfaced.
  • Grepped the lockfile for BouncyCastle/Portable.BouncyCastle/bcrypt: no matches — the WebPush crypto concern is a NuGet-side (unverifiable here) issue, not a JS-side one.
  • leaflet resolves to 1.9.4 with a BSD-2-Clause license and echarts 6.1.0 with Apache-2.0 — both permissive and license-compatible for a commercial SaaS.
  • Ingestion (src/Ingestion/Ccusage.Ingestion.csproj) and Reporter (src/Reporter/Ccusage.Reporter.csproj) declare no direct PackageReferences of their own, so they add no independent third-party supply-chain surface beyond their project references.
  • The Agent project (src/Agent/UsageIq.Agent.csproj) declares no direct NuGet PackageReferences — the WPF/WinForms tray dependencies come from the WindowsDesktop shared runtime (UseWPF/UseWindowsForms), so it introduces no extra NuGet supply-chain surface itself (its transitive risk flows through ReporterCore).
  • Confirmed there is no NuGet reproducibility infrastructure anywhere in the repo: no packages.lock.json, no Directory.Packages.props, no Directory.Build.props, and no nuget.config (checked via Glob/Grep) — corroborating the round-1 non-reproducible-restore finding across every .NET project, not just the API.
  • The test project correctly isolates test-only tooling (coverlet.collector 6.0.2, Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk 17.12.0, xunit 2.9.2, Testcontainers.PostgreSql 4.12.0) with IsPackable=false, so those do not leak into any shipped artifact.
  • src/Web/package-lock.json exists (lockfileVersion 3) with resolved+integrity for every dependency, so the frontend graph IS reproducible and pinned by hash; CI uses `npm ci` with cache-dependency-path pinned to the lock file — the web install path is sound (contrast with the .NET side which has no lock file).
  • SignalR v10 transitive deps resolve to patched versions: ws 7.5.11 (past CVE-2024-37890), node-fetch 2.7.0 (past CVE-2022-0235), eventsource 2.0.2 (GHSA-6h5x-7c5m-7cr7 fix) — no known advisory hits at knowledge cutoff.
  • leaflet runtime resolves to exact 1.9.4 (BSD-2-Clause) in the lock; @types/leaflet 1.9.21 is dev-only type drift within the stable 1.9.x line — low functional risk since leaflet 1.9.x is patch-only.
  • jsdom ^29.1.1 resolves cleanly to a real published 29.1.1 and is dev/test-only (not shipped to users).
  • Test project pins are exact and current: Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk 17.12.0, xunit 2.9.2, xunit.runner.visualstudio 2.8.2, Testcontainers.PostgreSql 4.12.0, coverlet.collector 6.0.2 — good pinning discipline on the test-only tooling.
  • PDFsharp / PDFsharp-MigraDoc 6.2.4 and DocumentFormat.OpenXml 3.5.1 in the Api are exact-pinned (not floating) and the comment correctly documents the non-GDI cross-platform PDFsharp build choice for the Linux container; Swashbuckle.AspNetCore 10.2.3 is also exact-pinned.
  • Ccusage.Ingestion.csproj declares zero PackageReferences (framework-only), so it introduces no third-party supply-chain surface of its own.
  • The reporter/agent WMI dependency (System.Management) is correctly guarded by OperatingSystem.IsWindows() per the csproj comment and no-ops off Windows, so the cross-platform reference itself is not a runtime hazard (the issue is purely its floating 9.0.* version + lack of CI coverage).
Observability & ops readiness 24
  • Log redaction is thorough: LogRedaction.cs applies whole-value redaction for auth/notifications/shares/ingest-keys/calendar paths (IsSensitivePath) and field-level redaction of a broad secret-key list in JSON and urlencoded/query bodies before persistence; RequestLoggingMiddleware additionally drops bodies for email-bearing admin paths (BodyExcluded) and excludes live-token public routes (/api/share, /api/bill-share) from logging entirely.
  • Secrets are consistently kept out of logs across external providers: FitbitHealthProvider never logs token bodies (PostTokenAsync logs only the numeric status code), and Program.cs comments confirm Gemini/USDA/WorkoutX/FatSecret/OpenWeather/WebPush keys are sent only as headers/params and never logged.
  • Graceful degradation when external services fail is real and consistent: unconfigured providers degrade to configured:false / 503 rather than 500 (Fitbit IsConfigured guards, USDA/WorkoutX/Gemini 503-when-blank), and FitbitHealthProvider maps auth/rate-limit/error to typed statuses (HealthPullStatus) that back off without deleting the connection.
  • Background services isolate failures correctly: HealthSyncScheduler and the log writers catch exceptions per-tick/per-batch (with OperationCanceledException honored on shutdown) so a single failure never tears down the hosted service or the process.
  • The two observability queues (RequestLogQueue/AiUsageLogQueue) are correctly bounded with DropWrite and drained off the request path by background writers that batch-insert and prune to a fixed row window, so they cannot grow memory or the DB without bound (the only gap is the lack of a drop counter, noted as a finding).
  • Jwt:Key is validated fail-fast at startup (Program.cs:254-258 throws on missing/<32-byte key) rather than silently falling back to a known key.
  • The db service has a proper pg_isready healthcheck and the api service gates on db service_healthy before starting, and the api Dockerfile ships curl specifically so its HEALTHCHECK can probe — container-level restart: unless-stopped is set on every prod service.
  • ForwardedHeaders is configured with bounded KnownNetworks (RFC1918 ranges) so the logged ClientIp / rate-limit partitions reflect the real client and cannot be spoofed by an external caller — sound basis for the IP-keyed rate limiters.
  • GlobalExceptionHandler returns a safe generic 500 with no stack trace and logs the exception with method+path via structured ILogger — the shape itself is correct (the shipped-nowhere aggregation gap was already reported in round 1).
  • Both RequestLogWriter and AiUsageLogWriter correctly wrap the main loop, catch OperationCanceledException on shutdown to break cleanly, batch-insert, and prune to a bounded MaxRows window so the observability tables never grow unbounded (the Task.Delay-in-catch shutdown escape was already reported).
  • CapturingResponseStream correctly bounds its in-memory capture to maxCapture bytes while still forwarding the full body to the real stream, and disposes only its own buffer (not the server-owned inner stream) — no memory blowup on large CSV exports.
  • PresenceMiddleware is genuinely best-effort: it swallows all exceptions and never short-circuits the request, and runs after auth so User is populated — sound.
  • FitbitHealthProvider degrades gracefully on every failure mode (NotConfigured/AuthExpired/RateLimited/Error) and never throws a 500 into the sync/request path; token endpoint bodies are never logged; the rotating refresh token is re-stored on every refresh.
  • HealthSyncScheduler is correctly bounded (MaxConnectionsPerTick paging, MaxBackfillDays cap, per-connection cadence gate), stamps the cursor before pulling each day for crash-safety, and catches per-connection and per-tick exceptions so one bad connection can't kill the loop.
  • The rate limiter uses X-Forwarded-For-resolved RemoteIpAddress (UseForwardedHeaders runs first) for IP-keyed policies, and the ingest body-size cap is applied before model binding — both mechanically correct within the trust model (the XFF-trust caveat was already reported in round 1).
  • ObservabilityEndpoints /api/logs and /api/ai-usage are both gated behind RequireAuthorization + a specific permission (ActivityView / AiUsageView) and never expose raw emails (resolved to AppUser id/name) — the read side of the action log is access-controlled.
  • PresenceMiddleware (src/Api/Infrastructure/PresenceMiddleware.cs) is correctly best-effort: it wraps all presence bookkeeping in try/catch, never throws into the request, and runs after UseAuthentication so User is populated — sound.
  • AutoSyncBackgroundService (src/Api/Services/AutoSyncBackgroundService.cs) correctly guards EVERY await Task.Delay and the coordinator call with try/catch(OperationCanceledException) and breaks cleanly on shutdown, and falls back to safe defaults when the config read fails — a good contrast to the two log writers' unguarded catch-block Task.Delay.
  • IpGeoService.IsPublic (src/Api/Services/IpGeoService.cs line 84-109) is thorough: it excludes loopback, 10/8, 172.16-31, 192.168/16, 169.254/16 link-local, 100.64/10 CGNAT, 0/8, IPv6 link-local/site-local/unique-local (fc00::/7), and recurses on IPv4-mapped IPv6 — so it never leaks a private/LAN address to the upstream geocoder.
  • The fail-fast on a missing or <32-byte Jwt:Key (Program.cs line 254-258) is correct and prevents the API from ever starting with a forgeable signing key.
  • The 'discord' HttpClient explicitly disables AllowAutoRedirect (Program.cs line 80-81) to preserve the SSRF allowlist; the redirect-follow gap on other clients was noted in prior rounds, but this specific hardening is sound.
  • GlobalExceptionHandler (src/Api/Infrastructure/GlobalExceptionHandler.cs) does correctly log the exception with method+path and returns a generic 500 with no stack trace to the client — the client-facing traceId-not-in-log gap is a prior-round finding, but the redaction of internal detail from the response is sound.
  • CapturingResponseStream (src/Api/Infrastructure/CapturingResponseStream.cs) correctly bounds its capture buffer to maxCapture bytes while still forwarding the full body to the real response stream, and disposes only its own buffer (never the server-owned inner stream) — no memory-unbounded response buffering.
  • The RequestLog/AiUsageLog channels (RequestLogQueue.cs / AiUsageLogQueue.cs) are correctly bounded (capacity 2000, DropWrite, SingleReader) and the enqueue is non-blocking, so the request/AI hot paths never block on the DB writer — sound backpressure design (the missing drop counter is a prior-round finding).
Frontend architecture & duplication 22
  • Platform split is architecturally sound: page-registry.ts is a single source of truth driving routes, nav, and the home picker; toRoutes emits [mobileEntry(canMatch:isMobileGated), desktopEntry] with the desktop entry ALWAYS last as the universal fallback, so a device without the platform.mobile grant transparently gets the responsive desktop page.
  • platform.guard.ts isMobileGated correctly composes PlatformService.isMobile() AND AuthService.hasPermission(PERM.platformMobile); guards (perm/anyPerm/authOnly) are applied to BOTH the mobile and desktop route variants via the shared canActivate, so the mobile twin cannot bypass a permission gate.
  • PlatformService (platform.ts) is a clean, small, well-documented service: signal-based override persisted to localStorage with try/catch storage-safety, computed platform resolution, and a documented re-navigation (onSameUrlNavigation:'reload') to re-run canMatch on toggle.
  • State management is consistently signal-based across the feature set (268 files use signal()/computed(); only 1 file uses BehaviorSubject/Subject) — no fragmented mix of RxJS-subject stores vs signal stores.
  • tracker-beta's optimistic-tracker.ts is a legitimate composition wrapper over the shared core/tracker-store.ts (it injects TrackerStore and re-exposes its read signals), not a copy-paste re-implementation — the mobile perf layer reuses the shared store rather than duplicating it.
  • Both the tracker desktop and tracker-beta mobile twins consume the shared core/tracker-store.ts (verified via import grep), so the core tracker day/load/mutate state is genuinely shared rather than duplicated.
  • Several mobile twins DO reuse desktop domain logic by importing shared helpers (renderMarkdown, ReplayEngine, computeStats, pickImage, FINANCE_DEFAULT_CATEGORIES) rather than copy-pasting — the reuse mechanism is real, the only issue is the misplacement of those helpers inside desktop feature folders (reported separately).
  • No dead/unused feature components were found via the registry review: every PageDef in page-registry.ts references an existing desktop and (where present) mobile loader, and every mobile feature folder scanned maps to a registry entry.
  • page-registry.toRoutes() ordering is correct: mobile route entries are emitted BEFORE the desktop entry at the same path with canMatch:[isMobileGated], so desktop is always the universal fallback; a page with no mobile twin (none currently) or a non-granted device transparently gets the responsive desktop page. Verified no path can be shadowed incorrectly.
  • The Family hub's mixed shape (top-level `mobile` leaf = FamilyBetaPage + desktop `children`) resolves correctly: on mobile /family loads the beta leaf, while /family/<child> falls through to the per-child familyChild() mobile/desktop entries. No route-matching conflict.
  • isMobileGated correctly double-gates on PlatformService.isMobile() AND AuthService.hasPermission(platform.mobile); the graduation gate isMobilePlatform exists but is intentionally not wired. Guard is injection-context-safe.
  • chat-beta correctly SHARES the core ChatRealtime root service and the shared Api rather than re-implementing the SignalR connection — the connection/state layer is not duplicated (only the UI is), which is the right split.
  • The offline interceptor's status===0-only queueing hinge is sound, and isQueueableUrl correctly restricts /api/chat/channels/ to the /messages sub-route so chat edit/delete (/chat/messages/{id}, PATCH/DELETE) are NOT queued; the DELETE method in QUEUEABLE_METHODS is harmless because no whitelisted URL resolves to a DELETE.
  • PlatformService.setOverride re-navigates with onSameUrlNavigation:'reload' to re-run canMatch and swap variants, preserving query+fragment via router.url — the mechanism is correct (though it discards unsaved component/form state on toggle, an accepted trade-off, not a defect).
  • OfflineQueue is IndexedDB-backed with every browser API feature-detected and guarded (degrades to no-op), FIFO ordering by enqueuedAt, and re-entrancy-guarded flush — the storage/plumbing layer is solid apart from the mid-flush token-expiry issue reported.
  • platform.ts / platform.guard.ts: the desktop/mobile platform resolution (persisted override composed with viewport, canMatch = isMobile AND platform.mobile grant, desktop entry emitted LAST as universal fallback) is coherent and correct; setOverride re-navigates with onSameUrlNavigation:'reload' so canMatch re-evaluates.
  • toRoutes() in page-registry.ts correctly orders [mobile(canMatch), desktop] per path so non-mobile/no-grant users fall through to the responsive desktop page; family children inherit the parent family.use gate + providers as intended.
  • nav-model.ts derives the grouped nav and bottom tabs purely from PAGE_REGISTRY with a caller-supplied has(perm) predicate — desktop dropdowns and mobile drawer genuinely share one source and cannot drift.
  • home-options.ts + HOME_PERMS is a real single source for the home picker and canAccessHome(); HOME_PERMS is derived (Object.fromEntries) so the picker and access check cannot drift from each other.
  • search-meta.ts is a clean single source of truth (SEARCH_DOMAINS + O(1) lookup + FALLBACK_META) shared by the desktop search page, mobile twin, and command palette results — good example of the pattern the rest of the app should follow.
  • TrackerStore exposes a coherent mutate-then-reload API surface over Api and is the documented single owner of the tracker day; addFoods() uses Promise.allSettled with a single reload, which is sound.
  • offline.interceptor.ts correctly restricts queueing to a narrow append-only whitelist and to status===0 true-network-failures only, and strips Authorization before persisting (the queue re-attaches a fresh bearer at replay) — the core replay-safety hinge is sound.

Module map

Backend (.NET 9) — src/Api minimal-API + src/Reporter, src/ReporterCore, src/Ingestion, src/Agent

Counts exclude build artifacts under obj/. Solution namespaces are Ccusage.* (Api, Reporter, Reporter.Core, Ingestion) and UsageIq.Agent. Key boundaries: (1) Ingestion pipeline is split three ways — src/Ingestion holds the reusable JSONL parsers, src/ReporterCore holds the scan/upload engine, and src/Api/Ingestion holds the server-side write path (mapper + pricing + write service). (2) Auth is filter-based, not attribute/policy-based: PermissionFilter enforces the per-endpoint RequirePermission and CurrentHouseholdAccessor/CurrentUserAccessor enforce household scoping; IngestKeyFilter is a separate API-key path for agent/reporter ingestion. (3) Sensitive domains (Cycle/Meds/Vitals health data, Location/GPS/Fleet, Finance/Payments, PII in AppUser/Contacts) each have dedicated Endpoints+Entities and rely on the same permission+household gating. (4) src/Api/Endpoints is by far the widest surface (56 files) and maps to the SPA feature set nearly 1:1. Migrations are 86 real + 85 Designer files. TokenProtector + ShareTokenEnc migration indicate share-link tokens are encrypted at rest; LogRedaction + AiUsageLog show request/AI logging is scrubbed. The Agent is Windows-only WPF (net9.0-windows) and the only non-cross-platform project.

ModulePathPurposeFiles
Api.Endpointssrc/Api/EndpointsMinimal-API route groups — one file per feature domain (usage/ingest, chat, family hub, tracker, cycle/meds/health, finance, location/fleet, resume, wrapped, AI, agents, etc.), each mapping HTTP endpoints guarded by RequirePermission + household scoping56
Api.Servicessrc/Api/ServicesDomain/business-logic + integration services (Google auth/calendar, Gemini AI, USDA/FatSecret food, Discord/WebPush notifiers, cycle prediction, tracker stats, finance categorization, sync coordination, background workers)62
Api.Services.Healthsrc/Api/Services/HealthHealth-data provider integration — Fitbit provider/options, sync mapper and scheduler feeding sleep/activity/vitals5
Api.Data.Entitiessrc/Api/Data/EntitiesEF Core entity/enum types — the full domain model (AppUser/Household spine, usage records, chat, family, tracker/food/fitness, cycle/meds/vitals, finance, location, resume, PII/health tables)101
Api.Data (context/seed)src/Api/DataUsageDbContext (DbSets + model config), design-time factory, and pricing/exercise-library seed data4
Api.Migrationssrc/Api/MigrationsEF Core PostgreSQL migrations tracking schema evolution across all domains86
Api.Infrastructuresrc/Api/InfrastructureCross-cutting middleware/plumbing — global exception handler, request-log + AI-usage-log capture queues/writers/middleware, presence middleware, log redaction9
Api.Authsrc/Api/AuthAuthN/AuthZ — RequirePermission endpoint filter, ingest-key filter, permission catalog, current-user/household accessors, SignalR user-id provider, home-route resolution6
Api.Ingestionsrc/Api/IngestionServer-side usage ingestion pipeline — JSONL ingestion service, project resolver, usage-record mapper, pricing matcher, ingest write service6
Api.Hubssrc/Api/HubsSignalR real-time hub — ChatHub for in-app chat/notifications/presence1
Api.Dtossrc/Api/DtosRequest/response DTO records for bill, ingest, resume, search, AI, location, and public API endpoints7
Api (host)src/Api/Program.csComposition root — DI/service registration, auth+JWT config, middleware pipeline, endpoint mapping, SignalR/EF setup1
ReporterCoresrc/ReporterCoreShared reporter engine library — log scanner, ingest/quick-add HTTP clients, file-state store, machine-info, config/options/events consumed by the desktop agent and console reporter10
Reportersrc/ReporterConsole reporter host — CLI entrypoint that drives ReporterCore to scan CLI logs and push usage to the API2
Ingestion (parsers)src/IngestionShared source parsers — Claude/Codex/Gemini JSONL parsers behind ISourceParser, plus JSONL model records5
Agentsrc/AgentWPF Windows desktop agent — tray app (MainWindow/Settings/QuickAdd views + controller, GPS locator, startup registry) that runs the reporter and reports usage/location11

Frontend — Usage IQ Angular 22 SPA (src/Web/src/app)

Stack: Angular 22.0.2 standalone (no NgModules), TypeScript 6.0, RxJS 7.8, @microsoft/signalr 10 (lazy-loaded for chat), Leaflet 1.9 (GPS/maps), builder @angular/build:application with PWA service worker (ngsw-config.json). Auth flow lives in core/auth.ts + auth.interceptor.ts (Google sign-in -> app JWT), mirrored to backend RequirePermission via permissionGuard using the PERM catalog in core/models.ts. Platform architecture is the key pattern: core/page-registry.ts is the single source of truth. Each PageDef can carry a `mobile`/`mobileChildren` loader; toRoutes() emits a mobile route (canMatch: [isMobileGated], gated on the platform.mobile grant, off by default) ordered BEFORE the desktop entry at the same URL, so phones render the *-mobile twin and everything else falls through to desktop. Three parallel families exist: desktop features/<x>, gated redesign features/<x>-beta (on the beta-ui kit), and per-device features/<x>-mobile twins. Rough totals: features/ has ~110 directories. Desktop-only heavy hitters: family (73 files), tracker (56), tracker-beta (29), marketing (23), beta (17), family-beta/meals-beta/fleet-beta (~14-15). Most *-mobile twins are thin (2 files: component + template). File counts are approximate rollups from `find -type f`. PII/health/payments/GPS touchpoints on the frontend: meds/meds-mobile + settings-health + cycle-mobile (health), bills/bills-beta (payments), location/locations-mobile/admin-locations-mobile + core/location-capture.ts + gis-loader.ts (GPS/Leaflet), family + users (household PII). These are the sensitive-surface entry points to prioritize in review.

ModulePathPurposeFiles
App bootstrap & configsrc/Web/src/appRoot standalone app: app.ts (root shell component, ~42KB), app.html (nav/layout), app.config.ts (providers: router, HTTP interceptors, SW), app.routes.ts (delegates to page-registry), app.scss.6
core/ — servicessrc/Web/src/app/core~30 injectable singletons: api.ts (typed HTTP client), auth.ts (Google->JWT session/permissions), theme.ts (light/dark + schemes), platform.ts + viewport.ts (mobile/touch detection), pwa.ts/sw-update.ts/push-notifications.ts (PWA), offline-queue.ts (queued mutations), chat-realtime.ts (SignalR, lazy), location-capture.ts/gis-loader.ts (GPS/Leaflet), tracker-store.ts/challenge-store.ts (feature state), command-palette.ts/command-registry.ts, nav-model.ts/mobile-nav.ts/home-options.ts/search-meta.ts, unit.service.ts/units.ts, tour.ts, haptics.ts, wake-lock.ts, client-info.ts, models.ts (PERM catalog + DTOs).30
core/ — guardssrc/Web/src/app/coreauthGuard (JWT session gate), permissionGuard + anyPermissionGuard (per-route RequirePermission mirror), platformGuard/isMobileGated (platform.mobile grant → mobile twin routing).3
core/ — interceptorssrc/Web/src/app/coreauth.interceptor.ts (attach bearer JWT / handle 401), offline.interceptor.ts (route failed mutations into the offline queue).2
page-registry (routing SSOT)src/Web/src/app/core/page-registry.tsCentral page table driving routes, nav, and home picker; expands each page into a mobile (canMatch isMobileGated) + desktop entry for same-URL render-per-device.1
shell/ (mobile chrome)src/Web/src/app/features/shellMobile app shell components: bottom-tab-bar, mobile-sidebar, mobile-topbar, theme-picker.5
Desktop feature pagessrc/Web/src/app/features/<x>~50 primary desktop features. Largest: family (73), tracker (56, food/fitness), marketing (23). Others: dashboard, agents, ai-usage, bills, calendar, chat, fleet, location, meal-planner, recipes, resume, users, wrapped, etc. Login/marketing/signin are the public/landing surfaces.300
Beta feature twins (*-beta)src/Web/src/app/features/<x>-beta~15 gated redesigned surfaces built on the beta-ui kit: dashboard-beta (10), tracker-beta (29), family-beta (15), meals-beta (14), fleet-beta (14), ask-beta, automations-beta, bills-beta, chat-beta, people-beta, trophies-beta, wrapped-beta.150
beta-ui kitsrc/Web/src/app/features/beta-uiShared Strata/FiMobile design-token kit + primitives consumed by the -beta and -mobile twins.22
Mobile feature twins (*-mobile)src/Web/src/app/features/<x>-mobile~45 per-device mobile twins matched via isMobileGated (mostly 2 files each): today-, calendar-, agents-, meds-, cycle-, family-*-, insights-, journal-, users-, profile-, search-, tracker-profile-, etc. Convention: one mobile twin per desktop page.90
shared/src/Web/src/app/sharedShared standalone UI components/pipes/directives reused across desktop and twins.20
Build & PWA configsrc/Webangular.json (@angular/build:application, 1.25MB warn budget), tsconfig.*.json, ngsw-config.json (service worker), proxy.conf.json (dev API proxy), nginx.conf + Dockerfile (prod serve), tools/ (beta-shot screenshot harness).10

Infrastructure / CI-CD / Tests

Two workflows only (ci.yml, deploy.yml); no separate lint/security workflow. Deploy path is fully AWS-native: OIDC -> ECR push -> SSM RunShellScript -> remote-deploy.sh -> compose pull/up on one t3.small EC2, with Caddy for TLS. Secrets flow: appsettings.Local.json (git-ignored, baked into image at build) for local, SSM Parameter Store SecureStrings (/usage-iq/*) for prod — nothing sensitive committed. Note appsettings.json ships a plaintext default 'EmailRevealKey: Starbucks' (overridable via env) which gates real-email visibility on the Users page — a weak default worth flagging given PII scope. One test project only (Ccusage.Api.Tests); no dedicated tests for Web (Angular), Reporter, ReporterCore, Ingestion, or the WPF agent — CI only build-checks those. Integration tests require a Docker daemon (Testcontainers). File counts exclude bin/obj artifacts, which dominate raw glob results. Duplicate infra files exist under .claude/worktrees/* (agent worktrees) and are not part of the live tree.

ModulePathPurposeFiles
GitHub Actions — CI.github/workflows/ci.ymlOn push/PR to main: 4 parallel jobs — build .NET API, build Reporter, run xUnit unit+integration tests (Testcontainers Postgres on ubuntu Docker), build Angular 22 app (node 22).1
GitHub Actions — Deploy.github/workflows/deploy.ymlOn push to main / manual: OIDC-assume AWS role (us-west-2), build+push usage-iq-api and usage-iq-web Docker images to ECR (398152420190), then trigger rollout on the EC2 host via SSM RunShellScript (git pull + remote-deploy.sh), polling for success.1
AWS CloudFormation stackdeploy/cloudformation.yamlOne-shot IaC: single t3.small EC2 (Amazon Linux 2023 + Docker via UserData), Elastic IP, SG (80/443 only, no SSH — SSM Session Manager), 2 ECR repos (scan-on-push), SSM+ECR-read instance role scoped to /usage-iq/* secrets, and scoped ECR-push/SSM-send deploy permissions on the GitHub OIDC role.1
Provision scriptdeploy/provision.shOne-time bootstrap run in AWS CloudShell: generates jwt-key + db-password SecureStrings in SSM Parameter Store, finds/creates default VPC+subnet, and cloudformation-deploys the stack (idempotent).1
Remote deploy scriptdeploy/remote-deploy.shRuns on the EC2 host (via SSM): pulls all app secrets/config from SSM Parameter Store (JWT, DB pw, Google, USDA, FatSecret, WorkoutX, Gemini), writes .env, logs into ECR, and docker-compose pulls + restarts the prod stack.1
Prod compose stackdeploy/docker-compose.prod.ymlProduction 4-service stack on EC2: postgres:16-alpine (db), ECR api image (JWT/Google/CORS/tracker keys via env), ECR web image, caddy:2-alpine (HTTPS termination + 80/443); named volumes for pgdata + caddy state.1
Caddyfiledeploy/CaddyfileCaddy reverse-proxy config: auto-HTTPS via Let's Encrypt for $DOMAIN, gzip, reverse_proxy to web:80.1
Deploy READMEdeploy/README.mdHuman runbook documenting the AWS provision/deploy flow.1
Root dev composedocker-compose.ymlLocal dev stack: default brings up only Postgres (16-alpine, host port 5433); optional 'full' profile also builds/runs api (with read-only bind mounts of local Claude/Codex logs) and web; healthchecks + pgdata volume.1
API Dockerfilesrc/Api/DockerfileMulti-stage .NET 9 build (context=./src to include shared Ccusage.Ingestion): sdk restore/publish then aspnet:9.0 runtime + curl, HEALTHCHECK on /api/health, listens :8080.1
Web Dockerfilesrc/Web/DockerfileMulti-stage build: node:22-alpine builds the Angular app, then nginx:alpine serves dist/ccusage-web/browser with custom nginx.conf on :80.2
API configuration (appsettings)src/Api/appsettings*.jsonCommitted base config (DB, CORS, JWT issuer/audience, AutoSync, integration placeholders, email-reveal gate, log levels) plus Local.example template and git-ignored Local/Development overrides; all real secrets injected via env/SSM in prod.4
Reporter configurationsrc/Reporter/appsettings.jsonConfig for the remote reporter/ingestion agent: ingest Url + API Key, Machine name, Claude/Codex/State paths, BatchSize, poll IntervalSeconds (blank = filled at runtime).1
Env template.env.exampleCopy-to-.env template for local Postgres creds, host connection string, agent-log paths, timezone, CORS origin, and full-stack compose port overrides.1
API test suite (xUnit)tests/Ccusage.Api.TestsSingle .NET 9 test project (~120 source files: 76 integration via WebApplicationFactory + Testcontainers Postgres + fake Google token validator; 44 unit) covering auth, permissions, household scoping, PII/email-visibility, cycle/health, payments/bill-split, location, ingestion, and AI (Gemini) endpoints.120
